topics.title: Energy Management - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Management - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management (EM)** is the systematic, organized, and cost-effective planning, implementation, monitoring, and control of energy use in an organization, building, process, or system, with the primary objective of conserving energy resources, minimizing energy costs, and reducing environmental impact while maintaining or improving the desired level of service, comfort, and productivity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Energy Management** is governed by the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle and the **General Energy Management (GEM)** framework, which provides a structured methodology for organizations to continuously improve their energy performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Energy Management (GEM)** is the broader, organization-wide approach to energy management that integrates policy, planning, technology, and human behavior to optimize energy use across all operational levels of an enterprise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Energy Management like managing your personal monthly pocket money:**
> - You have a **fixed income** (energy supply/availability).
> - You have **many needs** — food, transport, gadgets (different loads in a plant).
> - Without a budget, you overspend and run out of money by month-end (energy waste, high bills).
> - With a proper **budget, tracking, and discipline**, you save money for future use (energy conservation, cost reduction).
> - Just like a personal budget, energy management requires **planning (audit), execution (controls), review (monitoring), and improvement (upgrades)**.

**In simple words:** Energy Management = *Using less energy to do the same (or better) work, while spending less money and harming the environment less.*

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Energy Management (Must-Know Glossary)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Layman Understanding |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy** | The capacity to do work or produce change; measured in **Joules (J)**, **kWh**, or **calories** | The "fuel" that powers every machine, light, and process |
| **Energy Conservation** | The practice of reducing energy consumption by using less of an energy service | Switching off the fan when not needed |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Using technology that delivers the same output with less energy input | Using a 5-star LED bulb instead of an old incandescent bulb |
| **Energy Audit** | A systematic inspection and analysis of energy use to identify savings opportunities | A "health check-up" for your factory's energy system |
| **Energy Manager** | A certified professional responsible for overseeing the energy management program | The "CA (Chartered Accountant)" of a company's energy bills |
| **Baseline** | The reference energy consumption against which future performance is measured | Your "starting weight" before a diet program |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Students must NOT confuse *Energy Conservation* with *Energy Efficiency*.
> - **Conservation** = behavioural/lifestyle change (use less).
> - **Efficiency** = technological upgrade (use smarter, not necessarily less).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Energy Management

The following physical units and metrics are universally accepted in KTU examinations:

- **Energy**: Joules (**J**), kiloWatt-hour (**kWh**), TeraJoule (**TJ**)
- **Power**: Watts (**W**), kiloWatts (**kW**), MegaWatts (**MW**)
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: kWh per unit of production
- **Power Factor (cos φ)**: ratio of real power to apparent power (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)**: a measurable value showing energy effectiveness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conversion (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ**
> 1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = **41.868 GJ ≈ 11,630 kWh**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Energy Management (KTU 2024 Framework)

Energy Management, as defined for the KTU 2024 syllabus, rests on **five operational pillars**. Each pillar has a specific role in the overall energy strategy of an organization.

**Pillar 1: Energy Policy & Commitment**
- Top management commits to energy efficiency in writing.
- Sets the tone, allocates budget, and defines accountability.
- **Why?** Without leadership commitment, no energy program survives past the audit stage.

**Pillar 2: Energy Planning & Audit**
- Conduct detailed **walk-through**, **preliminary**, and **detailed** energy audits.
- Establish a **Baseline Energy Consumption (BEC)** and **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)**.
- Identify **Key Energy Uses (KEUs)** — typically 80% of energy is consumed by 20% of equipment (Pareto principle).

**Pillar 3: Implementation & Control**
- Install **energy-efficient equipment** (5-star motors, VFDs, LED lighting).
- Apply **operational controls** (scheduling, switching off idle loads).
- Invest in **renewable energy** integration (solar PV, wind, biomass).

**Pillar 4: Monitoring, Measurement & Verification (M&V)**
- Install **sub-meters** on major energy-consuming equipment.
- Track **kWh consumption**, **power factor**, **load factor**, and **demand**.
- Use the **M&V protocol** (IPMVP — International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol) to validate savings.

**Pillar 5: Review, Corrective Action & Continual Improvement**
- Compare actual vs. planned performance.
- Apply **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.
- Document **cost-benefit analysis (CBA)** and **payback period** for every project.

### 2.2 The General Energy Management (GEM) Framework

The **GEM framework** is the structured approach recommended by the KTU 2024 scheme, broken into **8 sequential steps**:

1. **Commitment** — Top management pledge and policy framing.
2. **Energy Review** — Baseline establishment and EnPI selection.
3. **Energy Baseline (EnB)** — A quantitative reference for tracking savings.
4. **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** — Metrics like kWh/ton, kWh/m².
5. **Objectives, Targets & Action Plans** — SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) goals.
6. **Operational Controls & Training** — SOPs and staff awareness.
7. **Monitoring & Measurement** — Real-time data and analysis.
8. **Management Review & Continual Improvement** — ISO 50001-aligned review.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you are likely to need in the KTU 2024 ESE for Energy Management — Definition and its related numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | kWh, if P in kW and t in hours |
| 2 | $C = E \times r$ | Energy Cost = Energy × Tariff rate | ₹ (rupees) |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{output}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Energy Efficiency (η) | % |
| 4 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage Energy Savings | % |
| 5 | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Output Quantity}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 6 | $P_{apparent} = \sqrt{P_{real}^{2} + P_{reactive}^{2}}$ | Apparent Power (kVA) | kVA |
| 7 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{real}}{P_{apparent}}$ | Power Factor (cos φ) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Simple Payback | years |
| 9 | $\text{PF Penalty} = \text{Bill} \times \left(0.95 - \cos\phi\right)$ | Power Factor Penalty (if PF < 0.95) | ₹ |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} \times \dfrac{\% \text{Savings}}{100}$ | Absolute Energy Savings | kWh/year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Always keep the **time unit** consistent. Mixing seconds with kWh will cost you full marks. Convert: 1 hour = 3600 s, 1 day = 24 h, 1 year ≈ 8760 h.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy Management is not a textbook concept — it is actively used in:

- **Industrial plants** (steel, cement, textile, paper) to reduce Specific Energy Consumption.
- **Commercial buildings** (malls, hospitals, IT parks) to achieve **IGBC / LEED / GRIHA** green ratings.
- **Utilities** (electricity boards) for **demand-side management (DSM)**.
- **ISO 50001 certification** — the international standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS), analogous to ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 14001 (Environment).
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India, where industries are given energy-saving targets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Fundamental Energy Equation to Cost Savings

**Step 1: The fundamental energy equation**

Energy is the integral of power over time. Power, in turn, is the rate of doing work. For a constant-power device (a very common assumption in KTU problems), this simplifies to:

$$E = P \times t$$

**Where:**
- $E$ = Energy consumed (in kWh)
- $P$ = Power rating of the device (in kW)
- $t$ = Operating time (in hours)

**Step 2: Converting to cost**

Since every kWh consumed has a price (called the **energy tariff** or **unit rate**), the total electricity cost is:

$$C = E \times r$$

**Where:**
- $C$ = Total cost in rupees (₹)
- $r$ = Tariff rate in ₹/kWh

**Step 3: Substituting (1) into (2)**

This gives the master cost equation for a constant-power device:

$$C = P \times t \times r$$

**Step 4: Generalization to multiple devices**

If a facility has *n* devices, each with power $P_i$ and operating time $t_i$, the total energy cost is:

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times t_i \times r_i$$

**Step 5: Deriving the percentage savings equation**

Suppose the baseline energy is $E_{baseline}$ and the actual (post-intervention) energy is $E_{actual}$. Then the percentage savings is:

$$\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$$

**Step 6: Deriving the absolute energy saved**

Multiplying the percentage savings by the baseline gives the absolute energy saved:

$$E_{saved} = E_{baseline} \times \dfrac{\% \text{Savings}}{100}$$

**Step 7: Linking energy saved to cost saved**

The annual monetary saving is simply:

$$C_{saved} = E_{saved} \times r$$

**Step 8: Payback period derivation**

If a one-time investment of ₹ *I* is made to achieve annual savings of ₹ *S*, the simple payback period (in years) is:

$$\text{Payback} = \dfrac{I}{S}$$

This is the time after which the project "pays for itself." Projects with payback < 3 years are generally considered highly attractive in KTU valuation.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A factory has a 75 kW motor that runs for 8 hours/day, 26 days/month. The electricity tariff is ₹ 7.50/kWh. The factory installs a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), which reduces motor power consumption by 22%. Calculate:
- (a) Original monthly energy consumption (kWh)
- (b) Monthly savings (kWh)
- (c) Monthly cost savings (₹)
- (d) Annual cost savings (₹)

**Given Data:**
- $P$ = 75 kW
- $t$ = 8 h/day
- Days = 26 days/month
- Tariff $r$ = ₹ 7.50/kWh
- Power reduction = 22%

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Original monthly energy consumption**

$$E_{baseline} = P \times t \times \text{days}$$

$$E_{baseline} = 75 \times 8 \times 26$$

$$E_{baseline} = 15{,}600 \text{ kWh/month}$$

**Step (b): Monthly energy savings**

The power saved is 22% of 75 kW:

$$P_{saved} = 75 \times 0.22 = 16.5 \text{ kW}$$

The energy saved per month is:

$$E_{saved} = 16.5 \times 8 \times 26$$

$$E_{saved} = 3{,}432 \text{ kWh/month}$$

**Step (c): Monthly cost savings**

$$C_{saved/month} = E_{saved} \times r$$

$$C_{saved/month} = 3{,}432 \times 7.50$$

$$C_{saved/month} = \text{₹ } 25{,}740$$

**Step (d): Annual cost savings**

$$C_{saved/year} = 25{,}740 \times 12$$

$$C_{saved/year} = \text{₹ } 3{,}08{,}880$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love such 7-mark numericals in Part B. Always write down the **Given Data** separately, label every step with its purpose, and **box the final answer** in ₹ or kWh with units.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Inputs vs. Outcomes of Energy Management

| Input / Activity | Energy Metric Affected | Typical Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Power factor correction (PFC) | $\cos\phi$ | 5–15% reduction in kVA demand, penalty avoided |
| LED retrofit | kWh lighting load | 50–70% energy reduction in lighting |
| VFD on motors | kWh motor load | 20–40% savings in pump/fan applications |
| Insulation upgrade | kW heating/cooling loss | 5–20% reduction in thermal energy |
| ISO 50001 certification | All EnPIs | 10–30% reduction over 5 years |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Energy Management Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of an Energy Management System (EnMS), from policy formulation to continual improvement, in accordance with the ISO 50001 / GEM framework adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Commitment and Energy Policy] --> B[Energy Review and Baseline Establishment]
    B --> C[Identification of Key Energy Uses and EnPIs]
    C --> D[Setting SMART Objectives and Action Plans]
    D --> E[Implementation of Operational Controls and Technology Upgrades]
    E --> F[Monitoring Measurement and Verification M and V]
    F --> G[Analysis of Deviations and Corrective Action]
    G --> H[Management Review and Continual Improvement]
    H --> A

    subgraph SUPPORT_LAYER
        I[Employee Training and Awareness]
        J[Documentation and Record Keeping]
        K[Communication and Internal Audit]
    end

    E --- I
    F --- J
    G --- K
```

**How to read this diagram for your exam:**

- The **outer loop** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → A) represents the **PDCA cycle** embedded in Energy Management.
- The **subgraph block** represents the **supporting activities** that run in parallel — they are not one-time events but continuous processes.
- Each block is a **functional stage**, and arrows indicate the **information and decision flow** from one stage to the next.

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram: Definition in Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Energy Management - The Master Discipline]
    A1[Policy and Planning Layer]
    A2[Technical Implementation Layer]
    A3[Monitoring and Verification Layer]
    A4[Review and Improvement Layer]

    A1 --> A1A[Energy Policy]
    A1 --> A1B[Energy Audit]
    A1 --> A1C[Baseline and EnPIs]

    A2 --> A2A[Efficient Equipment]
    A2 --> A2B[Operational Controls]
    A2 --> A3B[Renewable Integration]

    A3 --> A3A[Sub-metering and Data Logging]
    A3 --> A3B[M and V Protocol]
    A3 --> A3C[Energy Accounting]

    A4 --> A4A[Management Review]
    A4 --> A4B[PDCA Cycle]
    A4 --> A4C[Continual Improvement]

    ROOT --> A1
    ROOT --> A2
    ROOT --> A3
    ROOT --> A4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** When a 7-mark question asks *"Explain the concept of Energy Management,"* you can use this hierarchy to structure your answer into **4 well-defined layers** and earn full marks for logical organization.

### 4.3 The PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PLAN - Establish Policy Objectives and Action Plans] --> D[DO - Implement Operational Controls and Projects]
    D --> C[CHECK - Monitor Measure and Verify Results]
    C --> A[ACT - Take Corrective Action and Improve]
    A --> P
```

This **4-stage closed loop** is the philosophical backbone of every Energy Management System. KTU examiners often test whether students can **map a real-world activity** (say, "installing a VFD") onto the correct PDCA stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Energy Management. List any four objectives of an Energy Management program.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Energy Management is the systematic and proactive process of planning, organizing, controlling, and monitoring energy use in an organization to optimize consumption, reduce costs, ensure reliability, and minimize environmental impact, while maintaining the desired level of service and productivity.

**Any four objectives (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
1. To reduce energy cost per unit of production (Specific Energy Consumption).
2. To minimize environmental impact by reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
3. To improve equipment reliability and reduce downtime.
4. To ensure compliance with statutory and regulatory energy norms (e.g., BEE PAT scheme).
5. To enhance the organization's corporate image and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) rating.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between Energy Conservation and Energy Efficiency. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Energy Conservation | Energy Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Behavioural / Lifestyle change | Technological / Equipment upgrade |
| Goal | Reduce energy **use** | Reduce energy **waste** per unit output |
| Example | Switching off lights and fans when not in use | Replacing a 100 W incandescent bulb with a 15 W LED bulb for the same light output |
| Effort | Requires **awareness and discipline** | Requires **investment and technology** |

> **Conclusion:** Conservation is the **"will"** to save, while Efficiency is the **"skill"** to save. Both are complementary pillars of Energy Management. (1 mark for clear conclusion)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **General Energy Management (GEM) framework** in detail. Discuss the role of the **PDCA cycle** in the Energy Management System. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase induction motor of rating **22 kW** operates for **12 hours/day, 300 days/year**. The motor has an efficiency of **88%**. The same work can be done by a **premium-efficiency motor (IE4 class)** with an efficiency of **93%**.

Calculate:
- (i) The annual energy consumed (kWh) by the standard motor and the premium motor.
- (ii) The annual energy saved (kWh) by upgrading to the premium motor.
- (iii) The annual cost saved if the tariff is **₹ 8.00/kWh**.
- (iv) The simple payback period if the cost of the new motor is **₹ 1,40,000** (assume installation cost is included).

**(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — GEM Framework (7 marks):**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** The General Energy Management (GEM) framework is a structured, step-by-step methodology that enables any organization — industrial, commercial, or institutional — to systematically manage and continuously improve its energy performance. It forms the foundation of **ISO 50001: Energy Management System (EnMS)**.

> **Block Diagram (2 marks):** The 8 sequential stages are:
> 1. **Commitment** — Top management's formal commitment and energy policy declaration.
> 2. **Energy Review** — Identification of all energy sources, uses, and past consumption patterns.
> 3. **Energy Baseline (EnB)** — A fixed reference point of energy consumption against which improvements are measured.
> 4. **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** — Selected metrics (e.g., kWh/ton, kWh/m²) to track performance.
> 5. **Objectives, Targets & Action Plans** — SMART goals with allocated responsibilities and timelines.
> 6. **Operational Controls & Training** — Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), employee awareness programs, and skill development.
> 7. **Monitoring & Measurement** — Sub-metering, data logging, and analysis using M&V protocols.
> 8. **Management Review & Continual Improvement** — Periodic review meetings to refine strategy and update the action plan.

> **Role of PDCA Cycle (2 marks):**
> The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle is the **engine of continual improvement** in Energy Management:
> - **Plan:** Define energy policy, set objectives, identify KEUs, and plan actions (covers stages 1–5 of GEM).
> - **Do:** Implement the planned projects, operational controls, and training (covers stage 6 of GEM).
> - **Check:** Monitor and verify actual vs. planned performance, analyze M&V data (covers stage 7 of GEM).
> - **Act:** Take corrective action, document lessons, and update the action plan for the next cycle (covers stage 8 of GEM).
>
> The cycle then **repeats** with refined goals, leading to **sustained, compounding energy savings** year after year.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The GEM framework, powered by the PDCA cycle, transforms energy management from a one-time project into a **continuous, organization-wide discipline** that delivers measurable cost savings, environmental benefits, and regulatory compliance.

> **Examiner's Note:** A clean, well-labeled block diagram is **worth 2 marks by itself**. Always draw it before writing the text.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Output power of motor: $P_{out}$ = 22 kW
- Efficiency of standard motor: $\eta_1$ = 88% = 0.88
- Efficiency of premium motor: $\eta_2$ = 93% = 0.93
- Operating time: $t$ = 12 h/day × 300 days/year = **3,600 h/year**
- Tariff: ₹ 8.00/kWh
- New motor cost: ₹ 1,40,000

**Step (i): Annual energy consumed by each motor**

First, compute the **input power** for each motor (since electricity is billed on input):

$$P_{in,1} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{22}{0.88} = 25.00 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{in,2} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{22}{0.93} = 23.66 \text{ kW}$$

Annual energy:

$$E_1 = 25.00 \times 3{,}600 = 90{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$E_2 = 23.66 \times 3{,}600 = 85{,}161 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step (ii): Annual energy saved**

$$E_{saved} = E_1 - E_2 = 90{,}000 - 85{,}161 = 4{,}839 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step (iii): Annual cost saved**

$$C_{saved} = E_{saved} \times r = 4{,}839 \times 8.00 = \text{₹ } 38{,}712 \text{ per year}$$

**Step (iv): Simple payback period**

$$\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{1{,}40{,}000}{38{,}712} \approx 3.62 \text{ years}$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** A payback of **~3.6 years** is **moderately attractive**. With most industrial motors running 10+ years, the project yields a net lifetime saving of approximately **₹ 3.87 lakh − ₹ 1.40 lakh = ₹ 2.47 lakh**, which is highly justified.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Energy Audit** in detail. Differentiate between **Walk-through Audit, Preliminary Audit, and Detailed Audit**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small-scale industry consumes **5,00,000 kWh per year** at a tariff of **₹ 6.50/kWh**. The management proposes two energy-saving measures:
- **Measure 1:** Power factor improvement from **0.78 to 0.95**.
- **Measure 2:** Lighting retrofit with a saving of **18,000 kWh/year**.

Calculate:
- (i) The original annual electricity bill.
- (ii) The percentage reduction in energy consumption after implementing both measures.
- (iii) The new annual electricity bill and the total annual savings.
- (iv) The combined annual savings in ₹, and the **return on investment (ROI in %)** if the total investment is **₹ 2,50,000**.

**(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Energy Audit (7 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An Energy Audit is a systematic, technical, and economic assessment of energy use in a facility, plant, or process, conducted to identify energy wastage, recommend energy-saving measures, and quantify potential cost savings.

> **Objectives (1 mark):**
> - To identify the **energy consumption pattern** of all equipment and processes.
> - To **quantify energy wastage** in terms of kWh and ₹.
> - To recommend **cost-effective energy-saving measures** (ECMs).
> - To **prioritize** investments based on payback and ROI.
> - To **comply with statutory requirements** (e.g., BEE norms for designated consumers).

> **Types of Energy Audit (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Preliminary Audit | Detailed Audit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Visual / Rough audit | Mini-audit / General audit | Comprehensive / Investment-grade audit |
| **Duration** | 1–2 days | 1–2 weeks | 2–6 weeks (or longer) |
| **Data collected** | Visual observations, rough data | Utility bills, nameplate data, brief measurements | Continuous monitoring, sub-metering, load surveys |
| **Instruments used** | None (visual only) | Portable instruments (lux meter, clamp meter) | Advanced instruments (power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, data loggers) |
| **Output** | List of obvious no-cost/low-cost measures | Quantified savings for short-listed projects | Detailed ECMs with payback, ROI, and tender specifications |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **When used** | First visit, quick assessment | Decision-making for further investment | Before major retrofit or ISO 50001 implementation |

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The choice of audit type depends on the **complexity of the facility, the size of the investment**, and the **stage of the energy management program**. Mature organizations progress from walk-through → preliminary → detailed audits in a phased manner.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Original annual energy: $E_1$ = 5,00,000 kWh/year
- Tariff: $r$ = ₹ 6.50/kWh
- Energy saved by Measure 2: $E_{saved,2}$ = 18,000 kWh/year
- Total investment: $I$ = ₹ 2,50,000 (combined for both measures)

**Step (i): Original annual electricity bill**

$$C_1 = E_1 \times r = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ } 32{,}50{,}000$$

**Step (ii): Percentage reduction in energy consumption**

Note: Measure 1 (Power factor improvement) primarily reduces **kVA demand** and **avoids penalty**, not directly kWh consumption. The main kWh reduction comes from Measure 2. However, kVA demand reduction can also slightly reduce line losses. For this KTU-level problem, we consider only Measure 2 for kWh reduction.

$$E_{saved} = 18{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 3.6\%$$

**Step (iii): New annual energy, bill, and savings**

$$E_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}000 = 4{,}82{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$C_2 = 4{,}82{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ } 31{,}33{,}000$$

**Step (iv): Annual savings in ₹ and ROI**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = C_1 - C_2 = 32{,}50{,}000 - 31{,}33{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}17{,}000$$

$$\text{ROI (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1{,}17{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 46.8\%$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** A **46.8% annual ROI** with a payback of approximately **2.14 years** makes this investment **highly attractive**. Additionally, the power factor correction (Measure 1) provides a non-tariff benefit — avoidance of the **PF penalty** charged by the electricity board whenever PF < 0.90 lag.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in KTU 2024 Energy Management exams — avoid these to score full marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing kW and kWh** — Power (kW) is instantaneous; Energy (kWh) is power × time. Always multiply power by hours to get kWh. **Many students write "75 kWh" for a 75 kW motor in 1 hour, costing 1 full mark.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor** — When a problem gives the **output power** of a motor, you must divide by efficiency to get the **input (electrical) power** that the utility meter reads. Skipping this step is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in 7-mark numericals.
>
> 3. **Confusing percentage savings with absolute savings** — If the question asks for "savings in kWh," do not give the percentage. Always convert % → kWh using the baseline.
>
> 4. **Vague definitions** — Writing "Energy Management means saving energy" scores 1 out of 3 marks. You must use KTU-2024-style **systematic, organized, cost-effective planning, implementation, monitoring, and control** — the full process-based definition.
>
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — A block diagram without labeled arrows and box titles scores **0 out of 2 marks** allotted for the diagram. Always label each box and arrow.
>
> 6. **Units missing in final answer** — Writing "Annual savings = 3,432" is incomplete. Always write "**3,432 kWh/year**" or "**₹ 25,740/month**". Missing units = **−0.5 mark per answer**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision summary — read this the night before your KTU exam.**

**Core Definition:**
- Energy Management = systematic planning, implementation, monitoring, and control of energy use to optimize cost, efficiency, and sustainability.

**Five Pillars:**
- Policy → Planning → Implementation → M&V → Continual Improvement (PDCA loop).

**GEM Framework — 8 Stages:**
- Commitment → Energy Review → EnB → EnPIs → Objectives & Plans → Controls & Training → M&V → Review.

**Must-Know Formulas (Top 5 for this topic):**
- $E = P \times t$
- $C = E \times r$
- $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$
- $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{BL} - E_{actual}}{E_{BL}} \times 100$
- $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$

**Critical Distinctions (favourite KTU question):**
- Energy **Conservation** = behavioural (use less).
- Energy **Efficiency** = technological (use smarter).
- Energy **Management** = the umbrella discipline covering both.

**Three Types of Energy Audit:**
- Walk-through (visual, 1–2 days) → Preliminary (mini, 1–2 weeks) → Detailed (investment-grade, 2–6 weeks).

**Key Standards and Frameworks:**
- **ISO 50001** — International standard for Energy Management Systems.
- **IPMVP** — International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol.
- **BEE PAT** — India's Perform-Achieve-Trade scheme.
- **PDCA** — Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle (engine of continual improvement).

**Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
- 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
- 1 TOE ≈ 41.868 GJ ≈ 11,630 kWh
- 1 year ≈ 8,760 hours

**Common Exam Pitfalls (avoid at all costs):**
- Mixing kW with kWh.
- Forgetting efficiency in motor-related numericals.
- Giving percentage when kWh is asked.
- Missing units in final answer.
- Unlabeled block diagrams.

**One-Liner for 2-mark rapid-fire questions:**
> *"Energy Management is the PDCA-driven, organization-wide discipline of optimizing energy use to minimize cost and environmental impact while maintaining productivity."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Principles of Energy Management and Energy Management Planning, Energy Audit: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Principles of Energy Management and Energy Management Planning, Energy Audit: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Energy Management (EM)

**Energy Management** is the proactive, organized, and systematic process of **planning, operating, and controlling energy use** in a facility or organization to accomplish two simultaneous objectives:
1. Minimize energy consumption and its associated costs.
2. Maintain or improve comfort, productivity, and operational efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Energy Management):**
> *"Energy Management is the strategy of adjusting and optimizing energy usage through systematic planning, monitoring, and control of energy-consuming systems, with the goal of conserving resources, reducing costs, and minimizing environmental impact while maintaining quality of service."*

The discipline is governed by the international standard **ISO 50001:2018 – Energy Management Systems (EnMS)**, which provides the framework for establishing the policy, objectives, and action plans.

## 1.2 Definition of Energy Audit (EA)

**Energy Audit** is the formal, systematic, and documented process of inspecting, surveying, and analyzing the energy flow in a building, process, or system with the objective of **identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs)** and quantifying the savings potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Energy Audit):**
> *"An Energy Audit is a comprehensive review and analysis of energy use in a facility, encompassing measurement, observation, and evaluation of energy-consuming equipment and systems, to recommend measures that improve energy efficiency."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor and Patient" Model

Think of **Energy Management** as a long-term *health and fitness program* for a building or factory. Just as a person follows a fitness regimen (planning, monitoring, exercise, diet, periodic check-ups) to stay healthy, an organization follows Energy Management to keep its energy "fitness" optimal.

**Energy Audit**, in the same analogy, is like a **comprehensive medical check-up**. A doctor:
- Takes medical history → equivalent to **reviewing past energy bills**.
- Runs diagnostic tests (blood test, ECG) → equivalent to **instrumentation and measurements**.
- Provides a prescription → equivalent to **Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)**.

| Medical Analogy | Energy Management Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Patient | Building / Industry |
| Doctor | Energy Auditor |
| Blood Report | Energy Consumption Report |
| Prescription | ECOs (Energy Conservation Opportunities) |
| Follow-up visits | Continuous Monitoring & Review |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Board (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT — Frequently Asked KTU Terms:**

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy** | Capacity to do work / produce change | **kJ, kWh, MJ, TOE** |
| **Power** | Rate of doing work or using energy | **kW, MW, HP** |
| **Energy Intensity** | Energy used per unit of output/product | **kWh/tonne, MJ/kg** |
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | Energy per unit of production or service | **kWh/unit, MJ/kg** |
| **Energy Efficiency (η)** | Useful output ÷ Total energy input | **% (dimensionless)** |
| **Baseline** | Reference energy use before modification | **kWh/year** |
| **Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO)** | Identified measure that reduces energy | **Quantified in kWh & ₹** |
| **TOE** | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | **41.868 GJ** |
| **kg of Oil Equivalent (kgoe)** | 10,000 kcal = 41.868 MJ | — |
| **EUI** | Energy Use Intensity (kWh/m²/year) | **kWh/m²/yr** |

## 1.5 Need for Energy Management (Why Study This?)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 Pillars Justifying Energy Management (Board Exam Favorite!):**

1. **Economic Pillar:** Energy costs are typically **20–40%** of total operating expenses in industries. Even a **5–10% reduction** in energy cost directly improves the **bottom-line profit** without increasing production.
2. **Environmental Pillar:** Every kWh saved reduces **CO₂ emissions** (~0.82 kg CO₂/kWh for Indian grid average). India committed to **Net Zero by 2070** at COP26.
3. **Compliance Pillar:** Energy Conservation Act 2001 (India) makes energy audits mandatory for **Designated Consumers** (DCs) — large industries consuming > 1.5 TOE/year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Principles of Energy Management

The **5 Pillars of Energy Management** form the foundational framework that KTU expects every student to remember. These are universally applicable to any facility — commercial, industrial, or residential.

### Pillar 1: Top Management Commitment (Sponsor Leadership)
- Without **executive sponsorship**, energy projects die.
- The top management must allocate **budget, manpower, and authority** to an Energy Manager.
- KTU frequently tests this: *"Energy Management starts with Management Commitment"* — 3 marks question.

### Pillar 2: Energy Baseline & Accounting
- A **baseline** is the established energy consumption against which future performance is measured.
- Energy accounting tracks *how much*, *where*, *when*, and *why* energy is used.

### Pillar 3: Performance Indicators (EnPIs)
- **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** quantify performance.
- Examples: *kWh/tonne of cement*, *kWh/m² of floor area*, *MJ/kg of product*.
- Used to track improvements after implementing ECOs.

### Pillar 4: Continuous Improvement (PDCA Cycle)
- Energy Management follows the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle of **ISO 50001**.
- Improvement is *iterative*, not one-time.

### Pillar 5: Awareness & Training
- Human behavior alone can save **5–15%** of energy with no capital cost.
- Operators must be trained to identify wastage.

## 2.2 Energy Management Planning (EMP) — The Step-by-Step Blueprint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMP is a 6-Step systematic procedure (Board Exam — Frequently Asked):**

| Step | Activity | Deliverable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Commitment** | Top management signs an energy policy | Energy Policy Document |
| **2. Energy Review** | Survey all energy uses, identify consumers | Energy Flow Diagram |
| **3. Baseline & EnPIs** | Compute baseline and select indicators | Baseline Report + EnPI List |
| **4. Target Setting** | Define SMART energy objectives | Objective Document (e.g., 10% reduction in 2 years) |
| **5. Action Plan** | List projects with budget, responsibility, timeline | Action Plan Matrix |
| **6. Review & Update** | Monitor, verify, correct | Monthly/Annual Review Reports |

## 2.3 Types of Energy Audits (KTU Critical Topic)

The **type of audit** dictates the depth, cost, instrumentation, and the level of technical expertise required.

### Type 1: Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary Audit)
- **Definition:** A one-day, visual inspection of the facility to identify glaring wastage and obvious low-cost fixes.
- **Duration:** 1–3 days.
- **Instruments:** None (visual inspection only).
- **Tool:** Flashlight, checklist, walkthrough.
- **Output:** List of obvious ECOs, potential savings estimation.
- **Use:** Quick assessment for small facilities, pre-screening.

### Type 2: Detailed Energy Audit (Mini-Audit)
- **Definition:** A focused audit on a specific subsystem (e.g., HVAC, lighting, compressed air).
- **Duration:** 1–2 weeks.
- **Instruments:** Portable power analyzer, light meter, thermometer.
- **Output:** Quantified savings, basic payback.

### Type 3: Comprehensive Energy Audit (Investment-Grade Audit — IGA)
- **Definition:** A complete, in-depth audit of the entire facility with detailed measurements, modeling, and financial analysis.
- **Duration:** 4–8 weeks.
- **Instruments:** Power logger, flue gas analyzer, thermal camera, lux meter, ultrasonic flow meter.
- **Output:** Comprehensive report with audited savings, M&V (Measurement & Verification) plan, bankable DPR.

### Summary Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Walk-Through | Detailed | Comprehensive |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Time Required | 1–3 days | 1–2 weeks | 4–8 weeks |
| Instrumentation | None | Basic | Advanced |
| Cost | Low (₹10–25k) | Medium (₹50–100k) | High (₹2–10 Lakh) |
| Accuracy of Savings | ±30% | ±15% | ±5% |
| M&V Plan | No | Optional | Yes |
| Bankable DPR | No | No | Yes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Energy Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in EVERY KTU Energy Management paper:**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | kWh |
| 2 | $\eta = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | Energy Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 4 | $EUI = \frac{\text{Annual Energy}}{\text{Floor Area}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | kWh/m²/yr |
| 5 | $CO_2 = E \times EF$ | Emissions = Energy × Emission Factor | kgCO₂ |
| 6 | $S_{annual} = E_{saved} \times C_{unit}$ | Annual Savings | ₹/year |
| 7 | $SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 8 | $ROI = \frac{S_{annual}}{I} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % |
| 9 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| 10 | $\text{TOE} = \frac{E_{GJ}}{41.868}$ | Conversion to Tonne Oil Equivalent | TOE |
| 11 | $1\,kWh = 3.6\,MJ$ | Energy Unit Conversion | — |
| 12 | $1\,HP = 0.746\,kW$ | Power Unit Conversion | — |

### Engineering Application Insights
- **Formula 3 (SEC)** is the *single most important metric* in industrial energy management. Lower SEC = more efficient.
- **Formula 7 (SPP)** is the *board's favorite*. ECOs with SPP < 2 years are auto-approved; SPP > 5 years need justification.
- **Formula 10 (TOE)** is essential for compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act 2001**, since DCs are notified in TOE.

## 2.5 Phases of a Detailed Energy Audit (Process Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board often asks: "List the phases/steps of an energy audit." (3 or 7 marks)**

**Phase A — Pre-Audit Phase**
1. Site walk-through.
2. Collect historical utility bills (12 months minimum).
3. Identify major energy consumers.
4. Plan the audit scope and instruments.

**Phase B — Audit Phase**
1. Install portable loggers on major feeders.
2. Conduct tests (combustion efficiency, lighting lux, compressed air leakage).
3. Interview operators and maintenance staff.
4. Build the energy balance.

**Phase C — Post-Audit Phase**
1. Analyze data.
2. Develop ECOs (Energy Conservation Opportunities).
3. Calculate savings, payback, ROI.
4. Prepare final audit report with recommendations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Energy, Power, and Cost

**Starting Principle:** Energy is the integral of Power over time.

$$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\, dt$$

For a constant power load (which is the standard assumption in KTU textbook problems):

$$E = P \times \Delta t$$

Where:
- $E$ = Electrical Energy in kWh
- $P$ = Electrical Power in kW
- $\Delta t$ = Time duration in hours

**Deriving Annual Cost from Energy:**

The cost of electricity in India is billed in *kilo-volt-ampere-hours* (kVAh) for industrial consumers. The conversion uses the **power factor (pf)**:

$$\text{Cost per kWh} = \text{Tariff (₹/kVAh)} \times pf$$

Therefore, **annual electricity cost** $C_{annual}$ is:

$$C_{annual} = E_{annual} \times T \times pf$$

Where:
- $E_{annual}$ = Annual energy in kWh
- $T$ = Tariff in ₹/kVAh
- $pf$ = Power factor (typically 0.85–0.95)

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Simple Energy & Cost Calculation)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A 5 HP motor runs for 8 hours/day, 300 days/year. The electricity tariff is ₹7.50/kVAh, and the operating power factor is 0.9. Calculate:
> (i) Annual energy consumption in kWh.
> (ii) Annual electricity cost in ₹.
> (iii) Specific energy consumption if the motor processes 600 tonnes of material per year.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Convert HP to kW**

$$P = 5 \times 0.746 = 3.73\,kW$$

**Step 2: Calculate daily energy consumption**

$$E_{daily} = P \times t = 3.73 \times 8 = 29.84\,kWh/day$$

**Step 3: Calculate annual energy consumption**

$$E_{annual} = 29.84 \times 300 = 8952\,kWh/year$$

**Step 4: Calculate annual electricity cost**

$$C_{annual} = 8952 \times 7.50 \times 0.9$$

$$\boxed{C_{annual} = 60{,}426\,₹/\text{year}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Specific Energy Consumption**

$$SEC = \frac{E_{annual}}{\text{Output}} = \frac{8952}{600}$$

$$\boxed{SEC = 14.92\,kWh/\text{tonne}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Simple Payback Period Calculation)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A factory installs a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) on a 10 kW pump that currently operates at a constant speed. The pump is required to run for 6000 hours/year. The VFD reduces average power draw to 6 kW. Tariff is ₹8/kWh. The VFD costs ₹1,20,000. Calculate the simple payback period.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Annual energy saved**

$$\Delta E = (P_{old} - P_{new}) \times t = (10 - 6) \times 6000$$

$$\Delta E = 24{,}000\,kWh/year$$

**Step 2: Annual monetary savings**

$$S_{annual} = 24{,}000 \times 8 = 1{,}92{,}000\,₹/\text{year}$$

**Step 3: Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}92{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{SPP = 0.625\,\text{years} \approx 7.5\,\text{months}}$$

**Inference (Board Note):** Since SPP < 2 years, the project is **bankable and auto-approved** under typical industry investment criteria.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right ECO

This matrix is what a *real* Energy Manager uses to prioritize ECOs.

| ECO Project | Investment (₹) | Annual Savings (₹) | SPP (years) | Priority |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Replace 100 fluorescent lamps with LED | 50,000 | 40,000 | 1.25 | 🟢 High |
| Install VFD on pump | 1,20,000 | 1,92,000 | 0.63 | 🟢 High |
| Heat recovery from boiler flue gas | 5,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 3.33 | 🟡 Medium |
| Solar PV rooftop (50 kWp) | 25,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 8.33 | 🔴 Low (long-term) |

**Decision Rule:** Implement 🟢 first, then 🟡, then 🟴. Note: Even long-term projects may be selected for ESG/CSR reasons, but only after high-priority projects are exhausted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Energy Management Planning (EMP) Lifecycle

This diagram depicts the entire EMP workflow as expected by ISO 50001 and the KTU Energy Management syllabus.

```mermaid

    stepB[Step 1: Top Management Commitment and Energy Policy] --> stepC

    stepE[Step 4: Set SMART Energy Objectives and Targets] --> stepF
    stepF[Step 5: Develop Action Plan with Budget and Timeline] --> stepG
    stepG[Step 6: Implement ECOs and Monitor Performance] --> stepH
    stepH{Achieved Target?} -->|Yes| stepI
    stepH-->|No| stepF
    stepI[Step 7: Review, Document, and Continual Improvement] --> stepJ
    stepJ([Energy Performance Sustained])

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

    style stepC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

    style stepE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comprehensive Energy Audit Process

This functional block diagram shows the three-phase audit methodology used by certified energy auditors.

```mermaid

        a1[Receive Audit Request from Client]

        a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4
    end

    subgraph PhaseB[Phase B: On-Site Audit and Measurements]

        b2[Measure Power, Light, Air, Heat Inputs]

    end

    end

    PhaseA ==> PhaseB
    PhaseB ==> PhaseC
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree Diagram: Classification of Energy Audits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Energy Audit Classification] --> branchB
    rootA --> branchC
    rootA --> branchD

    branchB[Walk-Through Audit]
    branchC[Detailed Energy Audit]
    branchD[Investment Grade Comprehensive Audit]

    branchB --> b1[Visual Inspection Only]
    branchB --> b2[Duration 1 to 3 Days]
    branchB --> b3[Output: List of Obvious ECOs]

    branchC --> c1[Focused on One Subsystem]
    branchC --> c2[Duration 1 to 2 Weeks]
    branchC --> c3[Uses Portable Instruments]
    branchC --> c4[Output: Quantified Savings Estimate]

    branchD --> d1[Entire Facility Covered]
    branchD --> d2[Duration 4 to 8 Weeks]
    branchD --> d3[Advanced Instrumentation]
    branchD --> d4[Bankable DPR and MV Plan]

    style rootA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```
