```mermaid
    style branchC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style branchD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Energy Management. List any FOUR principles of Energy Management.**

#### Model Answer:
> **Energy Management** is the systematic and coordinated approach of planning, controlling, and optimizing energy use in an organization to minimize energy consumption and costs while maintaining productivity and comfort. It is governed by **ISO 50001:2018**.

**Four Principles of Energy Management:**
1. **Top Management Commitment:** Without executive sponsorship, energy initiatives fail.
2. **Energy Baseline & Accounting:** Establish reference energy use and track consumption accurately.
3. **Performance Indicators (EnPIs):** Use metrics like kWh/tonne to measure improvement.
4. **Continual Improvement (PDCA Cycle):** Apply Plan-Do-Check-Act repeatedly.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Define Energy Audit. Differentiate between Walk-Through Audit and Comprehensive Energy Audit.**

#### Model Answer:
> **Energy Audit** is the systematic inspection and analysis of energy flow in a facility to identify energy conservation opportunities (ECOs).

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Comprehensive Audit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Duration | 1–3 days | 4–8 weeks |
| Instrumentation | None / Visual | Advanced loggers, analyzers |
| Output | List of obvious ECOs | Bankable DPR with M&V plan |
| Accuracy | ±30% | ±5% |
| Investment Grade | No | Yes |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Regulation Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Attempt **either full OR A (a) + B (b)** as a whole.

---

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Energy Management Planning (EMP)** procedure with a neat flowchart. Why is top management commitment considered the foundation of EMP?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An industry consumes **5,00,000 kWh/year** at a tariff of **₹8/kWh**. The plant operates **300 days/year**. The audit reveals that the compressed air system leaks **15%** of its total energy. Calculate:
(i) The annual energy wastage in kWh.
(ii) The annual monetary loss in ₹.
(iii) The annual CO₂ emissions avoided (in kg) if the leakage is fixed (use EF = 0.82 kgCO₂/kWh).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Energy Management Planning (EMP):**

The Energy Management Planning procedure is a structured 6-step approach (with optional 7th review step):

**Step 1 — Top Management Commitment:**
The Energy Policy is signed and communicated. A certified **Energy Manager** (per BEE / CII norms) is appointed. Without this step, no other step has authority.

**Step 2 — Energy Review:**
All energy uses are surveyed (electricity, thermal, fuels). A **Sankey diagram** is drawn to identify major energy consumers.

**Step 3 — Baseline & EnPIs:**
The **Energy Baseline (EnB)** is the reference energy use (e.g., 50,000 kWh/month). EnPIs are chosen, such as kWh/tonne of product.

**Step 4 — Target Setting:**
SMART targets are set: *"Reduce SEC from 14.92 to 13.5 kWh/tonne within 18 months."*

**Step 5 — Action Plan:**
ECOs are prioritized by Simple Payback Period (SPP). Responsibility and deadlines are assigned.

**Step 6 — Implementation & Monitoring:**
ECOs are executed. Data is logged and compared against baseline.

**Step 7 — Review & Continual Improvement:**
Management review meetings compare actual vs target. Corrective actions are taken.

**Why is Top Management Commitment the Foundation?**
- It provides **budgetary authority** and removes financial barriers.
- It establishes **organizational priority** over competing interests (production, sales).
- It enables **cross-departmental coordination** (Maintenance, Production, Purchase).
- It is the **mandatory clause of ISO 50001** — clause 5.1.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Annual Energy $E_{total} = 5{,}00{,}000$ kWh
- Tariff $T = ₹8$/kWh
- Leakage fraction = 15% = 0.15
- Emission Factor $EF = 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh

**(i) Annual Energy Wastage:**

$$\Delta E = E_{total} \times 0.15 = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 75{,}000\,kWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual Monetary Loss:**

$$\text{Loss} = \Delta E \times T = 75{,}000 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{\text{Loss} = 6{,}00{,}000\,₹/\text{year}}$$

**(iii) Annual CO₂ Emissions Avoided:**

$$CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF = 75{,}000 \times 0.82$$

$$\boxed{CO_2 = 61{,}500\,kg\,CO_2 = 61.5\,tonnes\,CO_2/year}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Fixing compressed air leaks typically costs ₹50,000–1,50,000 with an SPP of **less than 2 months**. This ECO is an immediate no-brainer.

---

### ❓ Question B (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three types of Energy Audits** in detail. Mention the instruments used in a comprehensive energy audit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 100 W incandescent lamp is replaced by a 20 W LED lamp in an office with 200 such fittings. The office operates 10 hours/day, 300 days/year. Tariff = ₹9/kWh. The LED cost is ₹400/lamp and the cost of installation per lamp is ₹100. Calculate:
(i) Annual energy saved (kWh).
(ii) Annual cost savings (₹).
(iii) Total investment and Simple Payback Period.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Three Types of Energy Audits:**

**1. Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary Audit):**
- Visual inspection lasting 1–3 days.
- No instruments, only checklists.
- Identifies obvious low-cost / no-cost opportunities.
- Suitable for small buildings (e.g., offices, small hotels).

**2. Detailed Energy Audit (Mini Audit):**
- Focused on specific subsystems — HVAC, lighting, pumping, compressed air.
- Duration: 1–2 weeks.
- Instruments: portable power clamp meter, digital thermometer, light (lux) meter, stopwatch for time-of-use studies.
- Output: Quantified savings with engineering calculations.

**3. Comprehensive / Investment-Grade Audit (IGA):**
- Full facility audit with detailed instrumentation.
- Duration: 4–8 weeks.
- Instruments used:
  - **Power Quality Analyzer** (for harmonics, pf, voltage)
  - **Thermal Imager** (for insulation losses, electrical hot spots)
  - **Ultrasonic Flow Meter** (for compressed air leak detection)
  - **Flue Gas Analyzer** (for boiler combustion efficiency)
  - **Lux Meter** (for lighting adequacy)
  - **Data Loggers** (for long-term load profiling)
- Output: **Bankable DPR** (Detailed Project Report) with M&V (Measurement & Verification) plan.

**Standards Followed:** ASHRAE Level I/II/III, IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Power per lamp: $P_{old} = 100$ W, $P_{new} = 20$ W
- Number of fittings: $N = 200$
- Operating hours: $t = 10 \times 300 = 3000$ hours/year
- Tariff: $T = ₹9$/kWh
- LED cost: ₹400/lamp, installation: ₹100/lamp

**(i) Annual Energy Saved:**

Power saved per lamp:
$$\Delta P = 100 - 20 = 80\,W$$

Total power saved:
$$P_{total} = 80 \times 200 = 16{,}000\,W = 16\,kW$$

Annual energy saved:
$$\Delta E = P_{total} \times t = 16 \times 3000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 48{,}000\,kWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual Cost Savings:**

$$S_{annual} = 48{,}000 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{S_{annual} = 4{,}32{,}000\,₹/\text{year}}$$

**(iii) Total Investment & Simple Payback:**

Cost per lamp (with installation) = 400 + 100 = ₹500
Total investment:
$$I = 500 \times 200 = 1{,}00{,}000\,₹$$

Simple Payback Period:
$$SPP = \frac{I}{S_{annual}} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{4{,}32{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{SPP = 0.231\,\text{years} \approx 2.78\,\text{months}}$$

**Inference:** This is an extraordinarily attractive project with SPP well below 1 year.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Marks (Compiled from Past Valuation Reports):**

1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to convert **Watts to kW** before multiplying by hours. Always write: *"P (in kW) × t (in h) = E (in kWh)"*.

2. **Wrong Payback Formula:** Students confuse **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** with **Return on Investment (ROI)**. KTU explicitly tests this:
   - $SPP = I / S_{annual}$ → units = **years**
   - $ROI = (S_{annual}/I) \times 100$ → units = **%**

3. **Mixing HP and kW:** Always remember **1 HP = 0.746 kW**. Many KTU problems give motor rating in HP.

4. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Even if the numerical answer is correct, *always state units* (₹/year, kWh/tonne, years).

5. **Missing Top Management Commitment:** In Part B questions on EMP, *at least 2 marks* are reserved for the **policy + commitment clause**. Don't skip it.

6. **Energy Audit Type Confusion:** Don't write "Walk-Through" when the question asks for "Comprehensive" instrumentation list. Memorize the comparison table.

7. **Power Factor Blindness:** In cost calculations, if tariff is in **₹/kVAh**, you **must multiply by pf** to get ₹/kWh. Many students skip this.

---

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flashcards — Print This Section Before Exam!**

✅ **Energy Management** = Systematic planning + control of energy use to reduce cost and emissions while maintaining productivity.

✅ **Energy Audit** = Inspection + measurement + analysis to identify ECOs (Energy Conservation Opportunities).

✅ **5 Principles of EM:** Top Management Commitment, Baseline, EnPIs, PDCA, Awareness.

✅ **6 Steps of EMP:** Commitment → Energy Review → Baseline & EnPIs → Targets → Action Plan → Review.

✅ **3 Types of Audits:** Walk-Through (visual, 1–3 days) → Detailed (focused subsystem, 1–2 weeks) → Comprehensive (full facility, 4–8 weeks).

✅ **Most Important Formula:** $SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Total Output}}$ — units = kWh/unit.

✅ **Payback Formula:** $SPP = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ — units = years.

✅ **Unit Conversions (Board Favorites):**
- 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ**
- 1 HP = **0.746 kW**
- 1 TOE = **41.868 GJ**
- 1 kgoe = **41.868 MJ**

✅ **Indian Compliance:** Energy Conservation Act 2001 → Designated Consumers → Mandatory Energy Audit every 3 years.

✅ **Standard:** ISO 50001:2018 (Energy Management System).

✅ **Emission Factor (Indian Grid Avg.):** 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh.

✅ **Decision Rule:** SPP < 2 years → Auto-approve; SPP 2–5 years → Evaluate; SPP > 5 years → Strategic decision.

✅ **Audit Instruments (Comprehensive Audit):** Power analyzer, thermal camera, lux meter, flue gas analyzer, ultrasonic leak detector, data loggers.

✅ **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (The foundation of ISO 50001).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Energy Management (EM)**, the term **"Need"** refers to the *compelling, multi-dimensional rationale* that justifies the systematic and continuous control of energy use across industrial, commercial, and institutional sectors. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEEET612, Module 1)**, the "Need for Energy Management" is established by analyzing the intersection of **three driving pillars**:

1. **Economic Necessity** – Escalating fuel costs, import dependency, and industrial competitiveness.
2. **Environmental Imperative** – Climate change, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and sustainable development goals.
3. **Energy Security & Scarcity** – Depleting fossil reserves, supply-demand imbalances, and national energy independence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Energy Management is essential because of the increasing scarcity of energy resources, rising cost of energy, environmental degradation, and the necessity to comply with national and international energy regulations."*

Formally, the **Need for Energy Management (NfEM)** can be expressed as a **multi-objective driver function**:

$$
NfEM = f(E_c, E_e, E_s, E_r, E_p)
$$

Where:
- $E_c$ = Economic drivers (cost reduction, ROI)
- $E_e$ = Environmental drivers (emissions, carbon footprint)
- $E_s$ = Security drivers (resource scarcity, import dependency)
- $E_r$ = Regulatory drivers (Energy Conservation Act, ISO 50001)
- $E_p$ = Productivity drivers (efficiency, competitiveness)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a household that has a **leaking water tank** on its roof. The family is paying for water (energy), but a large portion is wasted through cracks (inefficiency). 

- **Without Energy Management**: The family keeps buying more water (more fuel/electricity) without fixing the leaks. Eventually, water bills (energy costs) skyrocket, and they struggle financially.
- **With Energy Management**: The family plugs the leaks (audits equipment), installs a flow regulator (energy-efficient motors), monitors usage (metering & MIS), and harvests rainwater (renewable integration). They now use **less water, pay less, and the tank lasts longer**.

**Energy Management** is exactly this — it is *not about using less energy to compromise comfort or productivity*, but about **eliminating energy waste** to deliver the same (or better) output at a lower cost and environmental cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Energy is invisible, intangible, and abstract — that's exactly why it gets wasted. A structured management framework makes it visible, measurable, and controllable.

---

### 1.3 Key Driving Factors — Overview Table

| **Driver Category** | **Primary Concern** | **Stakeholder Affected** |
|---|---|---|
| Economic | High operating cost | Industry, Commercial units |
| Environmental | GHG emissions, global warming | Society, Future generations |
| Resource Scarcity | Fossil fuel depletion | Nation, Global community |
| Regulatory | Compliance with laws | Industry, Government |
| Strategic | Competitiveness | Corporate, National |
| Social | Energy access, affordability | Common man |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **"4-E Rule of Need"**: **E**conomy, **E**nvironment, **E**nergy Security, and **E**fficiency. KTU examiners love this mnemonic!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Need — Detailed Analysis

#### **Pillar 1: Economic Necessity**

Industries today spend between **20% to 40%** of their operating costs on energy. In energy-intensive sectors like cement, steel, aluminum, fertilizers, and textiles, this figure can exceed **50%**. The **specific energy consumption (SEC)** directly affects the profit margin.

$$
\text{Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Unit of Output (tonne, kg, m}^2\text{)}}
$$

A reduction of even **5% in SEC** translates to a **direct 5% reduction in energy cost**, which lands as **pure profit** at the bottom line.

**Why it matters in India:**
- India imports **~85% of its crude oil** and **~50% of its natural gas** demand.
- A single **1 USD increase in crude oil price** raises India's import bill by approximately **₹14,000 crore annually**.
- Industrial electricity tariffs have risen by **~7–10% CAGR** over the last decade.

---

#### **Pillar 2: Environmental Imperative**

Every unit of energy generated from fossil fuels produces **greenhouse gases (GHGs)**. The standard emission metric is:

$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ Emission} = \text{Fuel Consumption (kg)} \times \text{Emission Factor (kg CO}_2\text{/kg fuel)}
$$

**Standard Emission Factors (IPCC Default Values):**

| **Fuel Type** | **Emission Factor (kg CO₂ / kg of fuel)** |
|---|---|
| Natural Gas | **2.75** |
| Diesel | **3.17** |
| Furnace Oil | **3.19** |
| Lignite | **0.91** |
| Coal (Bituminous) | **2.42** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **electrical energy**, the grid emission factor for India (CEA, 2023 baseline) is approximately **0.716 kg CO₂ / kWh**.

**India's Climate Commitment:**
- **Panchamrit Pledge (COP26, Glasgow 2021):**
  - Reach **500 GW** of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by **2030**.
  - Reduce **GDP carbon intensity by 45%** by 2030 (vs. 2005 levels).
  - Achieve **net-zero emissions by 2070**.

These pledges make **energy management not optional but legally and morally binding** for Indian industries.

---

#### **Pillar 3: Energy Scarcity & Security**

Global reserves-to-production (R/P) ratios (approximate, BP Statistical Review 2023):

| **Resource** | **R/P Ratio (years)** | **Status** |
|---|---|---|
| Coal | ~139 | Moderate |
| Natural Gas | ~49 | Critical |
| Oil | ~53 | Critical |
| Uranium | ~90 | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> At current consumption rates, oil and gas reserves may last only **~50 years**. This is the strongest long-term driver for energy management.

**India's Import Dependency:**
- Crude Oil: **~85%** imported
- Natural Gas: **~50%** imported (rising to 70%+ by 2030 if domestic production stagnates)

---

#### **Pillar 4: Regulatory Compliance**

The **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (amended in 2010) established:
- **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** as the nodal agency.
- Designated Consumers (DCs) – mandatory energy audit obligation.
- **Perform, Achieve, Trade (PAT)** scheme – a market-based mechanism for trading Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts).

**ISO 50001:2018** – International standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS).

$$
\text{Compliance Score} = \frac{\text{Adherence to Norms}}{\text{Required Standard}} \times 100
$$

---

#### **Pillar 5: Productivity & Competitiveness**

Lower energy consumption → Lower production cost → Higher competitiveness → More market share. This forms a **virtuous cycle**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{O_{output}}$ | $\dfrac{kWh}{tonne} \text{ or } \dfrac{MJ}{kg}$ | Benchmarking industry efficiency |
| Energy Cost Reduction | $\Delta C = C_i - C_f$ | ₹ or USD | Savings calculation |
| Percentage Savings | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100$ | % | Impact assessment |
| CO₂ Emission | $E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times EF$ | kg CO₂ | Carbon accounting |
| Energy Intensity (Economy-wide) | $EI = \dfrac{\text{TPEC}}{\text{GDP}}$ | $\dfrac{kgoe}{USD}$ | National macro-indicator |
| Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | years | Project viability |
| R/P Ratio | $R/P = \dfrac{\text{Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | years | Resource sustainability |
| Carbon Footprint | $CF = \sum_i (E_i \times EF_i)$ | tonnes CO₂e | GHG inventory |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Boiler/furnace |
| kWh to TOE conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$ | conversion | Energy accounting |
| Calorific value of fuel | $CV$ | $\dfrac{kcal}{kg} \text{ or } \dfrac{MJ}{kg}$ | Boiler efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversions KTU Examiners Expect:**
> - $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 860 \text{ kcal}$
> - $1 \text{ TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$
> - $1 \text{ Btu} = 1055 \text{ J}$

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Sector** | **Typical Energy Share in OPEX** | **Major EM Opportunity** |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 30–40% | Clinker cooler, pre-heater optimization |
| Steel | 25–35% | Waste heat recovery, coke oven gas |
| Aluminum | 35–45% | Electrolytic cell efficiency |
| Textiles | 20–30% | Motor systems, HVAC, lighting |
| Pulp & Paper | 25–35% | Steam system, dryer optimization |
| IT/ITES | 10–15% | Data center cooling, PUE reduction |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linking Energy Cost to Profitability

Let us derive the **direct impact of energy savings on operating profit**.

**Step 1:** Let total revenue be $R$, total operating cost be $C_{total}$.

$$
\text{Operating Profit } (P) = R - C_{total}
$$

**Step 2:** Decompose total cost into energy component and non-energy component.

$$
C_{total} = C_E + C_{NE}
$$

Where $C_E$ is the energy cost component and $C_{NE}$ is all other costs (raw material, labor, maintenance, etc.).

**Step 3:** Suppose energy cost reduces by a fraction $\alpha$ due to an EM initiative. New energy cost:

$$
C_E^{new} = C_E (1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute new profit assuming revenue and other costs remain constant.

$$
P^{new} = R - C_E(1 - \alpha) - C_{NE}
$$

**Step 5:** Profit improvement (delta):

$$
\Delta P = P^{new} - P = R - C_E(1-\alpha) - C_{NE} - (R - C_E - C_{NE})
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$
\Delta P = C_E - C_E(1-\alpha) = C_E \cdot \alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A reduction in energy cost by fraction $\alpha$ yields a **direct profit improvement** of $C_E \times \alpha$. Since energy cost is a *pure outflow* with no revenue offset, **every rupee saved is a rupee earned**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A manufacturing plant consumes **10,000 MWh** of electricity annually at an average tariff of **₹8/kWh**. The plant has implemented an Energy Management System (EnMS) and achieved a **5% reduction** in specific energy consumption. Calculate:

(a) Total annual energy cost
(b) Annual savings
(c) Annual CO₂ emission reduction (using grid factor 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh)
(d) Percentage reduction in operating cost if energy constitutes 25% of total operating cost of ₹40 crore

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Annual Energy Cost:**

$$
C_E = 10{,}000 \text{ MWh} \times 1000 \text{ kWh/MWh} \times ₹8/\text{kWh}
$$

$$
C_E = 10{,}000 \times 1000 \times 8 = ₹8{,}00{,}00{,}000 = ₹8 \text{ Crore}
$$

**(b) Annual Savings (5% reduction):**

$$
\Delta C = C_E \times \alpha = ₹8 \text{ Crore} \times 0.05 = ₹0.40 \text{ Crore} = ₹40 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**(c) Annual CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\text{Energy saved} = 10{,}000 \times 1000 \times 0.05 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.716 = 3{,}58{,}000 \text{ kg} = 358 \text{ tonnes CO}_2
$$

**(d) Percentage Reduction in Total OPEX:**

$$
\text{Total OPEX} = ₹40 \text{ Crore}; \quad \text{Savings} = ₹0.40 \text{ Crore}
$$

$$
\%\text{OPEX reduction} = \frac{0.40}{40} \times 100 = 1\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"How much CO₂ emission can be avoided by a given energy-saving measure?"* Always use **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** as the Indian grid emission factor unless a specific fuel is mentioned.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cost of Inaction vs. Cost of Action

| **Parameter** | **Without EM** | **With EM** |
|---|---|---|
| Annual energy cost (₹ Cr) | 8.00 | 7.60 |
| Annual savings (₹) | 0 | 40,00,000 |
| Annual CO₂ (tonnes) | 7,160 | 6,802 |
| Carbon liability (@ ₹2,000/tonne)* | ₹1.43 Cr | ₹1.36 Cr |
| **Net Position (₹ Cr)** | **9.43** | **8.96** |
| *Carbon Credit Market (CBAM, EU)* | *Penalty* | *Trading benefit* |

*Indicative carbon price under emerging Indian carbon market framework.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Impact on Energy Intensity at National Level

**Step 1:** Energy Intensity (EI) is defined as energy consumption per unit GDP.

$$
EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption (TPEC)}}{\text{GDP}}
$$

**Step 2:** Let $EI_0$ be the initial intensity and $EI_t$ be the intensity after EM interventions.

$$
\Delta EI = \frac{EI_0 - EI_t}{EI_0} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** For India to achieve its **Panchamrit target of 45% reduction in GDP carbon intensity by 2030**, the required annual reduction rate (compounded) is:

$$
(1-r)^9 = 0.55
$$

$$
r = 1 - (0.55)^{1/9} = 1 - 0.936 = 0.0639 \approx 6.4\% \text{ per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India must reduce energy intensity by **~6.4% year-on-year** to meet its COP26 commitment. This mathematically underscores why energy management is **not a choice but a national obligation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Pillar Architecture of Need for Energy Management

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NEED FOR ENERGY MANAGEMENT] --> B[Economic Necessity]
    A --> C[Environmental Imperative]
    A --> D[Energy Security]
    A --> E[Regulatory Compliance]
    A --> F[Productivity and Competitiveness]
    
    B --> B1[Cost Reduction]
    B --> B2[ROI Maximization]
    B --> B3[Import Bill Reduction]
    
    C --> C1[GHG Emission Reduction]
    C --> C2[Net Zero Commitments]
    C --> C3[CBAM Compliance]
    
    D --> D1[Fuel Reserve Depletion]
    D --> D2[R/P Ratio Pressure]
    D --> D3[Strategic Reserves]
    
    E --> E1[Energy Conservation Act 2001]
    E --> E2[PAT Scheme]
    E --> E3[ISO 50001 Standards]
    
    F --> F1[SEC Reduction]
    F --> F2[Process Optimization]
    F --> F3[Market Leadership]
```

---

### 4.2 Cause-and-Effect Mapping (Ishikawa-Style Fishbone)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Problem[HIGH ENERGY INTENSITY] --> C1[Machine: Inefficient Motors]
    Problem --> C2[Method: No Monitoring System]
    Problem --> C3[Material: Poor Insulation]
    Problem --> C4[Measurement: No Metering]
    Problem --> C5[Manpower: Untrained Operators]
    Problem --> C6[Environment: High Ambient Temp]
    
    C1 --> Sol[ENERGY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM]
    C2 --> Sol
    C3 --> Sol
    C4 --> Sol
    C5 --> Sol
    C6 --> Sol
```

---

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decision Flow: Why EM Matters?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Why Do We Need Energy Management] --> Q1{Is Energy Cost Significant?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, 20-40 percent of OPEX| Q2{Are Resources Finite?}
    Q1 -->|No| Skip[Low Priority Industry]
    
    Q2 -->|Yes, Fossil Reserves Depleting| Q3{Environmental Concern?}
    Q2 -->|No| Trad[Traditional Approach]
    
    Q3 -->|Yes, Climate Change Real| Q4{Regulatory Pressure?}
    Q3 -->|No| OptIn[Voluntary Adoption]
    
    Q4 -->|Yes, EC Act 2001| EM[Implement Energy Management]
    Q4 -->|No| CSR[Adopt via CSR]
    
    EM --> Out1[Reduced Cost, Emissions, Improved Compliance]
    CSR --> Out1
    OptIn --> Out1
```

---

### 4.4 Interaction Map: Stakeholders Influenced by the "Need"

```mermaid
graph TD
    Need[Need for EM] --> Gov[Government - BEE, MNRE]
    Need --> Ind[Industry - DCs, SMEs]
    Need --> Soc[Society - Common Citizens]
    Need --> Int[International Bodies - UNFCCC, IEA]
    Need --> Fin[Financial Institutions - Banks, ESG Funds]
    
    Gov --> P1[Policy and Subsidies]
    Ind --> P2[Audits and ISO 50001]
    Soc --> P3[Awareness and DISCOMs]
    Int --> P4[Climate Pledges]
    Fin --> P5[Green Financing]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"List and briefly explain any three driving forces that necessitate Energy Management in modern industries."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each point):**

1. **Economic Necessity:** Energy constitutes 20–40% of industrial operating cost. A 5% reduction in specific energy consumption (SEC) translates directly into a proportional profit increase since energy cost is a pure outflow. *(1 mark)*

2. **Environmental Imperative:** Conventional fossil-based energy generation produces CO₂ and other GHGs. India pledged a 45% reduction in GDP carbon intensity by 2030 (Panchamrit), making EM a regulatory necessity. *(1 mark)*

3. **Energy Security & Scarcity:** With oil R/P ratio at ~53 years and gas at ~49 years, finite reserves demand efficient use. India imports ~85% of its crude oil, creating a strategic vulnerability. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*"With the help of a formula, define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and state its significance in energy management."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (MJ or kWh)}}{\text{Unit of Output (tonne, kg, m}^2\text{, etc.)}}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- Acts as a **normalized benchmark** for comparing energy efficiency across plants of varying capacities.
- Forms the **baseline KPI** for any Energy Management System (EnMS).
- Allows **target-setting** for reduction (e.g., reduce SEC by 5% per year).
- Independent of production volume, making year-on-year comparison meaningful.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]*

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **economic and environmental drivers** that necessitate the adoption of Energy Management in Indian industries. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A process industry consumes **8,500 MWh** of electricity annually at an average tariff of **₹9.50/kWh**. After implementing an Energy Management System, the consumption drops by **7%**. Calculate:
   (i) Annual cost savings *(2 marks)*
   (ii) Annual CO₂ emission reduction using the Indian grid factor of **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Payback period if the EM system investment is **₹28 lakh** *(3 marks)*

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Economic and Environmental Drivers (7 marks):**

**Economic Drivers:**
- **High OPEX share:** Energy costs 20–40% of industrial operating expenditure. For energy-intensive sectors (cement, steel, aluminium, fertilizers), it can exceed 50%.
- **Rising tariffs:** Indian industrial electricity tariffs have grown at 7–10% CAGR, directly squeezing margins.
- **Import burden:** India imports ~85% of crude oil and ~50% of natural gas. A ₹1 hike in crude price adds ~₹14,000 crore to the import bill.
- **Global competitiveness:** Lower SEC = lower product cost = competitive advantage in exports.
- **Direct profit impact:** Since energy cost is a pure outflow with no offsetting revenue, every rupee saved equals one rupee earned (proved by: $\Delta P = C_E \times \alpha$).

**Environmental Drivers:**
- **GHG Emissions:** Combustion of fossil fuels emits CO₂. India is the 3rd largest emitter globally (~3.0 Gt CO₂/year).
- **Climate Commitments:** Panchamrit pledges at COP26 — 500 GW non-fossil capacity, 45% reduction in carbon intensity by 2030, net-zero by 2070.
- **CBAM (EU):** From 2026, EU will levy carbon border taxes on imports. Energy-intensive Indian exports (steel, aluminium, cement) will need verifiable carbon reduction.
- **Air Quality:** Cities like Delhi, Mumbai frequently breach PM2.5/PM10 norms, traced largely to fossil-based energy use.
- **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs):** SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy), SDG 13 (Climate Action) directly tie in.

*(Award 3.5 marks for economic and 3.5 marks for environmental — distribute points across 4–5 sub-points each.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E = 8{,}500 \text{ MWh} = 85{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh}$
- Tariff = ₹9.50/kWh
- Reduction = 7% = 0.07
- Grid emission factor = 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh
- Investment = ₹28,00,000

**(i) Annual Cost Savings:**

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = 85{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07 = 5{,}95{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta C = 5{,}95{,}000 \times 9.50 = ₹56{,}52{,}500 \approx ₹56.53 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Annual CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 5{,}95{,}000 \times 0.716 = 4{,}26{,}020 \text{ kg} = 426.02 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Simple Payback Period:**

$$
SPP = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{56{,}52{,}500} = 0.495 \text{ years} \approx 6 \text{ months}
$$

**(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often use **0.07** instead of multiplying it with **energy in kWh**, OR they forget to convert **MWh to kWh** (multiply by 1000). Also, ensure tariff is correctly applied to the *saved* energy, not the *total* energy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **role of the Energy Conservation Act 2001** and the **PAT scheme** in promoting Energy Management in India. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A steel plant has an annual energy bill of **₹50 crore**, out of which **electricity contributes 60%** and **fossil fuels contribute 40%**. The plant aims to reduce total energy cost by **10%** through EM measures.
   (i) Calculate the savings in ₹ crore. *(2 marks)*
   (ii) If electricity savings come from motor efficiency improvements and fuel savings come from waste heat recovery, which has a higher *priority* and why? *(3 marks)*
   (iii) Compute the carbon footprint reduction in tonnes CO₂, assuming: electricity savings = 20 million kWh, fuel savings = 5,000 tonnes of coal replaced (EF = 2.42 kg CO₂/kg coal). *(2 marks)*

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Energy Conservation Act 2001 & PAT Scheme (7 marks):**

**Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (Amended 2010):**
- Established the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the Ministry of Power.
- Defined **Designated Consumers (DCs)** — industries consuming above a threshold (e.g., cement ≥ 1.86 million tonnes, steel ≥ 30,000 tonnes).
- **Mandatory Energy Audits** by BEE-certified Energy Auditors.
- Standards & Labelling (S&L) programme for appliances.
- Building Energy Performance Certification.
- Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) for commercial buildings.

*(3.5 marks)*

**PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme:**
- Launched in 2012 as a market-based mechanism under the National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE).
- 8 sectors covered: Aluminium, Cement, Chlor-Alkali, Fertilizer, Iron & Steel, Paper & Pulp, Thermal Power, Textile.
- Each DC is given a **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) target**.
- If the plant over-achieves → earns **ESCerts (Energy Saving Certificates)** which can be traded.
- If under-achieves → must **buy ESCerts** from the market or pay penalties.
- This creates a **financial incentive** to improve energy efficiency beyond mandated levels.

*(3.5 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total energy bill = ₹50 Crore
- Electricity share = 60% = ₹30 Crore
- Fuel share = 40% = ₹20 Crore
- Target reduction = 10%

**(i) Total Savings:**

$$
\Delta C = 50 \times 0.10 = ₹5 \text{ Crore}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Priority Justification (3 marks):**
Electricity savings have **higher priority** because:
- Electricity tariff (₹/kWh basis) is **higher per unit of energy** than fossil fuels when conversion losses are accounted for.
- Electricity is a **secondary form** of energy derived from primary fuels; saving 1 kWh of electricity actually saves **~2.5–3 kWh of primary fuel** at the power plant level (avoiding 35–40% conversion loss).
- **Scope 2 emissions** (from purchased electricity) typically dominate Scope 1 emissions (direct fuel combustion) in most Indian industries.
- Therefore, motor efficiency, VFDs, power factor improvement, peak load management should be prioritized.

**(iii) Carbon Footprint Reduction:**

*From Electricity Savings:*

$$
CO_{2,elec} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.716 = 14{,}32{,}000 \text{ kg} = 1{,}432 \text{ tonnes}
$$

*From Fuel Savings (Coal):*

$$
CO_{2,fuel} = 5{,}000 \times 1000 \times 2.42 = 1{,}21{,}00{,}000 \text{ kg} = 12{,}100 \text{ tonnes}
$$

*Total Reduction:*

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 1{,}432 + 12{,}100 = 13{,}532 \text{ tonnes CO}_2
$$

**(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **Scope 1 (direct fuel combustion)** with **Scope 2 (purchased electricity)** emissions. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.
> - Always state units clearly: **kWh vs MWh**, **kg vs tonnes**.
> - When computing payback, ensure investment and savings are in the **same time period** (annual vs monthly).
> - Do NOT use the global average emission factor (~0.4 kg CO₂/kWh); use **Indian grid factor of 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** unless specified otherwise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Energy Management is driven by **4Es**: Economy, Environment, Energy Security, Efficiency (plus Regulation).
- [x] **SEC** (Specific Energy Consumption) is the most important KPI — expressed as energy per unit output.
- [x] India's **Panchamrit** pledge demands **45% reduction in carbon intensity by 2030** and **net-zero by 2070**.
- [x] India imports **~85% crude oil** and **~50% natural gas** — a key energy security driver.
- [x] Indian grid emission factor: **0.716 kg CO₂ / kWh** (memorize this number!).
- [x] **Energy Conservation Act 2001** + **PAT scheme** = regulatory backbone of EM in India.
- [x] **ISO 50001:2018** = international EnMS standard.
- [x] Every **rupee saved in energy** = **one rupee of pure profit** (no revenue offset).
- [x] Energy cost typically forms **20–40% of industrial OPEX**.
- [x] Oil R/P ratio ≈ **53 years**, Gas ≈ **49 years**, Coal ≈ **139 years**.
- [x] **PAT scheme** allows trading of **ESCerts** among industries.
- [x] **Designated Consumers (DCs)** are mandated to conduct energy audits.
- [x] **CBAM (EU)** will impose carbon border taxes from **2026** — affects Indian exports.
- [x] Unit conversions: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh.
- [x] Simple Payback Period = Investment ÷ Annual Savings.
- [x] Always include **Scope 1, 2, and 3** emissions in carbon accounting.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:**
> In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: *Introduction (2 marks) → 4–5 well-explained points (8–10 marks) → Conclusion or numerical example (2 marks)*. Use bullet points and sub-headings liberally — KTU examiners reward *clarity* and *structured presentation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Methodologies, Instruments for Energy Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types and Methodologies, Instruments for Energy Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Energy Audit?

An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, documented verification process involving the inspection, survey, and analysis of energy flows in a building, process, or system with the objective of identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs) and establishing a baseline for energy performance. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET612 – Energy Management)**, an energy audit is defined as the *quantitative and qualitative assessment of energy use*, performed to identify wastage, recommend improvements, and verify actual savings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"An energy audit is a systematic procedure to obtain an adequate knowledge of the existing energy consumption profile of a system, identify the quantities and types of energy used, and recommend measures for energy conservation."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. The doctor doesn't just prescribe medicine blindly — they:
1. **Check vitals** (Preliminary Audit)
2. **Order detailed blood tests and scans** (Detailed Audit)
3. **Specialist surgery/recovery planning** (Investment Grade Audit)

Similarly, an energy auditor acts as a *"doctor for a facility"* — measuring its energy "vitals," diagnosing inefficiencies, and prescribing savings. The instruments are like the stethoscope, thermometer, and blood-pressure cuff of this doctor.

## 1.2 Types of Energy Audits

The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, India, classifies energy audits into **three distinct types**, based on the depth of study, instruments deployed, and the level of investment analysis required.

| **Audit Type** | **Alternate Name** | **Depth** | **Instruments Used** | **Investment Analysis** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 — Walk-through Audit** | Preliminary Audit | Low | Visual, basic meters | Qualitative / Order-of-magnitude |
| **Type 2 — Detailed Audit** | Comprehensive Audit | Medium-to-High | Power analyzer, flow meters, lux meters, combustion analyzer | Engineering / Payback-based |
| **Type 3 — Investment Grade Audit (IGA)** | Precision Audit | Highest | Calibrated ISO-grade instruments, data loggers | Detailed financial (IRR, NPV, Risk) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Walk-through audits answer "WHERE is energy being wasted?"* — *Detailed audits answer "HOW MUCH energy is wasted and WHAT is the payback?"* — *Investment Grade Audits answer "Will the project deliver verified financial returns under risk?"*

## 1.3 Energy Audit Methodology — The ASHRAE/ISO 50002 Approach

The **ISO 50002:2014** standard (Energy audits — Requirements with guidance for use) prescribes a **6-step methodology** adopted in KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Planning & Scoping** — Define boundaries, baseline, and audit objectives.
2. **Data Collection (Site Walk-through & Interviews)** — Energy bills, operational logs.
3. **On-site Measurements & Monitoring** — Using calibrated instruments.
4. **Analysis & Identification of ECOs (Energy Conservation Opportunities)**.
5. **Reporting & Documentation** — Audit report with findings.
6. **Verification & Follow-up** — Post-implementation measurement and verification (M&V) using **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked *"List the types of energy audit"*, always begin with the BEE classification (Walk-through, Detailed, Investment Grade) AND mention ISO 50002 for a complete, KTU-aligned answer. Examiners award bonus marks for standards awareness.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard units and conversion factors are **must-know** for KTU calculations:

| **Quantity** | **SI Unit** | **Common Conversions** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy | Joule (J) | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ = **3600 kJ** |
| Power | Watt (W) | $1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$ ≈ **0.746 kW** |
| Heat | Calorie (cal) | $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$ |
| Calorific Value of Furnace Oil (typical) | — | **10,200 kcal/kg** |
| Calorific Value of Natural Gas | — | **8,250 kcal/Nm³** (NCV basis) |
| Specific Heat of Water | $c_p$ | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Density of Water | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Types of Energy Audits — A Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Walk-Through (Preliminary) Audit

A **walk-through audit** is the *first-pass review* of a facility. It is **qualitative** in nature and typically lasts **1–3 days** for a small-to-medium facility.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Energy bills** for the past **24 months** are analyzed to detect trends.
- **No permanent instrumentation** is installed; only portable handheld devices.
- **Walk-through path** covers the major energy consumers: boilers, chillers, compressors, HVAC, lighting, motors.
- A **list of low-cost/no-cost ECOs** is generated.
- Output: A *Preliminary Audit Report* with rough paybacks (often < 2 years).

**Process Flow:**
1. Collect utility bills (electricity, fuel, water, steam).
2. Conduct facility walk-through with facility manager.
3. Identify obvious losses: leaks, idle running, oversizing, poor insulation.
4. Estimate **simple payback period (SPP)** for each ECO using:

$$
SPP = \frac{\text{Capital Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Monetary Savings (₹/year)}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Detailed (Comprehensive) Audit

A **detailed audit** involves **quantitative measurements** using **portable instruments** over a period of typically **2–6 weeks**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Detailed measurements** of electrical parameters, flow rates, temperatures, lux levels, flue gas composition.
- **Energy balance** is established for each major system: boilers, furnaces, chillers, compressed air, HVAC, lighting.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is computed:

$$
SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Unit of Output (tonne, m}^2\text{, passenger-km, etc.)}}
$$

- **Baseline determination** using regression analysis on historical data.
- **Engineering economic analysis** — Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Benefit-Cost (B/C) ratio.
- Output: *Detailed Audit Report* with costed ECOs.

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Investment Grade Audit (IGA)

An **IGA** is the most rigorous, performed before **major capital investment** (e.g., new chiller, cogeneration plant, HVAC retrofit). It is mandated under **BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** for Designated Consumers (DCs).

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Long-term data logging** (typically 3–12 months) using Class 0.2/0.5 calibrated instruments.
- **Uncertainty analysis** at every measurement point.
- **Risk assessment** of each ECO: technical, financial, operational.
- **Third-party verification** by accredited energy auditors (e.g., **Empaneled Accredited Energy Auditor — EA-XXXX**).
- Output: *Investment Grade Audit Report* with **Class-A, B, C** ranked projects based on **NPV and confidence level**.

## 2.2 Energy Audit Methodology — The BEE 5-Phase Model

The **BEE India** prescribes a **5-phase methodology** for auditors:

| **Phase** | **Activity** | **Key Output** |
|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 — Pre-Audit | Planning, data collection, bill analysis | Audit plan, baseline SEC |
| Phase 2 — Audit | On-site measurements, walk-through | Energy balance, ECO list |
| Phase 3 — Post-Audit Analysis | Engineering calculations, savings estimation | Costed ECOs with payback |
| Phase 4 — Reporting | Final audit report submission | Detailed report to client |
| Phase 5 — Implementation Support | Handholding, M&V | Verified savings |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the KTU Board Examiner's reference — memorize the formulas and units verbatim.**

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{\Delta C_{annual}}$ | Walk-through audits | years |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Q_{output}}$ | Baseline setting | kWh/tonne or MJ/m² |
| Annual Energy Savings | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}$ | Energy conservation | kWh/year or MJ/year |
| Annual Monetary Savings | $\Delta C = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff}$ | Financial analysis | ₹/year |
| Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{(\Delta C - M)_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{inv}$ | Detailed / IGA | ₹ |
| Internal Rate of Return | $NPV = 0$ at $i = IRR$ | Detailed / IGA | % |
| Energy Performance Index | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | Buildings | kWh/m²/year |
| Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam} \times (h_s - h_w)}{m_{fuel} \times GCV_{fuel}}$ | Thermal audit | % |
| Power Factor Penalty | $P_{penalty} = k \times P_{max} \times (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$ | Electrical audit | ₹/month |
| Cooling Load | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | HVAC audit | kW or TR |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
- SPP assumes **constant annual savings** (no escalation) — used only in Type 1 audits.
- NPV assumes a **discount rate (i)** = MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) = typically **12–15%** in Indian industrial context.
- For cooling load: $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**, $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ for water, $\Delta T$ in **K**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Energy Audits

- **Industrial Sector:** Heavy industries (steel, cement, fertilizers) reduce Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) by 5–20% post-audit. **Mandatory** for DCs under PAT scheme.
- **Commercial Buildings:** Achieve **EPI reductions** of 15–30% through HVAC and lighting retrofits — vital for **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** compliance.
- **Municipal & Residential:** Municipal corporations audit water pumping stations, streetlights, and sewage treatment plants.
- **Cross-Disciplinary Use:** Energy audits integrate **electrical engineering** (power quality), **mechanical engineering** (HVAC, thermal systems), and **economics** (payback analysis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) for Energy Conservation Projects

**Context:** A detailed energy audit identifies an ECO. The auditor must compute NPV to justify the investment.

### Step 1: Define the Cash Flow Stream

Let:
- $C_{inv}$ = Initial capital investment (₹) at $t = 0$
- $\Delta C_t$ = Annual monetary savings in year $t$ (₹/year)
- $M_t$ = Annual maintenance cost in year $t$ (₹/year)
- $n$ = Project life (years), typically **10–15 years** for industrial projects
- $i$ = Discount rate (MARR), typically **10–15%**

### Step 2: Net Cash Flow in Year t

The net cash flow in year $t$ is:
$$
NCF_t = \Delta C_t - M_t
$$

### Step 3: Discount Each Year to Present Value

Each future rupee is worth less today. Discount factor for year $t$ is:
$$
DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}
$$

### Step 4: Sum All Discounted Cash Flows and Subtract Investment

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{inv}
$$

### Step 5: Acceptance Criterion

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Project is economically viable — ACCEPT} \\
NPV = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Indifferent (MARR met exactly)} \\
NPV < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Project does NOT meet MARR — REJECT}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

A **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** retrofit for a 50 HP motor has:
- $C_{inv}$ = ₹ 2,00,000
- Annual energy savings = ₹ 60,000/year
- Annual maintenance = ₹ 5,000/year
- $n = 10$ years
- $i = 12\%$

**Net cash flow per year:** $NCF = 60{,}000 - 5{,}000 = \text{₹ 55,000/year}$

**Present Worth of Annuity (PWA) factor:**
$$
PWA = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

Substituting $i = 0.12$, $n = 10$:
$$
PWA = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-10}}{0.12}
$$

Computing the intermediate:
$$
(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585
$$

$$
(1.12)^{-10} = \frac{1}{3.10585} = 0.32197
$$

$$
PWA = \frac{1 - 0.32197}{0.12} = \frac{0.67803}{0.12} = 5.6502
$$

**NPV calculation:**
$$
NPV = (55{,}000 \times 5.6502) - 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
NPV = 3{,}10{,}761 - 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ 1,10,761}
$$

Since **NPV > 0**, the VFD project is **economically viable** and must be **recommended** in the audit report.

**Simple Payback Period (Cross-check):**
$$
SPP = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{55{,}000} = 3.64 \text{ years}
$$

This is a **healthy payback** (typically < 5 years is considered attractive in KTU 2024 energy projects).

## 3.2 Derivation: Boiler Efficiency by Direct Method

A common **detailed energy audit** measurement is boiler efficiency:

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \frac{\text{Heat Output}}{\text{Heat Input}} \times 100\%
$$

**Heat Output (steam side):**
$$
Q_{out} = m_s (h_s - h_w)
$$

**Heat Input (fuel side):**
$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times GCV_f
$$

**Final equation:**
$$
\eta_{boiler} = \frac{m_s (h_s - h_w)}{m_f \times GCV_f} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $m_s$ = mass of steam generated (kg/hr)
- $h_s$ = enthalpy of steam (kJ/kg)
- $h_w$ = enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg)
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel burned (kg/hr)
- $GCV_f$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Instrument

The following decision matrix helps auditors select the correct instrument for each measurement — **a high-yield KTU question**:

| **Measurement Type** | **Parameter** | **Recommended Instrument** | **Accuracy Class** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrical — Power | kW, kVAr, kVA, PF | **Three-Phase Power Analyzer** (e.g., Fluke 435) | Class 0.5 or better |
| Electrical — Energy | kWh over time | **Energy Logger / Data Logger** | Class 1.0 |
| Electrical — Harmonics | THD, individual harmonics | **Power Quality Analyzer** | Class A (IEC 61000-4-30) |
| Light Level | Illuminance | **Lux Meter** | ±3% |
| Temperature — Surface | Surface temp (motors, pipes) | **Infrared (IR) Thermometer** | ±2% |
| Temperature — Gas Flue | Flue gas temp | **K-type Thermocouple** with indicator | ±1°C |
| Flow — Water/Steam | Volumetric/mass flow | **Ultrasonic Flow Meter** (clamp-on) | ±1–2% |
| Combustion Analysis | O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ, efficiency | **Combustion / Flue Gas Analyzer** | ±1% O₂ |
| Air Velocity | HVAC ducts | **Vane Anemometer / Hot-wire Anemometer** | ±3% |
| Vibration | Motor condition | **Vibration Analyzer / Tachometer** | ±5% |
| Pressure | Steam, compressed air, water | **Digital Manometer** | ±0.5% FS |
| Humidity | Indoor air quality | **Hygrometer** | ±2% RH |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Energy Audit Methodology (ISO 50002 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Planning and Scoping] --> A2[Phase 2: Data Collection Site Walk-through Utility Bill Analysis]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: On-site Measurements Power Flow Light Temperature Combustion]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Analysis Energy Balance Identify ECOs]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Reporting Detailed Audit Report NPV IRR Payback]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: M and V IPMVP Verification of Savings]
    A6 --> A1
    styleA1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    styleA2 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    styleA3 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff
    styleA4 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000
    styleA5 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000
    styleA6 fill:#deebf7,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Types of Energy Audit Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Client Requests Energy Audit] --> B1{Is it a quick screening or large capex}
    B1 -->|Quick screening| B2[Type 1 Walk-through Audit 1 to 3 days Qualitative]
    B1 -->|Moderate investment| B3[Type 2 Detailed Audit 2 to 6 weeks Quantitative]
    B1 -->|Major capex above 1 crore| B4[Type 3 Investment Grade Audit 3 to 12 months ISO grade]
    B2 --> C1[Output: Preliminary ECO list Simple Payback]
    B3 --> C2[Output: Costed ECOs NPV IRR Engineered savings]
    B4 --> C3[Output: Bankable Project Report with Uncertainty and Risk]
    styleB0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    styleB1 fill:#ffc000,color:#000
    styleB2 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000
    styleB3 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    styleB4 fill:#548235,color:#fff
    styleC1 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000
    styleC2 fill:#d9ead3,color:#000
    styleC3 fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Instrument Deployment Architecture in a Detailed Audit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Three Phase Power Analyzer at Main LT Panel] --> EDB[Central Data Aggregator Laptop with Software]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Walk-through energy audit and a Detailed energy audit.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Walk-through audit** (Type 1) is a *qualitative, low-cost, short-duration* assessment, typically lasting 1–3 days, that involves a physical tour of the facility, review of utility bills, and identification of obvious energy wastage. It does **not** involve detailed instrumented measurements and produces only **rough payback estimates** for low-cost/no-cost measures.

A **Detailed audit** (Type 2) is a *quantitative, instrumented, longer-duration* assessment lasting 2–6 weeks. It involves **on-site measurements** using power analyzers, flow meters, combustion analyzers, and lux meters. It establishes a complete **energy balance**, computes the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**, and provides **engineered economic analysis** (NPV, IRR, SPP) for costed Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs).

| **Basis** | **Walk-through** | **Detailed** |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| Instruments | Visual, handheld | Calibrated, logged |
| Output | Qualitative ECOs | Costed ECOs with NPV |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six instruments used in an energy audit and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Three-Phase Power Analyzer** — measures kW, kVAr, PF, THD at LT panels.
2. **Ultrasonic Flow Meter** — measures chilled water / steam flow rate.
3. **Combustion / Flue Gas Analyzer** — measures O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ in flue gas.
4. **Lux Meter** — measures illuminance (lux) for lighting audit.
5. **Infrared (IR) Thermometer** — measures non-contact surface temperature of motors, bearings, pipes.
6. **Vane Anemometer** — measures air velocity in HVAC ducts.
7. *(Optional)* **Vibration Analyzer** — measures motor/pump condition.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three types of energy audits** recognized by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India. Compare them based on the depth of study, instruments used, and the level of investment analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A factory plans to install a **solar PV system** at a capital cost of **₹ 12,00,000**. The system is expected to generate savings of **₹ 2,50,000 per year** with annual maintenance of **₹ 20,000**. The project life is **12 years** and the discount rate is **10%**. Compute the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** and the **Net Present Value (NPV)**. Should the project be accepted?

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to **Section 2.1** of these notes. The three types are:

1. **Type 1 — Walk-through (Preliminary) Audit**
   - Duration: 1–3 days
   - Purpose: Identify low-cost/no-cost ECOs
   - Instruments: Visual, basic handheld meters
   - Investment analysis: Qualitative; order-of-magnitude SPP only

2. **Type 2 — Detailed (Comprehensive) Audit**
   - Duration: 2–6 weeks
   - Purpose: Establish energy balance and quantify ECOs
   - Instruments: Power analyzer, flow meters, combustion analyzer, lux meter, IR thermometer
   - Investment analysis: SPP, NPV, IRR based on engineered savings

3. **Type 3 — Investment Grade Audit (IGA)**
   - Duration: 3–12 months
   - Purpose: Bankable documentation for large capex
   - Instruments: ISO-grade, Class 0.2/0.5 calibrated, long-term data loggers
   - Investment analysis: Full NPV, IRR, **uncertainty analysis, risk-adjusted returns**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{inv} = \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000$
- Annual savings = ₹ 2,50,000/year
- Annual maintenance = ₹ 20,000/year
- $n = 12$ years, $i = 10\%$

**Step 1: Net Annual Cash Flow (NCF)**
$$
NCF = 2{,}50{,}000 - 20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}30{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Step 2: Simple Payback Period**
$$
SPP = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{2{,}30{,}000} = 5.22 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3: Present Worth of Annuity (PWA) Factor**

$$
PWA = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-12}}{0.10}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{12} = 3.1384
$$

$$
(1.10)^{-12} = \frac{1}{3.1384} = 0.3186
$$

$$
PWA = \frac{1 - 0.3186}{0.10} = \frac{0.6814}{0.10} = 6.8137
$$

**Step 4: NPV Calculation**
$$
NPV = (2{,}30{,}000 \times 6.8137) - 12{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
NPV = 15{,}67{,}151 - 12{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}67{,}151
$$

**Conclusion:** Since **NPV = ₹ 3,67,151 > 0** and **SPP = 5.22 years** (acceptable for industrial PV), the project should be **ACCEPTED**.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **methodology of conducting a detailed energy audit** in an industrial facility, mentioning the role of **ISO 50002** and **IPMVP** standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle and applications of a **Three-Phase Power Analyzer** and a **Combustion Analyzer** in energy audit.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **ISO 50002:2014** standard prescribes a **6-step detailed energy audit methodology**:

1. **Planning and Scoping** — Define the audit boundaries, gather historical utility data (minimum 12 months of electricity, fuel, and water bills), identify the team, and set objectives.

2. **Site Walk-through and Data Collection** — Conduct physical inspection, interview operators, collect equipment nameplate data, operating schedules, and maintenance logs.

3. **On-site Measurements** — Deploy calibrated instruments:
   - Power analyzer at LT panels
   - Flow meters on water/steam lines
   - Combustion analyzer on boiler flue
   - Lux meter for lighting
   - IR thermometer for motors/bearings

4. **Analysis and Identification of ECOs** — Construct **Sankey diagrams** of energy flows, compute SEC, identify energy conservation opportunities (ECOs).

5. **Reporting** — Submit the detailed audit report with costed ECOs, payback analysis, and implementation plan.

6. **Verification and Follow-up (M&V)** — Use the **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** with its 4 options (A: Retrofit Isolation, B: Retrofit Isolation with M&V, C: Whole Facility, D: Calibrated Simulation) to verify actual savings post-implementation.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Three-Phase Power Analyzer:**
- **Working:** Uses current transformers (CTs) and voltage probes to sense all three phase voltages and currents simultaneously. Internal DSP computes **kW, kVAr, kVA, Power Factor, Frequency, THD, individual harmonics, and energy (kWh)** as per **IEC 61000-4-30 Class A**.
- **Applications:** Measures motor loading, identifies phase imbalance, detects PF penalty, evaluates VFD performance, documents baseline kWh for M&V.

**Combustion Analyzer (Flue Gas Analyzer):**
- **Working:** Insertion probe placed in the boiler flue draws a gas sample. **Electrochemical sensors** measure O₂, CO, NOₓ, and SO₂. **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) sensor** measures CO₂. Internal microprocessor computes **combustion efficiency** using the **Siegert formula**:
$$
\eta_{comb} = 100 - \left(\frac{T_{flue} - T_{air}}{CO_2\% \times \text{Factor}}\right)
$$
- **Applications:** Tunes air-fuel ratio, calculates boiler efficiency, monitors emissions for CPCB/SPCB compliance, identifies heat loss due to excess air.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract maintenance** from annual savings before computing NPV — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing GCV and NCV** in boiler efficiency questions — students must state which calorific value is used.
> 3. **Wrong PWA formula** — Some students use $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — both forms are correct, but arithmetic errors are common.
> 4. **Not stating ISO 50002 / IPMVP** in methodology answers — KTU 2024 examiners specifically look for **standards awareness** (2 marks).
> 5. **Naming instruments without applications** — A list of instruments without "what it measures and why" loses **2–3 marks** in 7-mark questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Energy Management — Module 1**

- ✅ **Three Types of Energy Audits:** Walk-through (Type 1), Detailed (Type 2), Investment Grade (Type 3) — remember the **duration, instrument depth, and analysis level** of each.
- ✅ **Standards to know verbatim:** **ISO 50002:2014** (audit methodology), **IPMVP** (M&V), **ECBC** (buildings), **BEE PAT** (industries), **IEC 61000-4-30** (power quality).
- ✅ **Methodology phases:** Planning → Data Collection → Measurement → Analysis → Reporting → M&V.
- ✅ **Key formulas (memorize!):**
  - $SPP = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{NCF}$
  - $NPV = \sum \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{inv}$
  - $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Q_{output}}$
  - $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s(h_s - h_w)}{m_f \times GCV_f}$
- ✅ **Essential Instruments:** Power Analyzer, Combustion Analyzer, Ultrasonic Flow Meter, Lux Meter, IR Thermometer, Anemometer, Vibration Analyzer, Manometer.
- ✅ **Constant values:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$, $1 \text{ HP} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$, $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- ✅ **Key Acceptance Criterion:** $NPV > 0$ → ACCEPT; $SPP < 5$ years → Attractive.
- ✅ **Typical savings post-audit:** Walk-through = 5–10%, Detailed = 10–20%, IGA = 15–30% of total energy cost.
- ✅ **PAT Scheme relevance:** Designated Consumers (DCs) in 13 sectors **must** undergo energy audits and meet SEC reduction targets or purchase Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts).
- ✅ **Examiner's favorite question format:** *"Differentiate between the three types of energy audits"* — always answer in **tabular form** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
