# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Audit Report — The Diagnostic Blueprint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Energy Audit Report** is a comprehensive, structured document that systematically records the findings, measurements, analysis, and recommendations resulting from an energy audit of a facility. It serves as the definitive communication medium between the auditor and the client, documenting current energy performance, identifying losses, quantifying savings potential, and prescribing actionable conservation measures.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Health Report"

Imagine you visit a doctor for a full body check-up. The doctor:
1. Takes your **measurements** (BP, sugar, cholesterol)
2. **Diagnoses** the problems
3. **Prescribes** medicines, diet, and exercise
4. Provides a **report** you can hand to a specialist

An **energy audit report works exactly the same way** for a building or industrial plant. Instead of a human body, the "patient" is a facility consuming electricity, fuel, or steam. The "vitals" are **kWh, kVA, kg of coal, cubic meters of gas**. The "prescription" is a set of **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** with payback periods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The energy audit report is **not** just a measurement sheet — it is a **decision-making tool** that justifies capital investment in energy-saving projects. Without a proper report, the audit has no lasting value.

### Purpose & Objectives of the Audit Report

- **Document** baseline energy consumption against which savings are measured
- **Quantify** energy losses in terms of rupees (₹) and energy units
- **Recommend** technically and financially viable ECMs
- **Establish** an **Energy Management Information System (EMIS)**
- **Comply** with statutory requirements (e.g., BEE notification under EC Act 2001)
- **Provide** a roadmap for **Management** to plan capital expenditure

### Energy Audit Report — Standard Structure (KTU-Compliant Template)

| Section | Content | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Executive Summary** | Key findings, savings, payback | For top management (1-page) |
| **Audit Methodology** | Type of audit, instruments used, standards followed | Establishes credibility |
| **Facility Description** | Process flow, equipment inventory, operating hours | Sets the context |
| **Energy Consumption Analysis** | Baseline data, Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), energy bills | Quantitative baseline |
| **Energy Cost Analysis** | Tariff structure, demand charges, ToU impact | Financial baseline |
| **Measurements & Data Logger Results** | kWh, kVA, PF, harmonics, temperatures | Engineering evidence |
| **Major Energy-Using Systems (MEUS)** | HVAC, lighting, motors, compressed air, boilers | System-level breakdown |
| **Identified Losses & Wastages** | Idle running, leaks, poor PF, harmonics | Problem statement |
| **Recommended ECMs** | Technical description, savings, cost, payback | Actionable recommendations |
| **Implementation Schedule** | Phased plan, responsibilities, milestones | Project plan |
| **Annexures** | Instrument calibration, raw data, datasheets | Supporting evidence |

---

## 1.2 Power Quality Audit — The Heartbeat of Electrical Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Power Quality (PQ) Audit** is a systematic assessment of the electrical power supplied to and used by a facility to identify disturbances (deviations from the ideal sinusoidal waveform at rated voltage and frequency) that cause equipment malfunction, overheating, downtime, or energy waste. The audit uses specialized PQ analyzers to capture and diagnose events such as **harmonics, sags, swells, transients, and flicker**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Blood Pressure Monitor"

A person's heart pumps **clean, rhythmic blood** at the right pressure. If the heart skips beats (irregular), the pressure drops (sag), or pumps too hard (swell), the body suffers. Similarly:
- The **electrical grid** is the "heart"
- **Clean 50 Hz sinusoidal voltage** is the "healthy blood"
- **PQ disturbances** are the "irregularities" that damage sensitive equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The ideal power supply is a **pure sinusoidal voltage of constant magnitude (e.g., 415 V)** and **constant frequency (50 Hz in India)**. Any deviation in **waveform shape, magnitude, frequency, or symmetry** is a power quality problem.

### Why Power Quality Audit is Essential

> [!CAUTION]
> Modern industrial loads — **VFDs, UPS, SMPS, IT loads, LED drivers** — are major sources of **harmonic distortion**. Poor power quality causes:
> - **Overheating** of transformers, cables, and motors
> - **Nuisance tripping** of breakers
> - **Misoperation** of protective relays
> - **Premature capacitor bank failure**
> - **Loss of production** (a single PQ event can cause lakhs of rupees in downtime)

### IEEE Power Quality Standards (Must-Know for KTU)

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| **IEEE 519-2014** | Harmonic control in electrical power systems — sets limits on voltage and current distortion |
| **IEEE 1159-2019** | Recommended practice for monitoring electric power quality — categorizes PQ events |
| **IEEE 1453-2015** | Voltage flicker and related phenomena |
| **IEEE 1100-2020** | Powering and grounding electronic equipment (the "Emerald Book") |

---

## 1.3 Energy Conservation in Buildings — ECBC Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (BEE, Government of India):**
> The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** is a document developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the Ministry of Power, Government of India, that prescribes the **minimum energy performance standards** for the design and construction of **commercial buildings** having a **connected load of 100 kW or more** or a **contract demand of 120 kVA or more**. ECBC was first launched in **2007** and updated in **2017**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Vehicle's Fuel Efficiency Standard"

When you buy a car, you see a sticker: *"20 km/litre."* This standard tells you **how much fuel the car is allowed to consume per unit of performance**. 

**ECBC does the same for buildings.** It tells architects, HVAC engineers, and builders: *"Your building must not consume more than X kWh/m²/year."* Buildings that perform better than the code receive a **star rating (1★ to 5★)** from BEE.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** ECBC applies only to **commercial buildings** (offices, hotels, hospitals, malls, IT parks). **Residential buildings** are covered by **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)**. The code is **prescriptive** (specifies minimum requirements) and **performance-based** (sets an overall EPI target).

### The Five Pillars of ECBC

| # | Building System | ECBC Concern |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Building Envelope** (walls, roof, windows) | U-factor, SHGC, Visible Light Transmittance |
| 2 | **HVAC Systems** (Heating, Ventilation, AC) | Chiller efficiency, COP, ventilation rates |
| 3 | **Service Hot Water & Pumping** | Pumping efficiency, insulation |
| 4 | **Lighting** | LPD (Lighting Power Density), luminaire efficacy |
| 5 | **Electrical Power** | Transformer losses, PF correction, harmonics |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** ECBC is **not a law in Kerala** (only some Indian states have adopted it). However, it is the **de-facto standard** for all green-building projects in India, including **GRIHA, LEED-India, and IGBC** ratings.

### Key ECBC Compliance Metrics

- **EPI (Energy Performance Index)** = Total annual energy consumed (kWh/year) ÷ Built-up area (m²)
  - **Units:** kWh/m²/year
  - **ECBC Threshold:** Typically **≤ 120 kWh/m²/year** for offices
- **LPD (Lighting Power Density)** = Total lamp wattage ÷ Illuminated area
  - **Units:** W/m²
  - **ECBC Office Limit:** ≤ **9.9 W/m²** (typical)
- **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** — fraction of solar heat passing through glazing
- **U-factor** — heat transfer coefficient of envelope (W/m²K)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Audit Report — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Energy Audit Pyramid

Energy audits are classified into **three levels** of increasing depth and cost. KTU expects students to know the difference precisely.

```
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │   LEVEL 3 (Detailed) │  ←  Investment-grade
              │  Rs. 5–10 Lakh cost  │
              ├──────────────────────┤
              │  LEVEL 2 (Preliminary)│  ←  Mid-depth
              │  Rs. 1–3 Lakh cost   │
              ├──────────────────────┤
              │  LEVEL 1 (Walk-through)│  ←  Quick check
              │  Rs. 10,000–25,000   │
              └──────────────────────┘
```

| Level | Name | Duration | Tools | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Walk-through / Walkdown Audit | 1–3 days | Visual inspection, utility bills, nameplate readings | List of low-cost/no-cost ECMs, identify major losses |
| **Level 2** | Preliminary / Mini Audit | 1–3 weeks | + Portable instruments (lux meter, clamp meter, thermometer, tachometer) | Quantified savings for major ECMs, detailed measurements |
| **Level 3** | Detailed / Comprehensive / Investment-Grade Audit | 2–6 months | + Data loggers, PQ analyzers, flue gas analyzer, ultrasonic flow meter | Engineering design, M&V (Measurement & Verification), bankable report |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Level:** A walk-through audit is enough to spot **obvious** problems (lights ON in daylight, leaking steam traps). A detailed audit is needed when you must convince a **bank** to finance a **crore-rupee** retrofit project.

### 2.1.2 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — The Universal Yardstick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEC** is the **single most important metric** in any energy audit report. It allows fair comparison between two facilities of different sizes.

#### KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Audit Core Equations

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{annual}}$ | kWh/tonne, kWh/unit | Energy per unit of production |
| 2 | **Energy Cost Index (ECI)** | $ECI = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Cost}{P_{annual}}$ | ₹/unit | Cost competitiveness |
| 3 | **Per Capita Energy** | $P_c = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N \times D}$ | kWh/person/day | Energy awareness indicator |
| 4 | **Energy Intensity (EPI)** | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{built-up}}$ | kWh/m²/year | Building energy efficiency |
| 5 | **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | $PLF = \dfrac{kWh\ generated}{Max\ demand \times 8760}$ | dimensionless | Plant utilization (power) |
| 6 | **Utilization Factor** | $UF = \dfrac{Max\ Demand}{Connected\ Load}$ | dimensionless | Equipment use efficiency |
| 7 | **Demand Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{Max\ Demand}{Connected\ Load}$ | dimensionless | Coincidence of loads |
| 8 | **Diversity Factor** | $DiF = \dfrac{\sum Connected\ Loads}{Max\ Demand}$ | dimensionless | Load diversity |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A textile mill's SEC might be **2.5 kWh/kg of fabric** — a cement plant's might be **110 kWh/tonne of cement**. Without SEC, comparing them would be meaningless.

---

## 2.2 Power Quality Audit — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Six Classical Power Quality Disturbances

| # | Disturbance | Definition | Typical Cause | Typical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transient (Impulsive)** | Sudden, brief spike (microseconds) | Lightning, capacitor switching | Insulation breakdown |
| 2 | **Transient (Oscillatory)** | Decaying oscillation at high frequency | Switching events, faults | Relay misoperation |
| 3 | **Sag (Dip)** | RMS voltage drop 10–90% for 0.5 cycles – 1 min | Motor starting, fault | PLC/computer reset |
| 4 | **Swell** | RMS voltage rise 110–180% for 0.5 cycles – 1 min | Large load disconnection | Equipment overstress |
| 5 | **Interruption** | Voltage < 10% for > 1 cycle | Breaker trip, fault | Production stoppage |
| 6 | **Waveform Distortion** | Steady-state deviation from sine | Harmonics from non-linear loads | Overheating, resonance |

### 2.2.2 The Concept of Harmonics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harmonics** are sinusoidal components whose frequencies are **integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (50 Hz)**. A 5th harmonic has a frequency of **5 × 50 = 250 Hz**, a 7th has **350 Hz**, and so on.

#### Why Harmonics Occur

Non-linear loads draw current in **short pulses** rather than smooth sinusoids. This is because they use **solid-state switches** (diodes, thyristors, IGBTs) that conduct only during part of the cycle. The result: **current is distorted**, which then **distorts the voltage** at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC).

| Order | Frequency | Typical Source | Effect on 3-Phase System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3rd** | 150 Hz | SMPS, PC, lighting | **Triplen harmonic** — adds in neutral (worst case) |
| **5th** | 250 Hz | 6-pulse VFD, UPS | **Negative sequence** — opposes rotation, causes motor heating |
| **7th** | 350 Hz | 6-pulse VFD | **Negative sequence** — additional heating |
| **11th** | 550 Hz | 12-pulse VFD (residual) | Negative sequence |
| **13th** | 650 Hz | 12-pulse VFD | Negative sequence |

> [!WARNING]
> **3rd harmonic special case:** In a 3-phase, 4-wire system (with neutral), **3rd, 9th, 15th, 21st...** harmonics (called **triplen harmonics**) **add arithmetically in the neutral conductor**. This can cause **neutral conductor overheating** even when the phase currents are balanced.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Power Quality Metrics

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage)** | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100$ | % | Voltage purity |
| 2 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (Current)** | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100$ | % | Current distortion |
| 3 | **Total Demand Distortion (TDD)** | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Current distortion vs. rated load |
| 4 | **Crest Factor** | $CF = \dfrac{I_{peak}}{I_{RMS}}$ | dimensionless | Peakiness of waveform (1.414 = pure sine) |
| 5 | **True Power Factor** | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos \phi_1 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | dimensionless | Includes harmonic effect |
| 6 | **Displacement PF** | $DPF = \cos \phi_1$ | dimensionless | Fundamental-only PF |
| 7 | **Distortion PF** | $DPF_{dist} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | dimensionless | Harmonic contribution |
| 8 | **K-Factor (Transformer)** | $K = \dfrac{\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} I_h^2}$ | dimensionless | Transformer derating factor for harmonics |
| 9 | **Voltage Sag Magnitude** | $V_{sag} = \dfrac{V_{during}}{V_{nominal}} \times 100$ | % | Retained voltage during sag |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in engineering:** Every PQ audit report must report **THD_V, THD_I, individual harmonics (up to 50th), sag/swell events, PF, and crest factor**. A utility bill with "low PF penalty" must be cross-checked with **true PF (including harmonics)** because DPF and true PF differ when THD is high.

### 2.2.4 IEEE 519 Voltage and Current Distortion Limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 519-2014** is the most cited standard in any PQ audit. KTU students must remember the **5% THD_V limit** for general power systems (≤ 69 kV).

| Bus Voltage at PCC | Individual Harmonic (%) | Total Harmonic Distortion THD_V (%) |
|---|---|---|
| **V ≤ 1 kV** | 5.0 | 8.0 |
| **1 kV < V ≤ 69 kV** | 3.0 | 5.0 |
| **69 kV < V ≤ 161 kV** | 1.5 | 2.5 |
| **V > 161 kV** | 1.0 | 1.5 |

---

## 2.3 ECBC Code — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Applicability of ECBC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECBC applies to buildings that meet ALL of the following:**
> - Building type: **Commercial** (offices, hotels, hospitals, malls, IT parks, airports, etc.)
> - Connected load: **≥ 100 kW** (electrical)
> - OR Contract demand: **≥ 120 kVA**
> - OR Conditioned floor area: **≥ 500 m²**

### 2.3.2 The Five ECBC Compliance Categories

```
                    ┌────────────────────────────┐
                    │   ECBC+ (Super Energy Eff.)│  EPI ≤ ECBC × 0.75
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │   ECBC Compliant            │  EPI ≤ ECBC Reference
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │   ECBC Trade-off Compliant  │  All mandatory, some
                    │   (Prescriptive path)       │  optional not met
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │   Partial ECBC Compliant    │  Only envelope + HVAC
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │   Non-Compliant             │  Fails mandatory reqts.
                    └────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3.3 ECBC Building Envelope Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **Building Envelope** includes the **roof, walls, floor, windows (fenestration), and infiltration**. ECBC specifies:
> - **U-factor** (W/m²K) — lower is better
> - **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** — fraction of solar heat admitted
> - **Visible Light Transmittance (VLT)** — fraction of visible light admitted
> - **Air leakage / Infiltration**

**Climate Zones of India (ECBC uses 5 zones):**

| Climate Zone | Indian Cities | Building Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot & Dry** | Jaipur, Ahmedabad | Heavy insulation, light-coloured roof, minimize glazing |
| **Warm & Humid** | Kochi, Chennai, Mumbai | High ventilation, shading, high VLT glazing |
| **Composite** | Delhi, Lucknow | Balance heating & cooling |
| **Temperate** | Bangalore, Pune | Moderate insulation |
| **Cold** | Srinagar, Leh, Shimla | Heavy insulation, solar gain is beneficial |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Special Note:** Kerala (Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, Calicut) falls in the **Warm & Humid Climate Zone**. Buildings here should use **high VLT glazing, light-coloured roofs, cross-ventilation, and shading devices**.

### 2.3.4 ECBC HVAC Requirements (Simplified)

| Parameter | ECBC Requirement |
|---|---|
| Chiller COP | Minimum COP = 4.10 (water-cooled) at ARI conditions |
| Chiller IPLV | Minimum IPLV = 4.50 |
| Fan Power | ≤ **1.6 W/CFM** (for clean rooms) |
| Ventilation Rate | Per ASHRAE 62.1 or 0.6 L/s per m² |
| Duct Insulation | R-value as per climate zone |
| Pipe Insulation | Per ECBC tables |

> [!NOTE]
> **COP vs IPLV:** COP (Coefficient of Performance) is the ratio of cooling output (W) to power input (W) at a single full-load point. **IPLV (Integrated Part Load Value)** is the weighted average of COP at 100%, 75%, 50%, and 25% loads — it represents **real-world** performance.

### 2.3.5 ECBC Lighting Requirements (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** is the **single most-tested parameter** in KTU exams.

$$LPD = \frac{\text{Total Connected Lighting Load (Watts)}}{\text{Total Illuminated Area (m}^2\text{)}}\ \left[\frac{W}{m^2}\right]$$

| Building Type | ECBC LPD Limit (W/m²) |
|---|---|
| **Office** | 9.9 |
| **Conference Room** | 13.0 |
| **Hospital (Patient Room)** | 7.5 |
| **Lobby/Corridor** | 9.7 |
| **Retail (Malls)** | 14.7 |
| **Classroom** | 11.4 |
| **Parking Garage** | 2.0 |

### 2.3.6 ECBC Electrical Requirements

| Requirement | ECBC Specification |
|---|---|
| Power Factor | Maintain **≥ 0.95** at the meter |
| Transformer Efficiency | Minimum **98%** at 50% loading |
| Maximum Demand | Use ToU (Time-of-Use) optimization |
| Harmonics | Comply with **IEEE 519** |
| Power Distribution Losses | Use **star–delta** distribution, locate transformer at load centre |

### 2.3.7 Energy Performance Index (EPI) — The Master Number

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EPI = Total annual energy consumed (kWh/year) ÷ Total built-up area (m²)**
>
> Unit: **kWh/m²/year** (often read as *kilo-Watt-hour per square-metre per year*)

**ECBC EPI Benchmarks (Approximate Reference Values):**

| Building Type | ECBC Reference EPI (kWh/m²/yr) |
|---|---|
| **Office (Large)** | 120 |
| **Office (Small)** | 90 |
| **Hotel (3-star)** | 180 |
| **Hospital** | 240 |
| **Shopping Mall** | 190 |
| **BPO/IT Office** | 130 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a building's actual EPI > ECBC reference → **non-compliant**. If actual EPI ≤ reference × 0.75 → **ECBC+ (Super Energy Efficient)**.

### 2.3.8 BEE Star Rating Programme for Buildings

```
  ★         (1 star)  →  EPI = (0.90 to 1.00) × Reference
  ★★        (2 star)  →  EPI = (0.80 to 0.90) × Reference
  ★★★       (3 star)  →  EPI = (0.70 to 0.80) × Reference
  ★★★★      (4 star)  →  EPI = (0.60 to 0.70) × Reference
  ★★★★★     (5 star)  →  EPI ≤ 0.60 × Reference
```

> [!NOTE]
> **BEE Star Rating** is a **labelling programme** (voluntary). ECBC is a **code** (prescriptive, mandatory when adopted by a state). Both are BEE initiatives.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Energy Audit Calculations — Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations and worked examples follow the KTU 2024 evaluation style. Each step is annotated with a *Reasoning Tag* (`R1`, `R2`...) so you can reproduce the logic in the exam hall.

### Example 3.1 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Cost Reduction

**Problem:** A foundry consumes **3,60,000 kWh** of electricity and **40 tonnes** of coal (each tonne = 6,000 kCal) per month. Production = **500 tonnes** of castings/month. The electricity tariff is **₹ 8/kWh** and coal cost is **₹ 12,000/tonne**. After an audit, the foundry reduces electricity by **8%** and coal by **5%**. Calculate:
1. Baseline SEC (kWh/tonne) and SEC-thermal (kCal/tonne)
2. Total energy cost before & after
3. Annualized monetary savings

#### Solution

**Step R1: Convert coal to equivalent kCal**

$$\text{Coal energy} = 40 \times 6000 = 2{,}40{,}000\ kCal$$

**Step R2: SEC — Electrical (kWh/tonne)**

$$SEC_{el} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000\ kWh}{500\ tonnes} = 720\ \frac{kWh}{tonne}$$

**Step R3: SEC — Thermal (kCal/tonne)**

$$SEC_{th} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000\ kCal}{500\ tonnes} = 480\ \frac{kCal}{tonne}$$

**Step R4: Baseline Cost**

$$C_{el} = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹}\ 28{,}80{,}000$$
$$C_{coal} = 40 \times 12{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 4{,}80{,}000$$
$$C_{total} = 28{,}80{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 33{,}60{,}000\ /\ month$$

**Step R5: Post-Audit Consumption**

$$E_{el,new} = 3{,}60{,}000 \times (1 - 0.08) = 3{,}31{,}200\ kWh$$
$$M_{coal,new} = 40 \times (1 - 0.05) = 38\ tonnes$$

**Step R6: Post-Audit Cost**

$$C_{el,new} = 3{,}31{,}200 \times 8 = \text{₹}\ 26{,}49{,}600$$
$$C_{coal,new} = 38 \times 12{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 4{,}56{,}000$$
$$C_{new} = 26{,}49{,}600 + 4{,}56{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 31{,}05{,}600\ /\ month$$

**Step R7: Monthly Savings**

$$S_{monthly} = 33{,}60{,}000 - 31{,}05{,}600 = \text{₹}\ 2{,}54{,}400$$

**Step R8: Annualized Savings**

$$S_{annual} = 2{,}54{,}400 \times 12 = \text{₹}\ 30{,}52{,}800\ /\ year$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for the report:** The SEC fell from **720 → 663.4 kWh/tonne (7.9% improvement)** and **480 → 456 kCal/tonne (5% improvement)**. These **specific** numbers are more meaningful than absolute consumption.

---

### Example 3.2 — Power Factor Correction and PF Penalty Avoidance

**Problem:** A factory's average monthly energy data is:
- **Energy (kWh) = 2,00,000**
- **Maximum demand (kVA) = 500 kVA**
- **Existing PF = 0.75 lagging**
- **Tariff: ₹ 200/kVA on max demand, PF penalty: 1% of bill per 0.01 PF below 0.90**

Compute:
1. Real power (kW), Reactive power (kVAr)
2. Bill at 0.75 PF vs bill at corrected PF = 0.95
3. Annual savings

#### Solution

**Step R1: Real Power**

$$P = S \times PF = 500 \times 0.75 = 375\ kW$$

**Step R2: Reactive Power**

$$Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2} = \sqrt{500^2 - 375^2} = \sqrt{2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}625} = \sqrt{1{,}09{,}375} \approx 330.7\ kVAr$$

**Step R3: kW consumed from kWh**

$$\text{Approx. power} \approx \frac{2{,}00{,}000\ kWh}{720\ h} = 277.8\ kW \quad \text{(assumed load pattern)}$$

(For simplicity, we use the **MD-based** PF for billing analysis.)

**Step R4: New kVA at PF = 0.95 (same kW)**

$$S_{new} = \frac{P}{PF_{new}} = \frac{375}{0.95} = 394.7\ kVA$$

**Step R5: kVAr required from capacitor bank**

$$Q_{new} = \sqrt{S_{new}^2 - P^2} = \sqrt{394.7^2 - 375^2} = \sqrt{1{,}55{,}794 - 1{,}40{,}625} = \sqrt{15{,}169} \approx 123.2\ kVAr$$

$$Q_{capacitor} = Q_{old} - Q_{new} = 330.7 - 123.2 = 207.5\ kVAr$$

**Step R6: Demand Charge Saving**

$$\Delta S = 500 - 394.7 = 105.3\ kVA$$
$$\text{Saving} = 105.3 \times 200 = \text{₹}\ 21{,}060\ /\ month$$

**Step R7: PF Penalty Avoidance**

At PF 0.75 → penalty = (0.90 − 0.75)/0.01 = 15 × 1% = 15% of demand bill
At PF 0.95 → no penalty

$$\text{Penalty saved} = 0.15 \times (500 \times 200) = 0.15 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 15{,}000\ /\ month$$

**Step R8: Total Monthly Saving**

$$S = 21{,}060 + 15{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 36{,}060\ /\ month$$
$$S_{annual} = 36{,}060 \times 12 = \text{₹}\ 4{,}32{,}720\ /\ year$$

---

### Example 3.3 — Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) Calculation

**Problem:** A 3-phase VFD-fed motor draws the following RMS currents:
- Fundamental: **I₁ = 200 A**
- 5th harmonic: **I₅ = 40 A**
- 7th harmonic: **I₇ = 25 A**
- 11th harmonic: **I₁₁ = 15 A**
- 13th harmonic: **I₁₃ = 10 A**

Calculate:
1. THD of current (%)
2. True PF if displacement angle φ₁ = 25°
3. Crest factor if measured I_peak = 320 A

#### Solution

**Step R1: Sum of squared harmonic currents**

$$\sum_{h \neq 1} I_h^2 = 40^2 + 25^2 + 15^2 + 10^2 = 1600 + 625 + 225 + 100 = 2550$$

**Step R2: THD_I**

$$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{2550}}{I_1} \times 100 = \frac{50.50}{200} \times 100 = 25.25\ \%$$

**Step R3: Distortion Power Factor**

$$DPF_{dist} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.2525^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0638}} \approx 0.9696$$

**Step R4: Displacement Power Factor**

$$DPF = \cos 25° = 0.9063$$

**Step R5: True Power Factor**

$$PF_{true} = DPF \times DPF_{dist} = 0.9063 \times 0.9696 = 0.8788$$

**Step R6: Crest Factor**

$$CF = \frac{I_{peak}}{I_{RMS,total}}$$

$$I_{RMS,total} = \sqrt{200^2 + 2550} = \sqrt{42{,}550} \approx 206.3\ A$$

$$CF = \frac{320}{206.3} \approx 1.55$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Even though the displacement PF is **0.9063**, the **true PF is only 0.8788** — a drop of about **3%**. This is why a kVAr-only capacitor bank **under-compensates** in a harmonic-rich environment. A **detuned capacitor bank** (tuned below 5th harmonic) or **active harmonic filter** is required.

---

### Example 3.4 — ECBC LPD Compliance Check

**Problem:** An office floor of **20 m × 15 m = 300 m²** has:
- 40 fluorescent tube lights × **40 W** each
- 20 downlighters × **15 W** each
- 10 task lights × **25 W** each (dedicated to workstations, not on full building)
- Emergency lights: 8 × **10 W** (not counted as part of LPD per ECBC)

Calculate:
1. Total LPD
2. Compliance status against ECBC office limit (9.9 W/m²)

#### Solution

**Step R1: Connected load (general lighting)**

$$L_1 = 40 \times 40 = 1600\ W$$
$$L_2 = 20 \times 15 = 300\ W$$
$$L_{task} = 10 \times 25 = 250\ W \quad \text{(counts in LPD)}$$

**Step R2: Total load**

$$L_{total} = 1600 + 300 + 250 = 2150\ W$$

**Step R3: LPD**

$$LPD = \frac{2150}{300} \approx 7.17\ \frac{W}{m^2}$$

**Step R4: Compliance**

$$7.17 < 9.9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ECBC COMPLIANT}$$

**Step R5: Performance Margin**

$$\text{Margin} = 9.9 - 7.17 = 2.73\ W/m^2 \quad (27.6\%\ better\ than\ code)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Tip for KTU:** The emergency lighting load is **excluded** from LPD per ECBC. However, task lighting **is included**. Many students wrongly exclude task lighting in exam answers.

---

### Example 3.5 — ECBC EPI Calculation and Star Rating

**Problem:** A commercial office building in Kochi (warm-humid climate):
- Total built-up area = **5,000 m²**
- Annual electricity consumption = **5,00,000 kWh**
- ECBC reference EPI for the building type = **120 kWh/m²/year**

Find:
1. Actual EPI
2. ECBC compliance status
3. BEE star rating

#### Solution

**Step R1: EPI**

$$EPI_{actual} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = 100\ \frac{kWh}{m^2}{year}$$

**Step R2: ECBC+ Threshold**

$$EPI_{ECBC+} = 0.75 \times 120 = 90\ \frac{kWh}{m^2}{year}$$

**Step R3: Compliance Status**

$$90 < 100 \leq 120 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ECBC COMPLIANT (but not ECBC+)}$$

**Step R4: Star Rating Range**

$$\frac{EPI_{actual}}{EPI_{ref}} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.833$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio **0.833** falls in the **2-star range (0.80–0.90)** of the BEE rating. Hence the building qualifies for **★★ (2-star)** under BEE's labelling programme.

---

## 3.2 The KTU 2024 Analytical Decision Matrix

This matrix maps each topic to its typical question type, marks, and what examiners look for.

| Topic | Question Type | Marks | Key Points Examiner Checks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Audit Report | Structured question (steps, format) | 7–14 | Correct level of audit, SEC formula, ECM payback |
| Power Quality Audit | Definitions + Numerical (THD/PF) | 3–14 | Identification of disturbance, IEEE 519 limits, true PF formula |
| ECBC — Envelope | Short answer or numerical | 3–7 | Climate zone, U-factor, SHGC, KERALA = Warm-Humid |
| ECBC — LPD | Numerical | 3–7 | Correct LPD formula, ECBC table value |
| ECBC — EPI | Numerical + classification | 7–14 | EPI formula, comparison to reference, star rating band |
| ECBC — HVAC | Short answer | 3–7 | COP vs IPLV, chiller efficiency |
| ECBC — Electrical | Short answer | 3 | PF ≥ 0.95, IEEE 519 compliance |

flowchart TD

    B --> C[Step 3: Walk-Through Survey\nVisual inspection, interviews]

    D -- Level 1 --> E1[Walk-Through\nIdentify low-cost ECMs]
    D -- Level 2 --> E2[Preliminary\nPortable instruments]

    E1 --> F[Energy Balance Diagram]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

graph LR

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part A consists of **8 questions × 3 marks = 24 marks**. Students answer **all** Part A questions. Answers must be concise, 3–5 sentences.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEEET612]

**Differentiate between a Walk-Through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — must include 3 key differences):**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit (Level 1) | Detailed Audit (Level 3) |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 months |
| **Instruments** | Visual inspection, utility bills, nameplate readings | Data loggers, PQ analyzers, flue gas analyzers, ultrasonic flow meters |
| **Output** | List of low-cost ECMs, identify major losses | Engineering design, M&V-ready report, bankable investment-grade recommendations |
| **Cost** | ₹ 10,000–25,000 | ₹ 5–10 Lakh |

A walk-through audit **identifies opportunities**, while a detailed audit **quantifies and validates** them for capital investment.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEEET612]

**Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of current. What is the IEEE 519 limit for voltage THD at a 415 V bus?**

**Model Answer:**

**THD of current** is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental component, expressed as a percentage:

$$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\ \%$$

It quantifies the deviation of the current waveform from a pure sinusoid. For a **415 V bus (≤ 1 kV)**, **IEEE 519-2014** prescribes:
- Individual harmonic limit: **5.0 %**
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD_V) limit: 8.0 %**

(For 1 kV < V ≤ 69 kV, the THD_V limit tightens to **5.0 %**.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B has internal choice — students answer either Option A or Option B from each module. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. The model solutions below are written exactly as a **topper's answer script** would be presented.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEET612 Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a comprehensive **Energy Audit Report** with all standard sections. List **at least 8 sections** and explain any **two in detail**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing plant has the following data for one month:
- Electricity consumption: **5,00,000 kWh** at ₹ 7.50/kWh
- Coal consumption: **80 tonnes** (CV = 6,500 kCal/kg) at ₹ 14,000/tonne
- Diesel consumption: **2,000 litres** (CV = 10,200 kCal/kg, density 0.85 kg/L) at ₹ 95/litre
- Production: **1,000 tonnes**
- After implementing ECMs, the total energy cost reduced by **12%** while production remained the same.

Calculate the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — both kWh/tonne and kCal/tonne** — and the **annualized monetary savings**. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 marks

**Structure of a Comprehensive Energy Audit Report:**

A professionally prepared energy audit report, conforming to BEE/EMSI standards, contains the following sections:

1. **Executive Summary** (1 page for top management — management summary of key findings, savings, and recommendations)

2. **Introduction & Scope** (objectives, boundaries, type of audit, duration)

3. **Facility Description** (process flow diagram, plant layout, equipment inventory with nameplate data)

4. **Energy Consumption Analysis** (historical utility bills, energy mix, SEC, EPI)

5. **Audit Methodology** (instruments used, measurement points, standards followed — IS, IEEE, ISO 50001)

6. **Measurements & Data Logger Results** (kWh, kVA, PF, harmonics, temperature profiles, flue gas analysis, lighting levels)

7. **Major Energy-Using Systems (MEUS)** (HVAC, lighting, motors, compressed air, boilers, pumps, fans)

8. **Energy Loss Identification** (idle running, leakages, poor PF, harmonics, waste heat)

9. **Recommended Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** (technical description, savings, cost, simple payback)

10. **Implementation Schedule & M&V Plan** (phased rollout, responsibilities, M&V option as per IPMVP)

11. **Annexures** (calibration certificates, raw data, datasheets)

**Detailed Explanation of Two Sections:**

**(i) Executive Summary:** This is a one-page summary prepared for top management (MD, Plant Head) who do not read technical details. It contains: total baseline energy cost, identified savings potential (₹/year and kWh/year), recommended investment, average payback, and a clear recommendation for implementation. It uses bullet points, infographics, and **Sankey diagrams** to communicate quickly.

**(ii) Recommended ECMs:** This is the heart of the report. Each ECM is presented as a card with:
- **Description** of the measure
- **Baseline consumption** (before)
- **Expected savings** (kWh, kCal, ₹)
- **Capital cost** (₹)
- **Simple Payback** = Capital Cost ÷ Annual Savings
- **Implementation complexity** (low/medium/high)
- **CO₂ reduction** (environmental co-benefit)

The ECMs are typically **rank-ordered by payback period** (shortest first) for easy prioritization.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Convert all fuels to kCal**

$$E_{el} = 5{,}00{,}000\ kWh \times 860\ \frac{kCal}{kWh} = 4{,}30{,}00{,}000\ kCal$$

$$E_{coal} = 80{,}000\ kg \times 6{,}500 = 52{,}00{,}00{,}000\ kCal$$

$$E_{diesel} = 2{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 10{,}200 = 1{,}73{,}40{,}000\ kCal$$

**Step 2: Total energy in kCal**

$$E_{total} = 4.30 + 52.00 + 1.734 = 58.034 \times 10^7\ kCal$$

**Step 3: SEC — Energy per tonne of production**

$$SEC_{thermal} = \frac{58.034 \times 10^7}{1{,}000} = 5.80 \times 10^5\ \frac{kCal}{tonne} = 5.80\ \frac{Lakh\ kCal}{tonne}$$

**Step 4: SEC — Electrical (kWh/tonne)**

$$SEC_{el} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}000} = 500\ \frac{kWh}{tonne}$$

**Step 5: Baseline Cost**

$$C_{el} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\ 37{,}50{,}000$$
$$C_{coal} = 80 \times 14{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 11{,}20{,}000$$
$$C_{diesel} = 2{,}000 \times 95 = \text{₹}\ 1{,}90{,}000$$
$$C_{total} = 37{,}50{,}000 + 11{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}90{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 50{,}60{,}000\ /\ month$$

**Step 6: Annualized Baseline**

$$C_{annual} = 50{,}60{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}\ 6{,}07{,}20{,}000\ /\ year$$

**Step 7: Annual Savings (12% reduction)**

$$S_{annual} = 0.12 \times 6{,}07{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 72{,}86{,}400\ /\ year$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake 1:** Converting kWh to kCal using **860 kCal/kWh** (1 kWh = 860 kCal, where 1 kCal = 4.184 kJ; hence 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kCal). Some students use 1000 by mistake — the examiner deducts **1 mark**.
>
> **Common Student Mistake 2:** SEC must be calculated **per tonne of product**, not per month. Writing the answer as "5,00,000 kWh / 1000 tonnes = 500" is correct, but stating "500 kWh/month" is wrong.
>
> **Common Student Mistake 3:** Diesel density (0.85 kg/L) is often missed. Without this, you cannot convert litres to kg, and hence cannot compute thermal kCal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEET612 Module 1]**

**(a)** Define **Power Quality**. List and briefly explain any **six major power quality disturbances** with their typical causes and effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A facility has the following current harmonic spectrum at a 415 V bus:
- I₁ = 150 A (fundamental, phase angle 22° lagging)
- I₃ = 60 A
- I₅ = 30 A
- I₇ = 20 A

Calculate:
1. THD of current (%)
2. True power factor
3. Whether IEEE 519 limit is violated (per-phase, individual harmonic < 5% at 415 V bus)
**(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 marks

**Definition of Power Quality:**

Power Quality refers to the **degree to which the electrical power supplied to a load matches the ideal characteristics** of a **pure sinusoidal voltage of constant magnitude (e.g., 415 V line-to-line in India) and constant frequency (50 Hz)**. Any deviation in waveform shape, magnitude, frequency, or symmetry constitutes a **power quality problem**.

**Six Major Power Quality Disturbances (with cause and effect):**

| # | Disturbance | Definition | Typical Cause | Typical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transient — Impulsive** | Unidirectional voltage spike of microsecond duration | Lightning, capacitor switching, ESD | Insulation breakdown, equipment damage |
| 2 | **Transient — Oscillatory** | Decaying oscillation at high frequency (0.3–5 MHz) | Line/capacitor switching, fault | Relay misoperation, electronics upset |
| 3 | **Voltage Sag (Dip)** | RMS voltage drop of 10–90% for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute | Motor starting, short circuit, large load energization | PLC/computer reset, process trip |
| 4 | **Voltage Swell** | RMS voltage rise of 110–180% for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute | Large load disconnection, capacitor bank energization | Equipment overstress, insulation breakdown |
| 5 | **Harmonic Distortion** | Steady-state presence of frequencies that are integer multiples of 50 Hz | VFD, UPS, SMPS, LED drivers, arc furnaces | Overheating of cables, transformers, motors; capacitor failure |
| 6 | **Voltage Flicker** | Rapid, repetitive voltage fluctuations | Arc furnaces, welding machines, starting of large motors | Visible lighting flicker, irritation, fatigue |

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Sum of squared harmonic currents**

$$\sum_{h \neq 1} I_h^2 = 60^2 + 30^2 + 20^2 = 3600 + 900 + 400 = 4900\ A^2$$

**Step 2: THD of current**

$$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{4900}}{150} \times 100 = \frac{70}{150} \times 100 = 46.67\ \%$$

**Step 3: Displacement Power Factor**

$$DPF = \cos 22° = 0.9272$$

**Step 4: Distortion Power Factor**

$$DPF_{dist} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (THD_I)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.4667^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.2178}} = 0.9062$$

**Step 5: True Power Factor**

$$PF_{true} = DPF \times DPF_{dist} = 0.9272 \times 0.9062 = 0.8403$$

**Step 6: Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD) check**

$$IHD_3 = \frac{60}{150} \times 100 = 40\ \%$$
$$IHD_5 = \frac{30}{150} \times 100 = 20\ \%$$
$$IHD_7 = \frac{20}{150} \times 100 = 13.33\ \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **IEEE 519 Violation Analysis (CRITICAL for full marks):**
> At a **415 V bus (≤ 1 kV)**, the IEEE 519-2014 limit is:
> - Individual harmonic: **≤ 5 %**
> - THD_V: **≤ 8 %**
>
> In this case, **IHD₃ = 40 %** (limit 5%), **IHD₅ = 20 %** (limit 5%), **IHD₇ = 13.33 %** (limit 5%).
> **All individual harmonics VIOLATE the 5% limit.** The facility requires harmonic mitigation (passive detuned filters, active harmonic filters, or 12-pulse VFDs).
>
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to check **individual harmonics** — many students check only THD. **Both** are required. The examiner allocates **2 marks** for the IEEE 519 compliance discussion.

---

## 5.5 ECBC-Specific KTU Question (Bonus, 14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEET612 Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **scope and applicability** of the **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017** in India. List the **five major building systems** covered by ECBC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An office building in **Kochi** has the following characteristics:
- Built-up area = **8,000 m²**
- Annual electricity = **9,60,000 kWh**
- Office lighting load = **200 fluorescent lights × 36 W**, plus **100 LED panels × 18 W**
- The building is in the **Warm and Humid** climate zone

Calculate:
1. The actual **EPI** and ECBC compliance status (ECBC office reference EPI = 120 kWh/m²/yr)
2. The **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** and check ECBC compliance (office LPD limit = 9.9 W/m²)
3. Recommend a **BEE star rating** (if any)
**(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 marks

**ECBC — Scope and Applicability (BEE, 2017):**

The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017** was developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001**. It sets minimum energy performance standards for new commercial buildings.

**Applicability:** ECBC applies to:
- **Commercial buildings** having **connected load ≥ 100 kW** OR
- **Contract demand ≥ 120 kVA** OR
- **Air-conditioned floor area ≥ 500 m²**

**Building types covered:** Offices, hotels, hospitals, shopping malls, airports, IT parks, educational institutions, and similar commercial facilities.

**Note:** ECBC does **not** apply to **residential buildings** (covered by **Eco Niwas Samhita/ENS**) and industrial process buildings.

**Five Major Building Systems Covered by ECBC:**

1. **Building Envelope** — opaque walls, roof, fenestration, infiltration. ECBC specifies U-factor, SHGC, VLT limits based on **5 climate zones of India**.

2. **HVAC System** — chillers (COP, IPLV), pumps, fans, ductwork insulation, ventilation rates, controls. ECBC mandates minimum chiller efficiency (COP ≥ 4.10 for water-cooled).

3. **Service Hot Water & Pumping** — efficient boiler selection, distribution losses, pipe insulation, pumping efficiency.

4. **Lighting System** — LPD (W/m²) limits, luminaire efficacy, automatic controls (occupancy sensors, daylight sensors).

5. **Electrical Power Distribution** — PF ≥ 0.95, transformer efficiency, harmonics compliance (IEEE 519), distribution layout.

**Kerala Note:** Kerala (Kochi, Calicut, Trivandrum) falls in the **Warm & Humid Climate Zone** — strategy: high VLT glazing, light-coloured roofs, shading devices, cross-ventilation, dehumidification.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: EPI Calculation**

$$EPI_{actual} = \frac{9{,}60{,}000\ kWh/year}{8{,}000\ m^2} = 120\ \frac{kWh}{m^2}{year}$$

**Step 2: ECBC Compliance**

$$EPI_{actual} = 120 = 120 = EPI_{reference}$$

$$\therefore \text{Building is BORDERLINE ECBC COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 3: BEE Star Rating**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{EPI_{actual}}{EPI_{ref}} = \frac{120}{120} = 1.00$$

$$\therefore \text{Building is at the THRESHOLD of 1-star (lowest band, 0.90–1.00).}$$

The building qualifies as **1-star** but is **not** 2-star or higher.

**Step 4: Lighting Load**

$$L_{fluorescent} = 200 \times 36 = 7{,}200\ W$$
$$L_{LED} = 100 \times 18 = 1{,}800\ W$$
$$L_{total} = 7{,}200 + 1{,}800 = 9{,}000\ W$$

**Step 5: LPD Calculation**

$$LPD = \frac{9{,}000\ W}{8{,}000\ m^2} = 1.125\ \frac{W}{m^2}$$

**Step 6: ECBC Compliance Check**

$$1.125 < 9.9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ECBC COMPLIANT (excellent — 89% better than code)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Discussion Point (carry 1 mark):** The lighting system is **highly efficient** (1.125 W/m²), but the **overall EPI** is at the borderline. This means the **HVAC system or other loads** (IT equipment, lifts, plug loads) are driving the energy consumption. The audit recommendation should focus on **HVAC efficiency** rather than lighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert kWh/year to EPI = kWh/m²/year. The unit is the **per-square-meter-per-year** unit, not just kWh/m².
>
> **Common Student Mistake 2:** Using **total built-up area** vs **conditioned floor area** in EPI. ECBC uses **total built-up area**, but some rating systems use **conditioned area**. Stick with **total built-up area** for ECBC.
>
> **Common Student Mistake 3:** Treating the building as **"non-compliant"** when EPI equals the reference. The code threshold is **≤ reference**, so equality means **just compliant** (1-star).
>
> **Common Student Mistake 4:** In LPD calculation, students often **divide by total building area** even if only some floors are illuminated. The **ECBC LPD** uses the **illuminated area**, not total building area.

---

## 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Top Pitfalls (Module 1 — Energy Audit & ECBC):**
>
> **1. THD vs Individual Harmonics:** Examiners **deduct 2 marks** if a student computes THD but **fails to check individual harmonic limits** (e.g., 3rd = 40% >> 5% IEEE 519 limit). Always show **both** IHD and THD.
>
> **2. True PF vs Displacement PF:** Many students use only **cos φ (displacement PF)** and ignore harmonics. For modern industrial loads, **true PF = DPF × distortion PF** is the correct answer. Wrong use = **−1.5 marks**.
>
> **3. ECBC Applicability:** Students often state "ECBC applies to all buildings" — wrong. ECBC applies to **commercial** buildings above the load threshold. Residential = **ENS**.
>
> **4. LPD Units:** LPD is in **W/m²**, not W/m³ or W/m²/100-lux. Wrong unit = **−0.5 mark**.
>
> **5. EPI Unit:** EPI is in **kWh/m²/year**. Many students write kWh/m² only — examiner deducts for **missing /year**.
>
> **6. kWh to kCal Conversion:** Use **860 kCal/kWh**, not 1000 or 3600. Use **density 0.85 kg/L** for diesel.
>
> **7. Climate Zone Confusion:** Kerala = **Warm & Humid** (not Hot & Dry). Mixing it up costs **1 mark** in the ECBC envelope question.
>
> **8. SEC Definition:** Some students define SEC as "energy per unit time" (that's **power**). SEC = **energy per unit of production** (kWh/tonne, kWh/m², etc.).

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and read it 1 hour before the exam.**

### A. Energy Audit Report — Top 12 Points

- ☐ An energy audit report is the **deliverable** of an energy audit. Without a report, the audit has no value.
- ☐ The three audit levels are **Walk-through (Level 1), Preliminary (Level 2), Detailed (Level 3)**.
- ☐ **SEC = Annual Energy ÷ Annual Production** is the most important metric.
- ☐ Report sections: Executive Summary, Methodology, Facility Description, Data Analysis, ECMs, Payback, M&V, Annexures.
- ☐ **Simple Payback (years) = Capital Cost ÷ Annual Monetary Savings**.
- ☐ An ECM should be technically feasible, financially viable, and environmentally beneficial.
- ☐ **Sankey diagrams** are commonly used to show energy flows and losses.
- ☐ **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** is the global standard for M&V.
- ☐ M&V options: A (retrofitted isolation), B (retrofitted measurement), C (whole facility), D (calibrated simulation).
- ☐ Audit instruments include clamp meter, lux meter, power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, ultrasonic flow meter, tachometer.
- ☐ The report should always be **data-driven, not opinion-driven**.
- ☐ A good report uses **photos, diagrams, and tables** — not just text.

### B. Power Quality Audit — Top 12 Points

- ☐ Power quality = **how close the supply is to a clean 50 Hz sine wave at rated voltage**.
- ☐ Six classical disturbances: **transients, sags, swells, interruptions, harmonics, flicker**.
- ☐ **THD** = (RMS of all harmonics / RMS of fundamental) × 100.
- ☐ **3rd harmonic = triplen** — adds in neutral (worst case for 3-phase 4-wire systems).
- ☐ **5th and 11th are negative-sequence** — cause motor heating.
- ☐ **IEEE 519-2014** is the controlling standard for harmonics. At 415 V bus: **IHD < 5%**, **THD_V < 8%**.
- ☐ **True PF = DPF × Distortion PF = cos φ × (1/√(1+THD²))**.
- ☐ **Capacitor bank + harmonics = resonance risk**. Always use **detuned reactors** or **active filters**.
- ☐ **K-factor** of transformer increases with harmonics — K-rated transformers are needed for non-linear loads.
- ☐ PQ audit instruments: PQ analyzer (Class A per IEC 61000-4-30), oscilloscope, harmonic analyzer.
- ☐ **Crest factor > 1.414** indicates peaky non-linear load.
- ☐ **Voltage sag** is the most common and most costly PQ disturbance in industry.

### C. ECBC Code — Top 15 Points

- ☐ ECBC = **Energy Conservation Building Code**, developed by **BEE** under **EC Act 2001**.
- ☐ ECBC was launched in **2007**, updated in **2017**.
- ☐ Applies to **commercial buildings** with **connected load ≥ 100 kW OR demand ≥ 120 kVA OR AC area ≥ 500 m²**.
- ☐ **5 building systems** covered: Envelope, HVAC, Hot Water/Pumping, Lighting, Electrical Power.
- ☐ **5 climate zones** of India: Hot-Dry, Warm-Humid, Composite, Temperate, Cold. **Kerala = Warm-Humid**.
- ☐ **EPI (kWh/m²/year)** is the master metric.
- ☐ **LPD (W/m²)** is the lighting efficiency metric.
- ☐ **U-factor (W/m²K)** for envelope heat transfer. **SHGC** for solar heat gain. **VLT** for visible light.
- ☐ **ECBC+** = EPI ≤ 0.75 × reference.
- ☐ **BEE Star Rating** = 1★ (worst) to 5★ (best). 5★ requires EPI ≤ 0.60 × reference.
- ☐ ECBC office LPD limit = **9.9 W/m²**. ECBC office EPI reference = **120 kWh/m²/year**.
- ☐ **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** is the residential counterpart of ECBC.
- ☐ **PF must be ≥ 0.95** in ECBC-compliant buildings.
- ☐ ECBC references **ASHRAE 90.1** and **NBC 2016** for some design parameters.
- ☐ ECBC is **voluntary nationally but mandatory** in states that adopt it (e.g., Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Uttarakhand, Odisha, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala is in the process).

### D. KTU 2024 Numerical Master Formulas (Memorize These)

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Annual Energy}}{\text{Annual Production}}$$

$$LPD = \frac{\text{Connected Lighting Load}}{\text{Illuminated Area}}$$

$$EPI = \frac{\text{Annual Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Built-up Area (m}^2\text{)}}$$

$$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\ \%$$

$$PF_{true} = \cos\phi_1 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$$

$$K\text{-factor} = \frac{\sum h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum I_h^2}$$

$$\text{Simple Payback} = \frac{\text{Capital Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$$

$$\text{1 kWh} = 860\ kCal = 3.6\ MJ$$

### E. KTU Quick-Reference Table (Last-Minute Revision)

| Topic | Magic Number | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| ECBC Office LPD | **9.9 W/m²** | Direct table value for office LPD compliance |
| ECBC Office EPI Ref | **120 kWh/m²/yr** | Reference EPI for office buildings |
| IEEE 519 THD_V (415 V) | **8%** | Maximum allowed voltage distortion |
| IEEE 519 IHD (415 V) | **5%** | Maximum individual harmonic |
| ECBC PF | **≥ 0.95** | Mandatory at the billing meter |
| Climate Zone (Kerala) | **Warm & Humid** | Determines envelope specs |
| ECBC Connected Load Trigger | **100 kW** | Lower limit for ECBC applicability |
| 1 kWh = | **860 kCal** | Energy unit conversion (diesel/coal calculation) |
| Chiller COP (ECBC) | **≥ 4.10** | Minimum water-cooled chiller COP |
| True PF Formula | **DPF × Distortion PF** | Use in PQ numericals |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for OEEET612 Module 1:**
> 1. Always start with a **definition** (1 mark) before diving into analysis.
> 2. In SEC/EPI/LPD numericals, **show the formula first, then plug in values** — this earns "method marks" even if the final arithmetic is wrong.
> 3. In PQ numericals, **always state IEEE 519 limits** before concluding whether the system is compliant.
> 4. In ECBC questions, **mention the climate zone** — it shows examiner that you understand the engineering context, not just the numbers.
> 5. End every answer with a **practical recommendation** (e.g., "Therefore, an active harmonic filter is recommended") to demonstrate engineering judgment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Management System (BMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Building Management System (BMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Building Management System (BMS)** is a centralized, computer-based intelligent control and monitoring network installed in modern commercial, industrial, and institutional buildings to govern, supervise, and optimize the operation of **Mechanical, Electrical, Electromechanical (MEE)** services — most notably **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)**, lighting, fire safety, security, access control, and power distribution — with the primary objective of ensuring occupant comfort, operational safety, and **energy efficiency**.

The BMS operates on a hierarchical **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** architecture where field-level sensors and actuators communicate with networked controllers, which in turn report to a centralized supervisory workstation running **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms frequently used in KTU textbooks and ISHRAE/IGBC literature:** Building Automation System (BAS), Energy Management and Control System (EMCS), Building Energy Management System (BEMS), and Smart Building System.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building as a Human Body

To a first-time learner, a BMS can be visualized exactly like the **human nervous system**:

| Human Body | Building Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Brain (Central Nervous System)** | Central Supervisory Workstation (Master PC + SCADA Software) |
| **Spinal Cord (Nerves)** | Communication Backbone (BACnet / Modbus / LonWorks / Ethernet) |
| **Local Nerves (Motor Neurons)** | DDC Controllers (AHU Controllers, VAV Controllers) |
| **Sensory Receptors** | Field Sensors (Temperature, CO₂, Humidity, Occupancy, Light) |
| **Muscles / Effectors** | Actuators (Damper Motors, Valve Actuators, VFDs, Relays) |
| **Blood (Energy Carrier)** | Electricity, Chilled Water, Hot Water, Compressed Air |

Just as our brain continuously monitors temperature via skin sensors and signals muscles to shiver or sweat to maintain homeostasis (~**37 °C**), a BMS continuously monitors zone conditions and commands actuators to maintain thermal, lighting, and air-quality setpoints while **minimizing energy input**.

## 1.3 Why BMS is Indispensable in Energy Management

A typical commercial building in India consumes:

$$E_{total} = E_{HVAC} + E_{Lighting} + E_{Plug Load} + E_{Lifts} + E_{Pumping} + E_{Others}$$

Where empirically:

$$E_{HVAC} \approx 40\%-60\% \text{ of } E_{total}$$

$$E_{Lighting} \approx 20\%-30\% \text{ of } E_{total}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without an automated control system, these two systems alone account for **60%–80% of total building energy**. A well-tuned BMS delivers **10%–30% energy savings** with typical payback periods of **2–4 years**, making it a cornerstone topic in any energy management syllabus.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Timeline

- **1960s:** Pneumatic controls (compressed air driven).
- **1970s:** Electronic analog controls (ASIC-based).
- **1980s:** First-generation DDC systems with proprietary protocols.
- **1990s:** Open protocols introduced — **BACnet (ASHRAE/ANSI 135)** and **LonWorks**.
- **2000s:** Web-enabled BMS, IP-based networks.
- **2010s:** Cloud integration, IoT sensors, AI-based optimization.
- **2020s+:** Digital Twin BMS, AI-driven predictive energy analytics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a BMS (The 4-Layer Model)

A BMS is logically structured into **four distinct layers**, each performing a well-defined function:

### Layer 1 — Field (Device) Level
- **Sensors:** Measure physical parameters.
  - Temperature (RTD, Thermistor, Thermocouple)
  - Humidity (Capacitive polymer)
  - CO₂ (NDIR sensor, range **0–2000 ppm**)
  - Occupancy (PIR, Microwave Doppler)
  - Lux (Photodiode, range **0–1000 lux** typical)
  - Flow, Pressure, Current, Voltage
- **Actuators:** Convert control signals to physical action.
  - Damper actuators (Torque **16 Nm** typical for AHV dampers)
  - Valve actuators (Linear, Rotary)
  - Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)
  - Contactor relays (Lighting panels)

### Layer 2 — Automation (Control) Level
- **DDC Controllers** — microprocessor-based (often ARM Cortex-M class).
- Execute **PID control loops** locally.
- Operate autonomously even if the supervisory layer fails.
- Typical control loop cycle: **1 to 15 seconds**.

### Layer 3 — Network (Communication) Level
- Acts as the data highway.
- Standard open protocols:
  - **BACnet (Building Automation and Control Networks)** — ASHRAE/ANSI/ISO 16484-5
  - **Modbus RTU/TCP**
  - **LonWorks (ANSI/CEA-709.1)**
  - **KNX (ISO/IEC 14543)**
  - **MQTT / OPC UA** (modern cloud-compatible)

### Layer 4 — Management (Supervisory) Level
- **SCADA / HMI Software** running on the central workstation.
- Provides **trend logging, alarming, scheduling, reporting**.
- Modern systems offer **BACnet/IP over Ethernet**, web dashboards, and mobile applications.

## 2.2 Core Functions of a BMS (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Monitoring** — Real-time data acquisition from all subsystems.
2. **Control** — Closed-loop PID control of HVAC, lighting, etc.
3. **Scheduling** — Time-of-day (TOD) and calendar-based operation.
4. **Alarming** — Email/SMS alerts on out-of-limit conditions.
5. **Data Logging and Trending** — Storage for analysis and ISO 50001 reporting.
6. **Optimization** — Chiller sequencing, free-cooling, demand limiting.
7. **Integration** — Single platform for HVAC, fire, security, elevators, plumbing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PID Controller Output** | $u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\!\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p$ = proportional gain, $K_i$ = integral gain (s⁻¹), $K_d$ = derivative gain (s), $e(t)$ = error | Standard DDC loop for zone temperature |
| 2 | **Chiller Plant Coefficient of Performance** | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{electrical}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_{in}-T_{out})}{P_{compressor}}$ | $Q$ in kW, $P$ in kW, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) | Energy benchmarking |
| 3 | **Air-Side Energy Savings (Free Cooling)** | $E_{saved} = \dot{V}\,\rho\,c_p\,\Delta T\,\tau_{F}/1000$ | $\dot{V}$ = m³/s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\Delta T$ in K, $\tau_F$ = hours of economizer mode | Quantifying economizer benefit |
| 4 | **Lighting Energy Reduction (Occupancy Control)** | $\Delta E_L = P_{installed}\cdot\tau_{occ,unocc}\cdot N_{fixtures}$ | Power in kW, time in hours | Daylight + occupancy savings |
| 5 | **Simple Payback Period** | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{BMS}}{A_{annual\,savings}}$ | $C$ in ₹ (or $), $A$ in ₹/year | Investment justification |
| 6 | **Return on Investment** | $ROI = \dfrac{A_{annual\,savings}}{C_{BMS}}\times 100\%$ | Percent | Project approval |
| 7 | **Power Demand** | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | $V_L$ in V, $I_L$ in A, $\cos\phi$ = power factor | Three-phase load monitoring |
| 8 | **Chilled Water Load** | $Q_{ch} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | kW | Cooling load tracking |
| 9 | **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{built-up}}$ | kWh/(m²·year) | BEE Star Rating |
| 10 | **Setpoint Reset Linear Law** | $T_{supply,reset} = T_{supply,design} + m\,(T_{OA} - T_{OA,design})$ | Slope $m$ dimensionless | Outdoor-air-based reset |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **closed-form, dimensionless or SI-unit consistent**, and directly usable in KTU numerical problems and Part B derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered here are directly applicable in:

- **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, India, 2007)** compliance audits.
- **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** star rating assessments for Grade II/III buildings.
- **ISO 50001:2018 Energy Management Systems** — EnPI tracking.
- **Green Building certifications:** IGBC, LEED, GRIHA.
- **Smart City infrastructure** — large IT parks, hospitals, airports, data centres.
- **Industrial Energy Audits** — chiller, AHU, and lighting plant optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — PID Control Output for a Zone Temperature Loop

This derivation is **highly important** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions on BMS control strategy.

**Step 1 — Define the control error.**
The deviation between the measured zone temperature and the desired setpoint is the fundamental input to any feedback controller.

$$e(t) = T_{zone}(t) - T_{setpoint}$$

**Step 2 — Define the proportional (P) action.**
The proportional action produces an immediate output proportional to the instantaneous error. It provides the "first response" but cannot eliminate steady-state offset.

$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

**Step 3 — Define the integral (I) action.**
The integral action accumulates past errors over time. This eliminates the steady-state offset inherent in pure-P control.

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Define the derivative (D) action.**
The derivative action anticipates future error by reacting to the rate of change. It damps overshoot and improves transient stability.

$$u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the three actions.**
The total control output is the algebraic sum of P, I, and D contributions. For a chilled-water valve controlling zone temperature, $u(t)$ is mapped to a valve position between **0 % (closed)** and **100 % (open)**.

$$u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t)$$

Substituting each component:

$$\boxed{u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

**Step 6 — Discrete-time implementation (DDC reality).**
Since microcontrollers operate in discrete time, the integral is replaced by a summation and the derivative by a backward difference over a sampling interval $\Delta t$.

$$\begin{aligned}
u[k] &= K_p\,e[k] + K_i\,\sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]\,\Delta t \\
&\quad + K_d\,\frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Anti-Windup safeguard.**
Without clamping, the integrator may saturate when the actuator reaches its physical limit (valve fully open). The conditional integrator prevents this.

$$u_I[k] = \begin{cases} u_I[k-1] + K_i e[k]\Delta t & \text{if } 0 \le u[k-1] \le 100 \\ u_I[k-1] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Free-Cooling Energy Savings (Air-Side Economizer)

**Step 1 — Identify available data.**
- Outside air flow rate: $\dot{V}$ in m³/s
- Air density: $\rho$ in kg/m³
- Specific heat: $c_p$ in kJ/(kg·K)
- Enthalpy or temperature difference enabling free cooling: $\Delta T$ in K
- Duration of free-cooling operation: $\tau_F$ in hours per year.

**Step 2 — Compute mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute thermal energy per unit time transferred by air.**

$$Q_{thermal} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the free-cooling duration to obtain annual energy saved (in kWh).**

$$E_{saved} = Q_{thermal} \cdot \tau_F = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot \tau_F$$

**Step 5 — Convert to commercial units (MWh or ₹).**

$$E_{saved,MWh} = \frac{E_{saved,kWh}}{1000}$$

$$\text{Money saved} = E_{saved,kWh} \cdot \text{Tariff (₹/kWh)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A hospital BMS controls an Air Handling Unit (AHU) delivering $\dot{V} = 5$ m³/s of air at $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K). The economizer mode operates for $\tau_F = 1500$ hours/year with an effective temperature differential $\Delta T = 8$ K. The grid tariff is ₹8 per kWh. Calculate the annual monetary savings.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 5 = 6 \text{ kg/s}$$

Step 2 — Thermal power avoided:

$$Q = \dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T = 6 \times 1.005 \times 8 = 48.24 \text{ kW}$$

Step 3 — Annual energy saved:

$$E_{saved} = 48.24 \times 1500 = 72{,}360 \text{ kWh/year}$$

Step 4 — Monetary savings:

$$\text{Savings} = 72{,}360 \times 8 = \text{₹ }5{,}78{,}880 \text{ per year}$$

This example quantifies the financial value of a single BMS-controlled economizer mode — an answer path commonly required for 14-mark questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem Coverage vs. BMS Functions

| Subsystem | Sensor(s) Used | Actuator(s) Used | Control Strategy | Typical Energy Saving |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chilled Water System | Supply/Return T, Flow | Chiller on/off, VFD on pump | Sequencing + Reset | **15 %–25 %** |
| AHU / HVAC | T, RH, CO₂, DP | Damper, Valve, Fan VFD | PID + Scheduling | **20 %–30 %** |
| Lighting | Lux, Occupancy | Dimmers, Contactors | Daylight harvesting + TOD | **30 %–50 %** |
| Pumping | Pressure, Flow | VFD | Differential pressure control | **20 %–40 %** |
| Power Monitoring | CT, PT, kWh meter | Demand limit logic | Peak shaving | **5 %–15 %** |
| Fire & Life Safety | Smoke, Heat | Dampers, Alarms | Code-driven logic | Safety + small energy impact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.5 Reading the Diagrams

- The **top-down** arrows denote command/control signals.
