<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a Building Management System (BMS). List any four functions of a BMS.**

**Model Answer:**
A Building Management System (BMS) is a computerized, networked control and monitoring system used to centrally supervise and regulate the mechanical, electrical, and electromechanical services of a building, with the goal of ensuring occupant comfort, safety, and energy efficiency.

Four key functions:
1. **Monitoring** of field parameters (T, RH, CO₂, etc.).
2. **Control** using PID-based closed loops.
3. **Scheduling** based on time-of-day and calendar.
4. **Alarm Management** with email/SMS notifications.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal | Converts an electrical signal into a physical action |
| Direction | Input to the controller | Output from the controller |
| Example 1 | Temperature sensor (RTD, Thermistor) | Damper actuator |
| Example 2 | CO₂ sensor (NDIR) | Valve actuator |

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Paper, Module 1 Choice-Based Question]**

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical architecture** of a typical Building Management System with a neat block diagram. List the functions performed at each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The BMS architecture is logically divided into four hierarchical layers:

**Layer 1 — Field Level:** Consists of sensors (T, RH, CO₂, occupancy, flow, pressure, current) and actuators (dampers, valves, VFDs, relays). These are physically installed in the plant/zone and interface with the process.

**Layer 2 — Automation Level:** Houses DDC (Direct Digital Controller) units, which execute PID control loops locally and provide first-level intelligence. They operate even if the upper layer fails.

**Layer 3 — Network Level:** The communication backbone using open protocols such as **BACnet (ASHRAE/ANSI 135)**, **LonWorks**, **Modbus**, or **KNX**. Modern deployments use Ethernet/IP.

**Layer 4 — Management Level:** The SCADA/HMI software on a central workstation, providing trend logging, alarming, scheduling, reporting, and web/mobile dashboards.

The block diagram is represented by the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.2 of these notes.

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** in BMS. Explain with a diagram how a **PID controller** maintains zone temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
DDC replaces conventional pneumatic/analog control with microprocessor-based digital logic. It allows precise, repeatable, and easily tunable control. A zone temperature PID loop operates as follows:

- **Setpoint (SP)** = desired temperature (e.g., **24 °C**).
- **Process Variable (PV)** = measured temperature from the sensor.
- **Error** $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- **PID Output** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d\,de/dt$.
- **Output drives** the chilled water valve or damper actuator.

The feedback diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes illustrates this loop. Proper tuning (Ziegler-Nichols or software auto-tuning) minimizes overshoot and settling time.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is meant by **free-cooling** in a BMS-controlled AHU? Derive the expression for annual energy saved via an air-side economizer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Free-cooling (economizer mode) is a strategy where outside air, when cooler than return air, is used to cool the building directly, reducing or eliminating mechanical chiller operation.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V}$ (kg/s).

Step 2 — Sensible cooling capacity transferred by air: $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ (kW).

Step 3 — Annual energy saved (kWh): $E_{saved} = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T \tau_F$.

Step 4 — Monetary savings = $E_{saved} \times \text{Tariff}$.

This was numerically demonstrated in Section 3.3.

**(b)** Explain any **four energy-saving control strategies** implemented through a modern BMS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Optimal Start/Stop (OSS):** The BMS calculates the latest possible time to start HVAC before occupancy to just reach setpoint, avoiding pre-conditioning waste.
2. **Chiller Sequencing and Load Balancing:** Selects the most efficient combination of chillers based on part-load efficiency (kW/RT) and ambient wet-bulb temperature.
3. **Chilled Water Temperature Reset:** Raises chilled water supply temperature (e.g., from 6 °C to 8 °C) when load is low, raising chiller COP.
4. **Demand-Limiting / Peak Shaving:** BMS sheds non-critical loads during peak tariff hours by shedding lighting, raising zone temperatures by 1–2 °C, or switching off auxiliary equipment.
5. **Daylight Harvesting:** Dim or switch off artificial lighting near windows based on photocell readings.
6. **Occupancy-Based Control:** PIR sensors turn off HVAC/lighting in vacant zones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. Students often **omit the units** in PID derivations → Lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **BACnet (ASHRAE 135)** with **LonWorks (ANSI 709.1)** — protocol names and standards must be exact.
> 3. Drawing the architecture diagram **without labels** on the layers → Lose up to 3 marks.
> 4. Failing to mention **Open vs. Proprietary protocols** when discussing the Network Layer.
> 5. In numericals, writing the final monetary answer **without showing the formula derivation** → Partial credit only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ BMS = **Computerized, centralized, network-based** building control system.
- ✅ Primary objective triad: **Comfort + Safety + Energy Efficiency**.
- ✅ Four-layer architecture: **Field → Automation → Network → Management**.
- ✅ DDC stands for **Direct Digital Control** — local microprocessor-based control.
- ✅ Open communication protocols to remember: **BACnet, LonWorks, Modbus, KNX**.
- ✅ HVAC consumes **40 %–60 %** of total building energy; lighting consumes **20 %–30 %**.
- ✅ Typical BMS-mediated energy savings: **10 %–30 %** with **2–4 year payback**.
- ✅ Master PID equation: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d\,de/dt$.
- ✅ Free-cooling energy formula: $E_{saved} = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T \tau_F$ (kWh).
- ✅ Chiller COP: $COP = Q_{cooling}/W_{electrical}$ (typically **3.0–6.0**).
- ✅ EPI benchmark for Grade A commercial buildings: **≤ 90 kWh/m²/year** (ECBC-compliant).
- ✅ Sensors: RTD/Thermistor (T), Capacitive polymer (RH), NDIR (CO₂), PIR (occupancy), Photodiode (Lux).
- ✅ Actuators: Damper, Valve, VFD, Contactor, Relay.
- ✅ BMS functions: Monitoring, Control, Scheduling, Alarming, Trending, Optimization, Integration.
- ✅ **Energy-saving strategies:** Optimal Start/Stop, Chiller Sequencing, CHW Reset, Demand Limiting, Daylight Harvesting, Occupancy Control.
- ✅ Standards to remember: **ASHRAE 135 (BACnet)**, **ANSI/CEA-709.1 (LonWorks)**, **ISO 50001:2018** (EnMS), **ECBC 2007** (India).
- ✅ Always write **units and standard conditions** in KTU numerical answers.
- ✅ Always pair every **derivation with a labeled block diagram** for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity Transmission and Distribution System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electricity Transmission and Distribution System # ⚡ Electricity Transmission and Distribution System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electricity Transmission and Distribution (T&D) System** is the **network of electrical components** deployed to **transmit electrical energy** from the **generating stations (power plants)** to the **end consumers (loads)** through a **hierarchically stepped-down voltage architecture**, governed by the laws of electromagnetism (Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, and Maxwell's equations). In the KTU 2024 framework, the T&D system is studied as an **integrated energy management domain** where the central objective is to **minimize losses**, **maximize reliability**, **optimize voltage profile**, and **ensure economic dispatch** of power from source to sink. The system is conventionally partitioned into **three functional blocks**: - **Generation Side (Above 11 kV, typically 11 kV bus at the power station)** - **Transmission Network (66 kV, 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV, 1200 kV HVDC)** - **Distribution Network (11 kV / 415 V / 240 V)** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Roadway of Electricity" 🚛 Imagine electricity is **water flowing through a giant plumbing network**: - **Generating Station** = A massive water pumping plant - **Step-Up Transformer** = A pressure booster that shoots water at high pressure - **Transmission Line** = A high-pressure trunk pipeline (just like a national highway) - **Step-Down Substation** = A pressure-reducing station near your town - **Distribution Line** = Small local streets branching from a main road - **Service Connection** = The garden hose directly reaching your house - **Load** = The tap (faucet) you open to use water > [!NOTE] > **Why do we step-up voltage for transmission?** > Because power $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$, at a fixed power level, increasing voltage $V$ **decreases current $I$**. Since line losses $P_{loss} = I^2 R$, **lower current means quadratically lower losses**. For example, transmitting at **400 kV instead of 11 kV** reduces losses by a factor of $\left(\frac{400}{11}\right)^2 \approx 1322$ times. ### 1.3 Standardized KTU Voltage Levels (Indian Power Grid) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)**, India follows the **standard voltage levels**: > - **Generation:** 11 kV / 21 kV / 25 kV > - **Primary Transmission (EHV):** 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV > - **Secondary Transmission:** 66 kV, 33 kV > - **Primary Distribution:** 11 kV > - **Secondary Distribution:** 415 V (3-phase), 240 V (1-phase) > - **HVDC Bipole links:** ±500 kV, ±800 kV The **two standard frequencies** used in India are: - **50 Hz** for the integrated grid (standard) - **16⅔ Hz** (historical, for old railway traction — largely discontinued) ### 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Plant
11-25 kV] --> B[Step-Up Transformer
400/220 kV] B --> C[EHV Transmission Line
Overhead Underground] C --> D[Primary Substation
400kV/220kV] D --> E[Secondary Substation
220kV/66kV] E --> F[Distribution Substation
11kV/415V] F --> G[Industrial Load
11kV] F --> H[Commercial Load
415V] F --> I[Domestic Load
240V] ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Transmission Line Electrical Parameters Every transmission line is characterized by **four distributed parameters per unit length**: | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Cause | Frequency Dependence | |-----------|--------|------|-------|----------------------| | Resistance | $R$ | $\Omega$/km | Conductor material & geometry | Increases with frequency (skin effect) | | Inductance | $L$ | H/km | Magnetic field around conductor | $L \propto \ln(D/r')$ | | Capacitance | $C$ | F/km | Electric field between conductors | $C \propto 1/\ln(D/r)$ | | Conductance | $G$ | S/km | Leakage over insulators, corona | Mostly dielectric losses | Where: - $D$ = Distance between conductors (GMD) - $r$ = Radius of conductor - $r' = r \cdot e^{-1/4} = 0.7788\,r$ (for GMR) ### 2.2 Inductance of a Three-Phase Line > [!NOTE] > **Inductance per phase** of a 3-phase symmetric line is: > $$L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \quad \text{H/m}$$ > Where **Geometric Mean Radius (GMR)** $r' = 0.7788\,r$ for a solid round conductor. **Inductive Reactance per phase per km:** $$X_L = 2\pi f L = 0.1445 \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) + 0.0157 \quad \Omega/\text{km}$$ ### 2.3 Capacitance of a Three-Phase Line > [!IMPORTANT] > **Capacitance per phase (to neutral)** of a 3-phase symmetric line: > $$C_n = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)} = \frac{0.02413 \times 10^{-9}}{\log_{10}(D/r)} \quad \text{F/m}$$ **Capacitive Reactance (Shunt Reactance):** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_n} \approx 0.1788 \times 10^6 \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{r}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{f} \quad \Omega \cdot \text{km}$$ ### 2.4 Classification of Transmission Lines (KTU 2024 Emphasis) | Type | Length | Voltage | Modeling Approach | |------|--------|---------|-------------------| | **Short Line** | $< 80$ km | $< 20$ kV | Lumped $R$, $L$ only; $C$ ignored | | **Medium Line** | $80$–$250$ km | $20$–$100$ kV | Lumped $R$, $L$, and half $C$; **Nominal-T** or **Nominal-$\pi$** | | **Long Line** | $> 250$ km | $> 100$ kV | **Distributed parameters**; wave propagation | ### 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table forms the **backbone of KTU Energy Management problems**. Memorize these! | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | Sending End Voltage | $V_S = \sqrt{(V_R \cos\phi_R + I_R R)^2 + (V_R \sin\phi_R + I_R X)^2}$ | V | | 2 | Voltage Regulation | $\%VR = \dfrac{V_S - V_R}{V_R} \times 100$ | % | | 3 | Line Losses (3-$\phi$) | $P_L = 3 I^2 R$ | W | | 4 | Transmission Efficiency | $\%\eta = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \dfrac{V_R I_R \cos\phi_R}{V_S I_S \cos\phi_S} \times 100$ | % | | 5 | Surge Impedance | $Z_C = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$ | $\Omega$ | | 6 | Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) | $SIL = \dfrac{V_L^2}{Z_C}$ | W | | 7 | ABCD Constants (Lossless) | $A = D = \cos\beta l$, $B = jZ_c \sin\beta l$, $C = j\dfrac{\sin\beta l}{Z_c}$ | — | | 8 | Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$ | 1/km | | 9 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | m | | 10 | Ferranti Effect (No-load) | $V_R = V_S \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\beta l}$ | V | | 11 | Maximum Power (Stability) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}$ (steady-state) | W | | 12 | Power Factor Correction | $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | VAR | | 13 | Charging Current | $I_C = \omega C V$ | A | | 14 | Corona Loss (Peek's) | $P_c = \dfrac{244}{\delta}(f+25)\sqrt{\dfrac{r}{D}}(V - V_d)^2 \times 10^{-5}$ | kW/km/phase | > [!NOTE] > **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** is a critical KTU concept: it is the **natural loading** at which a line transmits power with **flat voltage profile** — losses from $I^2R$ exactly equal charging VARs. Lines loaded above SIL see **voltage drop**; below SIL, voltage **rises** (Ferranti effect). ### 2.6 Generalized Circuit Constants (ABCD Parameters) The **two-port network** representation: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}$$ For a **short transmission line**: $A = D = 1$, $B = Z$, $C = 0$ For a **medium line (Nominal-$\pi$)**: $$A = D = 1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$$ For a **long line (exact)**: $$A = D = \cosh(\gamma l), \quad B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma l), \quad C = \dfrac{1}{Z_c}\sinh(\gamma l)$$ **Properties:** $AD - BC = 1$ (reciprocity); $A = D$ (symmetry for symmetric lines). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance of a Single-Phase Two-Wire Line **Setup:** Two long parallel conductors of radius $r$, separated by distance $D$, carrying currents $I$ (outward in one, return in the other). **Step 1 — Magnetic field due to a long straight conductor at distance $x$:** $$B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi x}$$ **Step 2 — Flux linkage with the conductor (from $x = r'$ to $x = D - r'$):** $$\phi = \int_{r'}^{D-r'} B \, dx = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \int_{r'}^{D-r'} \frac{dx}{x}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$\phi = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \left[ \ln x \right]_{r'}^{D-r'} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{D-r'}{r'}\right)$$ **Step 4 — For $D \gg r'$, $D - r' \approx D$:** $$\phi \approx \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Inductance (excluding internal flux, using GMR):** $$L = \frac{\phi}{I} = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) = 4 \times 10^{-7} \ln\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \quad \text{H/m}$$ **Step 6 — Converting to per km and using log base 10:** $$L = 0.4605 \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \quad \text{mH/km}$$ > [!NOTE] > For three-phase symmetric line, replace $D$ with **Geometric Mean Distance (GMD)**: > $$D_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$$ > If conductors are at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $D$, then $D_{eq} = D$. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitance of a Three-Phase Line **Step 1 — Potential difference between two conductors (one at $+q$, other at $-q$ per unit length):** $$V = \frac{q}{\pi \varepsilon_0} \ln\left(\frac{D}{r}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Capacitance between the two conductors:** $$C = \frac{q}{V} = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)} \quad \text{F/m}$$ **Step 3 — Capacitance to neutral per phase (3-phase symmetric):** $$C_n = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m:** $$C_n = \frac{0.02413}{\log_{10}(D/r)} \quad \mu\text{F/km}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Performance of Short Transmission Line **Model:** A short line is represented as a series impedance $Z = R + jX$. **Step 1 — Phasor diagram setup:** Let the receiving-end voltage be $V_R$ at angle $0^\circ$, and the load current be $I_R$ lagging by $\phi_R$. $$V_R = V_R \angle 0^\circ, \quad I_R = I_R \angle -\phi_R$$ **Step 2 — Voltage drop across the line:** $$I_R \cdot Z = I_R (R + jX)$$ **Step 3 — Sending-end voltage:** $$V_S = V_R + I_R(R + jX)$$ **Step 4 — Magnitude (separating real and imaginary parts):** $$V_S = \sqrt{(V_R \cos\phi_R + I_R R)^2 + (V_R \sin\phi_R + I_R X)^2}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage Voltage Regulation:** $$\%VR = \frac{V_S - V_R}{V_R} \times 100$$ **Step 6 — Power loss in the line:** $$P_L = 3 I_R^2 R = I_R^2 R \quad \text{(per phase)}$$ **Step 7 — Transmission efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_R}{P_S} = \frac{3 V_R I_R \cos\phi_R}{3 V_R I_R \cos\phi_R + 3 I_R^2 R} = \frac{V_R \cos\phi_R}{V_R \cos\phi_R + I_R R}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Power-Factor Correction Capacitor Sizing **Given:** Real power $P$ (W), initial power factor $\cos\phi_1$, desired power factor $\cos\phi_2$. **Step 1 — Initial reactive power:** $$Q_1 = P \tan\phi_1$$ **Step 2 — Final reactive power:** $$Q_2 = P \tan\phi_2$$ **Step 3 — Reactive power to be supplied by capacitor bank:** $$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$ **Step 4 — Required capacitance (3-phase, star-connected):** $$C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2} \quad \text{(per phase, in Farads)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark problems** in KTU Energy Management. KTU typically gives a load in kW at p.f. $\cos\phi_1$ and asks the kVAR rating of the capacitor bank to improve p.f. to $\cos\phi_2$. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model | Condition | Length (km) | Voltage | Capacitance Included? | Best Model | |-----------|-------------|---------|----------------------|------------| | Domestic feeders | $0$–$10$ | $415$ V | No | Short line | | 11 kV rural distribution | $10$–$50$ | $11$ kV | No (approx.) | Short line | | 66 kV sub-transmission | $50$–$100$ | $66$ kV | Yes (lumped half) | Nominal-$\pi$ | | 132 kV inter-city | $100$–$250$ | $132$ kV | Yes (lumped) | Nominal-T or $\pi$ | | 400 kV EHV | $> 250$ | $400$ kV | Distributed | Exact (hyperbolic) | ## 4. This preserves academic accuracy while remaining computationally renderable. ### 4.6 Distribution System Topologies — Comparative Analysis | Topology | Reliability | Cost | Complexity | Use Case | |----------|-------------|------|------------|----------| | **Radial** | Lowest | Lowest | Simplest | Rural feeders | | **Ring Main** | High | Moderate | Moderate | Urban 11 kV | | **Interconnected (Mesh)** | Highest | Highest | Complex | Metro cities, critical loads | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Surge Impedance Loading" (SIL) of a transmission line. Why is it considered the most economical loading of an EHV line?* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** is defined as the **power delivered by a lossless line to a load resistance equal to the surge impedance** $Z_c$ of the line. Mathematically, > $$SIL = \frac{V_L^2}{Z_c} \quad \text{(watts)}$$ > Where $V_L$ is the line-to-line voltage and $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ is the surge impedance. **Why economical?** At SIL loading, the **reactive power generated by the shunt capacitance** $I^2 X_C$ **exactly equals** the **reactive power consumed by the series inductance** $I^2 X_L$. The net reactive power balance is zero — no external reactive support is required, voltage profile is **flat**, and the line operates at its **natural efficiency**. Lines loaded above SIL absorb net VARs (voltage drops); below SIL, they generate net VARs (voltage rises — Ferranti effect). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the "Ferranti Effect" observed in long EHV transmission lines. What practical measures are adopted to mitigate it?* **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ferranti Effect** is the phenomenon where the **receiving-end voltage $V_R$ exceeds the sending-end voltage $V_S$** when the line is lightly loaded or on no-load. It occurs because the **distributed line capacitance draws a leading current** that produces a voltage rise across the line inductance. For a lossless long line: $$V_R = \frac{V_S}{\cos(\beta l)}$$ For a typical 400 kV, 400 km line, $\cos(\beta l) \approx 0.95$, so $V_R \approx 1.05\,V_S$ — a **5% voltage rise**. **Mitigation measures:** 1. **Shunt Reactors** connected at the receiving end (or intermediate substations) to absorb leading VARs 2. **Series Compensation** by capacitors 3. **SVC (Static VAR Compensators)** for dynamic reactive control 4. **Tap-changing transformers** at substations 5. **Operating at reduced voltage** during light-load conditions --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** for 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are provided below. --- #### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]` **A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 100 km long transmission line has the following per-phase parameters:** - Resistance $R = 0.25\;\Omega$/km - Inductance $L = 1.5$ mH/km - Capacitance $C = 0.012\;\mu$F/km - Line voltage = 132 kV - Load at receiving end = 30 MVA at 0.85 power factor lagging **Calculate using the Nominal-$\pi$ model:** **(a)** Sending-end voltage, sending-end current, sending-end power factor, and percentage voltage regulation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Transmission efficiency and total power loss in the line. State whether the line is being efficiently utilized. **(7 marks)** --- **Complete Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total per-phase line parameters:** $$R_{total} = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\;\Omega$$ $$L_{total} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.15\;\text{H}$$ $$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.15 = 47.12\;\Omega$$ $$C_{total} = 0.012 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 1.2\;\mu\text{F}$$ $$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2652.6\;\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Half-shunt admittance (Nominal-$\pi$ places $C/2$ at each end):** $$Y = j B_C = j\,\frac{1}{X_C/2} = j \frac{2}{2652.6} = j\,7.54 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S}$$ **Step 3 — Receiving-end phase voltage and current:** $$V_R(\text{phase}) = \frac{132{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 76{,}210\;\text{V}$$ $$I_R = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 132{,}000} = 131.22\;\text{A at } \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79^\circ \text{ lag}$$ Take $V_R = 76{,}210 \angle 0^\circ$ V, $I_R = 131.22 \angle -31.79^\circ$ A $$I_R = 131.22(\cos 31.79^\circ - j\sin 31.79^\circ) = 131.22(0.85 - j\,0.527) = 111.54 - j\,69.15\;\text{A}$$ **Step 4 — Current through series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$:** $$Z = 25 + j\,47.12\;\Omega$$ $$I_Z = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} = I_R + V_R \cdot j\,3.77 \times 10^{-4}$$ Wait, in **Nominal-$\pi$**, the **shunt branch is at the ends** and the **series branch is in the middle**. The current entering the series impedance is: $$I_1 = I_R + \frac{Y}{2} V_R$$ $$\frac{Y}{2} V_R = j\,3.77 \times 10^{-4} \times 76{,}210 \angle 0^\circ = j\,28.73\;\text{A}$$ $$I_1 = (111.54 - j\,69.15) + (0 + j\,28.73) = 111.54 - j\,40.42\;\text{A}$$ **Step 5 — Sending-end voltage (across the series branch + receiving-end):** $$V_S = V_R + I_1 Z$$ $$I_1 Z = (111.54 - j\,40.42)(25 + j\,47.12)$$ $$= 111.54 \times 25 + 111.54 \times j\,47.12 - j\,40.42 \times 25 - j\,40.42 \times j\,47.12$$ $$= 2788.5 + j\,5256.4 - j\,1010.5 + 1904.6$$ $$= 4693.1 + j\,4245.9\;\text{V}$$ $$V_S = 76{,}210 + 4693.1 + j\,4245.9 = 80{,}903.1 + j\,4245.9\;\text{V}$$ $$|V_S| = \sqrt{80903.1^2 + 4245.9^2} = \sqrt{6.546 \times 10^9 + 1.803 \times 10^7} \approx 81{,}015\;\text{V (phase)}$$ $$V_S(\text{line}) = 81{,}015 \times \sqrt{3} = 140{,}320\;\text{V} = 140.32\;\text{kV}$$ $$\angle V_S = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4245.9}{80903.1}\right) = 3.00^\circ$$ **Step 6 — Sending-end current:** $$I_S = I_1 + \frac{Y}{2} V_S = (111.54 - j\,40.42) + j\,3.77 \times 10^{-4} \times (80903.1 + j\,4245.9)$$ $$= 111.54 - j\,40.42 + j\,30.50 - 1.601$$ $$= 109.94 - j\,9.92\;\text{A}$$ $$|I_S| = \sqrt{109.94^2 + 9.92^2} = 110.39\;\text{A}$$ $$\angle I_S = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-9.92}{109.94}\right) = -5.16^\circ$$ **Step 7 — Sending-end power factor:** $$\cos\phi_S = \cos(\angle V_S - \angle I_S) = \cos(3.00 - (-5.16)) = \cos(8.16^\circ) = 0.9899 \text{ lag}$$ **Step 8 — Percentage Voltage Regulation:** $$\%VR = \frac{V_S - V_R}{V_R} \times 100 = \frac{140.32 - 132}{132} \times 100 = 6.30\%$$ **Step 9 — (Part b) Power loss and efficiency:** $$P_{loss} = 3 |I_1|^2 R = 3 \times (110.39^2 + 40.42^2)^{1/2 \times 2} \times 25$$ Wait, $|I_1|^2 = 111.54^2 + 40.42^2 = 12441 + 1634 = 14075\;\text{A}^2$ $$P_{loss} = 3 \times 14075 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 1055.6\;\text{kW}$$ Hmm, recomputing with the current magnitude (note: the loss uses the magnitude of the series current): $$|I_1| = \sqrt{111.54^2 + 40.42^2} = \sqrt{12441 + 1634} = \sqrt{14075} = 118.65\;\text{A}$$ $$P_{loss} = 3 \times 118.65^2 \times 25 = 3 \times 14078 \times 25 = 1{,}055{,}850\;\text{W} \approx 1056\;\text{kW}$$ **Real power delivered at receiving end:** $$P_R = 30 \times 0.85 = 25.5\;\text{MW}$$ **Sending-end real power:** $$P_S = P_R + P_{loss} = 25.5 + 1.056 = 26.556\;\text{MW}$$ **Transmission Efficiency:** $$\%\eta = \frac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \frac{25.5}{26.556} \times 100 = 96.02\%$$ **Comment on efficient utilization:** The efficiency of 96% is reasonable for a 100 km, 132 kV line. However, the voltage regulation of **6.30%** is on the higher side; standard limits (as per CEA) recommend **±5%** for 132 kV systems. **Conclusion:** The line is **reasonably efficient** but may require **shunt compensation** (capacitor banks) or **tap-changing transformer** to maintain voltage profile. --- #### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **A 400 kW, 3-phase industrial load operates at 0.72 power factor lagging. It is desired to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging. The supply is 3-phase, 50 Hz, 415 V.** **(a)** Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank required (in kVAR). What size capacitor per phase is needed if the bank is connected in delta? **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the capacitors are connected in star, recalculate the per-phase capacitance. Comment on which connection (delta or star) is preferred and why. **(7 marks)** --- **Complete Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial apparent power and reactive power:** $$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{400}{0.72} = 555.56\;\text{kVA}$$ $$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.94^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_1 = 0.9638$$ $$Q_1 = P \tan\phi_1 = 400 \times 0.9638 = 385.5\;\text{kVAR}$$ **Step 2 — Final reactive power at desired p.f.:** $$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.3287$$ $$Q_2 = P \tan\phi_2 = 400 \times 0.3287 = 131.5\;\text{kVAR}$$ **Step 3 — Reactive power to be supplied by capacitor bank:** $$\boxed{Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 385.5 - 131.5 = 254.0\;\text{kVAR}}$$ **Step 4 — Per-phase rating (delta connection):** In delta, each capacitor sees **line voltage** $V_L = 415$ V. Per-phase reactive power: $$Q_{C,\phi} = \frac{254}{3} = 84.67\;\text{kVAR}$$ Capacitance per phase: $$Q_{C,\phi} = \frac{V_L^2}{X_C} = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$ $$C_\Delta = \frac{Q_{C,\phi}}{2\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{84670}{2\pi \times 50 \times 415^2} = \frac{84670}{54{,}116{,}807} = 1.564 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{F}$$ $$\boxed{C_\Delta \approx 1564\;\mu\text{F per phase}}$$ **Step 5 — Per-phase rating (star connection):** In star, each capacitor sees **phase voltage** $V_{ph} = 415/\sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V. $$C_Y = \frac{Q_{C,\phi}}{2\pi f V_{ph}^2} = \frac{84670}{2\pi \times 50 \times 239.6^2} = \frac{84670}{18{,}034{,}982} = 4.695 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{F}$$ $$\boxed{C_Y \approx 4695\;\mu\text{F per phase}}$$ **Step 6 — Comparison and comment:** | Connection | Voltage across C | Required Capacitance | Cost | |------------|------------------|----------------------|------| | **Delta** | $V_L = 415$ V | $1564\;\mu$F (smaller) | Lower | | **Star** | $V_L/\sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V | $4695\;\mu$F (larger) | Higher | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Delta connection is preferred** because: > 1. **Smaller capacitance** is required (3 times less than star for the same kVAR) > 2. **Lower cost** of capacitor units > 3. **Better utilization** of voltage rating > 4. In case of one capacitor failing open in delta, the system can still operate in an "open delta" configuration with reduced capacity (66.7% rating), providing redundancy > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing line and phase voltage** in delta/star calculations (this is the #1 error — worth 3–4 marks loss) > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3** when converting total kVAR to per-phase kVAR > 3. **Wrong angle conversion** — $\cos^{-1}(0.72) \neq 0.72$ rad; always use degrees or compute $\tan\phi$ directly from $\sqrt{1-\cos^2\phi}/\cos\phi$ > 4. **Units mismatch** — capacitance must be in Farads, not $\mu$F during the formula; convert at the end > 5. **Forgetting to mention "delta is preferred"** with the reason — KTU's marking scheme gives 1 mark for the comment --- ### 5.3 Additional Practice Questions (For Self-Assessment) **Q3. [3 marks]** *A 3-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has conductors of 1.5 cm diameter spaced in an equilateral triangle of 4 m side. Calculate the inductance and capacitance per phase per km. (Model: $L = 1.262$ mH/km, $C = 0.0092\;\mu$F/km)* **Q4. [14 marks]** *A 250 km, 400 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has $R = 0.035\;\Omega$/km, $L = 1$ mH/km, $C = 0.011\;\mu$F/km, $G = 0$. Using the exact long-line model, compute ABCD constants and the sending-end voltage when delivering 200 MW at 0.9 p.f. lag at the receiving end. (Hint: First compute $\gamma$, $Z_c$, then use hyperbolic functions.)* **Q5. [3 marks]** *Why are bundle conductors used in EHV transmission lines? List any two advantages.* > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer Q5:** Bundle conductors are used in **EHV/UHV lines (≥ 400 kV)** to: > (i) **Reduce corona loss** and radio interference by effectively increasing the conductor radius > (ii) **Reduce the surge impedance** $Z_c$, thereby **increasing the SIL** of the line > (iii) **Improve the voltage gradient** distribution around conductors, reducing the maximum surface stress --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: T&D System** **⚡ A. Fundamentals** - Power $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (3-phase) - T&D exists because **high-voltage transmission reduces $I^2R$ losses** - Standard Indian voltages: 11 kV → 66/132 kV → 220/400/765 kV → 11 kV → 415/240 V **⚡ B. Line Parameters** - **Resistance $R$** → ohmic loss; increases with frequency due to **skin effect** - **Inductance $L$** → $L = 4 \times 10^{-7} \ln(D/r')$ H/m - **Capacitance $C$** → $C_n = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)}$ F/m to neutral - **GMD** (Geometric Mean Distance) for symmetric 3-phase line: $D_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} D_{bc} D_{ca}}$ - **GMR** (Geometric Mean Radius) for solid round conductor: $r' = 0.7788\,r$ **⚡ C. Line Models (Must Memorize ABCD)** - **Short:** $A=D=1$, $B=Z$, $C=0$ - **Nominal-T:** half-C at each end, full Z in middle - **Nominal-$\pi$:** half-C at each end, full Z in middle (just rearranged for calculation) - **Long line (exact):** hyperbolic functions of $\gamma l$ - Identity: $AD - BC = 1$ **⚡ D. Performance Metrics** - **% Voltage Regulation** = $\frac{V_S - V_R}{V_R} \times 100$ - **% Efficiency** = $\frac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100$ - **% Compensation** = reduction in kVAR / original kVAR **⚡ E. Phenomena** - **Ferranti Effect:** $V_R > V_S$ at no-load; mitigated by **shunt reactors** - **Skin Effect:** $R_{ac} > R_{dc}$; increases with frequency - **Corona:** power loss + ozone + radio interference; minimized by **bundled conductors** and **larger diameters** - **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL):** flat-voltage loading; $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C} \approx 400\;\Omega$ for typical 400 kV line; SIL = $V_L^2 / Z_c$ **⚡ F. Power Factor Correction** - $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ (total 3-phase kVAR) - **Delta-connected** capacitors preferred (smaller C, lower cost, redundancy via open-delta) **⚡ G. Distribution Topologies** - **Radial** → rural (low cost, low reliability) - **Ring Main** → urban 11 kV (high reliability, can isolate faults) - **Interconnected/Mesh** → metros (highest reliability, most complex) **⚡ H. Modern Trends (For 2-mark questions)** - **HVDC transmission** for bulk power over long distances (> 500 km) and subsea cables - **FACTS devices** (SVC, STATCOM, UPFC) for dynamic reactive control - **Smart Grid** integration with renewable energy sources - **GIS (Gas-Insulated Substations)** for urban compact installations > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** > - For **3-mark questions**, focus on **definitions + one real-world example**. > - For **14-mark numerical**, always: (i) draw the **circuit diagram** [1 mark], (ii) state **assumptions** [1 mark], (iii) show **formulae before substitution** [1 mark], (iv) box the **final answer with units** [1 mark]. The remaining 10 marks are for correct steps. > - Always mention **standard Indian voltage levels** wherever relevant — examiners love this context.

topics.title: Cascade Efficiency, Lighting: Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cascade Efficiency, Lighting: Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Cascade Efficiency — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cascade Efficiency** is defined as the overall (net) efficiency of a **series of sequential energy conversion processes**, in which the *waste energy* (or *by-product energy*) from one process becomes the useful *input energy* for the next downstream process. It is mathematically expressed as the **product of the individual stage efficiencies**. $$ \eta_{cascade} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \dots \times \eta_n $$ The concept is foundational in **Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power – CHP)**, **Combined Cycle Power Plants (CCPP)**, **District Heating Systems**, and **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** schemes. Under the KTU 2024 Energy Management syllabus, cascade efficiency is the key metric used to justify the thermodynamic and economic viability of integrating multiple energy conversion stages. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket Relay" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Water Bucket Relay** > Imagine four workers standing in a line, passing a bucket of water. > - Worker 1 scoops water from a well and pours 90% of it into a smaller bucket (spills 10%). > - Worker 2 receives that bucket and pours 85% into a jar (spills 15%). > - Worker 3 pours 80% into a bottle (spills 20%). > - Worker 4 pours 75% into a glass (spills 25%). > > **The glass finally contains**: 0.90 × 0.85 × 0.80 × 0.75 ≈ **45.6%** of the original water. > > This product (45.6%) is the **Cascade Efficiency**. The spillage at every stage is the *loss*, and the *useful output of one stage* becomes the *input of the next*. Energy behaves identically — every time energy is converted or transferred, some is lost (mostly as heat), and the remainder cascades forward. --- ### 1.3 Lighting — Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Modern Energy-Efficient Light Source** is an electric lamp technology that delivers a high **luminous efficacy** (lumens per Watt, lm/W), long **service life** (in hours), and good **Color Rendering Index (CRI)** while consuming significantly less electrical power than incandescent or older conventional sources. **Key Standard Metrics** (per the Bureau of Indian Standards IS 16101–16108 series, and IESNA guidelines): - **Luminous Efficacy (η)**: lumens output per Watt of electrical input — unit: **lm/W**. - **Luminous Flux (Φ)**: total light emitted by the source — unit: **lumen (lm)**. - **Color Rendering Index (CRI)**: ability to render colors faithfully (max = **100**). - **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)**: warmth/coolness of light — unit: **Kelvin (K)**. - **Lamp Life (L)**: hours of useful operation before failure — unit: **hours (h)**. - **Luminance / Illuminance (E)**: light falling on a surface — unit: **lux (lx) = lm/m²**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The energy management of lighting is judged on the **"watts saved per lux delivered"** principle. The Indian Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) and the SuperECBC+ mandate minimum efficacy thresholds of **≥ 100 lm/W** for indoor fixtures and **≥ 110 lm/W** for outdoor installations. --- ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Watering Can" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy for Efficacy** > Think of two watering cans filling a flower bed. Can A sprinkles 50 litres using 100 W (soaks a small patch). Can B sprinkles 150 litres using the same 100 W (soaks 3× the area). Both use the *same electrical energy*, but Can B is the **efficient light source** — same watts, **3× the useful light (lumens)**. Efficacy is simply: *"How much useful light do I get per Watt I pay for?"* ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Cascade Efficiency — Theoretical Breakdown In any real energy system, conversion is **not a single step**. A modern power plant converts fuel → thermal → mechanical → electrical → useful work. Each transition incurs losses due to the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, friction, radiation, and heat rejection. #### Step-by-Step Logic: - **Step 1 — Identify the energy chain.** List every conversion stage between the primary energy input (e.g., coal, gas) and the final useful energy (e.g., electricity + process steam). - **Step 2 — Calculate individual stage efficiencies.** Each stage $i$ has an efficiency $\eta_i = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output}_i}{\text{Input}_i}$. - **Step 3 — Multiply the stage efficiencies.** Because the output of stage $i$ becomes the input of stage $i+1$, the overall efficiency is the **product**, not the sum. - **Step 4 — Identify losses at every stage.** The "lost" portion $(1 - \eta_i)$ represents waste heat, friction, flue gas losses, electrical losses in auxiliaries, etc. - **Step 5 — Justify cascade design.** Cascade systems are designed to *recover* losses and reuse them as inputs to lower-grade processes (e.g., waste heat from electricity generation used for space heating). #### Typical Energy Cascade in a Cogeneration Plant: $$ \text{Fuel} \xrightarrow{\eta_1} \text{Steam (Thermal)} \xrightarrow{\eta_2} \text{Turbine (Mechanical)} \xrightarrow{\eta_3} \text{Generator (Electrical)} \xrightarrow{\eta_4} \text{Grid Output} $$ - The rejected heat from the turbine (around 60% of fuel energy) is captured in a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**. - This recovered heat is then supplied for **process heating** (overall cascade efficiency for combined heat + power can reach **80–90%**). --- ### 2.2 Lighting — Theoretical Breakdown A modern energy-efficient lighting system is evaluated on three pillars: - **Pillar 1 — Photopic Efficacy (η_v)**: The ratio of luminous flux to power consumed. Higher = better. - **Pillar 2 — Service Life & Lumen Maintenance**: LED sources lose lumens slowly (L70 ≥ 50,000 h) vs. CFL losing ~15% by 5,000 h. - **Pillar 3 — Quality of Light**: CRI ≥ 80 is mandated for office, educational, and healthcare spaces per ECBC. #### Categories of Modern Light Sources: - **Solid-State Lighting (SSL) — LED**: Most efficient (100–200 lm/W lab, 100–160 lm/W commercial). - **Compact Fluorescent Lamp (CFL)**: 50–70 lm/W. Being phased out under Minamata Convention. - **Linear Fluorescent (T5)**: 80–100 lm/W. Common in commercial retrofits. - **Induction Lamps**: 70–90 lm/W, very long life (100,000 h). - **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) — Metal Halide / HPSV**: 80–130 lm/W, used in street lighting and high-bay applications. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory formulas for Module 2 — preserve this table verbatim for revision.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary / Note | |---|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Cascade Efficiency (n stages) | $\eta_{cascade} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$ | $\eta_i$ = stage efficiency (dimensionless) | Applies only when stages are in *series* | | 2 | Total Energy Out | $E_{out} = E_{in} \cdot \eta_{cascade}$ | Energy in Joules or kWh | Cumulative output after n stages | | 3 | Cumulative Loss | $L_{total} = 1 - \eta_{cascade}$ | Dimensionless fraction | Useful for "what-if" loss analysis | | 4 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | $\Phi$ in lm, $P$ in W → lm/W | Higher = more efficient | | 5 | Luminous Flux from Efficacy | $\Phi = \eta_v \cdot P$ | lm = lm/W × W | Used to size lamp output | | 6 | Illuminance (Inverse Square Law) | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | E in lx, d in metres | Point source approximation | | 7 | Energy Saved by Retrofit | $\Delta E = (P_{old} - P_{new}) \cdot N \cdot t$ | P in W, N = no. of lamps, t in h | kWh = ΔE / 1000 | | 8 | Annual Cost Saving | $S = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot C_{kWh}}{1000}$ | $C_{kWh}$ = ₹/kWh | Direct monetary benefit | | 9 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta E \cdot C_{kWh} / 1000}$ | $I$ = investment ₹ | Years to recover capital | | 10 | CRI-weighted Efficacy | $\eta_{v,CRI} = \eta_v \cdot \dfrac{CRI_{actual}}{100}$ | Dimensionless correction | Used in ECBC audits | | 11 | Power Density (LPD) | $LPD = \dfrac{\sum P_{i}}{A}$ | W/m² of floor area | ECBC limit: office 8.5–10 W/m² | | 12 | CHP Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{W_{elec} + Q_{thermal}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | W, Q in same energy unit | Cascade of two useful outputs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: In numerical problems, always convert **hours to years** by multiplying by the annual operating schedule (e.g., 12 h/day × 365 = 4,380 h/year). Also remember **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 1 unit on the electricity bill**. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cascade efficiency** is the cornerstone of **IGCC (Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle)** plants, **Combined Heat, Power and Cooling (CCHP / Tri-generation)** systems, and **Pinch Analysis** in process industries. - **Efficient lighting retrofits** are the single largest, lowest-hanging energy-saving measure in Indian commercial buildings — typically yielding **30–60%** energy reduction with **payback < 2 years**. - Both topics are directly assessed in the **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) Grade-1, Grade-2, and Grade-3 Energy Manager/Auditor certification exams**, which KTU graduates frequently pursue. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of Cascade Efficiency from First Principles Let us consider a generic energy conversion chain with $n$ stages in series. Let the energy input to stage $i$ be $E_{i-1}$ and the useful output of stage $i$ be $E_i$. The efficiency of stage $i$ is defined as: $$ \eta_i = \frac{E_i}{E_{i-1}} $$ This gives the recurrence relation: $$ E_1 = \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ $$ E_2 = \eta_2 \cdot E_1 $$ Substituting the first relation into the second: $$ E_2 = \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ Extending this to stage $n$: $$ E_n = \eta_n \cdot E_{n-1} = \eta_n \cdot \eta_{n-1} \cdot \eta_{n-2} \cdots \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ The overall cascade efficiency is the ratio of final useful output to the initial input: $$ \eta_{cascade} = \frac{E_n}{E_0} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i $$ This is the classical **product rule for series-coupled efficiency blocks**, equivalent to the **dB-multiplication rule in cascaded amplifiers** (though dB uses a logarithmic scale). --- ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Cogeneration Cascade **Problem**: A sugar mill cogeneration plant has the following stage efficiencies. Compute the cascade efficiency and the electrical output per kg of bagasse fired (calorific value = 8 MJ/kg). Also compute the energy recovered as useful process heat. - Boiler efficiency, $\eta_1 = 0.88$ - Steam turbine (mechanical) efficiency, $\eta_2 = 0.35$ - Generator (electrical) efficiency, $\eta_3 = 0.96$ - Heat recovery from turbine exhaust to process, $\eta_4 = 0.60$ (fraction of turbine input heat recovered as useful process heat) **Step 1 — Compute the cascade electrical efficiency (Stages 1, 2, 3 are in series)**: $$ \eta_{elec,cascade} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 $$ $$ \eta_{elec,cascade} = 0.88 \times 0.35 \times 0.96 $$ $$ \eta_{elec,cascade} = 0.88 \times 0.336 = 0.2957 $$ So the electrical cascade efficiency is **29.57%** — typical for a biomass plant. **Step 2 — Compute the useful electrical energy per kg of bagasse**: $$ E_{in} = 8 \text{ MJ/kg} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg} $$ $$ E_{elec} = \eta_{elec,cascade} \times E_{in} = 0.2957 \times 8 = 2.366 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the useful thermal (process heat) energy per kg of bagasse**: The heat input to the turbine is: $$ E_{turbine,in} = \eta_1 \times E_{in} = 0.88 \times 8 = 7.04 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ The waste heat from the turbine exhaust (heat that did not convert to shaft work) is: $$ E_{waste} = (1 - \eta_2) \times E_{turbine,in} = (1 - 0.35) \times 7.04 = 0.65 \times 7.04 = 4.576 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ Recovered useful process heat: $$ E_{thermal} = \eta_4 \times E_{waste} = 0.60 \times 4.576 = 2.7456 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the overall CHP cascade efficiency**: $$ \eta_{CHP} = \frac{E_{elec} + E_{thermal}}{E_{in}} = \frac{2.366 + 2.7456}{8} = \frac{5.1116}{8} = 0.6390 $$ So the **overall cascade efficiency is 63.90%**, which is **2.16× better** than the 29.57% electrical-only efficiency. This is why cogeneration is mandated in energy-intensive industries in India under the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme** of the BEE. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — LED Lighting Retrofit **Problem**: An office floor has **200 incandescent bulbs** of **60 W** each, replaced by **9 W LED tubes** delivering equal lumens. The office operates **10 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Electricity tariff is **₹7.50 per kWh**. Initial cost of each LED = **₹250**, total retrofit cost = **200 × 250 = ₹50,000**. **Step 1 — Compute the reduction in power per lamp**: $$ \Delta P = P_{old} - P_{new} = 60 - 9 = 51 \text{ W/lamp} $$ **Step 2 — Total connected load reduction**: $$ \Delta P_{total} = 200 \times 51 = 10{,}200 \text{ W} = 10.2 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Annual operating hours**: $$ t = 10 \times 300 = 3{,}000 \text{ h/year} $$ **Step 4 — Annual energy saved**: $$ \Delta E = \Delta P_{total} \times t = 10.2 \times 3{,}000 = 30{,}600 \text{ kWh/year} $$ **Step 5 — Annual monetary saving**: $$ S = \Delta E \times C_{kWh} = 30{,}600 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}2{,}29{,}500 \text{ per year} $$ **Step 6 — Simple Payback Period**: $$ SPP = \frac{I}{S} = \frac{50{,}000}{2{,}29{,}500} \approx 0.218 \text{ years} \approx 2.6 \text{ months} $$ **Step 7 — Annual CO₂ avoided (Grid emission factor ≈ 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh for Kerala)**: $$ CO_2 \text{ avoided} = 30{,}600 \times 0.82 = 25{,}092 \text{ kg} = 25.09 \text{ tonnes/year} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: A payback of under 3 months is exceptional — this is why LED retrofits are the *first recommended measure* in every Energy Audit report. The luminous efficacy comparison: incandescent ≈ **10–15 lm/W**, LED ≈ **100–130 lm/W**, giving a **8–10× efficacy improvement**. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Light Sources > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip**: Memorize the efficacy ranges and the CRI of LED (≥ 80) vs. HPSV (≤ 25). This is a frequently asked comparison question. | Parameter | Incandescent | CFL | T5 Fluorescent | HPSV (Sodium) | LED | |-----------|--------------|-----|----------------|---------------|-----| | Power (W) | 60 | 14 | 28 | 70 | 9 | | Luminous Flux (lm) | 720 | 800 | 2,600 | 6,300 | 1,000 | | Efficacy (lm/W) | 12 | 57 | 93 | 90 | 111 | | CRI | 100 | 82 | 85 | 25 | 80–95 | | CCT (K) | 2,700 | 2,700–6,500 | 4,000–6,500 | 2,000 | 2,700–6,500 | | Life (h) | 1,000 | 8,000 | 20,000 | 24,000 | 50,000 | | Mercury content | None | 3–5 mg | 5 mg | 10–20 mg | None | | Switching tolerance | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Good | Excellent | | Dimmable | Yes | Limited | Limited | No | Yes | | Annual Energy (kWh) at 3,000 h | 180 | 42 | 84 | 210 | 27 | | Annual Cost @ ₹7.50/kWh | ₹1,350 | ₹315 | ₹630 | ₹1,575 | ₹202.5 | > **Inference from matrix**: LED is the dominant technology on **every** parameter except initial cost and historically lower CRI (now resolved with high-CRI LEDs at CRI ≥ 95). HPSV is best for **monochromatic yellow-light** applications (street lighting, foggy areas) but terrible for colour-critical tasks. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Cascade Energy Flow in a Cogeneration (CHP) System The following block-level functional architecture maps how fuel energy cascades through a sugar-mill cogeneration plant and how waste heat is recovered as a second useful output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FUEL INPUT
Bagasse / Coal / Gas
Ein = 100 units] --> B[BOILER
eta1 = 0.88
Steam generation] B --> C[TURBINE
eta2 = 0.35
Mechanical shaft work] C --> D[GENERATOR
eta3 = 0.96
Electrical output] D --> E[GRID
Eelec = 29.6 units
Cascade Electrical Efficiency] C --> F[EXHAUST HEAT
65% of turbine input
Ewaste = 41.3 units] F --> G[HRSG
Heat Recovery Steam Generator
eta4 = 0.60] G --> H[PROCESS HEAT
Useful Thermal Output
Ethermal = 24.8 units] B -.->|Flue gas loss 12%| X[STACK LOSS
12 units] C -.->|Condenser loss 4%| Y[COOLING TOWER
3.2 units] style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style E fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style G fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style H fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style X fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Y fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` **Reading the diagram**: - **Blue** = Energy input. **Green** = Energy conversion cascade. **Orange** = Waste energy. **Purple** = Recovery stage. **Red** = Useful outputs. **Grey** = Rejected losses. - The two red blocks (Grid + Process Heat) represent the **two useful outputs** of a cascade system — the foundation of CHP economics. --- ### 4.2 Comparison Architecture: Energy Flow in Different Light Sources ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INC[INCANDESCENT BULB] I1[Electrical Input 60 W] --> I2[Tungsten Filament
Heated to 2700 C] I2 --> I3{Energy Partition} I3 -->|Visible Light 8 W| I4[12 lm W efficacy] I3 -->|Infrared Heat 45 W| I5[Largely wasted] I3 -->|Conduction Convection 7 W| I6[Lost to surroundings] end subgraph LED[LED BULB] L1[Electrical Input 9 W] --> L2[Semiconductor Junction
Electron Hole Recombination] L2 --> L3{Energy Partition} L3 -->|Visible Light 7.5 W| L4[110 lm W efficacy] L3 -->|Heat via Heatsink 1.5 W| L5[Managed by thermal design] end style I1 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I4 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I5 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I6 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L4 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L5 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Reading the diagram**: - Incandescent loses **~88% of input energy** as heat; LED retains **~83% of input energy as useful light**. The numbers explain the global transition mandated by the **BEE Star Label programme** and the **EESL (Energy Efficiency Services Limited) UJALA scheme** in India. --- ### 4.3 Lighting Energy Management Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Step 1: Audit current
lighting load and LPD] --> Q2{LPD exceeds
ECBC limit?} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Step 2: Identify lamps
with low efficacy] Q2 -->|No| Q4[Step 3: Optimize controls
occupancy daylight] Q3 --> Q5{Replace with LEDs
achieving 100 lm W?} Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Step 4: Validate
payback under 3 years] Q5 -->|No| Q7[Step 4 alt: Install
T5 or induction lamps] Q6 --> Q8[Step 5: Add dimming
and smart controls] Q7 --> Q8 Q4 --> Q8 Q8 --> Q9[Step 6: Verify with
lux meter and re-audit] style Q1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Q6 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Q8 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Q9 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Style**: Part A is direct, expects crisp definition or short derivation. No choice. Time allocation ~1–2 minutes per mark. --- #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define cascade efficiency. Why is the overall efficiency of a series of energy conversion processes computed as a product, not a sum, of the individual stage efficiencies?** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: **Definition (1 mark)**: Cascade efficiency is the overall (net) efficiency of a series of sequential energy conversion processes, defined as: $$ \eta_{cascade} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i $$ **Why product, not sum (2 marks)**: In a series cascade, the *output of stage i* becomes the *input of stage i+1*. Mathematically, $E_1 = \eta_1 E_0$, $E_2 = \eta_2 E_1 = \eta_2 \eta_1 E_0$, and so on. Since each output is scaled by the next stage's efficiency, the cumulative effect is a **multiplicative chain**, not additive. Adding efficiencies would incorrectly treat losses and useful output on the same scale, violating conservation of energy. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define luminous efficacy. A 12 W LED produces 1,320 lumens. Calculate its efficacy and compare it with a 60 W incandescent lamp producing 720 lumens.** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: **Definition (1 mark)**: Luminous efficacy $\eta_v$ is the ratio of luminous flux (in lumens) emitted by a source to the electrical power (in watts) consumed by it, expressed in lm/W. **LED Calculation (1 mark)**: $$ \eta_{v,LED} = \frac{1320}{12} = 110 \text{ lm/W} $$ **Incandescent Calculation & Comparison (1 mark)**: $$ \eta_{v,inc} = \frac{720}{60} = 12 \text{ lm/W} $$ **Comparison**: The LED has an efficacy **9.17 times higher** than the incandescent lamp. For the same light output of 720 lm, the LED consumes only **6.5 W**, saving **~89% electrical energy**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. Marks are awarded for: Correct formula (2M), Substitution (2M), Final answer with units (2M), Interpretation/comment (1M). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** A small-scale industry uses a cogeneration system with the following efficiencies in series: boiler = 0.90, turbine = 0.30, generator = 0.95. The fuel input is natural gas with a calorific value of 38 MJ/m³ and the plant consumes 1,000 m³/day. Calculate the daily electrical energy output in kWh and the cascade efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of cascade efficiency with reference to a combined heat and power (CHP) plant. Show how waste heat recovery improves the overall efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**: **Step 1 — Cascade electrical efficiency (2 marks)**: $$ \eta_{cascade} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 = 0.90 \times 0.30 \times 0.95 $$ $$ \eta_{cascade} = 0.2565 \text{ or } 25.65\% $$ **Step 2 — Total fuel energy input per day (1 mark)**: $$ E_{in} = 1{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \times 38 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 38{,}000 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 3 — Electrical energy output per day (2 marks)**: $$ E_{elec} = \eta_{cascade} \times E_{in} = 0.2565 \times 38{,}000 = 9{,}747 \text{ MJ/day} $$ Convert MJ to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$ E_{elec} = \frac{9{,}747}{3.6} = 2{,}707.5 \text{ kWh/day} $$ **Step 4 — Final answer with units (1 mark)**: **Daily electrical output = 2,707.5 kWh/day, Cascade efficiency = 25.65%** **Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark)**: This efficiency is typical for small gas-based cogeneration units. With waste heat recovery, the total useful energy (electrical + thermal) can exceed 70%, justifying CHP investment. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**: **Concept (2 marks)**: Cascade efficiency in a CHP plant arises because the plant has two sequential useful outputs — electrical (from the generator) and thermal (from the turbine exhaust). The cascade is: $$ \text{Fuel} \xrightarrow{\eta_1} \text{Steam} \xrightarrow{\eta_2} \text{Shaft Work} \xrightarrow{\eta_3} \text{Electricity} $$ $$ \text{Turbine Exhaust} \xrightarrow{\eta_4} \text{Process Heat} $$ **Energy balance (3 marks)**: For every 100 units of fuel energy, a typical Rankine-cycle boiler-turbine-generator cascade gives: - ~30 units → Electricity - ~50 units → Low-grade turbine exhaust heat - ~12 units → Flue gas loss - ~8 units → Condenser + auxiliaries The 50 units of exhaust heat, normally wasted, are captured in a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** and used for **process steam, space heating, or absorption chilling**. **Overall CHP efficiency (2 marks)**: $$ \eta_{CHP} = \frac{W_{elec} + Q_{thermal}}{Q_{fuel}} = \frac{30 + 30}{100} = 60\% $$ This is **double** the electrical-only efficiency, validating the cascade approach. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat comparison table, describe the salient features of at least four modern energy-efficient light sources used in commercial buildings. Define luminous efficacy and CRI in your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A street-light installation uses 250 sodium-vapour lamps of 150 W each, operating 11 hours per day for 365 days per year. The proposal is to replace them with 50 W LED street lights of equivalent lumen output. Calculate: (i) the annual energy saved in kWh, (ii) the annual monetary saving at ₹8.00/kWh, and (iii) the simple payback period if the total LED replacement cost is ₹6,00,000. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**: **Definitions (2 marks)**: - **Luminous efficacy ($\eta_v$)**: lumens of light produced per Watt of electrical power consumed (unit: lm/W). - **Color Rendering Index (CRI)**: a unitless index (0–100) measuring how accurately a light source reveals the true colours of objects compared to a reference source (sunlight = 100). **Comparison Table (4 marks)**: | Parameter | LED | CFL | T5 Fluorescent | Induction | HPSV | |-----------|-----|-----|----------------|-----------|------| | Efficacy (lm/W) | 100–150 | 50–70 | 80–100 | 70–90 | 80–120 | | CRI | 80–95 | 80 | 85 | 80 | 20–25 | | Life (kh) | 50 | 8 | 20 | 100 | 24 | | Warm-up time | Instant | 30 s | Instant | Instant | 5–10 min | | Mercury | None | 3–5 mg | 5 mg | None | 10–20 mg | | Cost per klm | ₹80 | ₹50 | ₹70 | ₹150 | ₹60 | **Conclusion (1 mark)**: LED dominates in all categories except initial cost; lifecycle cost is the lowest. HPSV is retained for fog-prone outdoor applications. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**: **Step 1 — Compute power reduction per lamp (1 mark)**: $$ \Delta P = 150 - 50 = 100 \text{ W/lamp} $$ **Step 2 — Total connected load reduction (1 mark)**: $$ \Delta P_{total} = 250 \times 100 = 25{,}000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Annual operating hours (1 mark)**: $$ t = 11 \times 365 = 4{,}015 \text{ h/year} $$ **Step 4 — Annual energy saved (1 mark)**: $$ \Delta E = 25 \text{ kW} \times 4{,}015 \text{ h} = 1{,}00{,}375 \text{ kWh/year} $$ **Step 5 — Annual monetary saving (1 mark)**: $$ S = 1{,}00{,}375 \times 8.00 = \text{₹}8{,}03{,}000 \text{ per year} $$ **Step 6 — Simple Payback Period (1 mark)**: $$ SPP = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}03{,}000} \approx 0.747 \text{ years} \approx 9 \text{ months} $$ **Step 7 — Final comment (1 mark)**: A payback of ~9 months is excellent. The street-light installation also reduces the carbon footprint by **~82 tonnes CO₂/year** (at 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid factor) and reduces load on distribution transformers, lowering T&D losses by a further 6–8%. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in This Topic:** > > 1. **Adding efficiencies instead of multiplying** (Loss: up to 4 marks). Cascade efficiencies are always products, never sums. > 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** from MJ to kWh. Always write: $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$. Examiners *deliberately* mix units to test this. > 3. **Ignoring the "useful thermal output"** in CHP cascade problems. Many students compute only electrical efficiency, losing 3–4 marks. CHP is dual-output. > 4. **Confusing luminous efficacy (lm/W) with wattage (W)**. These are inversely related to efficiency — a 9 W LED is "more efficient" than a 60 W incandescent. > 5. **Not stating units in final answer**. Always append units (kWh, ₹, years, lm/W). Missing units = 0.5 to 1 mark lost. > 6. **Using wrong calorific value**. Bagasse ≈ 8 MJ/kg, Natural gas ≈ 38 MJ/m³, Diesel ≈ 45 MJ/kg, Coal (Indian) ≈ 18–25 MJ/kg. Wrong GCV = full-marks loss. > 7. **Skipping the interpretation/comment line**. Examiners allocate the final 1 mark for "engineering judgment" — e.g., *"This payback makes the project financially attractive."* --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall.** - **Cascade efficiency is a product**, not a sum: $\eta_{cascade} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$. - A **CHP / cogeneration** plant has **two useful outputs** (electrical + thermal) and can exceed **70–80%** overall efficiency vs. ~35% for a conventional plant. - **Cumulative loss** in a cascade: $L_{total} = 1 - \eta_{cascade}$. - **Luminous efficacy** $\eta_v = \Phi / P$, unit **lm/W** — the *single most important metric* for comparing light sources. - **LED efficacy** in commerce: **100–150 lm/W**; incandescent: **10–15 lm/W**; CFL: **50–70 lm/W**; HPSV: **80–120 lm/W** (but poor CRI). - **CRI** is rated on a **0–100 scale**; sunlight/incandescent = 100. ECBC mandates **CRI ≥ 80** for indoor commercial spaces. - **CCT** ranges: Warm white ≈ 2,700 K; Cool white ≈ 4,000 K; Daylight ≈ 6,500 K. - **Lamp life**: Incandescent 1,000 h → CFL 8,000 h → T5 20,000 h → HPSV 24,000 h → LED 50,000 h → Induction 100,000 h. - **Energy saved** by retrofit: $\Delta E = (P_{old} - P_{new}) \times N \times t$ — always use **Watts, number of lamps, hours**. - **Simple Payback** $SPP = I / S$ in years; **good retrofit** = SPP < 2 years; **excellent** = SPP < 1 year. - **LPD (Lighting Power Density)** is regulated by ECBC: offices 8.5–10 W/m², hospitals 12 W/m², retail 14 W/m². - **CO₂ emission factor** of Indian grid ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2023 data). Kerala's grid is slightly lower due to hydro share. - **Mercury content** matters: LED has zero, while CFL/HPSV have 3–20 mg — drives e-waste policy and the **UJALA** LED distribution scheme. - Remember the **dB analogy**: just as cascaded amplifier gain in dB adds, cascaded efficiencies in *fraction* multiply. - For KTU 2024 Scheme — expect at least **one Part A question on cascade efficiency definition and one Part B numerical on CHP or LED retrofit** every exam cycle.

topics.title: Life and Efficacy Comparison with Older Light Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Life and Efficacy Comparison with Older Light Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lamp Life** is defined as the total number of operating hours after which the light output of a lamp degrades to a specified percentage (typically **70%** for LED, known as **L70**) of its initial luminous flux, OR the time until complete failure occurs, whichever happens first. **Luminous Efficacy** is the ratio of luminous flux (lumens) emitted by a source to the electrical power (watts) consumed, measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)**.

In the context of Energy Management, the **Life and Efficacy Comparison** of modern solid-state lighting (primarily LEDs) against older conventional sources (Incandescent, Halogen, Fluorescent, CFL, HID) forms the empirical backbone for any lighting energy-audit recommendation. The comparison is evaluated on three KTU-defining pillars:

1. **Rated Life (Hours):** Mean operational lifetime before failure or catastrophic lumen depreciation.
2. **Luminous Efficacy (lm/W):** Photopic conversion efficiency of electrical energy into visible light.
3. **Lumen Maintenance:** The ability of the source to retain its initial brightness over its operational life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model:**
> Imagine two taps delivering water into a bucket:
> - The **Incandescent bulb** is like a huge tap that gushes water (electricity) but mostly splashes outside the bucket (wasted as heat) — very little useful water (light) reaches the bucket. Also, the tap's rubber seal wears out quickly (short life).
> - The **LED bulb** is like a precision nozzle that directs almost all the water straight into the bucket (high efficacy) and is made of nearly indestructible material (long life).
>
> The bucket is your **illuminated room**, the water is **electricity**, and the heat splashing outside is **energy wastage**. This is why shifting to LEDs is the single largest energy-saving intervention in commercial buildings.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following metrics are universally cited in **IS 3646 (Indian Standard for Lighting)**, **BEE Star Rating**, and **IESNA** guidelines:

- **Photopic Luminous Efficacy of Radiation:** $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ (at 555 nm, peak photopic sensitivity)
- **Lumen Depreciation Threshold (LED):** **L70 = 70%** of initial lumens
- **Lumen Depreciation Threshold (Fluorescent):** Typically rated at **L80** or end of life
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI):** Scale of **0 to 100** (100 = reference illuminant)
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT):** Measured in **Kelvin (K)**
- **Mortality Rate (Older Sources):** Statistical failure rate, typically Gaussian distributed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always quote values with their units. A bare "100" is worth zero marks; "100 lm/W" or "100 W" is a complete answer.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Energy Management

Energy management in lighting typically yields **20% to 40%** of total electrical energy savings in commercial facilities. The IEA estimates that a global phase-out of inefficient lighting could save over **1,000 TWh/year** — equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of Germany. Kerala's own **KSERC (Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission)** mandates efficient lighting in HT/EHT industrial consumers, making this comparison a high-yield topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Comparison Framework

The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluates the comparison under three orthogonal axes:

### Axis A: Rated Life (Longevity)
- The **rated life** of a lamp is the time at which 50% of a statistically large sample of lamps has failed (median life, denoted **B50**).
- LED life is **not a hard failure** but a soft degradation. Therefore, LED life is quoted as **L70** (time to reach 70% of initial lumens).
- **Mortality curves** differ drastically:
  - Incandescent: Exponential failure (burnouts cluster early).
  - Fluorescent/CFL: Bell-shaped mortality curve peaking at end of rated life.
  - LED: Gradual lumen depreciation with very few catastrophic failures.

### Axis B: Luminous Efficacy (Efficiency)
- Efficacy = Light Output ÷ Power Input
- A higher efficacy means **less electrical energy** is required for the same illuminance.
- Efficacy is **spectrally weighted** — efficacy is high when the source emits strongly near **555 nm** (yellow-green, where the human eye peaks).
- LEDs can be spectrally tuned to peak at 555 nm, while incandescent emits a broad blackbody curve mostly in the infrared.

### Axis C: Lumen Maintenance & Degradation
- Every light source loses lumens over time. This is called **lumen depreciation**.
- Causes include:
  - **Filament evaporation** (Incandescent)
  - **Electrode sputtering & phosphor degradation** (Fluorescent/CFL)
  - **Phosphor thermal quenching & LED chip degradation** (LED)
- The **Lumen Maintenance Factor (LMF)** is used in lighting design:
  $$ \text{LMF} = \frac{\text{Lumens at given time}}{\text{Initial Lumens}} $$

## 2.2 The Degradation Equation (Exponential Model)

The lumen output $L(t)$ at time $t$ is modeled exponentially:

$$ L(t) = L_0 \cdot e^{-\beta t} $$

Where:
- $L_0$ = initial lumens
- $\beta$ = decay constant (lamp-specific)
- $t$ = operating hours

For a known **L70 life** $T_{L70}$, we have $L(T_{L70}) = 0.7 L_0$:

$$ 0.7 = e^{-\beta T_{L70}} \implies \beta = \frac{-\ln(0.7)}{T_{L70}} = \frac{0.3567}{T_{L70}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same form as radioactive decay or capacitor discharge — a classic exponential decay problem. KTU examiners love this.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P}$ | $\text{lm/W}$ | Comparing source efficiency |
| 2 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi_v = 683 \int V(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{e,\lambda}\, d\lambda$ | $\text{lm}$ | Total visible light output |
| 3 | Lumen Maintenance | $\text{LMF} = \dfrac{L(t)}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | Design calc. for illuminance drop |
| 4 | Exponential Decay | $L(t) = L_0 e^{-\beta t}$ | $\text{lm}$ | Predicting lumen depreciation |
| 5 | Decay Constant | $\beta = \dfrac{\ln(1/0.7)}{T_{L70}}$ | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ | LED life characterization |
| 6 | Energy Saved | $\Delta E = P_{\text{old}} \cdot t - P_{\text{new}} \cdot t$ | $\text{kWh}$ | Retrofit savings calculation |
| 7 | Annualized Cost | $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless | Life-cycle cost analysis |
| 8 | Efficacy Improvement | $\% \text{ Gain} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{new}} - \eta_{\text{old}}}{\eta_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Retrofit justification |
| 9 | Lamp Replacement Cycles | $N = \dfrac{T_{\text{project}}}{\text{Rated Life}}$ | cycles | Maintenance planning |
| 10 | Color Efficacy Factor | $\text{CEF} = \eta \times \dfrac{\text{CRI}}{100}$ | $\text{lm/W}$ | Quality-weighted efficacy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Note that the **$\vert$ symbol (absolute value)** has been replaced with $\vert x \vert$ syntax to avoid breaking the markdown table — the system rule is: **never use raw $\vert$ inside a table row**. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting Audits (BEE Certified Energy Auditors):** This comparison forms Table 1 of every lighting audit report.
- **KSERC Compliance:** Industrial consumers in Kerala must demonstrate efficient lighting in their **Energy Audit Reports** filed under Regulation 4 of KSERC (Terms and Conditions for Determination of Tariff) Regulations, 2021.
- **ESCO Project Evaluation:** Energy Service Companies use efficacy data to model **payback periods** for retrofit projects.
- **BEE Star Labelling:** India's BEE Star Rating for LED bulbs uses efficacy thresholds (e.g., 5-star: $\geq 110 \text{ lm/W}$).
- **Green Building Certification (IGBC/LEED):** Efficacy & CRI weightage contribute to LEED v4.1 EQ Credit: Quality Views.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Decay Constant from L70 Life

**Given:** A LED panel has rated life **L70 = 50,000 hours**.
**Find:** The decay constant $\beta$ and the lumen output at $t = 10,000$ hours.

### Step 1 — State the L70 Definition
By definition, at $t = T_{L70}$, lumen output is 70% of initial:

$$ L(T_{L70}) = 0.70 \cdot L_0 $$

### Step 2 — Apply the Exponential Decay Model
Substitute into $L(t) = L_0 e^{-\beta t}$:

$$ 0.70 \cdot L_0 = L_0 \cdot e^{-\beta T_{L70}} $$

### Step 3 — Cancel $L_0$ (since $L_0 \neq 0$)

$$ 0.70 = e^{-\beta T_{L70}} $$

### Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm of Both Sides

$$ \ln(0.70) = -\beta \cdot T_{L70} $$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\beta$

$$ \beta = -\dfrac{\ln(0.70)}{T_{L70}} = \dfrac{0.3567}{50{,}000} $$

$$ \beta = 7.135 \times 10^{-6} \text{ hr}^{-1} $$

### Step 6 — Compute Lumen Output at $t = 10,000$ hours

$$ L(10{,}000) = L_0 \cdot e^{-(7.135 \times 10^{-6})(10{,}000)} $$

$$ L(10{,}000) = L_0 \cdot e^{-0.07135} $$

$$ L(10{,}000) = L_0 \cdot 0.9312 $$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\beta = 7.135 \times 10^{-6} \text{ hr}^{-1} \quad \text{and} \quad L(10{,}000) = 0.931 \cdot L_0 \; (\text{i.e., } 93.12\% \text{ retained})}$

This is a **6.88% lumen drop in 10,000 hours** — far less than a fluorescent tube at the same point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficacy-Based Energy Savings Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark question):**
A small office in Kerala currently uses **40 nos. of 40W fluorescent tubes** (T12, each 2500 lumens, efficacy = **62.5 lm/W**). They are to be replaced with **20W LED tubes** (each 2200 lumens, efficacy = **110 lm/W**). The office operates **10 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Electricity tariff is **₹7.50/kWh**.

**(a)** Calculate the annual energy savings in kWh.
**(b)** Calculate the annual monetary savings in ₹.

### Step 1 — Initial Power Consumption

$$ P_{\text{old}} = 40 \times 40 \text{ W} = 1600 \text{ W} = 1.6 \text{ kW} $$

### Step 2 — Retrofitted Power Consumption

$$ P_{\text{new}} = 40 \times 20 \text{ W} = 800 \text{ W} = 0.8 \text{ kW} $$

### Step 3 — Annual Operating Hours

$$ t_{\text{annual}} = 10 \text{ hr/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 3000 \text{ hr/yr} $$

### Step 4 — Annual Energy — Old System

$$ E_{\text{old}} = P_{\text{old}} \times t_{\text{annual}} = 1.6 \times 3000 = 4800 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

### Step 5 — Annual Energy — New System

$$ E_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{new}} \times t_{\text{annual}} = 0.8 \times 3000 = 2400 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

### Step 6 — Annual Energy Savings (Part a)

$$ \Delta E = E_{\text{old}} - E_{\text{new}} = 4800 - 2400 = 2400 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

### Step 7 — Annual Monetary Savings (Part b)

$$ \Delta \text{Rs} = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 2400 \times 7.50 $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta E = 2400 \text{ kWh/yr}, \quad \Delta \text{Rs} = \text{₹}18{,}000/\text{yr}} $$

### Step 8 — Bonus Insight: Light Level Comparison
Total lumens old: $40 \times 2500 = 100{,}000$ lm
Total lumens new: $40 \times 2200 = 88{,}000$ lm

Illuminance drops by 12%. If office standard requires **300 lux** uniformly, this is acceptable only if spacing is recalculated. In many retrofits, designers **de-rate** the LED count — using 36 LEDs instead of 40 — to save *additional* energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lumen Equivalence for 1:1 Replacement

**Problem:** You have a 100W incandescent bulb producing 1500 lumens. Find the LED wattage required for equivalent luminous flux at **110 lm/W** LED efficacy.

### Step 1 — Required Lumens
For visual equivalence, LED must produce 1500 lm:

$$ \Phi_{\text{LED}} = 1500 \text{ lm} $$

### Step 2 — Power Required

$$ P_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{LED}}}{\eta_{\text{LED}}} = \dfrac{1500}{110} $$

$$ \boxed{P_{\text{LED}} \approx 13.64 \text{ W}} $$

### Step 3 — Power Reduction Percentage

$$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{100 - 13.64}{100} \times 100 = 86.36\% $$

> **Result:** An 86% power reduction is achievable by simple LED swap — this is the headline number KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Master Table for KTU 2024)

| Light Source | Rated Life (hrs) | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI | CCT Range (K) | Lumen Maintenance | Power for 1500 lm | Heat Output |
|--------------|------------------|-----------------|-----|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Incandescent** | 750 – 2,000 | 10 – 17 | 100 | 2700 | Poor (drops fast) | ~100 W | Very High (~90%) |
| **Halogen** | 2,000 – 4,000 | 15 – 25 | 100 | 2800 – 3200 | Moderate | ~60 W | High (~80%) |
| **CFL (Compact Fluorescent)** | 8,000 – 15,000 | 50 – 70 | 80 – 90 | 2700 – 6500 | Good | ~22 W | Moderate |
| **Fluorescent T8** | 15,000 – 25,000 | 60 – 90 | 75 – 85 | 3000 – 6500 | Good | ~18 W | Moderate |
| **Fluorescent T5 (HE)** | 25,000 – 36,000 | 90 – 105 | 80 – 90 | 3000 – 6500 | Very Good | ~15 W | Low–Moderate |
| **HID – Metal Halide** | 10,000 – 20,000 | 75 – 100 | 65 – 90 | 3000 – 6000 | Good | ~17 W | High |
| **HID – High Pressure Sodium (HPS)** | 20,000 – 40,000 | 85 – 150 | 20 – 30 | 2000 – 2200 | Excellent | ~12 W | Moderate |
| **LED (Generic 2020+)** | 25,000 – 100,000 | 80 – 200+ | 70 – 95 | 2200 – 6500 | Excellent (L70) | ~10 W | Very Low (~15%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation for KTU Viva:** LEDs are **NOT always the highest efficacy** — sodium vapor at low wattages can match or exceed LEDs in raw lm/W. However, LED wins on the **combination** of: high efficacy, excellent CRI, long life, dimmability, instant-on, and no mercury.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: The Decision Tree for Choosing a Light Source

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — "1500" without "lm" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing CRI with CCT** — CRI is quality (0–100), CCT is color appearance (K).
> 3. **Quoting LED life in B50** — LEDs are rated L70, not B50. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy terminology.
> 4. **Ignoring ballast losses** — When comparing fluorescent systems, include ballast power (~10W for magnetic, ~2W for electronic). This is a favorite trick question.
