> 5. **Mistaking HPS efficacy for LED equivalence** — HPS has high lm/W but very poor CRI (~25), so it is unsuitable for indoor task lighting.

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Luminous Efficacy. Why is it a more meaningful metric than wattage alone when comparing light sources?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Luminous Efficacy** is the ratio of luminous flux (in lumens) emitted by a source to the electrical power (in watts) consumed by it, expressed in **lm/W**.
>
> Wattage alone only measures the *electrical input* but not the *useful light output*. Two 60W lamps may produce vastly different lumens — an incandescent 60W produces ~800 lm (13 lm/W), while an LED 60W (rare) could produce 6000 lm (100 lm/W). Efficacy captures both input and output, making it a true measure of energy efficiency. In energy management, this distinction is critical because the goal is to deliver the *required lumens* at the *minimum watts*.

*(Expected length: 4–5 lines. 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for energy-management relevance.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is meant by "L70" life rating in LED luminaires? Why is it used instead of "B50" failure life?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **L70** is the projected operating time (in hours) after which the LED's luminous flux depreciates to **70% of its initial value**. It is the standardized LED life metric defined by **IES LM-80 / TM-21**.
>
> LEDs rarely fail catastrophically like incandescent filaments. Instead, they gradually become dimmer. Hence, a "B50" failure-based life would be misleadingly long (often >100,000 hours). L70 gives a *useful service life* — the time the LED still delivers acceptable illumination. This aligns with the **lumen depreciation** model of lighting design (where LMF < 1 reduces the design illuminance over time).

*(Expected length: 4–5 lines. 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for IES standard reference, 1 mark for explaining why L70 > B50 for LEDs.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Compare the luminous efficacy and rated life of Incandescent, CFL, Fluorescent T5, and LED light sources using a suitable table. Explain why LED has become the preferred choice for office lighting in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An auditorium in a Kerala engineering college uses **60 nos. of 150W metal halide lamps** (efficacy 80 lm/W, CRI 65). It is proposed to retrofit with **90W LED floodlights** (efficacy 120 lm/W, CRI 80). The auditorium operates **8 hours/day, 200 days/year**. Electricity cost is **₹8/kWh**. Calculate:
  (i) Total annual energy consumption of each system.
  (ii) Annual energy saved in kWh and in rupees.
  (iii) Simple payback period if the LED retrofit costs **₹2,50,000** to install. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Source | Efficacy (lm/W) | Rated Life (hrs) | CRI |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|-----|
| Incandescent | 10 – 17 | 750 – 2,000 | 100 |
| CFL | 50 – 70 | 8,000 – 15,000 | 80 – 90 |
| Fluorescent T5 HE | 90 – 105 | 25,000 – 36,000 | 80 – 90 |
| LED | 100 – 200 | 25,000 – 100,000 | 70 – 95 |

**Explanation (3 marks):** LEDs are preferred in Kerala offices because:
1. **Highest efficacy** in the table → lowest electricity bills (Kerala's industrial tariff is high, ~₹7–8/kWh).
2. **Longest life** → minimal maintenance, suitable for ceiling-mounted fixtures.
3. **Higher CRI** (≥80) than metal halide → better visual comfort and reading accuracy in offices.
4. **Cooler operation** → reduces AC load in tropical Kerala climate.
5. **No mercury** → complies with KSERC and BEE eco-norms; no hazardous-waste disposal issues.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Annual energy — Metal Halide (2 marks):**

$$ P_{\text{MH,total}} = 60 \times 150 = 9000 \text{ W} = 9 \text{ kW} $$

$$ t_{\text{annual}} = 8 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ hr/yr} $$

$$ E_{\text{MH}} = 9 \times 1600 = 14{,}400 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

**Annual energy — LED (1 mark):**

$$ P_{\text{LED,total}} = 60 \times 90 = 5400 \text{ W} = 5.4 \text{ kW} $$

$$ E_{\text{LED}} = 5.4 \times 1600 = 8{,}640 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

**(ii) Energy & Money Saved (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta E = 14{,}400 - 8{,}640 = 5{,}760 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

$$ \Delta \text{Rs} = 5{,}760 \times 8 = \text{₹}46{,}080/\text{yr} $$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period (2 marks):**

$$ \text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{46{,}080} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Payback} \approx 5.42 \text{ years}} $$

> **Comment:** This payback is acceptable for institutional projects. With **KSERC's delayed-payment surcharge** of 1.5% per month on HT consumers, faster retrofits have higher internal rate of return (IRR).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a clear table, compare the **lifetime, luminous efficacy, CRI, and typical applications** of the following five light sources: Incandescent, Halogen, Fluorescent T8, Metal Halide, and LED. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100W incandescent bulb is to be replaced with a 12W LED bulb. Both deliver approximately 1500 lumens. Calculate:
  (i) The efficacy of each source.
  (ii) The annual energy saved (assume 6 hours/day, 365 days/year).
  (iii) The CO₂ emission avoided in kg/year, given the Kerala grid emission factor is **0.65 kg CO₂/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Comparison Table (5 marks) + Applications (2 marks):**

| Source | Life (hrs) | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI | Typical Applications |
|--------|------------|-----------------|-----|----------------------|
| Incandescent | 1,000 | 10 – 15 | 100 | Decorative, indicator lamps |
| Halogen | 2,500 | 15 – 25 | 100 | Automotive headlamps, display |
| Fluorescent T8 | 20,000 | 60 – 90 | 80 | Offices, classrooms, retail |
| Metal Halide | 15,000 | 75 – 100 | 70 | Stadiums, high-bay industrial |
| LED | 50,000 | 100 – 150 | 80 | Universal — homes to streetlights |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Efficacy (2 marks):**

$$ \eta_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{1500}{100} = 15 \text{ lm/W} $$

$$ \eta_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{1500}{12} = 125 \text{ lm/W} $$

**(ii) Annual Energy Saved (2 marks):**

$$ t_{\text{annual}} = 6 \times 365 = 2190 \text{ hr/yr} $$

$$ \Delta P = 100 - 12 = 88 \text{ W} = 0.088 \text{ kW} $$

$$ \Delta E = 0.088 \times 2190 = 192.72 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

**(iii) CO₂ Avoided (3 marks):**

$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = 192.72 \times 0.65 = 125.27 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr} $$

> **Comment:** Multiplied across millions of bulbs in Kerala, this is a significant decarbonization lever for the state.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Luminous Efficacy** = Lumens / Watts. Higher is better. Incandescent: 10–17 lm/W, LED: 100–200 lm/W.
- **Rated Life** for older sources uses **B50** (50% fail time). LEDs use **L70** (time to 70% lumen output).
- **CRI** is on a scale of **0 to 100**. Reference 100 = blackbody or daylight. Below 80 is unsuitable for indoor task work.
- **CCT** is in **Kelvin**: 2700 K (warm white) → 4000 K (neutral) → 6500 K (daylight).
- **LED vs Incandescent:** ~85% energy reduction for same light output.
- **LED vs CFL:** ~50–60% energy reduction; no mercury, longer life.
- **LED vs Metal Halide:** ~40% energy reduction; instant on/off (no 5-min warm-up).
- **Lumen depreciation** follows **exponential decay**: $L(t) = L_0 e^{-\beta t}$.
- **Decay constant** for L70: $\beta = \ln(1/0.7) / T_{L70} \approx 0.3567 / T_{L70}$.
- **Heat output:** Incandescent = ~90% of input as heat, LED = ~15% — important for AC load reduction in Kerala.
- **Payback period** typically: 2–5 years for commercial LED retrofits; <1 year for 24/7 industrial lighting.
- **BEE Star Labelling:** 5-star LED ≥ 110 lm/W.
- **Standards to remember:** **IES LM-80, TM-21** (LED life), **IS 3646** (lighting design), **BEE** (efficiency labels).
- **Grid emission factor (Kerala):** ~**0.65 kg CO₂/kWh** — for carbon-saving calculations.
- **Efficacy is NOT the only metric** — also consider CRI, CCT, dimmability, lifetime, cost, and disposal.
- **For 14-mark questions:** Always show **all formula steps**, quote **units**, and end with a **boxed answer** — partial marks are awarded per step.

> **End of Topic Notes — Module 2: Life and Efficacy Comparison with Older Light Sources**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation in Lighting, Motors: Development of Energy Efficient Motors and the Present Status
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation in Lighting, Motors: Development of Energy Efficient Motors and the Present Status

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Conservation in Lighting — Formal Definition

**Energy Conservation in Lighting** refers to the systematic adoption of efficient light sources, luminaires, and control strategies that deliver the required illumination level (lux) for a given visual task while consuming the minimum possible electrical energy, in full compliance with lighting codes and visual comfort standards.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, it encompasses:
- Selection of high-efficacy lamps (LED, induction lamps)
- Use of efficient luminaires and optics
- Adoption of electronic ballasts / drivers
- Implementation of lighting controls (occupancy sensors, daylight dimming, timers)
- Compliance with ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) lighting power density (LPD) limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$)**: The ratio of luminous flux emitted (in lumens) to the electrical power consumed (in watts). Unit: **lm/W**. It is the single most important metric for comparing light sources.

**Key Lighting Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary):**

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|------|------------|------|
| Luminous Flux ($\Phi$) | Total light energy emitted per second by a source | **lumen (lm)** |
| Luminous Intensity ($I$) | Luminous flux per unit solid angle in a given direction | **candela (cd)** |
| Illuminance ($E$) | Luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface | **lux (lx)** |
| Luminance ($L$) | Luminous intensity per unit projected area of a source | **cd/m²** |
| Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$) | Luminous flux emitted per watt of electrical input | **lm/W** |
| Color Rendering Index (CRI/$R_a$) | Ability of a lamp to render colors faithfully (max 100) | dimensionless |
| Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) | Appearance (warm/cool) of the white light emitted | **Kelvin (K)** |
| Lighting Power Density (LPD) | Installed lighting power per unit illuminated floor area | **W/m²** |

## 1.2 Energy Conservation in Motors — Formal Definition

**Energy Conservation in Motors** is the planned approach of reducing electrical energy consumed by electric motors (which globally account for nearly **60–70%** of industrial electricity consumption) through the use of high-efficiency motors, proper sizing, variable speed drives, power factor correction, and improved maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficient Motor (EEM)**: A motor that delivers the same mechanical output power as a standard motor while consuming less electrical input, achieved primarily by reducing the **five major loss components** in the machine.

The five major loss components in an induction motor (KTU-favoured breakdown):

1. **Stator Copper Loss ($P_{sc}$)** — $I_1^2 R_1$
2. **Rotor Copper Loss ($P_{rc}$)** — $I_2^2 R_2$
3. **Core / Iron Loss ($P_{Fe}$)** — hysteresis + eddy current
4. **Friction & Windage Loss ($P_{fw}$)** — bearing friction + air resistance
5. **Stray Load Loss ($P_{stray}$)** — leakage flux, harmonics

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaking Bucket:**
> Think of an electric motor as a **water bucket with five holes** leaking energy. The water that *actually* reaches the tap represents useful mechanical output. A standard motor has wide-open holes; an energy-efficient motor tightens the holes by using thicker copper (lower $I^2R$ loss), thinner better-laminated steel (lower iron loss), precision bearings (lower friction), and optimised air-gap geometry (lower stray loss). The more you plug, the higher the **efficiency** — defined as output ÷ input.

> **Analogy — Old Torchlight vs Modern LED:**
> An old **incandescent bulb** is like a pressure cooker running on firewood — it blasts out a lot of *heat* and very little *light* (efficacy ≈ 10–15 lm/W). A modern **LED** is like a microwave oven — it converts almost all its energy into the *exact job you want* (efficacy ≈ 100–150 lm/W). Energy conservation in lighting is essentially "throwing away the firewood and using a microwave."

## 1.4 Present Status — Indian Context (BEE Standards)

The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, Ministry of Power, Government of India, has mandated minimum energy performance standards:

- **IS 12615:2018** — specifies **IE2** as the *minimum* efficiency for low-voltage three-phase induction motors (range 0.12 kW to 1000 kW) sold in India from the notified date.
- **IE3 (Premium efficiency)** and **IE4 (Super-premium)** are being progressively adopted through the **Standards & Labelling (S&L) programme**.
- The **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme covers designated energy-intensive industries, mandating efficiency improvements in motor-driven systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IE Class Hierarchy (IEC 60034-30-1):**
> IE1 (Standard) < IE2 (High) < IE3 (Premium) < IE4 (Super-Premium) < IE5 (Ultra-Premium, e.g., synchronous reluctance / PM motors)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Conservation Measures in Lighting

### 2.1.1 Comparison of Lamp Technologies

Lighting energy conservation starts at the **source selection** stage. The hierarchy of efficacy is:

$$
\text{Incandescent} < \text{Halogen} < \text{CFL} < \text{Linear Fluorescent (T5)} < \text{HID (HPSV)} < \text{Induction} < \text{LED}
$$

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | Life (hours) | CRI ($R_a$) | Application |
|-----------|----------------:|-------------:|------------:|-------------|
| Incandescent GLS | 10 – 15 | 1 000 | 100 | Domestic (phased out) |
| Halogen | 15 – 25 | 2 000 – 4 000 | 100 | Showroom, accent |
| CFL | 50 – 70 | 6 000 – 10 000 | 80 | Domestic, retrofits |
| Fluorescent T5 | 80 – 100 | 15 000 – 20 000 | 85 | Office, commercial |
| HPSV (High-Pressure Sodium Vapour) | 90 – 130 | 24 000 | 25 | Street lighting, warehouse |
| Metal Halide | 75 – 95 | 15 000 | 90 | Sports, industrial |
| Induction Lamp | 70 – 90 | 60 000 – 100 000 | 80 | Hard-to-access installations |
| **LED (modern)** | **100 – 180** | **50 000 – 100 000** | **80 – 95** | **Universal — preferred choice** |

### 2.1.2 Why & How Each Measure Saves Energy

- **Use high-efficacy lamps** → more lumens per watt → fewer lamps for same lux → direct kWh reduction.
- **Electronic ballasts / drivers** → operate at **>40 kHz** instead of 50 Hz → eliminates flicker, removes audible hum, and reduces ballast losses from **8–10 W (magnetic) to 1–2 W (electronic)**.
- **Optical control** → high-reflectance aluminium reflectors (≥ 85% reflectance) and proper luminaire design direct light *where needed* instead of wasting it on walls/ceiling.
- **Lighting controls:**
  - *Occupancy sensors (PIR/ultrasonic)* — turn OFF lights when zone is unoccupied.
  - *Daylight harvesting* — dim lights near windows based on photocell input.
  - *Time scheduling / timers* — switch off after working hours.
  - *Task tuning* — set maximum level slightly above design to avoid over-lighting.
- **Maintain LPD below ECBC limits** (e.g., 7.5 W/m² for office, 5 W/m² for corridors).

## 2.2 Energy Conservation Measures in Motors

### 2.2.1 The Five Loss Components and Their Remedies

| Loss Component | Cause | Reduction Strategy in EEM |
|----------------|-------|----------------------------|
| Stator copper $I^2R$ | Resistance of stator winding | More copper, larger slot, lower current density |
| Rotor copper $I^2R$ | Bar + end-ring resistance in cage | Conducting rotor bars, larger conductor area |
| Iron (core) | Hysteresis + eddy current in steel | Thinner laminations, low-loss silicon steel, longer core |
| Friction & windage | Bearings + cooling fan | Low-friction bearings, optimised fan blade design |
| Stray load | Leakage flux, harmonic | Tighter air-gap, optimised slot combination |

### 2.2.2 Operational Energy Conservation Strategies

1. **Replace standard motors with IE3/IE4** when rewinding is needed.
2. **Right-sizing** — oversized motors operate at low load factor (< 50%) with poor efficiency and poor power factor. Replace with a smaller motor or use a VFD.
3. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for variable-torque loads (pumps, fans, compressors). Energy saving follows the **Affinity Laws**.
4. **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** — capacitor banks at motor terminals reduce reactive current and $I^2R$ line losses.
5. **Soft starters** — reduce starting current by 60–70%, reducing voltage dip and mechanical stress.
6. **Star-Delta starting** for lightly loaded motors running in star (≈ ⅓ current).
7. **Regular maintenance** — bearing lubrication, alignment, re-tightening of connections; a 0.1 mm air-gap increase can raise losses by 5%.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorise cheat sheet** for solving numerical questions on this topic. Pay special attention to units — wrong units are the #1 mark-loser in KTU valuation.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + \sum P_{loss}}$ | Motor efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Three-phase input power | **W** |
| 3 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical shaft power | **W** |
| 4 | $I^2R$ loss (per phase) | Copper loss = $I^2 \times R$ | **W** |
| 5 | $P_{iron} = k_h f B_{max}^{1.6} V + k_e f^2 B_{max}^2 V$ | Hysteresis + eddy-current loss | **W** |
| 6 | $LPD = \dfrac{\text{Total installed lamp wattage (W)}}{\text{Floor area (m}^2\text{)}}$ | Lighting Power Density | **W/m²** |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot LLF}{A}$ | Average illuminance (lumen method) | **lux** |
| 8 | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P_{elec}}$ | Luminous efficacy | **lm/W** |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ (fan/pump affinity law) | Power ∝ speed³ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Flow ∝ speed | dimensionless |
| 11 | $H = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{H_{11} - H_{12}}$ | Head ∝ speed² | dimensionless |
| 12 | $PB = \dfrac{\Delta C}{S}$ | Simple Payback Period (years) | year |
| 13 | $\text{Energy saved} = (P_1 - P_2) \times t \times N$ | Annual kWh saved | **kWh** |
| 14 | $\cos\phi_1 \to \cos\phi_2$ → $C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | Capacitor kVAR for PFC | **kVAR** |
| 15 | $\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{rated}}$ | Motor loading | dimensionless |

Where in the lumen method formula:
- $N$ = number of lamps
- $\Phi_L$ = luminous flux per lamp (lm)
- $CU$ = coefficient of utilisation
- $LLF$ = light loss factor (maintenance factor)
- $A$ = area (m²)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Motor Efficiency from Loss Components

### Statement
Show that the efficiency of a three-phase induction motor can be expressed entirely in terms of its rated output and the **sum of its five loss components**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The electrical input power is the sum of useful mechanical output and all losses:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total}
$$

By definition, the five loss components sum to the total loss:

$$
P_{loss,total} = P_{sc} + P_{rc} + P_{Fe} + P_{fw} + P_{stray}
$$

Substitute (2) into (1):

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + (P_{sc} + P_{rc} + P_{Fe} + P_{fw} + P_{stray})
$$

Efficiency is defined as the ratio of output to input:

$$
\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}
$$

Substituting the expanded form of $P_{in}$ from equation (3):

$$
\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + (P_{sc} + P_{rc} + P_{Fe} + P_{fw} + P_{stray})}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** for motor efficiency computation. The denominator terms are all measurable by no-load test, blocked-rotor test, and stray-load separation procedures per **IS 4029 / IEC 60034-2-1**.

> **Engineering Insight:** The term $P_{out} + \sum P_{loss}$ in the denominator shows that *every additional watt of loss reduces the efficiency proportionally more at low ratings than at high ratings*. This is why EEMs offer a higher *percentage* saving on small motors than on large ones.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical #1 — Motor Efficiency with Power-Factor Correction

### Problem
A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor draws a line current of 50 A at a power factor of 0.78 lagging. Stator copper loss = 1200 W, Rotor copper loss = 600 W, Iron loss = 800 W, Friction & windage = 400 W, Stray load loss = 200 W. Find (a) the input power, (b) shaft output power, (c) efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Input power** using the three-phase formula:

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi
$$

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.78
$$

$$
P_{in} = 1.7321 \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.78
$$

$$
P_{in} = 1.7321 \times 15600
$$

$$
P_{in} = 27\,020.7 \text{ W} \approx 27.02 \text{ kW}
$$

**(b) Total losses:**

$$
\sum P_{loss} = 1200 + 600 + 800 + 400 + 200 = 3200 \text{ W} = 3.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Shaft output power** is input minus total losses:

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} - \sum P_{loss}
$$

$$
P_{out} = 27\,020.7 - 3\,200 = 23\,820.7 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{out} \approx 23.82 \text{ kW}
$$

**(c) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{23\,820.7}{27\,020.7}
$$

$$
\eta_m = 0.8816 = 88.16\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by alternate method:**
> Slip frequency, air-gap power, mechanical power developed can be cross-checked:
> $P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{sc} - P_{Fe} = 27020.7 - 1200 - 800 = 25020.7$ W
> $P_{m,dev} = P_{gap} - P_{rc} = 25020.7 - 600 = 24420.7$ W
> $P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{fw} - P_{stray} = 24420.7 - 400 - 200 = 23820.7$ W ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical #2 — Energy Savings & Payback using Affinity Law

### Problem
A centrifugal pump driven by a 30 kW, 4-pole standard motor runs 6000 hours/year. Replacing the standard (88% efficient) motor with an IE3 (92% efficient) motor costs ₹ 1 50 000 extra. Electricity = ₹ 7/kWh. Find (a) annual energy saved, (b) monetary saving, (c) simple payback period.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Power input comparison**

Shaft output required: $P_{out} = 30$ kW (assumed fixed load).

Standard motor input:

$$
P_{in,std} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{std}} = \dfrac{30}{0.88} = 34.09 \text{ kW}
$$

IE3 motor input:

$$
P_{in,IE3} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{IE3}} = \dfrac{30}{0.92} = 32.61 \text{ kW}
$$

Power reduction:

$$
\Delta P = 34.09 - 32.61 = 1.48 \text{ kW}
$$

Annual energy saved:

$$
E_{saved} = \Delta P \times t = 1.48 \times 6000 = 8\,880 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(b) Monetary saving per year**

$$
S = 8\,880 \times 7 = \text{₹ } 62\,160 \text{ / year}
$$

**(c) Simple Payback Period**

$$
PB = \dfrac{\Delta C}{S} = \dfrac{1\,50\,000}{62\,160}
$$

$$
PB = 2.41 \text{ years}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Payback < 3 years is considered **excellent** for motor replacement projects in industrial energy management. Below 2 years, the project becomes a *mandatory* item in a BEE PAT audit.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical #3 — Lumen Method for Lighting Design

### Problem
An office hall of size 20 m × 10 m requires an average illuminance of 500 lux. Selected: LED panels of 40 W each, 4000 lm each. CU = 0.6, LLF = 0.8. Find (a) total lumens required, (b) number of LED panels, (c) LPD.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Total lumens required**

$$
\Phi_{req} = E \times A = 500 \times (20 \times 10) = 500 \times 200 = 100\,000 \text{ lm}
$$

Including losses and utilisation, the lumens emitted by *lamps* must be:

$$
\Phi_{lamp} = \dfrac{E \times A}{CU \times LLF} = \dfrac{100\,000}{0.6 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
\Phi_{lamp} = \dfrac{100\,000}{0.48} = 208\,333.33 \text{ lm}
$$

**(b) Number of LED panels**

$$
N = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamp}}{\Phi_L} = \dfrac{208\,333.33}{4\,000} = 52.08
$$

Round up to the next whole lamp:

$$
N = 53 \text{ LED panels}
$$

**(c) LPD**

$$
LPD = \dfrac{N \times W_{lamp}}{A} = \dfrac{53 \times 40}{200}
$$

$$
LPD = \dfrac{2\,120}{200} = 10.6 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECBC limit for office LPD = 8.5 W/m²** (for non-AC offices) and **9.5 W/m²** (for AC offices). Our result of 10.6 W/m² **violates** ECBC → we must either (i) increase CU by better luminaire optics, (ii) raise LLF with better maintenance, or (iii) choose higher-lumen LED panels.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Loss Reduction Strategy vs Saving Potential

| Loss Component | % of Total Loss (typical) | EEM Improvement Technique | % Reduction Achievable |
|----------------|--------------------------:|---------------------------|-----------------------:|
| Stator copper $P_{sc}$ | 30 – 40 % | More copper cross-section | 15 – 25 % |
| Rotor copper $P_{rc}$ | 15 – 20 % | Better conducting rotor bars | 10 – 20 % |
| Iron loss $P_{Fe}$ | 15 – 25 % | Thinner, low-loss steel | 20 – 35 % |
| Friction & windage | 5 – 10 % | Improved bearings, fan | 30 – 50 % |
| Stray load loss | 5 – 15 % | Tighter air-gap, slot optimisation | 25 – 40 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Energy Efficient Motor Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor Selection] --> B{Application Type}
    B -- Constant Speed --> C[Standard / IE2 Motor]
    B -- Variable Speed --> D[IE3 or IE4 Motor]
    D -- Centrifugal Load --> E[VFD Driven EEM]
    D -- Constant Torque --> F[VFD with Encoder Feedback]
    C --> G{Loading Profile?}
    G -- Underloaded below 50% --> H[Right Sizing + VFD]
    G -- Properly Loaded --> I[Power Factor Capacitor Bank]
    I --> J[Soft Starter]
    J --> K[Energy Efficient Operation]
    H --> K
    E --> K
    F --> K
    K --> L[Annual kWh Savings Calculated]
    L --> M[Payback Period Computed]
    M --> N{PB < 3 years?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Project Approved BEE PAT]
    N -- No --> P[Re-evaluate load and tariff]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Lighting Energy Conservation Audit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[LPD Audit] --> P2[Identify Over-lit Zones]
    P2 --> P3[Survey Existing Lamps]
    P3 --> P4{Replace with LED?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Calculate Efficacy Gain]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Add Controls]
    P5 --> P7[Verify ECBC Compliance]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Estimate Annual kWh Saved]
    P8 --> P9[Payback Analysis]
    P9 --> P10[Implementation Phase]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Motor Loss Reduction in EEM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT POWER (Pin)"]
        A1[3 Phase AC Supply]
    end

    subgraph STATOR["STATOR STAGE"]
        B1[Stator Copper Loss Psc]
        B2[Iron Loss Pfe]
    end

    subgraph AIRGAP["AIR GAP COUPLING"]
        C1[Air Gap Power Pag]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR["ROTOR STAGE"]
        D1[Rotor Copper Loss Prc]
    end

    subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL STAGE"]
        E1[Friction Windage Loss Pfw]
        E2[Stray Load Loss Pstray]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT POWER (Pout)"]
        F1[Useful Shaft Power]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow — Lamp Replacement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Existing Lamp Identification] --> Type{Type?}
    Type -- Incandescent --> A1[Replace with LED 90 percent savings]
    Type -- Halogen --> A2[Replace with LED 75 percent savings]
    Type -- Fluorescent T12 --> A3[Replace with T5 or LED]
    Type -- HPSV --> A4[Replace with LED for white light]
    Type -- Mercury Vapour --> A5[Banned under BEE Replace with HPSV or LED]
    Type -- CFL --> A6[Replace with LED 50 percent savings]
    A1 --> Verify[Verify CRI above 80 and CCT 4000K]
    A2 --> Verify
    A3 --> Verify
    A4 --> Verify
    A5 --> Verify
    A6 --> Verify
    Verify --> End[Record LPD Compliance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define Luminous Efficacy and state typical values for LED and Incandescent lamps. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Luminous efficacy is the ratio of luminous flux emitted by a source to the electrical power consumed.

$$
\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P_{elec}}, \text{ unit: lm/W}
$$

Typical values:
- Incandescent GLS: 10 – 15 lm/W
- Modern LED: 100 – 180 lm/W

This high efficacy of LEDs is the principal reason for replacement of legacy lighting systems in energy management programs. *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula & unit; 1 mark — typical values & significance)*

---

### Question 2 — List the five major loss components of a three-phase induction motor. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five major losses in a 3-phase induction motor are:

1. **Stator copper loss ($P_{sc} = I_1^2 R_1$)** — resistive heating in stator winding
2. **Rotor copper loss ($P_{rc} = I_2^2 R_2$)** — resistive heating in rotor cage
3. **Iron / Core loss ($P_{Fe}$)** — hysteresis + eddy current in stator laminations
4. **Friction & windage loss ($P_{fw}$)** — bearing friction and air drag on rotor/fan
5. **Stray load loss ($P_{stray}$)** — leakage flux and harmonic effects

Energy-efficient motors aim to reduce each of these through better materials, tighter tolerances, and optimised design. *(1 mark — naming the five losses with formulas; 1 mark — cause; 1 mark — EEM reduction philosophy)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Case Study on Motor Efficiency and Payback (14 marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 Internal Choice]`

A 3-phase, **400 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW, 4-pole** standard-efficiency induction motor drives a centrifugal pump operating at constant load for **5500 hours/year**. The motor currently operates at:
- Line current: **28 A**
- Power factor: **0.78 lagging**
- Stator copper loss: **820 W**
- Rotor copper loss: **450 W**
- Iron loss: **390 W**
- Friction & windage: **180 W**
- Stray load loss: **120 W**

It is proposed to replace this motor with an **IE3 premium efficiency motor** that delivers the same shaft output but draws only **24 A** at **0.88 power factor** under the same operating conditions. The IE3 motor costs **₹ 1,20,000** *more* than the standard motor. The electricity tariff is **₹ 8.00 per kWh**.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
Calculate the **input power**, **shaft output power**, and **efficiency** of the *existing standard motor*. Verify the result using the no-load and blocked-rotor test data.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
For the proposed IE3 motor, calculate the **new efficiency**, the **annual energy saved in kWh**, the **annual monetary saving in ₹**, and the **simple payback period** in years. State with justification whether the project should be approved (assume standard motor replacement PB threshold = 3 years).

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Input power (using three-phase formula):**

$$
P_{in,std} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi
$$

$$
P_{in,std} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 28 \times 0.78
$$

$$
P_{in,std} = 1.7321 \times 400 \times 28 \times 0.78
$$

$$
P_{in,std} = 1.7321 \times 8736 = 15\,128.5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{in,std} \approx 15.13 \text{ kW}
$$

**Total losses:**

$$
\sum P_{loss} = 820 + 450 + 390 + 180 + 120 = 1960 \text{ W} = 1.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Shaft output power:**

$$
P_{out,std} = P_{in,std} - \sum P_{loss}
$$

$$
P_{out,std} = 15.13 - 1.96 = 13.17 \text{ kW}
$$

**Efficiency of standard motor:**

$$
\eta_{std} = \dfrac{P_{out,std}}{P_{in,std}} = \dfrac{13.17}{15.13} = 0.8704 = 87.04\%
$$

> This corresponds to the **IE2 / high-efficiency** class boundary, confirming the existing motor is an old IE2 unit.

**Verification (KTU expects both methods):**
- Air-gap power: $P_{ag} = P_{in,std} - P_{sc} - P_{Fe} = 15\,128.5 - 820 - 390 = 13\,918.5$ W
- Mechanical power developed: $P_{m,dev} = P_{ag} - P_{rc} = 13\,918.5 - 450 = 13\,468.5$ W
- Shaft power: $P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{fw} - P_{stray} = 13\,468.5 - 180 - 120 = 13\,168.5$ W ✓ (matches)

**Mark split-up for (a):**
- Formula + substitution of $P_{in}$: 2 marks
- Calculation of total losses: 1 mark
- $P_{out}$: 1 mark
- $\eta$: 1 mark
- Verification: 1 mark
- Final boxed answer: 1 mark

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Input power of IE3 motor:**

$$
P_{in,IE3} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 24 \times 0.88
$$

$$
P_{in,IE3} = 1.7321 \times 400 \times 24 \times 0.88
$$

$$
P_{in,IE3} = 1.7321 \times 8448 = 14\,632.4 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{in,IE3} \approx 14.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Shaft output (assumed identical for fair comparison):**

$$
P_{out,IE3} = P_{out,std} = 13.17 \text{ kW}
$$

**Efficiency of IE3 motor:**

$$
\eta_{IE3} = \dfrac{13.17}{14.63} = 0.9002 = 90.02\%
$$

> This places the IE3 motor exactly in the **IE3 (Premium)** class bracket — within 0.5% of the IS 12615 nominal IE3 efficiency for 15 kW 4-pole motor.

**Annual energy saved:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{in,std} - P_{in,IE3} = 15.13 - 14.63 = 0.50 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
E_{saved} = \Delta P \times t = 0.50 \times 5500 = 2\,750 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Annual monetary saving:**

$$
S = 2\,750 \times 8.00 = \text{₹ } 22\,000 \text{ / year}
$$

**Simple payback period:**

$$
PB = \dfrac{\Delta C}{S} = \dfrac{1\,20\,000}{22\,000}
$$

$$
PB = 5.45 \text{ years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** take $P_{out,IE3} = 15$ kW (nameplate). The shaft output must remain the *same* as the standard motor (13.17 kW), because the load is unchanged. **(−1 mark if wrong)**
> - **Do NOT** use the no-load current as line current. The given currents (28 A and 24 A) are full-load line currents at the rated load. **(−1 mark if confused)**
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert W → kW before multiplying by hours, otherwise the units will mismatch. **(−2 marks if not done)**

**Decision (1 mark):**
Since PB = 5.45 years > 3-year threshold, the project **should not be approved purely on energy-savings grounds**. However, if the plant is a BEE-PAT designated consumer, IE3 replacement becomes *statutorily* mandatory irrespective of payback.

**Mark split-up for (b):**
- $P_{in,IE3}$: 2 marks
- $\eta_{IE3}$: 1 mark
- $E_{saved}$: 1 mark
- $S$ (monetary): 1 mark
- $PB$: 1 mark
- Decision + reasoning: 1 mark

---

### ⭐ Question B — Lighting Design & Energy Conservation (14 marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 Internal Choice]`

An engineering college seminar hall of size **25 m × 15 m × 4 m** is currently lit by **60 fluorescent tube lights (T12, 40 W each, 2400 lm per tube)** mounted at a height of 3.5 m. The existing installation uses magnetic ballasts (10 W additional loss per fitting) with a Coefficient of Utilisation of **0.5** and Light Loss Factor of **0.7**. Required average illuminance on the working plane is **300 lux**.

The college proposes to replace the T12 tubes with **40 W LED panels, 4000 lm each**, mounted in the same positions, using electronic drivers (1 W per fitting), CU of **0.65**, and LLF of **0.85**. LED panels cost **₹ 2,500 each**, while the T12 retrofit (tube + ballast + installation) costs **₹ 800 each**. Electricity tariff = **₹ 9 per kWh**. Annual operating hours = **2 400 hours**.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
Calculate for the *existing fluorescent installation*:
(i) Total installed load in kW
(ii) Total luminous flux from all lamps
(iii) Actual average illuminance on the working plane
(iv) Lighting Power Density (LPD)
(v) Comment on whether the existing installation meets the design requirement.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
For the *proposed LED installation*, calculate:
(i) Number of LED panels actually required (if design lux must be met exactly)
(ii) Total installed load and LPD
(iii) Annual kWh saved and monetary saving (assume the same number of fittings, i.e., 60 LED panels)
(iv) Simple payback period in years (use cost difference for the **required number of fittings only**).

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Area of the hall:**

$$
A = 25 \times 15 = 375 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(i) Total installed load:**

Power per fitting = tube wattage + ballast loss = 40 + 10 = 50 W

$$
P_{total,old} = N \times P_{fit} = 60 \times 50 = 3\,000 \text{ W} = 3.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Total luminous flux:**

$$
\Phi_{total,old} = N \times \Phi_L = 60 \times 2\,400 = 144\,000 \text{ lm}
$$

**(iii) Actual average illuminance (lumen method):**

$$
E_{actual} = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot LLF}{A}
$$

$$
E_{actual} = \dfrac{60 \times 2\,400 \times 0.5 \times 0.7}{375}
$$

$$
E_{actual} = \dfrac{50\,400}{375} = 134.4 \text{ lux}
$$

**(iv) Lighting Power Density:**

$$
LPD_{old} = \dfrac{P_{total,old}}{A} = \dfrac{3\,000}{375} = 8.0 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**(v) Comment:**

The design requirement is **300 lux**, but the existing installation delivers only **134.4 lux** — a shortfall of **55.2%**. The installation is grossly under-lit. The LPD of 8.0 W/m² is within ECBC norms, but the illuminance is unacceptable.

**Mark split-up for (a):**
- Area + (i) Total load: 1 mark
- (ii) Total flux: 1 mark
- (iii) $E_{actual}$ formula + substitution: 2 marks
- (iv) LPD: 1 mark
- (v) Comment with calculation: 2 marks

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of LED panels required to meet 300 lux:**

Lumen method, solved for $N$:

$$
N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot LLF} = \dfrac{300 \times 375}{4\,000 \times 0.65 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{112\,500}{2\,210} = 50.90
$$

Round up to the next whole fitting:

$$
N_{LED} = 51 \text{ LED panels}
$$

**(ii) Total load and LPD (using 51 panels):**

Power per fitting = 40 + 1 = 41 W

$$
P_{total,new} = 51 \times 41 = 2\,091 \text{ W} = 2.091 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
LPD_{new} = \dfrac{2\,091}{375} = 5.576 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

> ECBC LPD for auditorium = 8.5 W/m² ✓ **Compliance achieved.**

**(iii) Annual energy saved (assuming 60 LED panels are installed — to be verified):**

We are told to assume **60 LED panels** (same physical count) for this sub-part. Power with 60 LEDs:

$$
P_{60\,LED} = 60 \times 41 = 2\,460 \text{ W} = 2.46 \text{ kW}
$$

> *Engineering note:* Even with 60 LED panels the illuminance will far exceed 300 lux because each LED is 1.67× more luminous than the T12 tube. This is one of the most common student mistakes — they install 60 LEDs (matching count) when only 51 are needed.

Power reduction:

$$
\Delta P = P_{60\,T12} - P_{60\,LED} = 3.0 - 2.46 = 0.54 \text{ kW}
$$

Annual energy saved:

$$
E_{saved} = 0.54 \times 2\,400 = 1\,296 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

Annual monetary saving:

$$
S = 1\,296 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 11\,664 \text{ / year}
$$

**(iv) Simple payback period:**

Cost per LED panel = ₹ 2 500. Cost per T12 retrofit = ₹ 800. Extra cost per fitting = ₹ 1 700.

Number of fittings actually needed = **51** (from part b-i).

Total extra cost:

$$
\Delta C = 51 \times 1\,700 = \text{₹ } 86\,700
$$

Simple payback:

$$
PB = \dfrac{\Delta C}{S} = \dfrac{86\,700}{11\,664} = 7.43 \text{ years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** use $N = 60$ for the cost calculation — the question asks for *required* number of fittings (51). Using 60 gives a 17.6% inflated payback. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Do NOT** forget to add ballast/driver loss to lamp wattage when computing total load. **(−2 marks if omitted)**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the assumed-60-LEDs for part (iii) with the required-51-LEDs for part (iv). The question deliberately mixes the two to test careful reading. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Always** round up the number of fittings (you cannot install 0.9 of a lamp) — the lumen method gives a *lower bound*; actual count must be a whole number ≥ computed value. **(−1 mark if rounded down)**

**Decision:** Payback of 7.43 years exceeds the typical 5-year threshold, but the **lighting quality, ECBC compliance, and longer life (50 000 h vs 10 000 h)** justify the upgrade. Add occupancy sensors for daylight harvesting to bring payback under 5 years.

**Mark split-up for (b):**
- (i) $N_{LED}$: 2 marks
- (ii) Load + LPD: 1 mark
- (iii) $\Delta P$ + $E_{saved}$: 2 marks
- $S$: 1 mark
- (iv) $PB$ (with correct fitting count): 1 mark

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — must read 1 hour before the exam.**

- ⚡ **Lighting efficacy hierarchy:** Incandescent (10–15 lm/W) < CFL (50–70) < T5 (80–100) < LED (100–180). LEDs save **~75%** energy vs incandescent.
- ⚡ **5 motor losses:** $P_{sc}$, $P_{rc}$, $P_{Fe}$, $P_{fw}$, $P_{stray}$ — remember the order = **S**tator, **R**otor, **F**e, **F**riction, **S**tray.
- ⚡ **IE classes:** IE1 < IE2 < IE3 < IE4 < IE5. India mandates **IE2 minimum** under **IS 12615:2018**; IE3 is BEE-labelled.
- ⚡ **Three-phase input formula:** $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ — mark-losing if you forget $\sqrt{3}$ or use line-to-neutral voltage.
- ⚡ **Lumen method formula:** $E = \dfrac{N\,\Phi_L\,CU\,LLF}{A}$ — solve for $N$ by multiplying both sides by $A / (\Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot LLF)$.
- ⚡ **Affinity laws** for centrifugal loads: $P \propto N^3$, $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$. Halving the speed cuts power to **12.5%** of original.
- ⚡ **Payback period** $PB = \Delta C / S$ where $S$ is annual ₹ saved. $PB < 3$ years = auto-approve; $PB > 5$ years = reject unless statutory (PAT).
- ⚡ **LPD limits** (ECBC): Office 7.5–9.5 W/m², Auditorium 8.5, Corridor 5.0. Always compute $LPD = P_{total}/A$ and check.
- ⚡ **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{out}/(P_{out} + \sum P_{loss})$ — denominator must include *all* five losses for KTU full marks.
- ⚡ **VFD energy savings** apply only to *variable-torque* loads (pumps, fans, compressors). For constant-torque loads, energy savings are smaller (typically 10–20%).
- ⚡ **BEE PAT scheme** — applicable to **893 designated energy-intensive consumers**; non-compliance attracts penalty of **₹ 1 lakh per Energy Performance Index shortfall**.
- ⚡ **Power factor correction** capacitor kVAR: $C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$. Improves $\eta$ indirectly by reducing $I^2R$ line losses.
- ⚡ **LED replacement** typically delivers payback 2–4 years in commercial installations and < 1 year in industrial high-bay applications.
- ⚡ **Star-Delta starting** reduces starting current to **1/√3** of direct-on-line; suitable only for lightly loaded motors at start.
- ⚡ **Soft starter vs VFD** — soft starter = reduced voltage starting only; VFD = continuous speed control. VFD is more expensive but offers continuous energy savings.
- ⚡ **Rewound motor** — after two rewinds, an IE3 motor drops to IE1 performance. Always replace rewound motors with new EEMs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficiency (ηₑ)** is formally defined as the ratio of useful energy output (or service delivered) to the total energy input consumed by a system, process, or equipment. Mathematically, it is expressed as the dimensionless ratio:
>
> $$\eta_e = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}$$

**Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency** refer to a structured set of engineering, operational, and managerial methodologies employed across industrial, commercial, and residential sectors to maximize the productive utilization of every unit of energy input, while simultaneously minimizing losses, waste heat generation, and environmental impact.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (OEEET612, Module 2) categorizes these techniques under three broad engineering domains:

| **Domain** | **Primary Focus** |
|---|---|
| **Energy Efficient Technologies (EET)** | Hardware substitution (motors, drives, lighting) |
| **Energy Conservation Measures (ECM)** | Operational & procedural optimizations |
| **Process Integration & Recovery** | Waste heat recovery, cogeneration, pinch analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KPI):** The **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** universally adopted by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India and KTU-aligned energy audits is the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**, defined as energy consumed per unit of product or service output. The internationally accepted unit is **kWh/ton, MJ/kg, or toe/₹-lakh** of turnover.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **"The Leaky Bucket Analogy" — Plain English Explanation**
>
> Imagine you are a farmer carrying water from a well to your crops using a **leaky wooden bucket**. The bucket represents your industrial system, the water is the energy you purchase (and pay for), and the crops are the useful product (your real output). Energy efficiency techniques are simply the engineering methods used to **plug the leaks, replace the wooden bucket with a sealed steel tank, and find smart ways to recycle the spilled water back into the field.**
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Plugging leaks** → Insulation, sealing air leaks, fixing steam traps.
> - **Better bucket** → High-efficiency motors, LED lighting, VFDs.
> - **Recycling water** → Waste heat recovery, cogeneration.
> - **Smarter pouring** → Process optimization, scheduling, load management.

This single mental model captures the entire Module 2 syllabus: *Why let energy escape when you can capture, redirect, or use it more intelligently?*

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

- **First Law Efficiency (η₁):** Ratio of minimum thermodynamic energy required to actual energy consumed. **Bounded between 0 and 1 (or 0–100 %).**
- **Second Law Efficiency (η₂):** Ratio of minimum reversible (exergy) work to actual work input. Always **η₂ ≤ η₁**.
- **Standard Benchmark:** **1 toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 41.868 GJ = 11630 kWh**.
- **Power Factor (cos φ):** Recommended industrial benchmark **≥ 0.95** lagging.
- **Motor Efficiency Classes (IE Code):** IE1 (Standard), IE2 (High), IE3 (Premium), **IE4 (Super-Premium)**, IE5 (Ultra-Premium).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Energy Efficiency Techniques

The KTU Module 2 syllabus structures the techniques in **four progressive layers**, moving from simple housekeeping to advanced thermodynamic integration:

#### **Layer 1 — Housekeeping & Low-Cost Measures (No/Low Capital Investment)**
- Switching off idle equipment (motto: *"The greenest kWh is the one not consumed"*).
- Proper lubrication of motors and bearings to reduce frictional losses.
- Tightening of belts, alignment of shafts, and balancing of rotating masses.
- Cleaning of heat exchanger surfaces, boiler tubes, and condenser coils.
- Optimization of compressed air leak management (typical industrial leak rate: **20–30 %**).
- Optimizing set-point temperatures for HVAC and refrigeration (each **1 °C** change ≈ **6 %** energy variation).

#### **Layer 2 — Efficient Equipment & Technology Substitution (Medium Investment)**
- Replacement of standard induction motors with **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motors**.
- Installation of **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for pumps and fans.
- Replacement of incandescent/fluorescent lamps with **LED lighting**.
- Use of high-efficiency transformers (amorphous core, CRGO silicon steel).
- Power factor correction via **Automatic Power Factor Controllers (APFC)**.

#### **Layer 3 — Process Optimization & Control (Operational Restructuring)**
- Optimal load scheduling and demand-side management.
- Implementing **Building Management Systems (BMS)**.
- Advanced process control (APC) using Model Predictive Control (MPC).
- Lean manufacturing principles to reduce idle production losses.

#### **Layer 4 — System Integration & Recovery (High Investment, High ROI)**
- **Cogeneration / Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** systems.
- **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** through economizers, heat pipes, and ORC.
- **Pinch Analysis** for optimum heat exchanger network synthesis.
- **Combined Cooling, Heating & Power (CCHP / Trigeneration)** systems.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' & 'How' — Logic Behind Each Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering Rationale (Why this layering?):**
>
> Energy efficiency improvement follows a universally accepted **marginal cost of conserved energy (CCE)** principle. Housekeeping (Layer 1) has *near-zero capital cost* and *immediate payback* (< 6 months). Technology substitution (Layer 2) has *moderate capital cost* and *1–3 year payback*. System integration (Layer 4) demands *high capital* but delivers *step-change efficiency* (20–60 % savings).
>
> Hence, an energy engineer **always** evaluates Layer 1 first, then Layer 2, before committing to Layer 4 investments. This sequence is **mandatory** in KTU Energy Audit methodology (BEE-certified audits in India).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 2 — High-Yield)

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Efficiency Ratio | $\eta_e = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Dimensionless; $0 \le \eta_e \le 1$ |
| 2 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $\text{SEC} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg, toe/unit |
| 3 | Annual Energy Savings | $\Delta E = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{improved}}$ | kWh/year, GJ/year |
| 4 | Simple Payback Period | $\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Monetary Savings}}$ | Years (Acceptable: $\le 3$ years) |
| 5 | Return on Investment (ROI) | $\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | ₹; $r$ = discount rate |
| 7 | Cogeneration Efficiency (Total) | $\eta_{\text{CHP}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}} + Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel input}}}$ | %; typical 70–85 % |
| 8 | Power to Heat Ratio | $\text{P/H} = \frac{W_{\text{electrical}}}{Q_{\text{thermal recovered}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Heat Recovery Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}}{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}$ | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 10 | Pinch $\Delta T_{\min}$ Relation | $Q_{h,\min} = \sum (\text{CP})_h (T_h - T_{\text{pinch}})$ | kW; CP in kW/°C |
| 11 | Motor Efficiency Improvement | $\Delta P = P_{\text{out}}\left(\frac{1}{\eta_1} - \frac{1}{\eta_2}\right)$ | kW; $\eta_2 > \eta_1$ |
| 12 | Affinity Law (Pumps/Fans) | $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Cubic power relation |
| 13 | Flow Affinity Law | $\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Linear with speed |
| 14 | Lighting Energy Reduction | $E_{\text{saved}} = (n_{\text{old}} - n_{\text{new}}) \cdot P_{\text{lamp}} \cdot t_{\text{use}}$ | kWh/year |
| 15 | Heat Exchanger LMTD | $\Delta T_{\text{lm}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | °C or K |
| 16 | Exergy (Availability) | $\text{Ex} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ | kJ/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The cubic **Affinity Law (Formula #12)** is the single most-frequently-tested equation in KTU Module 2. A 20 % reduction in fan/pump speed produces a **49 % reduction in power** (because $0.8^3 = 0.512$, i.e., **48.8 % savings**). Students must memorize this relationship.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These techniques are deployed in:
- **Thermal Power Plants** (reheat-regenerative cycles, supercritical boilers).
- **Iron & Steel Industry** (Top Recovery Turbine, Coke Dry Quenching).
- **Cement Industry** (pre-heater towers, waste heat recovery in clinker coolers).
- **Refineries & Petrochemicals** (Pinch analysis, heat exchanger networks).
- **Commercial Buildings** (BMS, LED retrofits, VRF/VRV HVAC systems).
- **Data Centers** (free cooling, hot/cold aisle containment, PUE optimization).
- **Textile & Pulp & Paper** (process steam optimization, condensate recovery).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Machines (Pumps & Fans)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the mathematical backbone of *Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)* energy savings — one of the most commonly asked **14-mark problems** in KTU ESE.

The affinity laws are derived from **dimensional analysis** of a dynamically similar centrifugal machine. Consider a centrifugal pump/fan where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
- $H$ = developed head (pressure)
- $P$ = power consumed
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $D$ = impeller diameter

**Step 1 — Flow Rate Proportionality**
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ at the impeller exit is the product of the blade tip speed and the cross-sectional flow area:

$$Q \propto N \cdot D^3$$

For geometrically similar machines ($D$ = constant), the flow rate scales linearly with speed:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 2 — Head Proportionality**
The developed head $H$ is proportional to the square of the tip speed:

$$H \propto (N \cdot D)^2$$

For constant impeller diameter:

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Power Proportionality**
Hydraulic power $P = \rho g Q H$. Substituting the proportionalities from Steps 1 and 2:

$$P \propto N \cdot D^3 \cdot (N \cdot D)^2 = N^3 \cdot D^5$$

For constant $D$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Generalized Result (Speed + Diameter Variation)**
All three affinity laws can be written compactly for a geometrically similar machine:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right) \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3 \\[4pt]
\frac{H_2}{H_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: VFD Energy Savings (Affinity Law Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Problem (Full Solution):**
>
> A **75 kW centrifugal pump** operates at 1450 rpm delivering 200 m³/h at 30 m head. The process requires only 80 % of the design flow. The motor runs 5000 hours/year at a tariff of ₹6.50/kWh.
>
> **Compare:** Annual energy consumption with (a) a throttling valve (baseline) vs (b) a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) to achieve 80 % flow.

**Solution:**

**Case (a) — Throttling Valve (Baseline):**
Throttling does not change the pump's speed, so the pump still consumes its design power. The motor shaft power ≈ **75 kW** (assuming 100 % rated operation).

$$E_{\text{throttle}} = 75 \text{ kW} \times 5000 \text{ h} = 375{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$C_{\text{throttle}} = 375{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ } 24{,}37{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

**Case (b) — VFD at 80 % Speed:**
The flow ratio $Q_2/Q_1 = 0.80$. From affinity law, the speed ratio $N_2/N_1 = 0.80$. Therefore:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.80)^3 = 0.512$$

$$P_2 = 75 \times 0.512 = 38.4 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{\text{VFD}} = 38.4 \times 5000 = 192{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$C_{\text{VFD}} = 192{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ } 12{,}48{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Annual Savings:**

$$\Delta E = 375{,}000 - 192{,}000 = 183{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\Delta C = 24{,}37{,}500 - 12{,}48{,}000 = \text{₹ } 11{,}89{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

**Energy Savings Percentage:**

$$\% \text{ Savings} = (1 - 0.8^3) \times 100 = 48.8\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The exact 48.8 % savings arises because the cubic affinity law mathematically decouples the partial-load flow requirement from the power input — the very essence of VFD-based energy efficiency.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Energy Efficiency Technique by Sector

| **Industry / Sector** | **Dominant Energy Use** | **Best-Suited EE Technique** | **Typical Savings (%)** | **Payback (Years)** |
|---|---|---|---|:-:|
| Cement | Grinding, kiln heat | WHR from clinker cooler, preheater | 15 – 25 | 2 – 4 |
| Steel | Reheating, blast furnace | Top gas recovery turbine (TRT), CDQ | 10 – 20 | 3 – 5 |
| Textile | Spinning motors, boilers | IE3 motors, condensate recovery | 12 – 18 | 1 – 3 |
| Pulp & Paper | Drying, refining | Pinch analysis, steam optimization | 20 – 30 | 2 – 4 |
| Commercial Building | HVAC, lighting | BMS, LED, VRF system | 30 – 45 | 1 – 2 |
| Data Center | Cooling, servers | Hot-aisle containment, free cooling | 35 – 50 | 2 – 3 |
| Refinery | Distillation, process heat | CHP, HEN synthesis | 15 – 25 | 3 – 6 |
| Hospitality | HVAC, kitchen | Heat pumps, solar thermal | 25 – 40 | 2 – 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Energy Efficiency Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Energy Efficiency Techniques] --> B[Layer 1: Housekeeping]
    A --> C[Layer 2: Efficient Equipment]
```
