<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). Why is it considered a vital Key Performance Indicator in industrial energy management?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the energy consumed per unit of product or service output, expressed as $\text{SEC} = E_{\text{total}} / \text{Production}$. Common units are **kWh/ton, MJ/kg, or toe/₹-lakh**.
- **Significance (1.5 Marks):** SEC enables *inter-plant* and *inter-temporal* benchmarking. Reduction in SEC directly indicates process efficiency improvement, energy cost savings, and reduced environmental impact. SEC is also normalized against production volume, making it independent of plant capacity utilization — a critical advantage over absolute energy consumption.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the cubic affinity law for centrifugal pumps. Why is it the foundation of VFD-based energy savings?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Law (1.5 Marks):** For a centrifugal pump with constant impeller diameter, the power varies as the cube of the rotational speed: $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$.
- **VFD Implication (1.5 Marks):** A small reduction in speed produces a disproportionately large reduction in power. For example, halving the speed reduces power to **12.5 %** (i.e., **87.5 % savings**). This makes VFDs the most energy-efficient flow control method compared to throttling valves (which waste energy as turbulence losses).

---

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power, CHP)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its classification based on the **Power-to-Heat Ratio (P/H)** and list **four industrial applications** where CHP is most beneficial. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sugar mill has a steam demand of **12,000 kg/h at 8 bar (saturated)**. It installs a back-pressure steam turbine (BPST) for cogeneration, exhausting at 2 bar. The boiler efficiency is 85 %, and fuel calorific value is 42 MJ/kg. Calculate:
   - (i) Enthalpy of steam at 8 bar and 2 bar (use steam tables).
   - (ii) Electrical power generated if turbine isentropic efficiency is 78 %.
   - (iii) Fuel consumption rate and overall CHP efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Cogeneration Concept (7 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Cogeneration is the simultaneous production of **electrical power and useful thermal energy** from a single fuel source. It exploits the otherwise-wasted heat from a prime mover to serve process or space-heating loads.
- **Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):** The schematic was shown in Section 4.2 above (Fuel → Prime Mover → Generator + Waste Heat Boiler → Steam → Process).
- **Classification by P/H Ratio (2 Marks):**
   - **P/H < 0.5** → Heating-dominant (vapour absorption machines, district heating).
   - **0.5 ≤ P/H ≤ 2.0** → Balanced cogeneration (most industrial applications).
   - **P/H > 2.0** → Power-dominant (large IPPs, utility-scale CHP).
- **Four Industrial Applications (2 Marks):** Sugar mills, paper mills, cement plants, refineries, textile processing, district heating, and large commercial complexes (any four).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

From saturated steam tables:
- $h_1$ (8 bar saturated steam) = **2769.1 kJ/kg**
- $h_f$ (2 bar saturated water) = **504.7 kJ/kg**
- $h_g$ (2 bar saturated steam) = **2706.7 kJ/kg**
- $s_1$ (8 bar sat. steam) = **6.6628 kJ/kg·K**
- $s_f$ (2 bar) = **1.5301 kJ/kg·K**
- $s_{fg}$ (2 bar) = **5.5970 kJ/kg·K**

**Step 1 — Isentropic Expansion to 2 bar:**

$$s_{2s} = s_1 = 6.6628 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

Quality at turbine exit (isentropic):

$$x_{2s} = \frac{s_{2s} - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.6628 - 1.5301}{5.5970} = 0.9170$$

$$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 504.7 + 0.9170 \times 2202.0 = 2523.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Enthalpy Drop (with 78 % efficiency):**

$$\Delta h_{\text{actual}} = 0.78 \times (h_1 - h_{2s}) = 0.78 \times (2769.1 - 2523.8) = 191.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Electrical Power Generated:**

$$\dot{m} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = 3.333 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{W}_{\text{elec}} = \dot{m} \times \Delta h_{\text{actual}} = 3.333 \times 191.3 = 637.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Consumption Rate:**

Useful energy out (CHP) per second = $\dot{W}_{\text{elec}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{process}}$
$\dot{Q}_{\text{process}} = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_f \text{ at 2 bar}) = 3.333 \times (2769.1 - 504.7) = 7553.0 \text{ kW}$

Total useful thermal + electrical = $637.7 + 7553.0 = 8190.7 \text{ kW}$

At boiler efficiency 85 %:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{8190.7}{0.85 \times 42{,}000} = 0.2288 \text{ kg/s} = 823.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Overall CHP Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{CHP}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{elec}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{process}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{CV}} = \frac{8190.7}{0.2288 \times 42{,}000} = 85.2\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Highlight:** The **85.2 % overall CHP efficiency** dramatically exceeds the **35–40 %** efficiency of a conventional separate heat-and-power plant. This is why BEE India strongly promotes CHP in energy-intensive industries.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **four hierarchical layers** of energy efficiency improvement techniques. Illustrate with suitable industrial examples and justify the order of implementation using the concept of **marginal cost of conserved energy (CCE)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 hp (186.5 kW) standard efficiency induction motor (η₁ = 88 %) operates 6000 hours/year. It is proposed to replace it with a premium efficiency motor (η₂ = 94.5 %). The motor costs ₹4,50,000 and the electricity tariff is ₹7.00/kWh.
   - (i) Calculate annual energy savings in kWh.
   - (ii) Calculate annual monetary savings.
   - (iii) Compute the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Four Layers of EE Techniques (7 Marks):**
*(As detailed in Section 2.1, the four layers are: Housekeeping → Efficient Equipment → Process Optimization → System Integration. The CCE rationale: each successive layer demands higher capital but offers higher absolute savings, while the per-unit cost of conserved energy increases with each layer. Implementation should always start with Layer 1 since CCE < ₹0.5/kWh, rising to Layer 4 at CCE = ₹2–4/kWh.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Input Power Comparison:**

$$P_{\text{in,1}} = \frac{186.5}{0.88} = 211.93 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{\text{in,2}} = \frac{186.5}{0.945} = 197.35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy Savings:**

$$\Delta E = (211.93 - 197.35) \times 6000 = 14.58 \times 6000 = 87{,}480 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$\Delta C = 87{,}480 \times 7.00 = \text{₹ } 6{,}12{,}360 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{6{,}12{,}360} = 0.735 \text{ years} \approx 8.8 \text{ months}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The premium motor pays back in **under 9 months** — well within the BEE-recommended threshold of 3 years, making this a *bankable* energy efficiency investment.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 2 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Affinity Law Misapplication (–2 Marks):** Students often mistakenly apply affinity laws to *positive displacement* pumps (reciprocating, screw). Affinity laws are valid **only for centrifugal (dynamic) machines**. Mentioning the constraint explicitly in your answer earns 1 grace mark.
> 2. **Ignoring Boiler Efficiency (–1 Mark):** In CHP problems, students often compute fuel input using the turbine work alone, ignoring boiler efficiency. Always compute *fuel input energy* = (Useful energy out) / η_boiler.
> 3. **Payback ≠ NPV (–2 Marks):** Simple Payback ignores time value of money. For projects > 3 years payback, examiners expect NPV/IRR discussion. Quote both SPP *and* ROI/NPV in long-answer solutions.
> 4. **Units Confusion (–1 Mark):** Always convert MJ ↔ kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ) and ton of refrigeration ↔ kW (1 TR = 3.517 kW). Mixing units is the #1 arithmetic error in KTU Energy Management papers.
> 5. **Power Factor (cos φ) Omission (–1 Mark):** In motor calculations, the input current is $I = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\varphi \cdot \eta)$. Skipping cos φ loses the mark for "complete solution."

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**
>
> ✅ **Energy Efficiency vs Conservation:** Efficiency = *same output, less input*; Conservation = *less service demanded*.
>
> ✅ **Cubic Affinity Law:** $P \propto N^3$ for centrifugal machines — the *single most tested* relation in Module 2.
>
> ✅ **CHP Efficiency:** Typical 70–85 % vs separate generation 35–45 %; **saves 30–40 %** of fuel.
>
> ✅ **IE Motor Classes:** IE1 → IE2 → IE3 → IE4 → IE5; each step ≈ 10–15 % reduction in losses.
>
> ✅ **VFD Savings:** A 20 % speed reduction yields **48.8 % power savings** (cubic law).
>
> ✅ **WHR Devices:** Economizer, air pre-heater, heat pipe, ORC (Organic Rankine Cycle).
>
> ✅ **Pinch Analysis:** Sets $Q_{h,\min}$ and $Q_{c,\min}$ utility targets; $\Delta T_{\min}$ is the design lever.
>
> ✅ **Payback Threshold:** SPP ≤ 3 years = acceptable EE investment; > 5 years = reject.
>
> ✅ **SEC Benchmarking:** Always normalize by production output; use *specific* not *absolute* metrics.
>
> ✅ **Lighting:** LED retrofits save 50–70 % over fluorescent, 80–90 % over incandescent.
>
> ✅ **Power Factor:** Industrial target **≥ 0.95**; APFC panels maintain this dynamically.
>
> ✅ **HVAC:** Each **1 °C** setpoint change = **~6 %** compressor energy variation.
>
> ✅ **Trigeneration (CCHP):** Adds absorption chiller to CHP for cooling, heating, and power from one fuel.
>
> ✅ **Audit Hierarchy:** Walkthrough → Detailed → Investment-grade (KTU BEE-aligned).
>
> ✅ **Energy Units Mastery:** 1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh = 10 GCal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Necessity for Load Matching and Selection of Motors for Constant and Variable Loads, Demand Side Management: Introduction to DSM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Necessity for Load Matching and Selection of Motors for Constant and Variable Loads, Demand Side Management: Introduction to DSM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Necessity for Load Matching

**Load Matching** is the engineering process of accurately determining the appropriate rating and capacity of an electric motor (or any prime mover) such that its output characteristics align optimally with the demand profile of the mechanical load it is intended to drive. It involves evaluating the load factor, duty cycle, speed requirements, torque profile, and operational hours of the driven equipment.

In the broader sense, load matching can also refer to aligning energy supply (generation) with energy demand (consumption) on a temporal and quantitative basis — a core principle of energy management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Load matching is NOT about simply picking the largest available motor. It is about selecting the motor that operates closest to its **best efficiency point (BEP)** for the majority of its operating hours.

**Why is Load Matching Necessary?**

1. **Avoid Overloading** – Motors operated beyond rated capacity suffer from insulation failure, overheating, bearing damage, and drastically reduced lifespan.
2. **Avoid Severe Underloading** – Motors operated below ~50% of rated load operate at poor efficiency and very poor power factor, leading to penalties from utilities.
3. **Economic Operation** – A properly matched motor reduces both energy cost (kWh charges) and demand cost (kVA charges).
4. **System Reliability** – Correctly sized motors reduce nuisance tripping, voltage drops, and harmonic disturbances.
5. **Code Compliance** – Standards such as IS 325, IEC 60034, and IE (International Efficiency) classifications mandate proper selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU/IEA Standard):**
> *"Load matching is the procedure of selecting an electric motor whose rated output power corresponds to the maximum continuous load demand of the driven equipment, after applying appropriate service factors, ambient corrections, and duty cycle considerations."*

---

**Conceptual Analogy — The Shoe Size Metaphor**

Imagine buying shoes. If the shoe is too large, you stumble and walk inefficiently (energy wasted as friction/heat). If it is too small, you get blisters and the shoe tears quickly (motor burnout). The perfect fit (load matching) lets you walk far with minimum effort. Similarly, a perfectly matched motor walks (operates) efficiently for thousands of hours without tripping, overheating, or wasting electricity.

---

### 1.2 Selection of Motors for Constant Loads

A **Constant Load** is one in which the torque and power demand remain essentially the same throughout the operating cycle — examples include centrifugal pumps running at fixed flow, conveyors with uniform material feed, fans at constant damper position, and compressors running at fixed discharge pressure.

**Selection Procedure:**
1. Calculate the power required by the load using:

$$P_{load} = \frac{T \cdot N}{9550}$$

where $P_{load}$ is in kW, $T$ in Nm, and $N$ in rpm.

2. Apply service factor (typically 1.10 to 1.25) for transient surges.
3. Choose the next standard motor rating (R20 series: 0.55, 0.75, 1.1, 1.5, 2.2, 3.7, 5.5, 7.5, 11, 15, 18.5, 22, 30, 37, 45, 55, 75, 90, 110 kW).
4. Verify that operating point lies between 75%–100% of rated load (sweet zone).
5. Check starting method (DOL, star-delta, soft starter, VFD).

---

### 1.3 Selection of Motors for Variable Loads

A **Variable Load** is one where torque and/or speed demand changes with time — typical examples are elevators, crane hoists, punch presses, rolling mills, mixers, reciprocating compressors, and electric vehicles.

**Selection Methods:**

- **Method A: Equivalent Power Method (RMS Power)** — Used when load cycle is repeating and known.
- **Method B: Mean Power Method** — Simple averaging, used when peak is short and infrequent.
- **Method C: Equivalent Thermal Method** — Considers heating equivalent based on losses (most accurate for squirrel cage motors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For variable loads, the chosen motor must satisfy BOTH the peak power demand AND the heating limit (thermal equivalent). A motor that handles peak mechanically may still burn out due to cumulative $I^2R$ losses during the cycle.

---

### 1.4 Demand Side Management (DSM) — Introduction

**Demand Side Management (DSM)** refers to the systematic planning, implementation, and monitoring of utility-sponsored and customer-side activities designed to influence consumer use of electricity in ways that will produce desired changes in the utility's load shape — i.e., the time pattern and magnitude of a utility's load.

**Origin:** The concept was formally introduced by **Clark W. Gellings** at the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) in the 1980s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU/IEA):**
> *"DSM is the planning, implementation, and monitoring of those utility activities designed to influence customer use of electricity in ways that will produce desired changes in the utility's load shape — i.e., changes in the time pattern and magnitude of a utility's load."*
> — Clark W. Gellings, EPRI

**Six Canonical DSM Strategies (Gellings' Classification):**

| # | Strategy | Goal |
|---|----------|------|
| 1 | **Peak Clipping** | Reduce peak demand |
| 2 | **Valley Filling** | Build up off-peak load |
| 3 | **Load Shifting** | Move load from peak to off-peak |
| 4 | **Strategic Conservation** | Reduce overall consumption |
| 5 | **Strategic Load Growth** | Increase sales in off-peak |
| 6 | **Flexible Load Shape** | Allow customer to control load |

**Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Jam**

Think of the electricity grid as a highway. During rush hour (5 PM–9 PM), traffic jams up (peak demand). DSM is like the traffic police using strategies such as: closing some on-ramps (peak clipping), encouraging people to work from home (conservation), promoting flex-time work hours (load shifting), and offering toll discounts during off-peak hours (valley filling). The goal is to keep traffic (electricity) flowing smoothly without building extra lanes (new power plants).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Key Outcome Mapping:**
> - **CO2:** Apply load matching and motor selection principles.
> - **CO3:** Evaluate DSM strategies for energy conservation.
> - **Bloom Levels:** Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Load Matching — Theoretical Framework

Load matching in electric motor systems is governed by three interdependent technical considerations:

**A) Mechanical Power Demand**
The driven equipment (pump, fan, compressor, conveyor) demands a specific shaft power that varies with process conditions. The motor must supply this power continuously without exceeding its thermal rating.

**B) Thermal Limit of Motor**
The motor's insulation class (A, E, B, F, H) determines the maximum permissible temperature rise. For continuous duty (S1), the motor must operate such that the steady-state temperature rise equals the permissible limit for its insulation class. Any sustained overload reduces insulation life exponentially (typically, every 10 °C rise halves insulation life — *Montsinger's Rule*).

**C) Efficiency Operating Window**
Electric motors exhibit a "sweet spot" — typically between 60% and 100% of rated load — where efficiency is maximum. Below 50%, efficiency drops sharply. Above 100%, the motor may stall or trip.

---

### 2.2 Why Overloaded Motors Fail

- Current increases proportionally to load → $I^2R$ copper losses increase as $I^2$.
- Slip increases with load → rotor $I^2R$ losses increase.
- Temperature rises above insulation limit → insulation degrades.
- Bearings overheat → lubricant fails.
- Reduced lifespan: insulation life halves every 10 °C above limit (Arrhenius/Montsinger rule).

### Why Underloaded Motors Waste Energy

- Iron (core) losses remain nearly constant regardless of load.
- Friction and windage losses are nearly constant.
- Therefore, total losses are spread over a small output → efficiency plummets.
- Power factor drops dramatically: at no-load, PF can be 0.1–0.3.
- The utility imposes **kVA penalties** (low PF charges) in addition to kWh charges.

---

### 2.3 Motor Selection for Constant Loads — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Determine the maximum continuous power required** at the worst-case operating condition.
2. **Apply service factor** (SF) for transient safety: usually SF = 1.10 to 1.15 for normal industrial applications.
3. **Apply altitude and ambient correction**: for ambient > 40 °C or altitude > 1000 m, derate by ~1% per °C or per 100 m.
4. **Round up** to the next standard motor frame size.
5. **Verify efficiency class**: prefer IE3 (premium) or IE4 (super premium) per IS 12615 / IEC 60034-30-1.
6. **Check starting current** and select appropriate starting method.
7. **Verify power factor** at operating load (PF should be ≥ 0.85 at full load for industrial motors).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula (Constant Load Motor Rating):**
> $$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{load} \times SF}{K_{alt} \times K_{temp}}$$
> where $SF$ = service factor, $K_{alt}$ = altitude derating factor, $K_{temp}$ = temperature derating factor.

---

### 2.4 Motor Selection for Variable Loads — Detailed Logic

For variable (fluctuating) loads, the RMS (root-mean-square) horsepower method is the most widely used in industry:

**Procedure:**
1. Divide the operating cycle into discrete time intervals $\Delta t_1, \Delta t_2, \dots, \Delta t_n$.
2. For each interval, determine the load power $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$.
3. Calculate the equivalent (RMS) power:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{P_1^2 \Delta t_1 + P_2^2 \Delta t_2 + \dots + P_n^2 \Delta t_n}{\Delta t_1 + \Delta t_2 + \dots + \Delta t_n}}$$

4. Choose a motor whose **continuous rating ≥ $P_{eq}$**.
5. Additionally, verify that the **peak power $P_{peak}$** of any interval does not exceed the motor's short-term overload capability (typically 110–150% for 60 s).

**Equivalent Current Method (more accurate for thermal):**

$$I_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum I_i^2 \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i}}$$

The motor must satisfy: $I_{rated} \geq I_{eq}$ AND $I_{peak,short\_time} \geq \max(I_i)$.

---

### 2.5 Demand Side Management — Deep Theory

DSM operates on the principle that **electricity demand is elastic and time-sensitive**. Consumers will modify their usage patterns if given proper price signals, technology, or incentives.

**The Six Load-Shape Objectives Explained:**

1. **Peak Clipping** — Reducing peak demand directly. Methods: Direct Load Control (DLC), interruptible tariffs, capacitor banks for PF correction.
2. **Valley Filling** — Increasing off-peak demand. Methods: Off-peak thermal storage (water heating, space heating with bricks), EV smart charging.
3. **Load Shifting** — Moving load from peak to off-peak. Methods: Thermal Energy Storage (TES), cold storage (ice-based AC), industrial process scheduling.
4. **Strategic Conservation** — Overall reduction. Methods: EE appliances, efficient lighting (LED), efficient motors (IE3/IE4), building envelope improvements.
5. **Strategic Load Growth** — Increasing off-peak sales. Methods: Time-of-Use (ToU) tariffs, promotional night-rate schemes.
6. **Flexible Load Shape** — Letting customers control when they use power. Methods: Real-time pricing, smart meters, home energy management systems (HEMS).

**Benefits of DSM:**

| Benefit | Quantity / Mechanism |
|---------|---------------------|
| Reduced peak demand | kW reduction → lower capacity charges |
| Reduced energy consumption | kWh saved → lower energy bills |
| Deferred generation investment | Years of T&D capacity addition postponed |
| Lower emissions | Less fossil fuel burnt at peaking plants |
| Improved reliability | Reduced grid stress during peak hours |
| Consumer savings | Lower electricity bills for participants |

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60,000}$ for $P$ in kW, $T$ in Nm, $N$ in rpm | Mechanical shaft power | kW |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{T \cdot N}{9550}$ | Same as above, simplified | kW |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = P_{load} \cdot SF$ | Required motor rating (constant load) | kW |
| 4 | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum P_i^2 \, \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i}}$ | RMS equivalent power for variable loads | kW |
| 5 | $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum I_i^2 \, \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i}}$ | RMS equivalent current for thermal sizing | A |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Motor efficiency | % |
| 7 | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Power factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}} \times 100\%$ | Load Factor | % |
| 9 | $DF = \dfrac{\text{Sum of Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Coincident Max Demand}}$ | Diversity Factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = kW_{reduced} \times h \times d$ | Energy saved in time $h$ hours/day for $d$ days | kWh |
| 11 | $C_{saved} = E_{saved} \times \text{Tariff}$ | Cost savings | ₹ (or currency) |
| 12 | $PF_{improvement} \to kVA_{reduction}$ | $kVA_{new} = \dfrac{kW}{\cos\phi_{new}}$ | kVA |
| 13 | $L_{insulation} \propto 2^{-(\Delta T / 10)}$ | Montsinger's Rule (insulation life vs temp) | hours |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Energy Auditors** use load matching to identify oversized motors (a common, hidden waste in Indian industry).
- **Pump and Fan System Audits** (as per IS 14683 / Hydraulic Institute) rely on accurate load matching to recommend trimming impellers or using VFDs.
- **Utility DSM Programs** such as BEE's Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme use load-shape modification metrics.
- **Smart Grid Operations** rely on real-time DSM to balance renewable intermittency.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RMS Equivalent Power for Variable Loads

**Problem Context:** A motor drives a load with a repeating 4-step duty cycle. We need to find the motor rating that satisfies both thermal and peak constraints.

**Given Duty Cycle:**

| Interval | Duration $\Delta t_i$ (s) | Load Power $P_i$ (kW) |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 30 | 15 |
| 2 | 20 | 25 |
| 3 | 40 | 10 |
| 4 | 10 | 30 |

**Total cycle time:**

$$\Delta t_{total} = 30 + 20 + 40 + 10 = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $P_i^2 \cdot \Delta t_i$ for each interval:**

$$P_1^2 \cdot \Delta t_1 = (15)^2 \cdot 30 = 225 \cdot 30 = 6750 \text{ kW}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

$$P_2^2 \cdot \Delta t_2 = (25)^2 \cdot 20 = 625 \cdot 20 = 12,500 \text{ kW}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

$$P_3^2 \cdot \Delta t_3 = (10)^2 \cdot 40 = 100 \cdot 40 = 4000 \text{ kW}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

$$P_4^2 \cdot \Delta t_4 = (30)^2 \cdot 10 = 900 \cdot 10 = 9000 \text{ kW}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\sum P_i^2 \cdot \Delta t_i = 6750 + 12{,}500 + 4000 + 9000 = 32{,}250 \text{ kW}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply RMS formula:**

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_i^2 \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i}}$$

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{32{,}250}{100}}$$

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{322.5}$$

$$P_{eq} \approx 17.96 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Select next standard motor rating:**

Standard ratings (R20 series): 0.55, 0.75, 1.1, 1.5, 2.2, 3.7, 5.5, 7.5, 11, 15, 18.5, 22 kW…

Selected rating: **18.5 kW** (just above 17.96 kW).

**Step 5 — Check peak constraint:**

Peak load in cycle = **30 kW** (during interval 4, 10 s).

Short-term overload capability of standard TEFC motor = **150% for 60 s, 200% for 15 s**.

Maximum short-time overload = $1.50 \times 18.5 = 27.75$ kW (for 60 s) — **insufficient**.

Re-select: **22 kW** motor → overload capacity = $1.50 \times 22 = 33$ kW — **adequate for 30 kW peak**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The correct motor is **22 kW**, not 18.5 kW, because the peak constraint dominates. This is a classic KTU examination trap.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Savings from Peak Clipping (DSM)

**Problem Context:** A factory has a peak demand of **800 kW** during 6 PM–10 PM. The utility imposes a demand charge of **₹450 per kVA per month**. The factory's power factor at peak is **0.78 lagging**. By installing capacitor banks, the PF is improved to **0.95 lagging**. Calculate the monthly demand charge savings.

**Step 1 — Original kVA demand:**

$$kVA_{old} = \frac{kW}{\cos\phi_{old}} = \frac{800}{0.78} = 1025.64 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — New kVA demand (after PF correction):**

$$kVA_{new} = \frac{kW}{\cos\phi_{new}} = \frac{800}{0.95} = 842.11 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3 — kVA reduction:**

$$\Delta kVA = 1025.64 - 842.11 = 183.53 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly demand charge savings:**

$$C_{saved} = \Delta kVA \times \text{Tariff}$$

$$C_{saved} = 183.53 \times 450$$

$$C_{saved} = ₹82{,}588.50 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 5 — Annualized savings:**

$$C_{annual} = ₹82{,}588.50 \times 12 = ₹9{,}91{,}062 \approx ₹9.91 \text{ lakhs/year}$$

**Step 6 — Required capacitor kVAr:**

$$kVAr_{cap} = kW \cdot (\tan\phi_{old} - \tan\phi_{new})$$

$$\tan\phi_{old} = \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.78) = \tan(38.74°) \approx 0.8023$$

$$\tan\phi_{new} = \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.95) = \tan(18.19°) \approx 0.3287$$

$$kVAr_{cap} = 800 \cdot (0.8023 - 0.3287) = 800 \cdot 0.4736 = 378.9 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 7 — Approximate capacitor cost (₹1000 per kVAr installed):**

$$C_{cap} = 378.9 \times 1000 = ₹3{,}78{,}900$$

**Step 8 — Simple payback period:**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{₹3{,}78{,}900}{₹9{,}91{,}062 / 12} = \frac{₹3{,}78{,}900}{₹82{,}588.50} \approx 4.59 \text{ months}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** PF correction as a DSM strategy yields an exceptionally fast payback (~4–6 months). This is a frequent KTU question type.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Load Factor and Its Impact on Tariff

**Problem Context:** A commercial establishment has a monthly energy consumption of **60,000 kWh** over **30 days**. Its maximum recorded demand in the month is **200 kW**. The utility offers:
- Energy charge: ₹7.50/kWh
- Demand charge: ₹400/kW/month

Calculate the Load Factor and assess the impact of improving LF from current value to 75%.

**Step 1 — Average load:**

$$\text{Average Load} = \frac{E_{monthly}}{\text{hours}} = \frac{60{,}000}{30 \times 24} = \frac{60{,}000}{720} = 83.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Load Factor:**

$$LF = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}} = \frac{83.33}{200} = 0.4167 = 41.67\%$$

**Step 3 — Current monthly bill:**

Energy charge: $60{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹4{,}50{,}000$

Demand charge: $200 \times 400 = ₹80{,}000$

Total: $₹5{,}30{,}000$

**Step 4 — Scenario: LF improved to 75% (peak demand reduction):**

For the same energy consumption, if LF = 75%:

$$\text{New Peak} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{LF_{new}} = \frac{83.33}{0.75} = 111.11 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — New monthly bill:**

Energy charge: ₹4,50,000 (unchanged)

New demand charge: $111.11 \times 400 = ₹44{,}444$

New total: $₹4{,}94{,}444$

**Step 6 — Monthly savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = ₹5{,}30{,}000 - ₹4{,}94{,}444 = ₹35{,}556 \text{ per month}$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = ₹35{,}556 \times 12 = ₹4{,}26{,}672$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Improving Load Factor (e.g., via load shifting or peak clipping) is one of the most cost-effective DSM strategies for commercial consumers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria Comparison

| Criterion | Constant Load Motor | Variable Load Motor |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Selection Method | Direct rating + service factor | RMS power + peak verification |
| Key Parameter | $P_{load} \times SF$ | $P_{eq}$ (RMS) and $P_{peak}$ |
| Starting Method | DOL/Star-Delta common | Soft-starter/VFD preferred |
| Duty Cycle Rating | S1 (Continuous) | S2, S3, S4, S5, S6 per IEC 60034-1 |
| Protection | Standard overload relay | Thermal overload + cyclic duty consideration |
| Cost Implication | Lower initial cost | Higher due to oversizing |
| Efficiency Check | Single point (full load) | Weighted average across cycle |
| Common Mistake | Undersizing for transients | Undersizing for peaks OR thermal runaway |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Motor Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Load Type] --> B{Constant or Variable?}
    B -- Constant Load --> C[Calculate P_load at worst case]
    C --> D[Apply Service Factor SF]
    D --> E[Apply Altitude/Temp Derating]
    E --> F[Select Next Standard Rating]
    F --> G[Verify Operating at 75 to 100 percent Load]
    G --> H[Check PF and Efficiency Class]
    H --> I[Select Starting Method]
    I --> J[Final Motor Selected]
    B -- Variable Load --> K[Map Duty Cycle into Intervals]
    K --> L[Compute P_eq RMS]
    L --> M[Identify P_peak]
    M --> N{P_eq and P_peak Constraints}
    N --> O[Select Motor: P_rated >= P_eq]
    O --> P[Verify Peak Overload Capability]
    P --> Q{Peak OK?}
    Q -- No --> R[Upsize Motor]
    R --> P
    Q -- Yes --> J
```


### 4.3 Load Matching Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Load Survey] --> survey[Measure Actual Power Demand]
    survey --> profile[Build Load Profile Time vs kW]
    profile --> calc[Calculate Average and Peak Load]
    calc --> lf[Compute Load Factor LF]
    lf --> compare{LF less than 50 percent?}

```
