## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

*"Define Demand Side Management. List any four DSM strategies with one example each."*

**Model Answer:**

Demand Side Management (DSM) is the systematic planning and implementation of utility and customer-side activities designed to influence consumer use of electricity so as to produce desired changes in the utility's load shape — both in magnitude and time pattern. (1.5 marks)

The four DSM strategies are: (1.5 marks)

1. **Peak Clipping** — Example: Direct Load Control of air conditioners by utility during 6 PM–10 PM.
2. **Load Shifting** — Example: Operating ice-based thermal storage air conditioning during off-peak hours.
3. **Strategic Conservation** — Example: Replacing standard motors with IE3 premium efficiency motors.
4. **Valley Filling** — Example: Off-peak electric vehicle smart charging stations.

---

**Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

*"Explain the necessity for load matching in electric motor-driven systems."*

**Model Answer:**

Load matching is necessary to ensure the motor operates in its highest efficiency zone (typically 75%–100% of rated load), thereby minimizing energy losses and reducing operating cost. (1 mark)

Key reasons: (2 marks)

1. **Avoid Overloading** — Prevents insulation failure, overheating, and reduced motor life (Montsinger's Rule).
2. **Avoid Underloading** — Motors below 50% load operate at poor efficiency (PF as low as 0.1–0.3) leading to utility PF penalties.
3. **Economic Operation** — Reduces both energy (kWh) and demand (kVA) charges.
4. **System Reliability** — Reduces nuisance tripping and voltage drops.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A 3-phase induction motor is to drive a load whose duty cycle is given below. Determine the size of the motor required using the RMS power method. (7 marks)

| Step | Duration (s) | Power Demand (kW) |
|------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 20 | 12 |
| 2 | 30 | 25 |
| 3 | 40 | 18 |
| 4 | 10 | 30 |
| 5 | 20 | 8 |

**(b)** Explain in detail the six DSM strategies proposed by Gellings with neat sketches. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

Total cycle time: $\Delta t_{total} = 20 + 30 + 40 + 10 + 20 = 120$ s. (1 mark)

Compute $P_i^2 \cdot \Delta t_i$:

$$P_1^2 \cdot \Delta t_1 = 144 \cdot 20 = 2880$$

$$P_2^2 \cdot \Delta t_2 = 625 \cdot 30 = 18{,}750$$

$$P_3^2 \cdot \Delta t_3 = 324 \cdot 40 = 12{,}960$$

$$P_4^2 \cdot \Delta t_4 = 900 \cdot 10 = 9000$$

$$P_5^2 \cdot \Delta t_5 = 64 \cdot 20 = 1280$$

Sum: $2880 + 18{,}750 + 12{,}960 + 9000 + 1280 = 44{,}870$ kW²·s. (2 marks)

RMS power:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{44{,}870}{120}} = \sqrt{373.92} = 19.34 \text{ kW}$$ (1 mark)

Peak power = 30 kW (during step 4). For short-term overload capability of 150% for 60 s:

Required rating such that $1.50 \times P_{rated} \geq 30$ kW ⇒ $P_{rated} \geq 20$ kW. Combined with $P_{rated} \geq 19.34$ kW, the **next standard rating is 22 kW**. (2 marks)

**Verification:**
- $1.50 \times 22 = 33$ kW ≥ 30 kW ✓
- Continuous rating 22 kW ≥ 19.34 kW RMS ✓

**Answer: 22 kW motor is selected.** (1 mark)

**(b) Model Answer Outline:**

The six DSM strategies (Gellings, EPRI): (6 × 1 mark each, with brief sketch concept)

1. **Peak Clipping** — Reducing peak demand by direct load control or tariffs. Sketch: load curve with peak cut off.
2. **Valley Filling** — Building up off-peak demand by encouraging use during low-demand hours. Sketch: load curve with valley raised.
3. **Load Shifting** — Moving peak load to off-peak using thermal storage. Sketch: peak shifted to off-peak.
4. **Strategic Conservation** — Overall reduction in consumption using efficient equipment. Sketch: entire curve lowered.
5. **Strategic Load Growth** — Increasing off-peak sales through promotional tariffs. Sketch: valley filled, peak unchanged.
6. **Flexible Load Shape** — Allowing customer-controlled response via smart meters. Sketch: irregular but customer-optimized shape.

(1 mark for overall diagram synthesis)

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for selecting a motor for a constant load application. A centrifugal pump requires 18 kW at 1450 rpm. The pump operates 5000 hours/year at full load. Recommend a suitable motor rating and efficiency class. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the various benefits of Demand Side Management to utilities, consumers, and society. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Model Answer:**

**Procedure for Constant Load Motor Selection:** (3 marks)

1. Determine maximum continuous power: 18 kW.
2. Apply service factor (SF = 1.10 for centrifugal pump with possible transients): $P_{required} = 18 \times 1.10 = 19.8$ kW.
3. Select next standard R20 rating: **22 kW motor** at 1450 rpm (4-pole, 50 Hz).
4. Verify operating load = $18/22 = 81.8\%$ — falls within sweet zone (75–100%). ✓
5. Recommend efficiency class: **IE3 Premium Efficiency** as per IS 12615:2018 (mandatory for motors ≥ 0.75 kW since 2017–2020 phased implementation).
6. Recommended starting method: Star-Delta (since motor > 5.5 kW to limit starting current).

**Calculation of Energy Use:** (2 marks)

$$E_{annual} = 18 \text{ kW} \times 5000 \text{ h} = 90{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

At IE3 efficiency (η ≈ 93.0%) vs IE2 (η ≈ 90.5%):

$$\Delta E = 90{,}000 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.905} - \frac{1}{0.930}\right) = 90{,}000 \times (1.105 - 1.075) = 90{,}000 \times 0.030 = 2700 \text{ kWh/year saved}$$

Annual savings at ₹8/kWh: $2700 \times 8 = ₹21{,}600$/year. (2 marks)

**Answer: 22 kW, 4-pole, IE3 motor selected.**

**(b) Model Answer:**

**Benefits of DSM:**

**To Utilities:** (3 marks)
- Reduction in peak demand → deferred generation, transmission, and distribution (T&D) investments.
- Improved load factor → better asset utilization.
- Reduced system losses → lower operating cost.
- Avoided cost of new peaking power plants.
- Enhanced grid reliability and stability.

**To Consumers:** (2 marks)
- Lower electricity bills (energy + demand charges).
- Improved comfort and process control (with smart devices).
- Choice and control through Time-of-Use tariffs.
- Subsidized efficient equipment (DSM programs).

**To Society:** (2 marks)
- Lower greenhouse gas emissions → climate change mitigation.
- Reduced air pollution (especially in coal-dependent grids).
- Conservation of fossil fuel resources.
- Sustainable development and energy security.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Motor Selection & DSM Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Peak Constraint:** Students often select the next standard rating based only on RMS power, ignoring the peak overload capability. Always check: $1.5 \times P_{rated} \geq P_{peak}$ for short-duration peaks.
> 2. **Mixing Constant and Variable Load Methods:** Do NOT apply service factor to RMS power for variable loads — the RMS method already inherently includes the safety margin through squaring of loads.
> 3. **Ignoring Power Factor Penalty:** When calculating kVA reduction in DSM, students often compute only kW savings. The bigger savings come from PF improvement (kVA reduction) — show both.
> 4. **Forgetting Altitude Derating:** For installations in Kerala (altitude generally low, ~0–2700 m in Wayanad), this is rarely a problem, but for high-altitude regions, derate motor by ~1% per 100 m above 1000 m.
> 5. **Using Mean Power Instead of RMS:** A common trap — for variable loads, mean power (simple average) underestimates the heating effect. Always use RMS for thermal considerations.
> 6. **Confusing Diversity Factor with Load Factor:** These are inverse-related but distinct. Diversity Factor is at the system/utility level; Load Factor is at the consumer level.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Recall Checklist:

- ✅ Load matching aligns motor rating with actual load demand to avoid inefficiency at both ends (over- and under-loading).
- ✅ Operating sweet zone: **75%–100% of rated load**.
- ✅ Constant load selection: $P_{motor} = P_{load} \times SF \times$ derating factors.
- ✅ Variable load selection: Use **RMS (equivalent) power method** AND verify peak overload capability.
- ✅ RMS formula: $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\sum P_i^2 \Delta t_i / \sum \Delta t_i}$.
- ✅ **Montsinger's Rule**: Every 10 °C above insulation limit **halves** insulation life.
- ✅ **IE3** is the minimum mandated efficiency class for industrial motors (per IS 12615 / BEE PAT scheme).
- ✅ **DSM = Demand Side Management** (Gellings, EPRI) — modifies load shape via consumer-side actions.
- ✅ Six DSM strategies: **Peak Clipping, Valley Filling, Load Shifting, Strategic Conservation, Strategic Load Growth, Flexible Load Shape.**
- ✅ PF correction (capacitors) = fastest-paying DSM technique (typically 4–6 month payback).
- ✅ Load Factor (LF) = Average Load / Peak Load; improvement reduces demand charge significantly.
- ✅ Diversity Factor (DF) = Sum of Individual Maxima / Coincident Maximum Demand.
- ✅ Energy savings: $E_{saved} = kW_{reduced} \times h \times d$; cost savings = $E_{saved} \times \text{tariff}$.
- ✅ For motors ≥ 5.5 kW, prefer Star-Delta or Soft-Starter; VFD if speed variation needed.
- ✅ Always express motor power in kW, torque in Nm, and speed in rpm when applying $P = TN/9550$.

### High-Yield Keywords for KTU 2024:

**Load Matching | Motor Selection | Constant Load | Variable Load | RMS Power | Service Factor | Derating | Montsinger's Rule | Best Efficiency Point | Power Factor Penalty | IE3/IE4 Motors | Demand Side Management | DSM Strategies | Peak Clipping | Load Shifting | Valley Filling | Strategic Conservation | Load Factor | Diversity Factor | Smart Meter | Time-of-Use Tariff | Perform Achieve Trade (PAT)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits of DSM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benefits of DSM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Demand Side Management (DSM)** refers to the planning, implementation, and monitoring of utility activities designed to influence consumer use of electricity in ways that will produce desired changes in the utility's load shape — i.e., changes in the time pattern and magnitude of a utility's load. The **Benefits of DSM** are the measurable, multi-dimensional positive outcomes (technical, economic, environmental, and social) obtained by successfully implementing DSM programs.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India** and the **CEA (Central Electricity Authority)** framework adopted in KTU 2024 Energy Management syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "Benefits of DSM encompass the full-spectrum value created by altering end-use electrical consumption patterns — including peak clipping, valley filling, load shifting, strategic conservation, strategic load growth, and flexible load shape — resulting in optimized system performance, reduced emissions, deferred capital expenditure, and enhanced end-user satisfaction."

## 1.2 Key DSM Strategies (Recall for Benefits Context)

| Strategy | Description | Direct Benefit Triggered |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Peak Clipping** | Reducing peak demand | Lower generation cost |
| **Valley Filling** | Building up off-peak load | Higher plant load factor |
| **Load Shifting** | Moving load from peak to off-peak | Better asset utilization |
| **Strategic Conservation** | Reducing overall consumption | Energy savings & emissions cut |
| **Strategic Load Growth** | Increasing off-peak sales | Higher revenue without new capacity |
| **Flexible Load Shape** | Real-time load controllability | Grid reliability |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Traffic Highway Analogy**
> Imagine a 4-lane highway that gets jammed only during 8 AM and 6 PM (peak hours). You have two choices to fix this:
> 1. **Supply-side solution:** Build a 6-lane highway (expensive, takes 5 years, new land acquisition).
> 2. **Demand-side solution (DSM):** Stagger office timings, promote car-pooling, encourage work-from-home, offer off-peak toll discounts.
>
> DSM is the **"car-pooling + flexible timing"** approach for electricity grids. Instead of building more power plants (supply-side), we **shape the demand** so that the existing infrastructure serves more people efficiently. The **benefits** are the savings in money, time, fuel, and pollution you get from this smart management.

## 1.4 Why "Benefits" Form a Critical KTU Topic

In the KTU 2024 OEEET612 syllabus, understanding benefits is essential because:
- It justifies **why** utilities invest in DSM programs.
- It forms the **quantitative backbone** of any energy audit project.
- It directly maps to **CO2 (Course Outcome 2): "Understand energy conservation techniques and demand side management."**

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The two most important metrics a KTU examiner expects you to know are:
> - **Load Factor (LF)** — measures how steadily a utility's capacity is used (ideal = 1.0).
> - **Diversity Factor (DF)** — measures how individual loads diversify in time (typical range = **1.1 to 1.5** for industrial feeders).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DSM Benefits (The "TEEF" Framework)

The benefits of DSM can be systematically classified into **four pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — **T**echnical Benefits
These relate to the physical performance of the power system.

- **Peak Demand Reduction** — lowers stress on grid infrastructure.
- **Improved Load Factor** — flattens the load curve.
- **Reduced T&D Losses** — since I²R losses are proportional to the square of current, reducing peak current dramatically cuts losses.
- **Enhanced Power Quality** — stable voltage and frequency.
- **Improved Grid Reliability** — fewer outages, better spinning reserve.
- **Deferred Capital Investment** — new generation/transmission postponed by 3–10 years.

### Pillar 2 — **E**conomic Benefits
These relate to monetary savings and financial returns.

- **Lower Electricity Bills for Consumers** — via efficiency + TOU tariffs.
- **Reduced Operating Costs for Utilities** — less fuel, less O&M.
- **Avoided Capacity Cost** — ₹/kW of new plant construction deferred.
- **Lower Tariff for All** — cross-subsidy reduction.
- **Increased Revenue per kWh** — better utilization of fixed assets.

### Pillar 3 — **E**nvironmental Benefits
These relate to ecological sustainability.

- **Reduced CO₂ Emissions** — direct climate benefit.
- **Reduced SO₂ and NOₓ** — less acid rain and smog.
- **Lower Particulate Matter (PM2.5/PM10)** — better public health.
- **Reduced Water Consumption** — thermal plants use massive water for cooling.
- **Lower Fuel Mining Impact** — reduced coal/oil extraction footprint.

### Pillar 4 — **S**ocial Benefits
These relate to human development and welfare.

- **Energy Access to More People** — same infrastructure serves more consumers.
- **Energy Security** — reduced import dependence.
- **Job Creation in Energy Services Sector** — ESCOs, auditors, retrofitters.
- **Public Health Improvement** — cleaner air, fewer respiratory diseases.
- **Consumer Empowerment** — informed, participatory energy users.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Benefit

### A. Why does Peak Reduction yield multiple cascading benefits?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Principle:** Reducing peak demand by 1 MW avoids approximately **2.5 to 3.0 MW** of new generation capacity requirement (rule of thumb: **1 MW peak reduction ≈ 2.5–3 MW generation deferral** when reserve margin and T&D bottlenecks are accounted for). This is because peak occurs only for a few hours, so flattening it allows the *entire chain* — generation, transmission, distribution — to operate at a lower rating.

**How it works step-by-step:**
1. Customer shifts/interrupts 1 MW of load during peak hours.
2. Utility's generation dispatch avoids running the **marginal (peak) plant** — typically the oldest, dirtiest, costliest unit.
3. Transmission lines carry less current → **I²R losses drop quadratically**.
4. Distribution transformers are de-stressed → longer life, fewer failures.
5. Fuel consumption drops → emissions fall.
6. Tariff stability improves → economy benefits.

### B. How does Load Factor Improvement save money?

A poor load factor (e.g., 0.45) means the utility built a plant to serve 100 MW peak but only sells energy equivalent to running at 45 MW continuously. The **fixed cost** is recovered over fewer units sold, raising the per-unit tariff. Improving LF to 0.65 spreads fixed costs over more units → tariff drops.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **complete arsenal of formulas** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the "Benefits of DSM" topic. Every formula has appeared in past KTU board examinations.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units | Real-World Meaning |
|---|--------------------|--------------------------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Average Load** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Time Period (h)}}$ | kW | Mean power drawn |
| 2 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $LF = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{P_{peak}} = \dfrac{\text{Units Generated in Period}}{\text{Peak Load} \times \text{Period Hours}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | How flat the demand is |
| 3 | **Capacity Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Output}}{\text{Max Possible Energy Output}}$ | dimensionless | Plant utilization |
| 4 | **Demand Reduction %** | $\Delta D\% = \dfrac{D_{original} - D_{new}}{D_{original}} \times 100$ | % | Peak cut achieved |
| 5 | **Energy Savings** | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{DSM}$ | kWh/year | Annual conserved energy |
| 6 | **Demand Savings (Monetary)** | $S_{D} = \Delta D \times \text{Demand Charge (₹/kW/month)} \times 12$ | ₹/year | Annual demand bill cut |
| 7 | **Energy Savings (Monetary)** | $S_{E} = \Delta E \times \text{Energy Charge (₹/kWh)}$ | ₹/year | Annual energy bill cut |
| 8 | **Total Annual Savings** | $S_{total} = S_{D} + S_{E}$ | ₹/year | Combined DSM benefit |
| 9 | **T&D Loss Reduction** | $\Delta L_{T\&D} \approx 2 \times \Delta D\%$ (rule) | % | Loss cut from peak cut |
| 10 | **CO₂ Emission Reduction** | $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF$ | kg CO₂ | Carbon benefit |
| 11 | **Simple Payback Period** | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment (₹)}}{S_{total} \text{ (₹/year)}}$ | years | Return time |
| 12 | **Return on Investment** | $ROI = \dfrac{S_{total}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Annualized return |
| 13 | **Capacity Credit** | $CC = \dfrac{DSM \text{ Capacity (kW)}}{\text{System Peak (kW)}} \times 100$ | % | Firm capacity value |
| 14 | **Diversity Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Combined Max Demand}}$ | dimensionless | Load diversity |
| 15 | **Coincidence Factor** | $CF_{coin} = \dfrac{\text{Coincident Demand}}{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of diversity |
| 16 | **Avoided Generation Cost** | $C_{avoid} = \Delta D \times \text{₹/kW Capital Cost}$ | ₹ | Deferred capex |
| 17 | **Levelized Cost of Saved Energy** | $LCOSE = \dfrac{\text{DSM Cost (₹/kW)} \times CRF}{\Delta E \text{ per kW}}$ | ₹/kWh | Cost of conservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):** Used in LCOSE formula:
> $$CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$
> where $i$ = interest rate (decimal), $n$ = project life (years). This is the most tested auxiliary formula in KTU Energy Management papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Benefit Equations

In actual engineering practice, these formulas are deployed in:

- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** of BEE — Indian industrial energy efficiency trading.
- **ESCO Business Models** — quantifying savings for performance contracts.
- **Utility Integrated Resource Planning (IRP)** — comparing DSM cost vs. new generation cost.
- **Carbon Credit/CDM Projects** — quantifying CO₂ reduction for monetization.
- **Tariff Design** — by SERCs (State Electricity Regulatory Commissions) for Time-of-Day (ToD) tariffs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Demand Reduction and Its Multiplier Effect

**Statement:** When peak demand is reduced by $\Delta D$, the equivalent generation capacity avoided is greater than $\Delta D$ by a factor called the **Reserve Margin Multiplier (RMM)**.

**Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Setup):**
- Original system peak demand: $D_0 = 1000$ MW
- Installed capacity: $C_{inst} = 1200$ MW
- Reserve margin: $RM = 20\%$

**Step 1 — Define Reserve Margin**

$$RM = \dfrac{C_{inst} - D_0}{D_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

$$20 = \dfrac{1200 - 1000}{1000} \times 100$$

$$20 = \dfrac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — DSM Reduces Peak Demand**

After DSM, new peak: $D_1 = D_0 - \Delta D = 1000 - 50 = 950$ MW (assuming 5% reduction).

**Step 4 — New Required Installed Capacity (Maintaining Same 20% Reserve)**

$$C_{inst,new} = D_1 \times (1 + RM) = 950 \times 1.20 = 1140 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Avoided Capacity**

$$\Delta C = C_{inst,old} - C_{inst,new} = 1200 - 1140 = 60 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Multiplier Effect**

$$\text{Multiplier} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta D} = \dfrac{60}{50} = 1.2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 50 MW peak reduction via DSM avoids the need to build 60 MW of new capacity. The multiplier of **1.2** comes purely from the reserve margin. In real grids with 15% spinning reserve + 10% contingency reserve, this multiplier typically rises to **2.5 – 3.0**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantification of Monetary Savings (Load Shifting Case)

**Problem Statement:** A textile mill operates a 200 kW load from 6 PM to 10 PM (peak hours) at ₹8/kWh. DSM proposal: shift this load to 10 PM – 2 AM (off-peak) at ₹4/kWh. Mill operates 300 days/year. Compute annual savings.

**Step 1 — Energy Consumed per Shift**

$$E = P \times t = 200 \text{ kW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 800 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Tariff Difference**

$$\Delta T = 8 - 4 = \text{₹}4/\text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Daily Monetary Savings**

$$S_{daily} = E \times \Delta T = 800 \times 4 = \text{₹}3200/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Monetary Savings**

$$S_{annual} = S_{daily} \times 300 = 3200 \times 300 = \text{₹}9,60,000/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — If Implementation Cost is ₹15,00,000, Compute Payback**

$$SPP = \dfrac{15,00,000}{9,60,000} = 1.5625 \text{ years} \approx 1 \text{ year } 7 \text{ months}$$

**Step 6 — ROI (Project Life = 10 years)**

Total savings over life:
$$S_{life} = 9,60,000 \times 10 = \text{₹}96,00,000$$

Net benefit:
$$\text{Net} = 96,00,000 - 15,00,000 = \text{₹}81,00,000$$

$$ROI = \dfrac{81,00,000}{15,00,000} \times 100 = 540\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CO₂ Emission Reduction

**Standard KTU Working:** Compute the CO₂ avoided by DSM for the textile mill above.

**Given:** Indian grid emission factor, $EF = 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh (CEA 2023 baseline).

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Shifted (No Change in kWh)**

$$E_{annual} = 800 \times 300 = 2,40,000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Note Carefully**

Since this is *load shifting* (not energy saving), **kWh consumption is identical**. The CO₂ benefit arises because off-peak power is typically supplied by **more efficient baseload plants** (e.g., large coal/nuclear) rather than peaking plants (e.g., gas turbines, diesel).

**Step 3 — Apply Differential Emission Factor**

If the off-peak plant emission factor is $EF_{off} = 0.72$ kg CO₂/kWh and the peak plant is $EF_{peak} = 1.05$ kg CO₂/kWh:

$$\Delta EF = 1.05 - 0.72 = 0.33 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Annual CO₂ Avoided**

$$\Delta CO_2 = E_{annual} \times \Delta EF = 2,40,000 \times 0.33 = 79,200 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

$$\Delta CO_2 = 79.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Tree Planting**

If 1 tree absorbs ~22 kg CO₂/year:

$$\text{Trees equivalent} = \dfrac{79200}{22} \approx 3600 \text{ trees}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For an actual energy conservation project** (e.g., LED replacement), the formula simplifies to $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF$ where $\Delta E$ is the *reduced* kWh, not the same kWh shifted.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Levelized Cost of Saved Energy (LCOSE)

This is the most analytically demanding derivation and is a **KTU favourite for 7-mark Part B sub-questions**.

**Statement:** Prove that DSM is more cost-effective than a new gas-based peaking plant if LCOSE $<$ Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE) of the new plant.

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) Derivation**

For an annuity of ₹A paid for $n$ years at interest rate $i$, present value is:

$$PV = A \times \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

Inverting to find $A$ from $PV$:

$$A = PV \times \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

Hence:

$$CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Numerical Values**

Take $i = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 10$ years.

$$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$$

$$CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.1627$$

**Step 3 — DSM Project Cost**

Say DSM cost is ₹10,000/kW. Annual saving per kW is 1500 kWh/year (e.g., efficient motor).

**Step 4 — Annualized Cost per kW**

$$A_{cost} = 10000 \times 0.1627 = \text{₹}1627/\text{kW/year}$$

**Step 5 — LCOSE**

$$LCOSE = \dfrac{1627}{1500} = \text{₹}1.08/\text{kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Peaking Plant LCOE**

A gas-based peaking plant typically has LCOE of ₹6–10/kWh.

**Step 7 — Conclusion**

Since $1.08 \ll 6$, DSM is **~5–9 times cheaper** than new peaking capacity. This is the **single most powerful argument** for DSM in any KTU essay-type question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Benefits Summary Table

| Stakeholder | Top 3 Direct Benefits | Quantified Typical Range |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| **End Consumer** | Lower bill, better comfort, control | 10–30% bill reduction |
| **Utility (DISCOM)** | Deferred capex, lower T&D losses, higher LF | ₹4–6 Cr/MW deferred |
| **Generator (Producer)** | Higher PLF, lower spinning reserve | +15–20 percentage points LF |
| **Government/Regulator** | Energy security, lower subsidy burden | 5–10% subsidy reduction |
| **Society/Environment** | Lower CO₂, better air quality, jobs | 0.5–1.0 kg CO₂/kWh saved |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of DSM Benefits

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Benefits of DSM] --> B[Technical Benefits]
    A --> C[Economic Benefits]
    A --> D[Environmental Benefits]
    A --> E[Social Benefits]

    B --> B1[Peak Demand Reduction]
    B --> B2[Improved Load Factor]
    B --> B3[Reduced T&D Losses]
    B --> B4[Deferred Capital Investment]
    B --> B5[Enhanced Power Quality]

    C --> C1[Lower Consumer Bills]
    C --> C2[Reduced Utility O and M Cost]
    C --> C3[Avoided Capacity Cost]
    C --> C4[Cross Subsidy Reduction]
    C --> C5[Higher Revenue per kWh]

    D --> D1[CO2 Emission Reduction]
    D --> D2[Lower SO2 and NOx]
    D --> D3[Reduced Water Usage]
    D --> D4[Lower Particulate Matter]
    D --> D5[Reduced Mining Footprint]

    E --> E1[Energy Access Expansion]
    E --> E2[Energy Security]
    E --> E3[Job Creation in ESCOs]
    E --> E4[Public Health Improvement]
    E --> E5[Consumer Empowerment]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a DSM Benefit Materializes End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DSMprog[DSM Program Launched] --> CustEng[Customer Engagement and Audit]
    CustEng --> TechInt[Technology Installation]
    TechInt --> BehavCh[Behavior Change]
    TechInt --> LoadShed[Load Shedding or Shifting]
    BehavCh --> LoadShed
    LoadShed --> LoadCurve[Modified Load Curve]
    LoadCurve --> PeakRed[Peak Reduction]
    LoadCurve --> LFImp[Load Factor Improved]
    PeakRed --> TechBen[Technical Benefits Block]
    LFImp --> TechBen
    TechBen --> EconBen[Economic Benefits Block]
    TechBen --> EnvBen[Environmental Benefits Block]
    EconBen --> SocBen[Social Benefits Block]
    EnvBen --> SocBen
    SocBen --> StakeFeedback[Stakeholder Feedback Loop]
    StakeFeedback --> DSMprog
```

## 4.3 Cost-Benefit Comparison Flow (DSM vs Supply-Side)

```mermaid
graph LR
    DSMChoice{Energy Demand Rising} -->|Build New Plant| SSP[Supply Side Path]
    DSMChoice -->|Manage Demand| DSMP[Demand Side Path]

    SSP --> SSP1[Capital Cost: High]
    SSP --> SSP2[Lead Time: 3 to 5 years]
    SSP --> SSP3[Emissions: High]
    SSP --> SSP4[Flexibility: Low]

    DSMP --> DSMP1[Capital Cost: Low to Medium]
    DSMP --> DSMP2[Lead Time: 3 to 12 months]
    DSMP --> DSMP3[Emissions: Lower]
    DSMP --> DSMP4[Flexibility: High]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every box represents a measurable benefit or cost. The "Stakeholder Feedback Loop" in the first flowchart shows that DSM is not a one-time project — it is a **continuous improvement cycle**, which is a frequent KTU 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Energy Management]**

*Define Demand Side Management. List any four benefits of DSM to the consumer.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Demand Side Management (DSM) refers to the systematic actions taken by utilities and consumers to modify the pattern and magnitude of electricity consumption, including planning, implementation, and monitoring of programs designed to influence consumer use of electricity.

**Four Consumer Benefits (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Reduced Electricity Bills** — through energy efficiency and shifting loads to off-peak times (Time-of-Use tariffs).
2. **Improved Comfort and Convenience** — modern efficient appliances often perform better (e.g., inverter ACs).
3. **Greater Control over Energy Use** — smart meters, home energy management systems.
4. **Enhanced Power Quality and Reliability** — fewer voltage fluctuations, fewer outages in their locality.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Energy Management]**

*Explain "Load Factor" and state how DSM improves it. Why is a higher load factor economically beneficial to a utility?*

**Model Answer:**

**Load Factor Definition (1 mark):**

$$LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load over a Period}}{\text{Peak Load during that Period}}$$

**How DSM Improves LF (1 mark):**
DSM flattens the load curve by **shifting** load from peak to off-peak hours (e.g., running washing machines, water heaters at night) and **clipping** peak demand. This raises the average load relative to the peak, mathematically increasing the ratio.

**Economic Benefit (1 mark):**
A higher LF means the utility's fixed costs (interest, depreciation, salaries) are spread over **more kWh sold**, lowering the **per-unit cost of electricity**. It also defers the need for new generation capacity, saving capital expenditure.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Classify the benefits of DSM into four major categories and explain each with **two specific examples**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 500 kW industrial consumer operates a uniform load for 12 hours/day, 300 days/year. The utility has a Time-of-Day tariff: ₹9/kWh for 6 PM–10 PM (peak) and ₹5/kWh for the remaining 14 hours (off-peak). The DSM proposal is to shift 2 hours of operation from peak to off-peak, with an implementation cost of ₹8,00,000. Calculate: *(i) Annual energy consumed, (ii) Annual savings, (iii) Simple payback period, (iv) CO₂ avoided per year, given emission factor = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh.* *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Classification (7 marks)

**The four categories are:**

**1. Technical Benefits (1½ marks):**
- **Peak Demand Reduction** — e.g., installing capacitor banks to improve power factor reduces kVA demand.
- **Improved Load Factor** — e.g., encouraging night-time industrial operations.

**2. Economic Benefits (2 marks):**
- **Lower consumer electricity bills** — e.g., TOU tariff savings, demand charge reduction.
- **Avoided capacity cost for utility** — e.g., deferring a ₹4000 Cr power plant by 5 years saves crores in interest.

**3. Environmental Benefits (1½ marks):**
- **CO₂ emission reduction** — e.g., 1 kWh saved = 0.82 kg CO₂ avoided (CEA baseline).
- **Reduced local air pollution** — e.g., less SO₂/particulates from peaking plants.

**4. Social Benefits (2 marks):**
- **Energy access to more households** — same infrastructure serves more people.
- **Job creation** — e.g., ESCO industry, BEE-certified energy auditors.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $P = 500$ kW
- Operating hours = 12 h/day
- Operating days = 300/year
- Peak tariff: ₹9/kWh (6 PM–10 PM = 4 hours)
- Off-peak tariff: ₹5/kWh (remaining 14 hours)
- Shift: 2 hours from peak to off-peak
- Implementation cost = ₹8,00,000
- EF = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh

**(i) Annual Energy Consumed (1 mark):**

$$E_{annual} = P \times h_{day} \times days = 500 \times 12 \times 300 = 18,00,000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual Savings (2 marks):**

Energy shifted = $500 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ h/day} \times 300 = 3,00,000 \text{ kWh/year}$

Tariff differential = ₹9 - ₹5 = ₹4/kWh

$$S_{annual} = 3,00,000 \times 4 = \text{₹}12,00,000/\text{year}$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period (2 marks):**

$$SPP = \dfrac{8,00,000}{12,00,000} = 0.667 \text{ years} \approx 8 \text{ months}$$

**(iv) CO₂ Avoided per Year (2 marks):**

Since this is *load shifting* and not energy conservation, **kWh consumption is unchanged**. However, we apply the differential emission factor principle. Assuming peak plant EF = 1.05 and off-peak EF = 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh:

$$\Delta EF = 1.05 - 0.72 = 0.33 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

$$\Delta CO_2 = 3,00,000 \times 0.33 = 99,000 \text{ kg} = 99 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

*Alternative simpler interpretation (if examiner expects):* If energy is genuinely saved, $\Delta CO_2 = 3,00,000 \times 0.82 = 2,46,000$ kg = **246 tonnes CO₂/year**.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain, with suitable justification, why DSM is considered more cost-effective than building a new peaking power plant. Support your answer with the **Levelized Cost of Saved Energy (LCOSE)** concept and a numerical example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A residential complex has 200 households. Each household has a 1.5 kW air conditioner. DSM proposal: replace 1.5 kW conventional ACs with 1.2 kW inverter ACs (same cooling output). ACs run 6 hours/day for 120 days/year. Tariff is ₹7/kWh. Cost of replacement is ₹15,000 per AC. Calculate: *(i) Per-household annual energy savings, (ii) Total complex annual savings, (iii) Payback period per household, (iv) Total CO₂ avoided per year (EF = 0.82).* *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Cost-Effectiveness Argument (7 marks)

**Introduction (2 marks):**
Building a new peaking power plant (typically gas-based) requires massive capital investment (₹4–6 Cr/MW), 3–5 years of gestation, environmental clearances, fuel supply arrangements, and ongoing fuel cost volatility. DSM, on the other hand, can be deployed in 3–12 months, requires ₹5,000–₹15,000 per kW saved, and the "saved kWh" is perpetual with minimal recurring cost.

**LCOSE Concept (2 marks):**
LCOSE is the per-kWh cost of energy saved through DSM, computed as:

$$LCOSE = \dfrac{C_{DSM} \times CRF}{\Delta E_{annual}/kW}$$

where $C_{DSM}$ = DSM cost per kW, $CRF$ = capital recovery factor, $\Delta E$ = annual energy saved per kW.

**Numerical Example (3 marks):**
Let $C_{DSM} = \text{₹}12,000/\text{kW}$, $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years, $\Delta E = 1500$ kWh/kW/year.

$$CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{1.5937} = 0.1627$$

$$LCOSE = \dfrac{12000 \times 0.1627}{1500} = \dfrac{1952.4}{1500} = \text{₹}1.30/\text{kWh}$$

A new gas peaker LCOE is typically ₹6–10/kWh. Therefore **LCOSE $\ll$ LCOE**, proving DSM is more cost-effective by a factor of **5–8×**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Residential Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Households = 200
- Old AC = 1.5 kW
- New AC = 1.2 kW
- Runtime = 6 h/day × 120 days = 720 hours/year
- Tariff = ₹7/kWh
- Replacement cost = ₹15,000 per AC
- EF = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh

**(i) Per-Household Annual Energy Savings (2 marks):**

Power reduction per AC = $1.5 - 1.2 = 0.3$ kW

$$\Delta E_{HH} = 0.3 \times 720 = 216 \text{ kWh/year per household}$$

**(ii) Total Complex Annual Savings (2 marks):**

$$\Delta E_{total} = 216 \times 200 = 43,200 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$S_{total} = 43,200 \times 7 = \text{₹}3,02,400/\text{year}$$

**(iii) Payback Period per Household (1 mark):**

$$SPP = \dfrac{15,000}{216 \times 7} = \dfrac{15,000}{1512} = 9.92 \text{ years} \approx 10 \text{ years}$$

**(iv) Total CO₂ Avoided (2 marks):**

$$\Delta CO_2 = 43,200 \times 0.82 = 35,424 \text{ kg} = 35.42 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Load Factor with Diversity Factor** — these are *different*. LF is for a single consumer or system; DF compares individual vs coincident demand of multiple loads.
> 2. **Forgetting Units** — energy in kWh, power in kW, demand in kVA (not kW) for industrial tariffs. Always write units.
> 3. **Misapplying Payback formula** — Simple Payback uses *annual* savings in the denominator, not lifetime savings.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by operating days** — a common 1-mark loss in numericals. Always state your operating days/hours assumption clearly.
> 5. **Ignoring Reserve Margin in capacity deferral** — a 1 MW peak cut ≠ 1 MW generation avoided; it avoids ~2.5–3 MW of installed capacity.
> 6. **Mixing up Peak Clipping vs Valley Filling** — peak clipping *reduces* peak; valley filling *increases* off-peak. Both improve Load Factor but are different strategies.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DSM = managing demand, not just saving energy.** The 6 strategies are: Peak Clipping, Valley Filling, Load Shifting, Strategic Conservation, Strategic Load Growth, Flexible Load Shape.
- ✅ **4 Pillars of Benefits:** Technical, Economic, Environmental, Social (memorize **T-E-E-S** or **TEEF**).
- ✅ **Load Factor** is the single most important derived metric. Formula: $LF = P_{avg}/P_{peak}$. Higher LF = better.
- ✅ **Cascade Multiplier:** 1 MW peak cut ≈ **2.5–3 MW** generation deferred (including reserve margin).
- ✅ **CO₂ avoided** = $\Delta E \times EF$. For India, $EF \approx 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh (CEA 2023).
- ✅ **Simple Payback** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings. Always quote in **years and months**.
- ✅ **LCOSE** is the *most powerful* analytical tool to compare DSM with new generation. If LCOSE $<$ LCOE, DSM wins.
- ✅ **TOU Tariff differential** is the financial engine that drives load shifting behavior.
- ✅ **T&D losses are I²R** — reducing peak by 5% reduces losses by ~10% (approximate, due to square-law).
- ✅ **Diversity Factor** is **always ≥ 1**; **Coincidence Factor** is **always ≤ 1**; they are reciprocals.
- ✅ **BEE's PAT scheme** and **ESCO business models** are real-world implementations of DSM in India.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark Part B questions, **always write units, show formula, substitute, compute, and interpret** — this 5-step structure fetches full marks.
- ✅ The two highest-weightage sub-topics in this module: **(1) Classifying benefits** and **(2) Numerical computation of savings/payback/CO₂**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Techniques of DSM, Power Factor Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Techniques of DSM, Power Factor Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Demand Side Management (DSM) — Formal Definition

**Demand Side Management (DSM)** refers to the systematic planning, implementation, and monitoring of utility-sponsored and consumer-oriented activities designed to influence the quantity and timing of electricity consumption. These activities aim to produce desirable changes in the utility's load profile — that is, the pattern of electricity demand over time — while ensuring that energy services to consumers are delivered efficiently, economically, and sustainably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> DSM is broadly classified into three primary categories: **Energy Efficiency**, **Load Management**, and **Strategic Load Growth / Conservation**. The focus of this module is to understand *load shape modification techniques* and how they translate into real-world energy savings for the utility, consumer, and the national grid.

## 1.2 Power Factor (PF) — Formal Definition

**Power Factor (PF)** is the ratio of **real (active) power** flowing to the load, to the **apparent power** in the circuit. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 to 1 (often expressed as a percentage or in decimal form, such as 0.85 lag).

$$\text{Power Factor (PF)} = \cos \phi = \frac{\text{Real Power (P in W)}}{\text{Apparent Power (S in VA)}}$$

Where $\phi$ is the **phase angle** between the voltage and current waveforms. When the current **lags** the voltage (inductive load), PF is said to be **lagging**. When the current **leads** the voltage (capacitive load), PF is **leading**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Beer Mug Principle" 🍺:**
> Imagine a large mug of beer (the *Apparent Power, S*). The actual drinkable liquid beer is the **Real Power, P** (which does useful work — running motors, lighting lamps). The frothy foam on top is the **Reactive Power, Q** (which does no useful work but occupies space and must be carried along).
> - A **Low Power Factor** = a mug with lots of foam and very little beer. You're paying to transport a lot of useless foam!
> - **Power Factor Correction** = a skilled bartender who adds a drop of "anti-foam" (capacitor bank) to dissolve the foam, leaving you with a mug full of pure, useful beer. You pay only for what you drink.

**For DSM — "The Traffic Flow Analogy" 🚗:**
- **Peak Clipping** = A traffic cop stopping cars from entering a crowded highway during rush hour.
- **Valley Filling** = Encouraging people to drive during off-peak hours (midnight, early morning) by offering free tolls.
- **Load Shifting** = Shifting office work-from-home days to weekends.
- **Strategic Conservation** = Promoting carpooling and electric vehicles to reduce total fuel use.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real Power | **P** | **Watt (W), kW, MW** | Power that does useful work |
| Reactive Power | **Q** | **VAR, kVAR, MVAR** | Power oscillating between source and load |
| Apparent Power | **S** | **VA, kVA, MVA** | Vector sum of P and Q |
| Power Factor | **cos φ** | Dimensionless (0–1) | Ratio of P to S |
| Phase Angle | **φ** | Degrees / Radians | Angle between V and I |
| Frequency | **f** | **Hz (50 Hz in India)** | Supply frequency |
| Angular Frequency | **ω** | rad/s | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Supply Frequency in India: 50 Hz**, and standard consumer voltages are **230 V (single-phase)** and **415 V (three-phase)** as per IS standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Different Techniques of Demand Side Management (DSM)

DSM techniques are designed to modify the **load curve** of a utility. Each technique has a specific objective and a unique effect on the load shape. The classical six techniques of DSM (per KTU and standard energy management literature) are:

### 2.1.1 Peak Clipping
- **Objective:** Reduce the peak demand during high-load periods.
- **How it works:** Direct load control (DLC) interrupts non-essential loads (e.g., ACs, water heaters) during peak hours. The utility may install ripple control systems to send signals to consumer appliances.
- **Why it's done:** Reduces the need to run expensive peak-load power plants, defers T&D capacity additions, and lowers electricity costs.
- **Example:** A utility remotely switches off 10,000 residential AC compressors for 30 minutes during 6 PM – 8 PM.

### 2.1.2 Valley Filling
- **Objective:** Build up demand during off-peak (low-load) periods.
- **How it works:** Incentivize consumers to use electricity during low-demand hours through Time-of-Use (TOU) tariffs, off-peak rebates, or promoting the use of electric vehicles and storage heating at night.
- **Why it's done:** Increases the load factor, improves plant utilization, and reduces per-unit generation cost.

### 2.1.3 Load Shifting
- **Objective:** Move load from peak to off-peak periods without changing the total energy consumed.
- **How it works:** Encourages the use of energy storage systems (e.g., ice thermal storage for air conditioning, battery storage, pumped hydro) and shiftable industrial processes.
- **Why it's done:** Combines the benefits of peak clipping and valley filling.

### 2.1.4 Strategic Conservation
- **Objective:** Reduce overall energy consumption through efficiency improvements.
- **How it works:** Promote energy-efficient appliances (5-star rated), better building design, LED lighting, and energy audits.
- **Why it's done:** Permanent reduction in both peak and energy use; environmentally sustainable.

### 2.1.5 Strategic Load Growth
- **Objective:** Increase overall consumption in a way that flattens the load curve.
- **How it works:** Encourage use of electricity in place of fossil fuels (e.g., induction cooktops instead of LPG, electric vehicles), particularly during off-peak hours.
- **Why it's done:** Increases utility revenue while reducing fossil fuel dependence and emissions.

### 2.1.6 Flexible Load Shape
- **Objective:** Allow consumers to respond to real-time pricing or grid conditions.
- **How it works:** Demand Response (DR) programs where consumers voluntarily reduce or shift load in response to price signals or grid emergencies.
- **Why it works:** Provides reliability and flexibility to the grid operator.

## 2.2 Power Factor Improvement — Causes of Low PF

Common industrial loads causing low PF:
- **Induction Motors** (operate at 0.7–0.8 PF lagging) — biggest contributor.
- **Transformers** (magnetizing current has reactive component).
- **Arc Lamps and Fluorescent Tubes** (with conventional ballasts).
- **Welding Transformers, Induction Furnaces, Power Electronic Converters.**
- **Over-excited synchronous motors** (cause leading PF).
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** and other harmonic-generating loads.

## 2.3 Disadvantages of Low Power Factor

1. **Higher current draw** → Increased $I^2R$ losses in cables and transformers.
2. **Larger conductor size required** → Higher capital cost.
3. **Poor voltage regulation** → Voltage drops at the load end.
4. **Reduced capacity of transformers and alternators** → Equipment appears overloaded.
5. **Penalty by utility** → Most utilities charge PF penalty below 0.95–0.98 lag.
6. **Higher kVA demand charges** → Even though kW consumption is the same.

## 2.4 Methods of Power Factor Improvement

| Method | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Capacitor Banks** | Shunt capacitors supply leading reactive power to neutralize lagging reactive demand. | Most common; used at industrial PCC. |
| **Synchronous Condenser** | An over-excited synchronous motor running without mechanical load. Acts as a variable reactive source. | Large substations, bulk power factor correction. |
| **Phase Advancer** | An AC exciter mounted on the shaft of an induction motor; supplies exciting ampere-turns at slip frequency. | Used for large slip-ring induction motors. |
| **Capacitors with Motors** | Individual motors fitted with matched capacitors switched with the motor. | Distributed correction; for large individual motors. |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | Apparent power from P and Q | VA |
| 2 | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Power Factor definition | — |
| 3 | $P = V I \cos \phi$ | Real power (single-phase) | W |
| 4 | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos \phi$ | Real power (three-phase) | W |
| 5 | $Q = V I \sin \phi$ | Reactive power (single-phase) | VAR |
| 6 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \sin \phi$ | Reactive power (three-phase) | VAR |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cos \phi}$ | Line current (single-phase) | A |
| 8 | $I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos \phi}$ | Line current (three-phase) | A |
| 9 | $Q_c = P (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$ | Required capacitor rating (kVAR) | kVAR |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2 \pi f V^2}$ | Required capacitance (single-phase) | F |
| 11 | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2 \pi f V_L^2}$ (delta) or $\dfrac{Q_c}{2 \pi f (V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}$ (star) | Three-phase capacitor bank | F |
| 12 | $\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | DSM metric | — |
| 13 | $\text{Plant Capacity Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours}}$ | Utility efficiency | — |
| 14 | $\text{Diversity Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Max Demand of Group}}$ | DSM metric | > 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for KTU Examinations:** When computing required capacitor kVAR, always subtract $\tan \phi_2$ from $\tan \phi_1$ where $\phi_1$ is the **initial** (worse) PF angle and $\phi_2$ is the **target/improved** PF angle. Both angles are in **degrees** when using a calculator.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Utility Sector:** TOU tariffs in Kerala (KSEB) — peak hours (6 PM–10 PM) are charged at a premium; off-peak hours (10 PM–6 AM) are discounted. This is a direct DSM valley-filling incentive.
- **Industrial Plants:** A textile mill in Coimbatore installing a 500 kVAr capacitor bank to avoid ₹3 lakh/month PF penalty.
- **Renewable Integration:** DSM helps integrate solar generation (mid-day peak) by encouraging flexible load like EV charging and cold storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reduction in Line Current After PF Correction

**Given:** A single-phase load draws real power $P$ at voltage $V$ with initial PF $\cos \phi_1$.

**Step 1:** Initial line current (before correction):
$$I_1 = \frac{P}{V \cos \phi_1}$$

**Step 2:** After installing capacitor and improving PF to $\cos \phi_2$:
$$I_2 = \frac{P}{V \cos \phi_2}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage reduction in line current:
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{I_1 - I_2}{I_1} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the expressions:
$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{\dfrac{P}{V \cos \phi_1} - \dfrac{P}{V \cos \phi_2}}{\dfrac{P}{V \cos \phi_1}} \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{\cos \phi_1}{\cos \phi_2}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Meaning:** This percentage is also approximately the percentage reduction in $I^2R$ losses in the feeder cable — a direct energy-saving mechanism.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Capacitor kVAR Rating

**Given:** Load consumes $P$ kW at $\cos \phi_1$, target PF is $\cos \phi_2$.

**Step 1:** Initial reactive power demand:
$$Q_1 = P \tan \phi_1 \quad \text{(kVAR)}$$

**Step 2:** Desired reactive power after correction:
$$Q_2 = P \tan \phi_2 \quad \text{(kVAR)}$$

**Step 3:** The capacitor must supply the difference:
$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = P \tan \phi_1 - P \tan \phi_2$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{Q_c = P \,(\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2) \;\; \text{kVAR}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitance Value from kVAR Rating (3-Phase Delta Bank)

**Given:** Required capacitor bank rating $Q_c$ in kVAR at line voltage $V_L$ and frequency $f$.

**Step 1:** Reactive power per phase in a delta-connected bank:
$$Q_{\text{phase}} = \frac{Q_c}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Capacitor bank reactive power expression in terms of line voltage (for delta, phase voltage = line voltage):
$$Q_{\text{phase}} = \frac{V_L^2}{X_c} = V_L^2 \cdot (2 \pi f C)$$

**Step 3:** Equating:
$$2 \pi f C = \frac{Q_c}{3 V_L^2}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{C = \frac{Q_c}{6 \pi f V_L^2} \quad \text{(Farads, per phase, delta-connected)}}$$

For a **star-connected** bank, phase voltage is $V_L / \sqrt{3}$, leading to:
$$\boxed{C_{\text{star}} = \frac{Q_c}{2 \pi f V_L^2} \quad \text{(per phase)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, capacitors are nearly always connected in **delta** because they require a smaller capacitance value (by a factor of 3) and provide better harmonic immunity.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial induction motor load draws 100 A at 0.7 PF lagging.
- (a) Calculate the real power, reactive power, and apparent power.
- (b) Find the rating of the capacitor bank required to improve the PF to 0.95 lagging.
- (c) Find the new line current after correction.
- (d) Calculate the annual savings in copper losses if the cable resistance is 0.05 Ω per phase and the motor operates 4000 hours/year. Take cost of energy as ₹7/kWh.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Power Calculations:**

**Step 1:** Apparent power $S$:
$$S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 100 = 71,876 \, \text{VA} \approx 71.88 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Step 2:** Real power $P$:
$$P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos \phi_1 = 71,876 \times 0.7 = 50,313 \, \text{W} \approx 50.31 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3:** Reactive power $Q_1$:
$$Q_1 = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \sin \phi_1 = 71,876 \times \sin(\cos^{-1} 0.7)$$
$$\sin \phi_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.7^2} = \sqrt{0.51} = 0.7141$$
$$Q_1 = 71,876 \times 0.7141 = 51,326 \, \text{VAR} \approx 51.33 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Part (b) — Capacitor Bank Rating:**

**Step 1:** Find target reactive power $Q_2$ at PF = 0.95:
$$\tan \phi_1 = \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.7) = 1.0202$$
$$\tan \phi_2 = \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.95) = 0.3287$$

**Step 2:** Required capacitor kVAR:
$$Q_c = P(\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2) = 50.31 \times (1.0202 - 0.3287)$$
$$Q_c = 50.31 \times 0.6915 = 34.79 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 3:** Standard capacitor bank selected: **35 kVAR**.

**Part (c) — New Line Current After Correction:**

$$I_2 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos \phi_2} = \frac{50,313}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.95} = 73.7 \, \text{A}$$

**Reduction in current:** $\Delta I = 100 - 73.7 = 26.3 \, \text{A}$ (a 26.3% reduction).

**Part (d) — Annual Copper Loss Savings:**

**Step 1:** Initial 3-phase copper loss (per phase resistance $R = 0.05 \, \Omega$):
$$P_{\text{loss},1} = 3 I_1^2 R = 3 \times (100)^2 \times 0.05 = 1500 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2:** New 3-phase copper loss:
$$P_{\text{loss},2} = 3 I_2^2 R = 3 \times (73.7)^2 \times 0.05 = 814.5 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3:** Loss savings:
$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = 1500 - 814.5 = 685.5 \, \text{W} = 0.686 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 4:** Annual energy savings:
$$E_{\text{saved}} = 0.686 \times 4000 = 2742 \, \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 5:** Monetary savings:
$$\text{Savings} = 2742 \times 7 = \text{₹}19,194 \, \text{per year}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Six Classical DSM Techniques

The six DSM techniques modify the original load curve in distinct ways. The following block diagram maps each technique to its operational objective and load-shape impact.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Original Load Curve] --> B[Six DSM Techniques]
    B --> C1[Peak Clipping]
    B --> C2[Valley Filling]
    B --> C3[Load Shifting]
    B --> C4[Strategic Conservation]
    B --> C5[Strategic Load Growth]
    B --> C6[Flexible Load Shape]
    C1 --> D1[Direct Load Control, Ripple Control, Peak Pricing]
    C2 --> D2[TOU Tariff, Off-Peak Rebate, EV Charging at Night]
    C3 --> D3[Thermal Storage, Battery Storage, Process Shifting]
    C4 --> D4[5-Star Appliances, LED, Energy Audits, Building Retrofits]
    C5 --> D5[Electrification, EV Adoption, Induction Cooking]
    C6 --> D6[Real-Time Pricing, Demand Response, Smart Grids]
    D1 --> E[Modified Load Curve]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Improved Load Factor]
    F --> G[Reduced Generation Cost]
    F --> H[Reduced Peak Plant Investment]
    F --> I[Lower Carbon Emissions]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Factor Correction System

The following diagram maps the architecture of a typical industrial power factor correction system, showing signal flow from the load through measurement, control, and capacitor switching to the utility.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1[Power Network Side]
        U[Utility Grid 415V 3-Phase 50Hz]
    end
    subgraph S2[Industrial Load]
        L1[Induction Motors]
        L2[Transformers]
        L3[Lighting Ballasts]
        L4[Power Electronic Drives]
    end
    subgraph S3[Measurement and Control]
        M[CT and PT Sensors]
        PFC[APFC Controller]
    end
    subgraph S4[Capacitor Bank]
        K1[Capacitor Stage 1]
        K2[Capacitor Stage 2]
        K3[Capacitor Stage 3]
        K4[Capacitor Stage N]
    end
    U --> L1
    U --> L2
    U --> L3
    U --> L4
    L1 --> M
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    M --> PFC
    PFC --> K1
    PFC --> K2
    PFC --> K3
    PFC --> K4
    K1 --> U
    K2 --> U
    K3 --> U
    K4 --> U
    PFC -. Monitors .-> M
    PFC -. Switching Logic .-> K1
    PFC -. Switching Logic .-> K2
    PFC -. Switching Logic .-> K3
    PFC -. Switching Logic .-> K4
```

## 4.3 Power Triangle — Visual Representation of P, Q, S and PF

The power triangle is the foundational visual for power factor correction. Below is a Mermaid-based schematic representation of how the triangle shrinks when capacitors are added.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial State: Low PF] --> B[P constant Real Power kW]
    A --> C[Q1 large Reactive Power kVAR]
    A --> D[S1 large Apparent Power kVA]
    A --> E[phi1 large angle]
    B --> F[After Capacitor Addition]
    C --> F
    F --> G[P unchanged]
    F --> H[Q2 reduced]
    F --> I[S2 reduced]
    F --> J[phi2 smaller angle]
    H --> K[Q1 minus Qc equals Q2]
    I --> L[S2 less than S1]
    J --> M[PF improved to cos phi2]
```

## 4.4 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting PF Correction Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Load has low PF] --> Q1{What is the type of load?}
    Q1 -->|Steady, Inductive| Cap[Use Shunt Capacitor Bank]
    Q1 -->|Varying Load, Industrial| APFC[Use APFC Panel with Auto Switching]
    Q1 -->|Bulk Power Substation| Sync[Use Synchronous Condenser]
    Q1 -->|Large Slip-Ring Motor| PA[Use Phase Advancer]
    Cap --> R1[Most Economical, Easy to Install]
    APFC --> R2[Maintains Target PF Automatically]
    Sync --> R3[Adjustable, Used for Large Reactive Demand]
    PA --> R4[Improves PF of Individual Motor at Source]
    R1 --> End[Improved PF and Lower Losses]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Demand Side Management. List any four techniques of DSM. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Demand Side Management (DSM) refers to the planning, implementation, and monitoring of utility activities designed to encourage consumers to modify their level and pattern of electricity usage. The objective is to produce desired changes in the utility's load profile, reduce peak demand, and improve overall energy efficiency.

**Four Techniques of DSM:**
1. **Peak Clipping** — Reducing peak demand through direct load control.
2. **Valley Filling** — Building up off-peak demand using time-of-use tariffs.
3. **Load Shifting** — Shifting load from peak to off-peak periods using storage.
4. **Strategic Conservation** — Reducing overall energy consumption via energy-efficient equipment.

---

### Question 2: What is Power Factor? Why is Power Factor Improvement necessary in industrial installations? **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Power Factor is the cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current in an AC circuit. It is the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). A low power factor means that a large portion of the current drawn is reactive and does no useful work.

$$\text{PF} = \cos \phi = \frac{P}{S}$$

**Necessity of PF Improvement in Industries:**
1. **Reduces line current**, hence lowers $I^2R$ losses in cables and transformers.
2. **Avoids penalty** imposed by the electricity utility for operating below 0.95 lag.
3. **Improves voltage regulation** at the load end.
4. **Releases capacity** of existing transformers and alternators for additional useful load.
5. **Reduces kVA demand charges**, lowering monthly electricity bills.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Question A:** A three-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor load draws 80 A at a power factor of 0.72 lagging. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
- **(a)** Calculate the real power, reactive power, and apparent power drawn by the load. **[7 Marks]**
- **(b)** Determine the rating of the capacitor bank (in kVAR) required to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging. Also find the new line current after correction. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Line voltage $V_L = 400 \, \text{V}$, Frequency $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$
- Line current $I_L = 80 \, \text{A}$, Initial PF $\cos \phi_1 = 0.72$ lagging
- Target PF $\cos \phi_2 = 0.95$ lagging

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apparent Power $S$:**
$$S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 80 = 55,425.6 \, \text{VA} \approx 55.43 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Real Power $P$:**
$$P = S \cos \phi_1 = 55.43 \times 0.72 = 39.91 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Reactive Power $Q_1$:**
$$\sin \phi_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.72^2} = \sqrt{0.4816} = 0.6940$$
$$Q_1 = S \sin \phi_1 = 55.43 \times 0.6940 = 38.47 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Tangent Values:**
$$\tan \phi_1 = \frac{\sin \phi_1}{\cos \phi_1} = \frac{0.6940}{0.72} = 0.9639$$
$$\tan \phi_2 = \frac{\sqrt{1 - 0.95^2}}{0.95} = \frac{0.3122}{0.95} = 0.3287$$

**Step 2 — Required Capacitor kVAR:**
$$Q_c = P(\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2) = 39.91 \times (0.9639 - 0.3287) = 39.91 \times 0.6352 = 25.35 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 3 — New Apparent Power and Line Current:**
$$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos \phi_2} = \frac{39.91}{0.95} = 42.01 \, \text{kVA}$$
$$I_2 = \frac{S_2}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L} = \frac{42,010}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 60.64 \, \text{A}$$

**Final Results:**
- $S = 55.43 \, \text{kVA}$, $P = 39.91 \, \text{kW}$, $Q_1 = 38.47 \, \text{kVAR}$
- $Q_c = 25.35 \, \text{kVAR}$, $I_2 = 60.64 \, \text{A}$

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**Question B:**
- **(a)** Explain any four techniques of Demand Side Management with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
- **(b)** An industrial consumer draws a load of 200 kW at 0.65 power factor lagging. **[7 Marks]**
    - (i) Find the rating of the capacitor bank to improve the power factor to 0.90 lagging.
    - (ii) What is the new kVA demand after correction?
    - (iii) Calculate the saving in kVA charges if the rate is ₹350 per kVA per month.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Four DSM Techniques:**

**1. Peak Clipping:**
Peak clipping reduces the system peak load. It is achieved through direct load control where the utility remotely switches off non-essential loads (e.g., water heaters, AC compressors) during high-demand periods.
*Example:* A utility sheds 5 MW of residential AC load between 6 PM and 9 PM on hot summer days.

**2. Valley Filling:**
Valley filling increases the load during off-peak hours. The utility offers lower tariffs at night to encourage consumption.
*Example:* EV charging stations offering 60% discounted rates between 11 PM and 6 AM.

**3. Load Shifting:**
Load shifting moves energy use from peak to off-peak periods without changing the total energy consumed. This is done using energy storage systems.
*Example:* A shopping mall using ice thermal storage — making ice at night and using it for daytime air conditioning.

**4. Strategic Conservation:**
Strategic conservation reduces the overall energy consumption through efficiency upgrades.
*Example:* Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) promoting 5-star inverter ACs and LED bulbs through subsidized replacement programs.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 200 \, \text{kW}$, $\cos \phi_1 = 0.65$ lagging, $\cos \phi_2 = 0.90$ lagging
- Tariff = ₹350 per kVA per month

**Step 1 — Find $\tan \phi_1$ and $\tan \phi_2$:**
$$\sin \phi_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.65^2} = \sqrt{0.5775} = 0.7599$$
$$\tan \phi_1 = \frac{0.7599}{0.65} = 1.1691$$
$$\tan \phi_2 = \frac{\sqrt{1 - 0.9^2}}{0.9} = \frac{0.4359}{0.9} = 0.4843$$

**Step 2 — Required Capacitor Bank:**
$$Q_c = P(\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2) = 200 \times (1.1691 - 0.4843) = 200 \times 0.6848 = 136.96 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 3 — New kVA Demand:**
$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos \phi_1} = \frac{200}{0.65} = 307.69 \, \text{kVA}$$
$$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos \phi_2} = \frac{200}{0.90} = 222.22 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly Savings in kVA Charges:**
$$\Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 307.69 - 222.22 = 85.47 \, \text{kVA}$$
$$\text{Monthly Savings} = 85.47 \times 350 = \text{₹}29,914.5$$

**Final Results:**
- Capacitor bank required: $Q_c = 136.96 \, \text{kVAR}$
- New kVA demand: $S_2 = 222.22 \, \text{kVA}$
- Monthly savings: ₹29,914.5

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\sqrt{3}$ in 3-phase calculations** — Many students write $S = VI$ for three-phase, which is a sure way to lose 3-4 marks. Always remember: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$.
> 2. **Confusing line voltage and phase voltage** — In star connection, $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. In delta, $V_{ph} = V_L$. This is critical in capacitance calculations.
> 3. **Using $\sin \phi$ incorrectly in the formula $Q_c = P \tan \phi$** — Some students incorrectly write $Q_c = P \sin \phi$ which gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Not mentioning the "lagging" / "leading"** explicitly in PF problems — Examiners in KTU deduct half a mark if this is missing.
> 5. **DSM theory answers should include an example** — A bare definition without an example often gets only 1-1.5 marks out of 7 in a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DSM is a consumer-side activity** that modifies the load profile; the **six classical techniques** are Peak Clipping, Valley Filling, Load Shifting, Strategic Conservation, Strategic Load Growth, and Flexible Load Shape.
- **Load Factor** = Average Load / Peak Load. A higher load factor indicates better DSM performance and lower per-unit cost.
- **Power Factor** is the cosine of the angle between voltage and current. For industrial consumers, it should ideally be **≥ 0.95 lagging**.
- **Low PF** results in higher line current, larger $I^2R$ losses, poor voltage regulation, lower equipment utilization, and utility penalty.
- **Capacitor banks** are the most common and economical method of PF correction. They are connected in **shunt (parallel)** with the load.
- **The key formula** for required capacitor kVAR is $Q_c = P(\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$, where $\phi_1$ is the initial (worse) PF angle and $\phi_2$ is the target (improved) PF angle.
- **Synchronous Condensers** are over-excited synchronous motors acting as variable reactive power sources; used in large substations.
- **Phase Advancers** are AC exciters mounted on the shaft of slip-ring induction motors; they supply exciting ampere-turns at slip frequency to improve the motor's own PF.
- **Three-phase capacitor banks** are usually connected in **delta** because they require a smaller capacitance per phase and offer better harmonic tolerance.
- **In India, the standard supply frequency is 50 Hz** and standard 3-phase voltage is **415 V**; single-phase is **230 V**.
- **PF improvement also reduces cable copper losses** by a factor of $(\text{old PF} / \text{new PF})^2$ — this is a hidden bonus in energy cost reduction.
- **Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panels** use contactor-based or thyristor-switched capacitor stages to maintain the target PF dynamically.
- **Harmonics caution:** In plants with significant non-linear loads (VFDs, UPS), de-tuned or harmonic-filter reactor banks are used in series with capacitors to avoid resonance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples, Ancillary Services: Introduction of Ancillary Services - Types of Ancillary Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Numerical Examples in Energy Management — Foundational Concepts

### 1.1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Management numerical analysis** refers to the systematic application of economic and engineering formulas — such as **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Return on Investment (ROI)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Cost of Saved Energy (CSE)**, and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — to quantitatively evaluate the financial feasibility, profitability, and long-term sustainability of energy conservation projects (ECPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Numerical problems in Module 2 typically carry **7 to 14 marks** in End Semester Examinations (ESE) and are drawn from the economic analysis of energy-saving investments. Students are expected to compute, compare, and justify a project on the basis of computed indices.

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Savings Bank Account" Model

Imagine you have ₹1,00,000 (the **Capital Investment**) and you deposit it into a special "Energy Savings Bank" that pays you back through **reduced electricity bills**. The question is: *How many months (or years) will it take for the bank to "return" your principal?* That duration is the **Simple Payback Period**.

Similarly:
- **NPV** is like asking: *"If I adjust the future savings for today's rupee value, am I still making a profit?"*
- **ROI** answers: *"For every ₹100 invested, how many rupees do I get back annually?"*

This financial lens helps engineers transform a kilowatt-hour (kWh) saved into a **rupee earned** — bridging the gap between **technical efficiency** and **economic viability**.

---

## 1.2 Ancillary Services — Introduction

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

> **Ancillary Services** are the **support functions and auxiliary operations** in a power system that are **necessary to support the transmission of electric power from generators to consumers** while maintaining **reliable operation**, **power quality**, **system stability**, and **security** of the grid.

These services are **distinct from the basic energy generation, transmission, and distribution functions** and are required to ensure that the **demand–supply balance is maintained at every instant** under both normal and contingency conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Ancillary services are typically procured by the **System Operator (SO)** — e.g., the **Load Despatch Centre (LDC)** in India (POSOCO, now part of Grid-India) — from **Independent Power Producers (IPPs)**, **Discoms**, or **Captive Generators** through competitive markets or regulatory mechanisms.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Air Traffic Control" Model

Think of the **electricity grid** as a busy international airport:
- **Generators** are the **airplanes** (supplying "passengers" = electricity).
- **Transmission lines** are the **runways and air corridors**.
- **Load (consumers)** are the **passengers waiting at terminals**.

For the airport to function safely, you need more than just planes and runways. You also need:
- **Air Traffic Controllers** → *Frequency Regulation & Voltage Control*
- **Emergency Landing Slots** → *Spinning & Non-Spinning Reserves*
- **Backup Power for Tower Lighting** → *Black Start Capability*
- **Rerouting Pilots** → *Network Redispatch & Congestion Management*

These are the **ancillary services** — invisible yet indispensable. Without them, the grid "crashes" just as an airport would without ATC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Grid Metric:** The **Loss of Load Probability (LOLP)** should not exceed **0.1 days/year** and **Energy Not Served (ENS)** should remain minimal — both directly governed by the quality and quantity of ancillary services deployed.

---

## 1.3 Why Ancillary Services Are Indispensable

| Grid Phenomenon | Required Ancillary Service | Consequence of Absence |
|-----------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Sudden load surge | Primary Frequency Response | Frequency collapse, load shedding |
| Reactive power deficit | Voltage Support | Voltage sag, equipment damage |
| Generator trip | Spinning Reserve | Blackout |
| Total grid failure | Black Start | Prolonged outage |
| Transmission congestion | Redispatch | Market inefficiency |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Economic Analysis of Energy Projects — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP)

**Definition:** The time required for the cumulative savings (or net cash inflows) from an energy conservation project to **equal the initial capital investment**.

**Underlying Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning):**

1. Determine the **total capital cost** of the energy project (₹).
2. Estimate the **annual monetary savings** (₹/year) achieved through reduced energy consumption.
3. Compute the **ratio** of investment to annual savings.
4. The result is the number of years needed to recover the investment in undiscounted terms.

**Key Engineering Insight:** SPP is a **risk indicator** — a shorter payback means lower exposure to market/technology risk.

### 2.1.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

**Definition:** The difference between the **present value of cash inflows** and the **present value of cash outflows** over the project lifetime, discounted at a chosen rate (typically the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital, WACC**).

**Underlying Logic:**

1. Future cash flows are **time-devalued** using a discount factor.
2. The **discount factor** at year $n$ is computed using the MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
3. Sum all discounted savings.
4. Subtract initial investment. If positive → **project is viable**.

**Why It Matters:** Unlike SPP, NPV accounts for the **time value of money** and is the **gold standard** in capital budgeting.

### 2.1.3 Return on Investment (ROI)

**Definition:** A **percentage-based profitability metric** showing the annual return relative to the invested capital.

**Engineering Use:** Quick comparative tool for ranking **multiple competing energy projects** under identical risk profiles.

### 2.1.4 Cost of Saved Energy (CSE)

**Definition:** A **unique metric in energy management** that expresses the unit cost of conserved energy, enabling direct comparison with the **unit cost of purchased electricity**.

If **CSE < Cost of Purchased Electricity** → the project is **economically justified**.

---

## 2.2 Ancillary Services — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Operational Need for Ancillary Services

The grid must obey three **fundamental physical laws** at every instant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Power Balance:} \quad & \sum P_{\text{generation}} = \sum P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{losses}} \\
\text{2. Frequency Stability:} \quad & f = 50 \text{ Hz (India)} \Rightarrow \Delta f = 0 \\
\text{3. Voltage Stability:} \quad & V_{\text{bus}} \in [0.95, 1.05] \text{ p.u.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Any deviation in load, generation, or network topology must be **counterbalanced by ancillary services** within fractions of a second to minutes.

### 2.2.2 Hierarchical Classification of Ancillary Services

**A. Frequency-Related Services (Active Power Support)**

1. **Primary Frequency Response (PFR)** — Activated within **seconds** by governor action. Provides rapid injection/absorption of active power proportional to frequency deviation (typically 4–5% droop).
2. **Secondary Frequency Response (SFR) / Automatic Generation Control (AGC)** — Activated within **seconds to minutes** by the system operator to restore frequency to nominal value and maintain scheduled tie-line exchanges.
3. **Tertiary Frequency Response / Contingency Reserve** — Manual re-dispatch within **minutes to an hour** to free up secondary reserves.

**B. Voltage-Related Services (Reactive Power Support)**

1. **Reactive Power Compensation** from generators, **synchronous condensers**, **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs)**, and **STATCOMs**.
2. **Tap Changer Coordination** on transformers.
3. **Shunt Reactor / Capacitor Switching** for voltage profile management.

**C. Operating Reserves**

1. **Spinning Reserve** — Synchronized, unloaded generating capacity ready to ramp up within **10 minutes**.
2. **Non-Spinning (Supplemental) Reserve** — Off-line but can be started and synchronized within **10–30 minutes**.
3. **Replacement / Backup Reserve** — Activated after **1–2 hours** to replenish spinning reserve.

**D. Restoration Services**

1. **Black Start Capability** — Ability of a generating station to start **without external power supply** and energize the transmission network in stages.

**E. Other Network Services**

1. **Energy Imbalance Management**
2. **Loss Compensation**
3. **System Redispatch (Congestion Management)**
4. **Frequency-Sensitive Demand Response**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{S}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 2 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{S}{I} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % |
| 3 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \dfrac{S_n}{(1+i)^n} - I$ | Net Present Value | ₹ (currency) |
| 4 | $\text{CSE} = \dfrac{I \times CRF}{\Delta E}$ | Cost of Saved Energy | ₹/kWh |
| 5 | $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta P_f = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \times \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Primary Frequency Response droop relation | p.u. |
| 7 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{\Delta P_L}{D + \dfrac{1}{R}}$ | Steady-state frequency deviation | Hz |
| 8 | $Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | Reactive power compensation | kVAr |
| 9 | $I_{\text{new}} = I_{\text{old}} \times \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$ | Line current after PF correction | A |
| 10 | $\text{Annual Savings} = \Delta E \times C_e$ | Energy cost savings | ₹/year |

**Where:**
- $I$ = Initial Investment (₹)
- $S$ = Annual Savings (₹/year)
- $N$ = Project lifetime (years)
