\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_i, H_i) - \lambda_p \!\left( \sum P_i - P_D \right) - \lambda_h \!\left( \sum H_i - H_D \right)
$$

*(KKT first-order conditions — 3 marks)*

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_i} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial P_i} = \lambda_p
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H_i} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial H_i} = \lambda_h
$$

For the quadratic model $F_i = \alpha_i P_i^2 + \beta_i P_i + \gamma_i H_i^2 + \delta_i H_i + \epsilon_i$, the equalities yield linear systems whose solution is:

$$
P_i = \frac{\lambda_p - \beta_i}{2\alpha_i}, \qquad H_i = \frac{\lambda_h - \delta_i}{2\gamma_i}
$$

The values of $\lambda_p, \lambda_h$ are obtained from the demand equations (as derived in Section 3.2). At boundary-corner solutions, the FOR constraints become active and the IC equalities hold only for the unconstrained units.

---

**(b)** A single cogeneration unit has a steam-balance relation $P + H \le 100$ MW-eq. with $P^{\max} = 70$ MW and $H^{\max} = 60$ units. Plot the **feasible operating region** and identify all four (or three) vertices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(Theoretical boundary identification — 3 marks)*

The FOR is bounded by:
1. $P + H = 100$ (steam cap)
2. $P = 70$ (condenser cap)
3. $H = 60$ (extraction cap)
4. $P \ge 0, H \ge 0$

*(Vertex calculation — 2 marks)*

- $V_1 = (0, 0)$ — origin
- $V_2 = (0, H^{\max}) = (0, 60)$ — point A on $H$-axis
- $V_3 = (P^{\max}, S_e^{\max}-P^{\max}) = (70, 30)$ — point B (intersection of steam-cap line and $P = 70$)
- $V_4 = (P^{\max}, 0) = (70, 0)$ — point C on $P$-axis

Note that $V_2 = (0, 60)$ lies *below* the steam cap line $(0+60=60 < 100)$, so the polygon has **five** effective vertices if we include the intersection of the steam-cap line with the $H$-axis at $(0,100)$; however, since $H$ cannot exceed $60$, $V_2=(0,60)$ is the binding vertex.

*(Plot description — 2 marks)*

The FOR is a **trapezoid** with vertices listed in order: $(0,0) \to (0,60) \to (70,30) \to (70,0) \to (0,0)$.

| Vertex | Co-ordinates | Active Constraint |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| Origin | $(0, 0)$ | $P=0, H=0$ |
| $V_2$ | $(0, 60)$ | $P=0$, $H=H^{\max}$ |
| $V_3$ | $(70, 30)$ | $P=P^{\max}$, steam cap |
| $V_4$ | $(70, 0)$ | $P=P^{\max}$, $H=0$ |

Edge lengths:
- Edge $V_2V_3$ length $= \sqrt{70^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{5800} \approx 76.16$ MW-eq.
- Edge $V_3V_4$ length $= 30$ units of $H$.
- Edge $V_4V_1$ length $= 70$ MW.
- Edge $V_1V_2$ length $= 60$ units of $H$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting to quote $\lambda_p$ and $\lambda_h$** — 1 mark is reserved for the multiplier values; omitting them costs a full grade band.
> 2. **Skipping FOR verification** — if a numerical answer violates $P_i + H_i \le C_i$, the **entire dispatch solution is marked wrong**, even if the algebra is flawless.
> 3. **Treating cogeneration as two independent dispatches** — this double-counts fuel. The IC equalities *must* emerge from the **single 2-constraint Lagrangian**, not from solving two separate single-constraint problems.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — always state "₹/MWh" for $\lambda_p$ and "₹/heat-unit" for $\lambda_h$. Mixing units silently loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring binding constraints** — when a solution lies on the FOR boundary, you must *say so* explicitly and explain which constraint replaced the IC equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist as your **last 5-minute revision** before entering the exam hall.

- ✅ Cogeneration = simultaneous production of **electrical power** AND **useful heat** from a single fuel input.
- ✅ The two canonical architectures are **back-pressure** (fixed $P/H$) and **extraction-condensing** (variable $P/H$).
- ✅ The **Feasible Operating Region (FOR)** is a **convex polygon** in the $P$–$H$ plane bounded by the steam cap, condenser cap, extraction cap, and the axes.
- ✅ The combined-output constraint $P + H \le S_e^{\max}$ is the **steam-balance line** — it is the diagonal of the FOR.
- ✅ Economic dispatch with cogeneration uses a **Lagrangian with TWO multipliers**: $\lambda_p$ and $\lambda_h$.
- ✅ The optimality conditions are $\partial F_i/\partial P_i = \lambda_p$ and $\partial F_i/\partial H_i = \lambda_h$ for all interior units.
- ✅ For the quadratic cost model, these reduce to **linear systems**: $P_i = (\lambda_p - \beta_i)/(2\alpha_i)$ and $H_i = (\lambda_h - \delta_i)/(2\gamma_i)$.
- ✅ **Always verify the FOR after solving.** Clip any variable that violates a boundary, recompute the relevant $\lambda$, and re-check.
- ✅ $\lambda_p$ is reported in **₹/MWh**; $\lambda_h$ in **₹/heat-unit** — never omit units.
- ✅ Overall CHP efficiency reaches **70 %–85 %**, compared with **35 %–45 %** for separate heat and power generation — the central economic argument for cogeneration.
- ✅ In a **back-pressure** turbine, $P$ and $H$ are *coupled by a fixed ratio*; in an **extraction-condensing** turbine, they are *decoupled within the FOR*.
- ✅ KTU numerical problems test **two units**, two quadratic cost functions, and require the full quadruple $(P_1^\star, H_1^\star, P_2^\star, H_2^\star)$ plus total cost.
- ✅ When asked for a "sketch", always label **all four (or more) vertices** and indicate the **active constraint** on each edge.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Cycle Electricity Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Combined Cycle Electricity Generation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Combined Cycle Electricity Generation (CCEG)** is an advanced thermodynamic configuration in which two distinct heat engines are operated in series on the *same heat source*, thereby converting a single fuel input into mechanical (and subsequently electrical) work with a substantially higher overall thermal efficiency than either cycle could achieve alone. The two cycles employed are the **Brayton Cycle** (driving a Gas Turbine) and the **Rankine Cycle** (driving a Steam Turbine). The exhausted hot gases from the Gas Turbine (GT) — which still retain a large fraction of their original thermal energy at temperatures typically between **450 °C and 600 °C** — are routed through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**. The HRSG acts as a waste-heat boiler that converts water into superheated steam, which is then expanded through a Steam Turbine (ST) to generate additional electricity. Because the second cycle "recovers" what would otherwise be waste heat, the overall plant efficiency of modern combined cycle power plants routinely reaches **55 % to 64 % (LHV basis)**, compared to roughly **35 % to 42 %** for a conventional open-cycle gas turbine and only **33 % to 40 %** for a subcritical coal-fired Rankine plant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Combined Cycle Generation is a direct application of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the principle of *cascade utilization of heat*. It is classified as a **Cogeneration / CHP** (Combined Heat and Power) variant in the broader sense because it converts fuel energy into two different forms of useful work output (mechanical shaft work + steam enthalpy recovery). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Relay Race Imagine two runners in a relay race. Runner 1 (the **Gas Turbine**) sprints very fast with the fuel's energy but hands over the baton while still having considerable kinetic energy left. Instead of letting this energy dissipate on the track (as in a simple open cycle), Runner 1 passes the baton to Runner 2 (the **Steam Turbine**), who uses that remaining energy to finish the race. - **Runner 1** = Gas Turbine (high-temperature, high-power-density cycle). - **The Baton (residual energy)** = Hot exhaust gas (≈ 60 % of fuel energy is still available). - **Runner 2** = Steam Turbine (lower-temperature cycle, perfect for picking up the "leftover" heat). - **The Track (where energy is lost)** = Condenser + Stack. In a *simple* gas turbine plant, that baton is simply dropped at the exhaust stack — wasted. Combined Cycle *catches* the baton and converts it into additional electrical megawatts. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range (Modern CCGT) | |---|---| | Net Plant Efficiency (LHV) | **55 % – 64 %** | | Net Plant Efficiency (HHV) | **52 % – 60 %** | | Gas Turbine Exhaust Temperature | **450 °C – 620 °C** | | HRSG Steam Pressure (Triple Pressure) | Up to **160 bar / 565 °C** | | CO₂ Emissions | **< 365 g/kWh** (best-in-class) | | Plant Footprint | Compact, ≈ **1/5** of coal plant per MW | > [!NOTE] > The acronym **CCGT** (Combined Cycle Gas Turbine) is the standard industry designation. In KTU textbooks, this is sometimes called a **Natural Gas Combined Cycle (NGCC)** plant when fuelled by natural gas. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Constituent Cycles — Conceptual Logic The combined cycle is essentially a **Topping + Bottoming** arrangement: 1. **Topping Cycle (Brayton / Gas Turbine)** - Operates between the highest temperature ($T_3$, Turbine Inlet Temperature) and the gas turbine exhaust temperature ($T_4$). - Receives fresh fuel (natural gas, syngas, or light distillate oil). - The exhaust gas leaving the gas turbine is the *heat source* for the bottoming cycle. 2. **Bottoming Cycle (Rankine / Steam Turbine)** - Operates between the HRSG steam temperature and the condenser temperature. - Has *no separate combustor* — entirely waste-heat driven. - Rejects heat to the condenser at the lowest temperature in the entire plant. > [!NOTE] > **Why a Topping–Bottoming order?** Because the **Carnot efficiency** of a heat engine increases with the *average temperature* of heat addition. The high-temperature Brayton cycle adds heat at the highest available temperature, so it must logically run *first*. The lower-temperature Rankine cycle then scavenges the remaining heat. Reversing the order would make the bottoming cycle's condenser *hotter* than its heat source — thermodynamically impossible. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic - **Step 1 — Compression:** Ambient air is drawn into the gas turbine compressor and compressed (typically to **15–30 bar**). - **Step 2 — Combustion:** Compressed air mixes with fuel in the combustion chamber and burns continuously, raising the temperature to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** of **1300 °C – 1600 °C** in modern F-class and H-class machines. - **Step 3 — Expansion (GT):** Hot gases expand through the gas turbine, producing shaft work. About **2/3 of the shaft work** is consumed internally to drive the air compressor; the remaining **1/3** is the *net* gas turbine output. - **Step 4 — Heat Recovery:** Exhaust gases (still at **450 °C – 620 °C**) enter the HRSG. Here, they flow across banks of tubes carrying feedwater, evaporating it and superheating it without any additional fuel. - **Step 5 — Steam Expansion (ST):** The superheated steam is expanded through a multi-stage Steam Turbine, producing additional electrical power. - **Step 6 — Condensation:** Spent steam is condensed in a surface condenser (cooled by sea water, cooling towers, or air-cooled condensers) and the condensate is pumped back to the HRSG. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-exam ready**. Memorize the form, the variables, and the units. The vertical bar $\vert$ is used instead of $\mid$ to denote absolute value in exam scripts. | # | Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{GT} = \dfrac{W_{T,GT} - W_{C}}{Q_{in,GT}}$ | Gas Turbine (Brayton) thermal efficiency | $W_{T,GT}$ = Turbine work, $W_{C}$ = Compressor work, $Q_{in,GT}$ = Fuel heat input (kW) | | 2 | $\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{W_{T,ST}}{Q_{in,HRSG}}$ | Steam Turbine (Rankine) cycle efficiency | $W_{T,ST}$ = ST shaft work, $Q_{in,HRSG}$ = Heat recovered in HRSG (kW) | | 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W_{net,GT} + W_{net,ST}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | **Combined cycle overall efficiency** | Total electrical output ÷ Total fuel input | | 4 | $Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times GCV$ | Fuel energy input rate | $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ in kg/s, $GCV$ in kJ/kg | | 5 | $Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{3} - T_{4})$ | Heat available in GT exhaust | $c_{p,g}$ ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**, $T_3$ = TIT, $T_4$ = Exhaust temp | | 6 | $Q_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{4} - T_{stack})$ | Heat actually transferred to steam in HRSG | $T_{stack}$ ≈ **100 °C – 130 °C** (pinch point limitation) | | 7 | $W_{ST} = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{2})$ | Steam turbine power output | $h_1$ = Steam inlet enthalpy, $h_2$ = Steam exit enthalpy (kJ/kg) | | 8 | $Heat\ Ratio = \dfrac{Q_{HRSG}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Fraction of fuel heat entering HRSG | Typically **0.45 – 0.55** | | 9 | $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{HRSG}}{(h_{1} - h_{fw})}$ | Steam mass flow rate from HRSG | $h_{fw}$ = Feedwater enthalpy at HRSG inlet (kJ/kg) | | 10 | $SFC = \dfrac{3600 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{net}}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption (kg/kWh) | Lower is better; typical CCGT: **0.18 – 0.22 kg/kWh** | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Utility-scale power generation:** CCGT is the **dominant technology** for new fossil-fuel plants worldwide because of its high efficiency and rapid ramping capability (ideal for peaking and load-following). - **Cogeneration / CHP integration:** The low-pressure steam extracted from the ST can be used for district heating, desalination, or industrial process steam, pushing total *utilization efficiency* above **80 %**. - **Renewable hybridization:** Combined cycle plants are being retrofitted to burn **green hydrogen** or **ammonia** to decarbonize the thermal generation sector. - **District cooling & desalination:** The condenser heat rejection can drive **Multi-Effect Distillation (MED)** or **Thermal Vapour Compression (TVC)** desalination plants. > [!NOTE] > In KTU answer sheets, always state the **LHV (Lower Heating Value) basis** when quoting efficiency values. Indian industry standards such as IS 13955 and CEA guidelines conventionally use **GCV (HHV)** for indigenous coal, but **LHV (NCV)** for natural gas and imported fuels. Mark loss occurs when students mix the two bases. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Overall Combined Cycle Efficiency We start from First Law energy balance on the *whole plant* taken as a control volume. **Step 1 — Define the total fuel heat input.** $$ Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV \quad [\text{kW}] $$ where $GCV$ is the gross calorific value of the fuel in $\text{kJ/kg}$ and $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ is the mass flow rate of fuel in $\text{kg/s}$. This is the only energy stream *entering* the plant boundary from outside (neglecting air enthalpy, which is part of the working fluid cycle). **Step 2 — Write the net gas turbine work output.** $$ W_{net,GT} = W_{T,GT} - W_{C,GT} $$ The gas turbine produces gross work $W_{T,GT}$ by expanding the combustion products. A portion $W_{C,GT}$ of this work is parasitically consumed by the air compressor mounted on the same shaft. The *net* work is the electrical equivalent available at the GT generator terminals. **Step 3 — Write the energy balance across the HRSG.** The exhaust gas enters the HRSG at $T_4$ (≈ 550 °C) and leaves the stack at $T_{stack}$ (≈ 120 °C). The heat transferred to the working fluid of the steam cycle is: $$ Q_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{4} - T_{stack}) $$ The text row above each formula already states the logic, so here we proceed: this heat raises the feedwater from liquid state to superheated steam. The total steam mass flow produced in the HRSG is therefore: $$ \dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{HRSG}}{h_{1} - h_{fw}} $$ where $h_1$ is the superheated steam enthalpy at ST inlet and $h_{fw}$ is the feedwater enthalpy at economizer inlet. **Step 4 — Net steam turbine work output.** $$ W_{net,ST} = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot \eta_{m,ST} \cdot \eta_{g,ST} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{2}) - W_{pump} $$ For simplicity in board calculations, pump work is neglected ($\approx$ 1 % of $W_{ST}$), and mechanical–electrical efficiencies are lumped into an overall ST efficiency $\eta_{ST,overall}$: $$ W_{net,ST} \approx \dot{m}_{s} \cdot \eta_{ST,overall} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{2}) $$ **Step 5 — Combine for total plant output.** $$ P_{net} = W_{net,GT} + W_{net,ST} $$ **Step 6 — Define overall plant thermal efficiency.** The overall plant efficiency is the ratio of *useful electrical output* to *fuel energy input*: $$ \eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W_{net,GT} + W_{net,ST}}{Q_{fuel}} $$ **Step 7 — Express in terms of individual cycle efficiencies and heat ratio.** Let $\eta_{GT} = W_{net,GT} / Q_{fuel}$ and $\eta_{ST} = W_{net,ST} / Q_{HRSG}$. Also define the **heat ratio** as $k = Q_{HRSG} / Q_{fuel}$ (the fraction of fuel heat recovered by the steam cycle). Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{overall} &= \eta_{GT} + \dfrac{W_{net,ST}}{Q_{fuel}} \\ &= \eta_{GT} + \dfrac{Q_{HRSG}}{Q_{fuel}} \cdot \dfrac{W_{net,ST}}{Q_{HRSG}} \\ &= \eta_{GT} + k \cdot \eta_{ST} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** for combined cycle efficiency. It is essential to memorize. > [!IMPORTANT] > The plus sign is the heart of combined cycle economics: the efficiencies *add* (weighted by the heat ratio), they do not multiply. A 35 % gas turbine combined with a 33 % steam cycle and a heat ratio of 0.50 yields: > $\eta_{overall} = 0.35 + (0.50 \times 0.33) = 0.35 + 0.165 = 0.515$ → **51.5 %** ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A CCGT plant uses natural gas with GCV = **50 MJ/kg**. The gas turbine produces a net electrical output of **120 MW** at an efficiency of **38 %**. The HRSG recovers **48 %** of the fuel heat input, and the steam turbine operates at an efficiency of **34 %** (based on HRSG heat input). Compute: (a) the fuel consumption rate in kg/s, (b) the steam turbine power output, and (c) the overall plant efficiency. ### Solution **(a) Fuel heat input rate:** $$ Q_{fuel} = \dfrac{W_{net,GT}}{\eta_{GT}} = \dfrac{120{,}000 \text{ kW}}{0.38} = 315{,}789.5 \text{ kW} \approx 315.8 \text{ MW} $$ Fuel mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{Q_{fuel}}{GCV} = \dfrac{315{,}789.5 \text{ kJ/s}}{50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 6.316 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(b) Steam turbine power output:** Heat available in HRSG: $$ Q_{HRSG} = k \cdot Q_{fuel} = 0.48 \times 315.8 \text{ MW} = 151.6 \text{ MW} $$ Steam turbine power: $$ W_{net,ST} = \eta_{ST} \cdot Q_{HRSG} = 0.34 \times 151.6 = 51.5 \text{ MW} $$ **(c) Overall plant efficiency:** $$ \eta_{overall} = \eta_{GT} + k \cdot \eta_{ST} = 0.38 + (0.48 \times 0.34) = 0.38 + 0.1632 = 0.5432 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} = 54.32 \,\%} $$ **Check by direct ratio:** $$ \eta_{overall} = \dfrac{120 + 51.5}{315.8} = \dfrac{171.5}{315.8} = 0.5431 = 54.31 \,\% \;\; \checkmark $$ The two methods agree within rounding error — confirming the canonical formula. > [!NOTE] > The improvement over the gas turbine alone is $\Delta\eta = 54.32 - 38.00 = 16.32$ percentage points. This is a **43 % relative increase** in fuel-to-electricity conversion efficiency — the single largest economic justification for adopting combined cycle technology. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Trade-offs | Configuration | TIT (°C) | Steam Pressure | Net Efficiency | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Simple GT** (no ST) | 1300 | — | 35 – 38 % | Peaking, remote sites | | **CC — Single Pressure HRSG** | 1300 | 60 bar | 48 – 51 % | Industrial CHP | | **CC — Dual Pressure HRSG** | 1400 | 90 / 12 bar | 52 – 56 % | Utility base load | | **CC — Triple Pressure + Reheat** | 1500+ | 160 / 30 / 5 bar | 58 – 60 % | World-class F-class | | **CC — H-class + 3PR + Duct Firing** | 1600 | 165 bar | 60 – 64 % | Latest GE 9HA, Siemens SGT-9000HL | | **CC + Carbon Capture (CCS-ready)** | 1500 | 100 bar | 49 – 53 % (net) | Future decarbonized fleets | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Combined Cycle Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture The diagram below is a **functional Mermaid block diagram** that traces both energy and working-fluid streams from fuel input to electrical output and waste-heat rejection. This is the most exam-relevant representation, as KTU frequently asks students to "draw the block diagram of a combined cycle plant and label all major components." ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Ambient Air
T0 = 25 C] --> B1[Air Compressor
Wc in kW] B1 --> C1[Combustion Chamber
Fuel in] C1 --> D1[Gas Turbine
Wt gross] D1 --> E1[GT Generator
Wnet GT in MW] D1 -- hot exhaust
T4 450 620 C --> F1[Heat Recovery
Steam Generator HRSG] F1 -- superheated steam
h1 --> G1[Steam Turbine
h1 to h2] G1 --> H1[ST Generator
Wnet ST in MW] H1 --> I1[Surface Condenser
Tcond 30 40 C] I1 --> J1[Condensate Pump
W pump] J1 --> F1 F1 -- flue gas
Tstack 100 130 C --> K1[Stack
to atmosphere] C1 -- Q fuel in --> X1[Fuel Supply
GCV MJ per kg] X1 --> C1 E1 --> Y1[AC Bus
Net Power Output] H1 --> Y1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **air and combustion-gas stream** runs along the upper loop (Compressor → Combustor → Gas Turbine → HRSG → Stack). - The **steam–water loop** runs along the lower loop (HRSG → Steam Turbine → Condenser → Pump → HRSG). - The two loops are **thermally coupled** at the HRSG only — they do *not* mix their working fluids. - Electrical outputs from both the GT and ST generators converge on a common AC bus. ## 4.2 T-s Diagram of the Combined Cycle (Schematic Description) Although a strict T-s diagram of a combined cycle has *two* superimposed cycles, the key features that examiners expect are: - **Brayton loop (1–2–3–4–1):** $1 \to 2$ = isentropic compression; $2 \to 3$ = isobaric heat addition in combustor; $3 \to 4$ = isentropic expansion in GT; $4 \to 1$ = exhaust cooling in HRSG (not isobaric in reality — a temperature glide). - **Rankine loop (a–b–c–d–a):** $a \to b$ = feedwater heating; $b \to c$ = evaporation + superheat in HRSG; $c \to d$ = isentropic expansion in ST; $d \to a$ = condensation. - The two cycles share **no state point in common**; they are linked only by the HRSG's tube-bank heat exchanger. > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners accept either a single superimposed T-s sketch or two separate T-s diagrams side by side. The side-by-side version is easier to draw cleanly in a 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BraytonCycle[Brayton Cycle Gas Turbine] direction LR P1[1 Compressor Inlet] --> P2[2 Compressor Exit] P2 --> P3[3 Turbine Inlet TIT] P3 --> P4[4 Turbine Exhaust 550 C] P4 --> P1 end subgraph RankineCycle[Rankine Cycle Steam Turbine] direction LR Q1[a Condensate Pump Exit] --> Q2[b Economizer Exit] Q2 --> Q3[c Steam Inlet to ST] Q3 --> Q4[d Steam Exhaust to Condenser] Q4 --> Q1 end P4 -. heat in HRSG .-> Q3 Q4 -. reject in condenser .-> P1 ``` ## 4.3 HRSG Internal Flow Architecture (Schematic) Modern triple-pressure HRSGs contain three separate pressure sections: ```mermaid flowchart LR G1[GT Exhaust
T4] --> ECO1[Economizer
High Pressure Feedwater] ECO1 --> EVAP1[Evaporator
High Pressure Drum] EVAP1 --> SH1[Superheater
High Pressure] SH1 --> STHP[Steam Turbine HP Section] STHP --> RH1[Reheater
Cold Reheat to Hot Reheat] G1 --> ECO2[Economizer
Intermediate Pressure] ECO2 --> EVAP2[Evaporator
IP Drum] EVAP2 --> SH2[Superheater
IP Section] SH2 --> STIP[Steam Turbine IP Section] G1 --> ECO3[Economizer
Low Pressure] ECO3 --> EVAP3[Evaporator
LP Drum] EVAP3 --> SH3[Superheater
LP Section] SH3 --> STLP[Steam Turbine LP Section] STHP --> STIP STIP --> STLP STLP --> COND[Condenser] ``` **Key features of the HRSG:** - **No combustor** — strictly a heat exchanger (waste-heat boiler). - May have a **duct burner** inserted between the GT exhaust and the HRSG to add heat, raising output at the cost of efficiency. - The pinch point (smallest temperature difference between hot gas and working fluid) is the limiting design constraint; it is typically kept ≥ **10 °C**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Q: Define combined cycle power generation. Why is its overall efficiency higher than that of a simple gas turbine plant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A combined cycle power plant uses the exhaust heat of a gas turbine (Brayton cycle) to generate steam in a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG), which then drives a steam turbine (Rankine cycle) to produce additional electricity **(1 mark)**. The overall efficiency is the sum of the GT efficiency and the product of the heat ratio and the ST efficiency, expressed as $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{GT} + k \cdot \eta_{ST}$ **(1 mark)**. Because the residual heat in the GT exhaust — which would otherwise be wasted — is recovered and converted to useful work in the ST, the plant converts a much larger fraction of the fuel's chemical energy into electricity **(1 mark)**. Modern CCGT plants achieve **55–64 %** efficiency versus **35–38 %** for a simple open-cycle gas turbine. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Q: What is a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)? List any four of its components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An HRSG is a heat exchanger that recovers energy from the hot exhaust gases of a gas turbine to produce steam for a steam turbine, without any additional fuel combustion **(1 mark)**. The four major components are: **(0.5 marks each)** 1. **Economizer** — preheats feedwater using residual exhaust heat. 2. **Evaporator (Steam Drum + risers)** — converts water to saturated steam. 3. **Superheater** — raises saturated steam to the required ST-inlet temperature. 4. **Reheater** — re-superheats steam partially expanded in the HP turbine (used in triple-pressure reheat HRSGs). ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working of a Combined Cycle Power Plant. Derive the expression for its overall efficiency and state any two advantages.** #### (a) Working of a Combined Cycle Power Plant — Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks) A combined cycle power plant integrates a **Brayton cycle (gas turbine)** as the topping cycle and a **Rankine cycle (steam turbine)** as the bottoming cycle **(1 mark)**. The block diagram was shown in Section 4.1 above. The working sequence is: 1. **Air Intake & Compression (1 mark):** Atmospheric air is drawn into the axial-flow compressor, where it is compressed to **15–30 bar**, consuming about **2/3** of the gross turbine work. 2. **Combustion (1 mark):** Compressed air enters the combustion chamber where natural gas (or distillate) is burned continuously, raising the gas temperature to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** of **1300–1600 °C**. 3. **Gas Turbine Expansion (1 mark):** Hot gases expand through the multi-stage gas turbine, producing shaft work. The *net* work — after subtracting compressor work — drives the GT generator. 4. **Heat Recovery in HRSG (1 mark):** Exhaust gases at **450–620 °C** pass through the HRSG, transferring heat to feedwater in three pressure sections (economizer → evaporator → superheater) without additional fuel. 5. **Steam Turbine & Condenser (1 mark):** Superheated steam expands through the HP, IP, and LP sections of the steam turbine, then is condensed in a surface condenser and pumped back to the HRSG. 6. **Electrical Output (1 mark):** Both GT and ST generators feed a common AC bus; the plant output is the algebraic sum of the two. #### (b) Derivation of Overall Efficiency & Advantages (7 marks) **Derivation (5 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the overall efficiency is: $$ \eta_{overall} = \eta_{GT} + k \cdot \eta_{ST} $$ where $k = Q_{HRSG} / Q_{fuel}$ is the heat ratio. The derivation steps shown in Section 3.1 (Steps 1–7) carry the full 5 marks. **Advantages (2 marks — any two):** - **High thermal efficiency** (55–64 %) leads to **lower specific fuel consumption** and reduced CO₂ per kWh **(1 mark)**. - **Rapid start-up** (≈ 30 minutes) and excellent **load-following capability** make it ideal for peaking and grid balancing with renewables **(1 mark)**. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: A combined cycle power plant uses natural gas of calorific value 52 MJ/kg. The gas turbine generates 80 MW of net electrical power at an efficiency of 36 %. The HRSG recovers 50 % of the total fuel heat and supplies it to a steam turbine operating at 32 % cycle efficiency. Calculate: (a) the fuel consumption in kg/hr, (b) the steam turbine power output in MW, (c) the overall plant efficiency, and (d) the specific fuel consumption in kg/kWh.** #### (a) Fuel Consumption in kg/hr (4 marks) Fuel heat input: $$ Q_{fuel} = \dfrac{W_{net,GT}}{\eta_{GT}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{0.36} = 222{,}222.22 \text{ kW} \tag{1} $$ Fuel mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{222{,}222.22}{52{,}000} = 4.2735 \text{ kg/s} \tag{2} $$ Converting to kg/hr: $$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = 4.2735 \times 3600 = 15{,}384.6 \text{ kg/hr} \tag{1} $$ #### (b) Steam Turbine Power Output (4 marks) Heat available in HRSG: $$ Q_{HRSG} = 0.50 \times 222{,}222.22 = 111{,}111.11 \text{ kW} \tag{1} $$ Steam turbine power: $$ W_{net,ST} = 0.32 \times 111{,}111.11 = 35{,}555.56 \text{ kW} = 35.56 \text{ MW} \tag{2} $$ #### (c) Overall Plant Efficiency (3 marks) $$ \eta_{overall} = \dfrac{80 + 35.56}{222.22} = \dfrac{115.56}{222.22} = 0.5200 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} = 52.00 \,\%} \tag{1} $$ Or using the canonical formula: $$ \eta_{overall} = 0.36 + (0.50 \times 0.32) = 0.36 + 0.16 = 0.52 = 52.00 \,\% \tag{2} $$ #### (d) Specific Fuel Consumption (3 marks) Total net output: $$ P_{net} = 80 + 35.56 = 115.56 \text{ MW} $$ Specific fuel consumption: $$ SFC = \dfrac{3600 \times \dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{net}} = \dfrac{3600 \times 4.2735}{115.56 \times 1000} \text{ kWh denominator correction...} $$ Re-doing it carefully with consistent units (kW): $$ SFC = \dfrac{3600 \times 4.2735}{115{,}560} = \dfrac{15{,}384.6}{115{,}560} = 0.1331 \text{ kg/kWh} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in SFC:** Students often write $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ in kg/s and $P_{net}$ in MW without converting to a common base. Always express $P_{net}$ in **kW** when $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ is in **kg/s**, or convert $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ to **kg/hr** and $P_{net}$ to **MW**. Mixing the two is a 1-mark loss. > 2. **Forgetting the + sign in $\eta_{overall}$:** Some students write $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{GT} \times k \times \eta_{ST}$ (multiplication). The correct form is an **addition** of $\eta_{GT}$ and $k \cdot \eta_{ST}$. The multiplicative error produces an absurdly low efficiency (≈ 5.7 %) and loses 2 marks. > 3. **Neglecting to mention LHV vs HHV basis:** Always specify which calorific value is used. For natural gas, **LHV is the convention** in international performance contracts. > 4. **Skipping the block diagram:** A 14-mark question on CCGT *always* expects a labeled block diagram. Missing the diagram costs a minimum of 2 marks. > 5. **Confusing HRSG with a conventional boiler:** The HRSG has *no* fuel input; this is its defining feature. Calling it a "steam generator that uses fuel" is incorrect and loses 1 mark. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist for the KTU board exam — Combined Cycle Electricity Generation:** - ✅ Combined cycle = **Brayton (topping) + Rankine (bottoming)** in series on the *same* fuel source. - ✅ The **HRSG is a heat exchanger, not a boiler** — no fuel is burned in it. - ✅ Canonical efficiency equation: **$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{GT} + k \cdot \eta_{ST}$** — note the **plus sign**, not multiplication. - ✅ **Heat ratio** $k = Q_{HRSG} / Q_{fuel}$ is typically **0.45–0.55**. - ✅ Modern CCGT efficiency: **55–64 % (LHV)** versus 35–38 % for a simple GT — an *absolute* gain of 15–25 percentage points. - ✅ Gas turbine **TIT** of 1300–1600 °C is the *primary* driver of overall plant efficiency; raising TIT by 20 °C typically adds ≈ 1 percentage point. - ✅ HRSG configurations are **single-, dual-, or triple-pressure**; triple-pressure reheat is used in 60 %+ class plants. - ✅ The HRSG **pinch point** is the temperature-difference bottleneck — kept ≥ 10 °C. - ✅ Specific fuel consumption (SFC) of CCGT is **0.18–0.22 kg/kWh** (LHV, natural gas) — far better than diesel gensets (0.25–0.30) or coal plants (0.40–0.55). - ✅ CO₂ emissions of CCGT are **< 365 g/kWh**, the lowest among all fossil-fuel technologies. - ✅ Cogeneration / CHP variant adds a **bottoming steam-extraction pass** for district heating, raising *total energy utilization* to > 80 %. - ✅ Duct firing in the HRSG raises output by ≈ 15–25 % at the cost of ≈ 3–4 percentage points of efficiency. - ✅ Future direction: **H₂ / NH₃-fuelled CCGT** for deep decarbonization of the power sector. - ✅ Always state the **calorific value basis** (LHV for natural gas; GCV/HHV for coal/Indian fuels) in your answer. - ✅ Memorize the **T-s diagram features** of both cycles and the **block diagram** of the plant — these are worth 4–6 marks together in a 14-mark question. - ✅ KTU exam tip: structure your 14-mark answer as **(a) 7 marks (diagram + working) + (b) 7 marks (derivation + numerical + advantages)** — exactly the pattern shown in Question A above.

topics.title: Economic Analysis: Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Analysis: Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Economic Analysis Methods in Energy Management

**Economic Analysis** in the context of energy management is the systematic process of evaluating the financial viability of energy conservation projects, capital investments, and energy-efficient technologies by comparing the costs incurred against the monetary benefits (energy cost savings) over the entire useful life of the project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Economic analysis methods are quantitative financial evaluation techniques used by energy managers and engineers to determine whether an energy conservation measure (ECM) is worth investing in, by accounting for the **time value of money**, project life, initial investment, operating costs, and salvage value.*

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Money Has a Memory" Intuition

Imagine you keep **₹1 Lakh** hidden in a wooden box under your bed. Your friend offers to borrow the same ₹1 Lakh for **5 years** and return it to you in one lump sum at the end. Would you accept?

Most people say **NO** — and the reason is simple: **money today is worth more than the same money tomorrow**.

If you had kept the money in a fixed deposit, after 5 years it would have grown to roughly:

$$
\text{Future Value} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.10)^5 \approx 1{,}61{,}051
$$

So your friend must return at least ₹1,61,051 to make you "even." This is the heart of **economic analysis** — every rupee spent or saved is shifted along the timeline to its *equivalent value at a common point* before being compared.

> [!NOTE]
> In energy projects:
> - **Today** → you spend money (Capital Cost)
> - **Future years** → you save money (Energy Bill Reduction)
> The job of economic analysis is to bring these "time-separated" cash flows to a **common yardstick** and decide if the project is profitable.

## Why Economic Analysis is Critical in Energy Management

| Situation | Without Economic Analysis | With Economic Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Replacing old motor with efficient motor | Looks "expensive" today | Quantified as a 4-year payback, 18% IRR |
| LED retrofit in factory | Unclear long-term value | NPV = ₹8.4 Lakh (Profitable) |
| Solar PV installation | Big upfront cost | Levelized cost = ₹3.2/kWh (Cheaper than grid) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations (MUST memorize):**
> - **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** — measured in **years**
> - **Return on Investment (ROI)** — measured in **%**
> - **Net Present Value (NPV)** — measured in **₹ (currency)**
> - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — measured in **%**
> - **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — dimensionless
> - **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** — measured in **₹ (currency)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Time Value of Money (TVM) — The Foundation

Every economic method in energy management rests on **TVM**. The premise is that a rupee received today can be invested to earn interest, so it is worth more than the same rupee received in the future.

### The Two Core Conversion Operations

1. **Single Payment Compound Amount (Future Value of Present Sum):**
   Moving money *forward* in time.

$$
F = P (1 + i)^n
$$

2. **Single Payment Present Worth (Present Value of Future Sum):**
   Moving money *backward* in time.

$$
P = \frac{F}{(1 + i)^n}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Present Worth (₹)
- $F$ = Future Worth (₹)
- $i$ = Interest rate per period (decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%)
- $n$ = Number of periods (years)

### Uniform Series Conversion Factors

| Factor | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Future Worth of Annuity | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Convert annual savings to a future lump sum |
| Present Worth of Annuity | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Convert annual savings to a present lump sum |
| Capital Recovery (A from P) | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Spread initial cost into equal annual payments |
| Sinking Fund (A to F) | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Build future value via equal annual deposits |

> [!NOTE]
> A → Annual cash flow (uniform end-of-period series)
> F → Future value at year *n*
> P → Present value at year 0

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The **simplest** economic indicator. It tells you *how long it takes* for cumulative energy cost savings to recover the initial capital cost.

$$
\text{Simple Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Net Savings (₹/year)}}
$$

- **SPP ≤ Target Payback → Accept the project**
- **SPP > Target Payback → Reject the project**

> [!WARNING]
> SPP **ignores** the time value of money, the project life beyond payback, and operating costs after payback. It is a *screening* tool, not a final decision tool.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Return on Investment (ROI)

ROI expresses the project's average annual return as a percentage of the initial investment.

$$
\text{ROI} (\%) = \frac{\text{Annual Net Savings}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100
$$

For example, ROI = 25% means the project returns 25% of the initial investment each year.

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Net Present Value (NPV) — The KTU Favourite

NPV is the **gold standard** of energy project evaluation. It converts *all* future cash flows to a common present-value basis and subtracts the initial cost.

$$
\text{NPV} = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}
$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = Initial investment (negative at $t = 0$)
- $A_t$ = Net annual cash flow (savings − operating costs) in year *t*
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of project life
- $i$ = Minimum acceptable rate of return (MARR) / discount rate
- $n$ = Project life in years

### Decision Rule
| NPV Value | Decision |
|---|---|
| NPV > 0 | **Accept** — project earns more than MARR |
| NPV = 0 | Indifferent — earns exactly MARR |
| NPV < 0 | **Reject** — project earns less than MARR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When comparing **mutually exclusive projects** (e.g., 3-star AC vs 5-star AC), pick the one with the **highest NPV**, not necessarily the lowest SPP.

---

## 2.5 Method 4 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

IRR is the discount rate that makes **NPV = 0**. It is the "true" rate of return the project generates.

$$
0 = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+IRR)^t} + \frac{S}{(1+IRR)^n}
$$

### Decision Rule
- If **IRR ≥ MARR (hurdle rate)** → Accept
- If **IRR < MARR** → Reject

> [!NOTE]
> IRR is solved by **trial-and-error interpolation** in KTU problems:

$$
\text{IRR} = i_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (i_H - i_L)
$$

Where $i_L$ and $i_H$ are the two guessed rates bracketing the true IRR.

---

## 2.6 Method 5 — Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$
\text{BCR} = \frac{\text{Present Worth of Benefits}}{\text{Present Worth of Costs}}
$$

- **BCR > 1** → Accept
- **BCR < 1** → Reject
- **BCR = 1** → Break-even

For mutually exclusive projects of different sizes, use the **incremental BCR** (BCR of the difference in cash flows).

---

## 2.7 Method 6 — Life Cycle Cost (LCC) Analysis

LCC is the **total cost of ownership** over the entire life of the equipment. It is used to compare alternatives with different operating costs but different lifespans.

$$
\text{LCC} = P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}
$$

The alternative with the **lowest LCC** is chosen.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Method | Core Formula | Units | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| TVM Single Payment | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | ₹ | — |
| TVM Annuity PW | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ | — |
| Simple Payback | $SPP = \dfrac{P_0}{A}$ | years | SPP ≤ Target |
| ROI | $ROI = \dfrac{A}{P_0} \times 100$ | % | ROI ≥ Target % |
| NPV | $NPV = -P_0 + \sum \dfrac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ | NPV > 0 |
| IRR (by interpolation) | $IRR = i_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | % | IRR ≥ MARR |
| BCR | $BCR = \dfrac{PW(Benefits)}{PW(Costs)}$ | ratio | BCR > 1 |
| LCC | $LCC = P_0 + \sum \dfrac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} - \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ | LCC = Minimum |

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Industrial Energy Auditing | Justify VFD installation, heat exchanger upgrades |
| Building Services | Compare HVAC, lighting, and insulation alternatives |
| Renewable Energy | Wind/Solar feasibility vs diesel generator extension |
| Utility Planning | Choose between new transmission line or distributed generation |
| ISO 50001 EnMS | Mandatory financial justification of every Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) action |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Present Worth of a Uniform Annual Series

We start with the future worth of an annuity $A$ deposited at the end of each year for $n$ years at interest rate $i$:

$$
F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0
$$

This is a **geometric progression** with first term $A$, common ratio $(1+i)$, and $n$ terms. Using the geometric series sum formula:

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

To convert this future worth into present worth $P$, apply the single-payment present-worth factor $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}}
$$

This is the **Present Worth of Annuity** factor — the most-used formula in NPV calculations for energy projects.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capital Recovery Factor (A from P)

If we borrow $P$ today and must repay it as an equal annual installment $A$ over $n$ years at interest $i$, then by definition $A$ is the annuity whose present worth equals $P$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

Inverting to solve for $A$:

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

This is the **Capital Recovery (CR) factor**, used in LCC to convert initial cost into equivalent annual cost.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sinking Fund Factor (A to F)

If you want to accumulate a future value $F$ by depositing $A$ every year, $A$ is the annuity whose future worth equals $F$:

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

Used to compute the **depreciation reserve** or **salvage fund contribution** in life-cycle costing.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> *A company is considering replacing its existing 50 kW motor (efficiency 88%) with a premium-efficiency motor (efficiency 94%). The motor runs 6,000 hours/year. The cost of electricity is ₹8/kWh. The new motor costs ₹3,00,000 including installation. The expected life is 15 years with a salvage value of ₹40,000. The MARR is 12%. Evaluate using:*
> *(a) Simple Payback Period*
> *(b) NPV*
> *(c) IRR (by interpolation)*

### Solution — Step 1: Compute Annual Energy Loss of Each Motor

Motor input power = Output / Efficiency. The losses are the difference.

**Existing motor losses (annual):**

$$
\text{Loss}_{\text{old}} = \text{Output} \times \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{\text{old}}} - \frac{1}{1}\right) = 50 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.88} - 1\right) = 50 \times 0.1364 = 6.818 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
E_{\text{old}} = 6.818 \times 6000 = 40{,}909 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**New motor losses (annual):**

$$
\text{Loss}_{\text{new}} = 50 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.94} - 1\right) = 50 \times 0.0638 = 3.191 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
E_{\text{new}} = 3.191 \times 6000 = 19{,}149 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Annual energy saved:**

$$
\Delta E = 40{,}909 - 19{,}149 = 21{,}760 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Annual monetary savings (gross):**

$$
A = 21{,}760 \times 8 = \text{₹}1{,}74{,}080 \text{ /year}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Simple Payback Period

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{P_0}{A} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1{,}74{,}080} = 1.72 \text{ years}
$$

**Decision:** Well within typical 3–5 year industrial targets. **ACCEPT.**

### Solution — Step 3: NPV at 12% Discount Rate

Use the present-worth-of-annuity factor for $n = 15$, $i = 0.12$:

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 15) = \frac{(1.12)^{15} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{15}}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
(1.12)^{15} = 5.4736
$$

$$
P/A = \frac{5.4736 - 1}{0.12 \times 5.4736} = \frac{4.4736}{0.6568} = 6.8109
$$

Now apply the NPV formula:

$$
\text{NPV} = -3{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}74{,}080 \times 6.8109) + \frac{40{,}000}{(1.12)^{15}}
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -3{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}85{,}929 + \frac{40{,}000}{5.4736}
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -3{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}85{,}929 + 7{,}309
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NPV} = \text{₹}8{,}93{,}238}
$$

**Decision:** NPV is strongly positive. **ACCEPT.**

### Solution — Step 4: IRR by Interpolation

Try $i_L = 80\%$:

$$
(1.80)^{15} = 678.57 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 80\%, 15) = \frac{677.57}{0.80 \times 678.57} = 1.248
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_L = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.248 + \frac{40{,}000}{678.57}
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_L = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}17{,}252 + 59 = -82{,}689
$$

Try $i_H = 75\%$:

$$
(1.75)^{15} = 427.79 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 75\%, 15) = \frac{426.79}{0.75 \times 427.79} = 1.330
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.330 + \frac{40{,}000}{427.79}
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}31{,}526 + 94 = -68{,}380
$$

Both negative — try a **lower** rate. Try $i_H = 70\%$:

$$
(1.70)^{15} = 252.93 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 70\%, 15) = \frac{251.93}{0.70 \times 252.93} = 1.4227
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.4227 + \frac{40{,}000}{252.93}
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}47{,}644 + 158 = -52{,}198
$$

Still negative. Try $i_H = 60\%$:

$$
(1.60)^{15} = 109.67 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 60\%, 15) = \frac{108.67}{0.60 \times 109.67} = 1.6513
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.6513 + \frac{40{,}000}{109.67}
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}87{,}438 + 365 = -12{,}197
$$

Try $i_H = 58\%$:

$$
(1.58)^{15} \approx 92.27 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 58\%, 15) \approx \frac{91.27}{0.58 \times 92.27} \approx 1.7046
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.7046 + \frac{40{,}000}{92.27}
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}96{,}703 + 433 = -2{,}864
$$

Try $i_H = 57\%$:

$$
(1.57)^{15} \approx 84.51 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 57\%, 15) \approx 1.733
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}080 \times 1.733 + \frac{40{,}000}{84.51} = -3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}01{,}720 + 473 = +2{,}193
$$

Now we bracket between $i_L = 58\%$ (NPV = −2,864) and $i_H = 57\%$ (NPV = +2,193):

$$
\text{IRR} = 58\% + \frac{2{,}864}{2{,}864 - (-2{,}193)} \times (57 - 58)\%
$$

$$
\text{IRR} = 58\% + \frac{2{,}864}{5{,}057} \times (-1)\% = 58\% - 0.566\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{IRR} \approx 57.4\%}
$$

**Decision:** IRR (57.4%) >> MARR (12%). **ACCEPT — extremely attractive project.**

---

## 3.5 Sensitivity Analysis — How Results Change with Inputs

A complete economic study must test how NPV or IRR shifts if key inputs change. The typical sensitivity table is:

| Input | Base Value | −20% | Base | +20% | Impact on NPV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electricity tariff (₹/kWh) | 8.00 | 6.40 | 8.00 | 9.60 | High |
| Annual operating hours | 6,000 | 4,800 | 6,000 | 7,200 | High |
| Discount rate MARR | 12% | 10% | 12% | 14% | Moderate |
| Motor efficiency new | 94% | 92% | 94% | 96% | Moderate |
| Project life (years) | 15 | 12 | 15 | 18 | Low–Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always present a **tornado chart** (or equivalent table) showing the most sensitive variable. Energy tariff and operating hours typically dominate — they are also the hardest to predict 15 years out.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline of an Energy Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0 [Year 0]
        A[Initial Investment P0 negative]
    end
    subgraph T1toN [Years 1 to n]
        B[Annual Net Savings A positive]
        C[Annual Net Savings A positive]
        D[Annual Net Savings A positive]
    end
    subgraph TN [Year n]
        E[Salvage Value S positive]
    end
    A -->|Outflow at t=0| T0
    B -->|Inflow each year| T1toN
    C -->|Inflow each year| T1toN
    D -->|Inflow each year| T1toN
    E -->|Inflow at end| TN
```

## 4.2 Economic Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Identify Energy Project] --> data[Collect Investment Savings Life Tariff MARR]
    data --> screen{Quick Screening Needed}
    screen -->|Yes| spp[Compute Simple Payback Period]
    spp --> sppcut{SPP less than Target Payback}
    sppcut -->|No| reject[Reject Project]
    sppcut -->|Yes| full[Proceed to Detailed Analysis]
    screen -->|No| full
    full --> npv[Compute NPV at MARR]
    npv --> irr[Compute IRR]
    irr --> bcr[Compute BCR]
    bcr --> lcc[Compute LCC for Alternatives]
    lcc --> sens[Perform Sensitivity Analysis]
    sens --> decide{Final Decision}
    decide -->|Accept| execute[Implement Energy Project]
    decide -->|Reject| reject
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inputs to Economic Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs [Required Project Data]
        P0[Initial Capital Cost in Rupees]
        S[Salvage Value in Rupees]
        N[Project Life in Years]
        Tariff[Electricity Tariff in Rupees per kWh]
        Hours[Annual Operating Hours]
        EffOld[Existing Equipment Efficiency]
        EffNew[New Equipment Efficiency]
        MARR[Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return]
    end
    subgraph Calc [Calculations]
        Save[Annual Energy Savings kWh]
        Money[Annual Monetary Savings Rupees]
    end
    subgraph Methods [Evaluation Methods]
        M1[Simple Payback]
        M2[NPV]
        M3[IRR]
        M4[BCR]
        M5[LCC]
    end
    subgraph Output [Decision]
        OK[Accept if all criteria met]
    end
    P0 --> Calc
    S --> Calc
    N --> Calc
    Tariff --> Calc
    Hours --> Calc
    EffOld --> Calc
    EffNew --> Calc
    MARR --> Methods
    Calc --> Methods
    Methods --> Output
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{What is the purpose of analysis}
    q1 -->|Quick screening| a1[Use Simple Payback]
    q1 -->|Detailed financial study| a2[Use NPV and IRR]
    q1 -->|Compare two equipment options| a3[Use LCC and BCR]
    q1 -->|Multiple uncertain variables| a4[Add Sensitivity Analysis]
    a2 --> note1[NPV is preferred over IRR for mutually exclusive projects]
    a3 --> note2[Always check incremental BCR for different project sizes]
    a4 --> note3[Vary tariff hours life discount rate]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Net Present Value (NPV). How is the decision taken based on NPV for an energy conservation project?*

**Model Answer:**
Net Present Value is the algebraic sum of the present worths of all cash flows (inflows and outflows) associated with a project over its entire life, discounted at the Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR).

**Formula:**

$$
\text{NPV} = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If NPV > 0 → Project earns more than MARR → **Accept**
- If NPV = 0 → Project earns exactly MARR → **Indifferent**
- If NPV < 0 → Project earns less than MARR → **Reject**

*(3 marks — definition 1, formula 1, decision rule 1)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is meant by Simple Payback Period? State its limitations.*

**Model Answer:**
The Simple Payback Period is the time (in years) required for the cumulative net cash savings of a project to recover the initial capital investment.

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Savings}}
$$

**Limitations:**
1. Ignores the **time value of money**.
2. Ignores cash flows **beyond** the payback period.
3. Ignores **salvage value** of equipment.
4. Does not measure **profitability** — only speed of recovery.
5. Subjective target payback can mislead investment decisions.

*(3 marks — definition 1, formula 1, any 2 limitations 1)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*(a)* Explain the **time value of money** concept. Derive the present worth of a uniform annual series. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A factory installs a 100 kW roof-top solar PV system costing ₹35,00,000. The annual energy generation is 1,50,000 kWh, displacing grid power at ₹9/kWh. The system has a life of 20 years, salvage ₹2,00,000, and the MARR is 10%. Compute the **NPV** and state whether the project is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

*(a)* Compare **NPV, IRR, and BCR** as methods of economic analysis. Highlight situations where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* An energy efficiency retrofit costs ₹8,00,000 and yields annual savings of ₹1,80,000. The project life is 10 years with negligible salvage. Using MARR = 14%, compute the **IRR** by interpolation. **(7 marks)**

---

## Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Time Value of Money and PW of Annuity Derivation

> **Time Value of Money:** A rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee available in the future because the present rupee can be invested to earn interest, generate returns, or maintain purchasing power.

The two fundamental operations are:

$$
F = P(1+i)^n \quad \text{and} \quad P = F(1+i)^{-n}
$$

**Derivation of Present Worth of Annuity:**

Let $A$ be a uniform end-of-year cash flow for $n$ years. Its future worth at year $n$ is:

$$
F = A + A(1+i) + A(1+i)^2 + \cdots + A(1+i)^{n-1}
$$

This is a geometric progression with $n$ terms, first term $A$, ratio $(1+i)$:

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Multiplying by $(1+i)^{-n}$ to convert $F$ to present worth $P$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

This is the **Present Worth of Annuity factor** — universally used in NPV calculations.

#### Part (b) — NPV of Solar PV Project

Given:
- $P_0 = 35{,}00{,}000$
- $A = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 9 = 13{,}50{,}000$ ₹/year
- $S = 2{,}00{,}000$
- $n = 20$ years
- $i = 10\%$

Step 1: Compute the present worth factor at 10%, 20 years:

$$
(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275
$$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 20) = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.6728} = 8.5136
$$

Step 2: Apply NPV formula:

$$
\text{NPV} = -35{,}00{,}000 + (13{,}50{,}000 \times 8.5136) + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{6.7275}
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -35{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}14{,}93{,}360 + 29{,}729
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NPV} = + \text{₹}80{,}23{,}089}
$$

**Decision:** NPV is **strongly positive** (₹80.23 Lakh). **Project ACCEPTED.**

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Comparison of NPV, IRR, BCR

| Aspect | NPV | IRR | BCR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sum of PW of all cash flows | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 | Ratio of PW of benefits to PW of costs |
| Unit | ₹ (currency) | % | Dimensionless |
| Decision Rule | NPV > 0 → Accept | IRR ≥ MARR → Accept | BCR > 1 → Accept |
| TVM Considered | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Reinvestment Assumption | At MARR | At IRR (often unrealistic) | At MARR |
| Multiple IRR Problem | None | Yes (for non-conventional cash flows) | None |
| Preferred For | Mutually exclusive projects | Standalone project ranking | Public sector / cost-effective studies |
| Limitation | Requires accurate MARR | Hard to compute, may have multiple roots | Sensitive to definition of "cost" and "benefit" |

#### Part (b) — IRR by Interpolation

Given: $P_0 = 8{,}00{,}000$, $A = 1{,}80{,}000$, $n = 10$ years, $S \approx 0$.

**Guess 1:** Try $i_L = 12\%$:

$$
(1.12)^{10} = 3.1058 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{2.1058}{0.12 \times 3.1058} = 5.6502
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{12\%} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 \times 5.6502 = -8{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}17{,}036 = +2{,}17{,}036
$$

Positive — so IRR is **lower** than 12%. Try higher discount.

**Guess 2:** Try $i_H = 18\%$:

$$
(1.18)^{10} = 5.2338 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 18\%, 10) = \frac{4.2338}{0.18 \times 5.2338} = 4.4941
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{18\%} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 \times 4.4941 = -8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}08{,}938 = +8{,}938
$$

Still positive. Try higher.

**Guess 3:** Try $i_H = 19\%$:

$$
(1.19)^{10} \approx 5.6947 \quad ; \quad (P/A, 19\%, 10) = \frac{4.6947}{0.19 \times 5.6947} = 4.3389
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{19\%} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 \times 4.3389 = -8{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}81{,}002 = -18{,}998
$$

**Negative.** We now bracket between $i_L = 18\%$ (NPV = +8,938) and $i_H = 19\%$ (NPV = −18,998).

Apply interpolation:

$$
\text{IRR} = 18\% + \frac{8{,}938}{8{,}938 - (-18{,}998)} \times (19 - 18)\%
$$

$$
\text{IRR} = 18\% + \frac{8{,}938}{27{,}936} \times 1\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{IRR} \approx 18.32\%}
$$

**Decision:** IRR (18.32%) > MARR (14%) → **Project ACCEPTED.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Discount rate confusion** — Students use the inflation rate instead of MARR. Always re-read the question.
> 2. **Sign convention** — Initial investment is **negative**; annual savings are **positive**; salvage is **positive**.
> 3. **Salvage omission** — Many KTU problems specify a salvage value; ignoring it gives a wrong NPV/IRR.
> 4. **P/A factor misuse** — Use the **end-of-period** annuity factor unless the question explicitly says "beginning of year" (then add 1 more year's interest or multiply by $(1+i)$).
> 5. **Arithmetic error in (1+i)^n** — This is the most common computation mistake. Always re-verify with a calculator.
> 6. **Decision rule** — State the rule *and* the conclusion. A correct numerical value with no decision = partial marks.
> 7. **IRR interpolation** — Always show both NPV calculations. Showing only the final IRR without the trial NPVs loses 2–3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Time Value of Money** is the foundation of all economic methods — without it, comparison of cash flows at different times is meaningless.
- **Six Core Methods**: SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR, BCR, LCC. NPV is the most academically rigorous and the KTU favourite.
- **Always use end-of-year annuity convention** unless the problem states otherwise.
- **Three must-have inputs for any NPV/IRR problem**: $P_0$ (initial cost), $A$ (uniform annual savings), $n$ (project life), plus $i$ (MARR) and $S$ (salvage, if any).
- **The Present Worth of Annuity factor** $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ is the single most-used factor in energy economics.
- **Decision rules (memorize verbatim):**
  - NPV > 0 → Accept
  - IRR ≥ MARR → Accept
  - BCR > 1 → Accept
  - SPP ≤ Target → Accept
  - LCC → Choose minimum
- **For mutually exclusive projects** (choose one OR the other), compare NPVs and pick the largest — **not** the one with shortest payback.
- **Sensitivity Analysis is mandatory** in any real-world energy investment proposal — vary tariff, hours, life, and discount rate by ±20%.
- **Rule of Thumb for Energy Projects:** A good project typically has SPP ≤ 3 years, IRR > 20%, and NPV > 0 at MARR ≥ 12%.
- **Don't forget salvage value** — it often accounts for 5–15% of the project benefits and changes the decision at the margin.
- **KTU 14-mark questions almost always combine**: (a) theory/derivation (7 marks) + (b) numerical on NPV or IRR (7 marks). Practice both halves separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cash Flow Model** is a quantitative financial engineering framework used in energy management to represent, time-sequence, and mathematically evaluate the inflows (revenue savings, salvage value, subsidies) and outflows (capital investment, operating costs, maintenance) occurring at different points in the life cycle of an energy conservation project. It operationalizes the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle, which states that a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee available a year from now, because of its earning potential.

In the KTU 2024 framework, the cash flow model is the foundational computational engine for techniques such as **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Annual Worth (AW)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, **Benefit-Cost (B-C) Ratio**, and **Simple/Discounted Payback Period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> "A cash flow model is a tabular and mathematical representation of all economic transactions (receipts and disbursements) associated with an energy project, plotted on a time axis, and reduced to a common equivalence base using an appropriate minimum attractive rate of return (MARR), denoted as $i$."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a farmer. You can either:
- **Option A:** Receive ₹1,00,000 *today* and invest it in a savings account earning 8% per year.
- **Option B:** Receive ₹1,00,000 *five years from now*.

The cash flow model tells you: **Option A is objectively better**, because that ₹1,00,000 today, if reinvested, will grow to:
$$1,00,000 \times (1.08)^5 = ₹1,46,933$$

So the "future value" of today's ₹1,00,000 is ₹1,46,933 in 5 years. The cash flow model is the tool that performs this invisible translation — converting cash at different times into a **single, comparable economic number** at a chosen reference point (present, future, or annual).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Energy Management:**
> Consider installing a **solar PV system** costing ₹5,00,000 today, but saving ₹1,00,000 per year in electricity bills for 25 years. Are you actually making a profit? At first glance, $25 \times 1,00,000 = 25,00,000$ looks like a 5× return. However, the cash flow model, after *discounting* future savings back to today's value, will reveal the true, inflation-adjusted, time-corrected profitability. This is precisely what KTU 2024 expects students to compute.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Engineering Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P$ | Present Worth / Present Value | ₹ (Base year value) |
| $F$ | Future Worth / Future Value | ₹ (End-of-project value) |
| $A$ | Annual Worth (uniform series) | ₹/year |
| $i$ | Interest rate per period (MARR) | **8% to 15%** for Indian energy projects |
| $n$ | Number of compounding periods | Project life (years) |
| $t$ | Time index (0, 1, 2, …, $n$) | years |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Convention:** Inflows (money received) are plotted as **upward arrows** $\uparrow$ on a cash flow diagram. Outflows (money paid) are plotted as **downward arrows** $\downarrow$. The year 0 is "now" (the present).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Foundational Cash Flow Factors

A cash flow model in energy economics is constructed using six standard *equivalence factors*. Each factor converts a cash flow of one type into an equivalent cash flow of another type at a different time, all governed by the master relationship:

$$F = P(1+i)^n \quad \text{(the "DNA" of all cash flow math)}$$

The six factors are derived directly from this relationship.

**Step 1 — The Master Equation:** The single-payment compound-amount factor transforms a present sum $P$ into a future sum $F$ after $n$ years at interest rate $i$. This is the only equation you need to *remember*; the other five are algebraic rearrangements.

**Step 2 — Inverse Operation (Discounting):** To go from $F$ back to $P$, simply divide by $(1+i)^n$. This is the *present worth* of a future amount.

**Step 3 — Uniform Series Transformations:** When cash flows repeat every year (e.g., annual energy savings of ₹1,00,000), we need factors that relate a uniform annual amount $A$ to $P$ or $F$. These are obtained by summing a geometric series of the form $1 + (1+i)^{-1} + (1+i)^{-2} + \dots + (1+i)^{-n}$.

**Step 4 — The Equivalence Principle:** Any of the six factors can be used to evaluate the same project, and they will all yield the *same* accept/reject decision. The choice of factor depends only on which cash flow ($P$, $F$, or $A$) is most convenient for the problem.

**Step 5 — Decision Criteria Application:**
- **PW method:** Accept if $PW \geq 0$
- **AW method:** Accept if $AW \geq 0$
- **IRR method:** Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$
- **B/C ratio:** Accept if $B/C \geq 1$
- **Payback:** Accept if payback $\leq$ target years

**Step 6 — Sensitivity Consideration:** Energy projects involve *uncertain* future electricity tariffs. The cash flow model allows *sensitivity analysis* by recomputing the decision metric at different values of $i$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used here in its LaTeX sense (absolute value / divisibility), NOT as a markdown table separator, to preserve table integrity.

| # | Factor Name | Symbol $(X/Y,i,n)$ | Formula | Use Case in Energy Project |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P,i,n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | Future salvage value of capital |
| 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F,i,n)$ | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discount future energy cost savings |
| 3 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A,i,n)$ | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Sum of 20 years of energy savings at year 20 |
| 4 | Sinking Fund | $(A/F,i,n)$ | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual deposit to reach replacement cost |
| 5 | Capital Recovery | $(A/P,i,n)$ | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annualized cost of capital investment |
| 6 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A,i,n)$ | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | PV of 25 years of electricity savings |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Energy Engineering

- **Solar PV Feasibility:** Compute PW of 25 years of solar electricity generation savings versus ₹5 Lakh upfront cost.
- **Energy-Efficient Motor Replacement:** Compare annual energy cost savings (₹/year) against incremental cost of premium-efficiency motor.
- **Combined Heat & Power (CHP):** Use AW method to compare per-year operating cost of CHP versus grid power.
- **Industrial Energy Audits:** IRR method tells whether a recommended Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) beats the company's hurdle rate.
- **ESCO Project Financing:** Payback period determines whether a client can recover the Energy Service Company's investment within contract tenure.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A sum $P$ is deposited at time $t=0$. It earns interest at rate $i$ per period. No withdrawals are made.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Value at end of year 1: } F_1 &= P + P \cdot i = P(1+i) \\
\text{Value at end of year 2: } F_2 &= F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2 \\
\text{Value at end of year 3: } F_3 &= F_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^2(1+i) = P(1+i)^3 \\
\text{Generalising, value at end of year } n: F_n &= P(1+i)^{n-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^n}$$

**Textual Logic:** Each year, the existing balance is multiplied by $(1+i)$, because the original principal plus last year's interest both earn interest this year. After $n$ such multiplications, we have $P(1+i)^n$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single Payment Present Worth Factor $(P/F, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** Algebraically invert Derivation 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P(1+i)^n \\
\text{Divide both sides by } (1+i)^n: \quad \frac{F}{(1+i)^n} &= P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{P = F(1+i)^{-n}}$$

**Textual Logic:** To find what a future amount $F$ is worth *today*, shrink it by the compound growth factor $(1+i)^n$. This shrinkage is called *discounting*. A KTU favourite question type is "What is the present worth of ₹5,00,000 to be received 10 years from now at $i = 10\%$?"

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A uniform amount $A$ is received at the end of each year for $n$ years. We want the equivalent present worth $P$ at $i\%$.

Step 1: Write each annual receipt as a single-payment present worth at $t=0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + A(1+i)^{-3} + \dots + A(1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Factor out the common term $A(1+i)^{-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} \Big[ 1 + (1+i)^{-1} + (1+i)^{-2} + \dots + (1+i)^{-(n-1)} \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: The bracket is a geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = (1+i)^{-1}$, and $n$ terms. The sum of such a series is $\dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4: Simplify the denominator: $1 - (1+i)^{-1} = \dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} = \frac{(1+i)\big[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\big]}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5: Substitute back into the expression for $P$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} \cdot \frac{(1+i)\big[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\big]}{i} \\
P &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6: Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$ to obtain the standard KTU form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{P = A \left[ \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \right]}$$

**Textual Logic:** This factor is the workhorse of energy management. It converts a *stream* of annual energy savings into one equivalent lump-sum present value, enabling direct comparison with the initial capital outlay of, say, a new chiller or VFD.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A present amount $P$ is financed over $n$ years at interest $i$. We want the equivalent annual payment $A$ that amortizes the loan.

Step 1: Take the reciprocal of the $(P/A, i, n)$ factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= P \cdot \frac{1}{\left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Invert the fraction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{A = P \left[ \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \right]}$$

**Textual Logic:** The capital recovery factor is used to find the *equivalent annual cost* of an energy investment. For example, "What is the equivalent annual cost of a ₹10 Lakh chiller over 15 years at 12% interest?" — Multiply ₹10,00,000 by $(A/P, 12\%, 15)$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A uniform amount $A$ is deposited at the end of each year for $n$ years. Find the accumulated value $F$ at year $n$.

Step 1: The last deposit (year $n$) earns no interest. The second-to-last (year $n-1$) earns 1 year of interest, and so on.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \dots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Factor out the common term $A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \Big[ (1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \dots + (1+i) + 1 \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: The bracket is a geometric series with first term $1$, ratio $(1+i)$, and $n$ terms. Sum is $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{F = A \left[ \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \right]}$$

**Textual Logic:** Useful in energy projects for computing the *future replacement cost* of a component. Example: A boiler must be replaced every 10 years; if it costs ₹3,00,000 today and inflation is 6%, what uniform annual sinking-fund deposit will ensure the money is available?

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A future sum $F$ is needed at year $n$. Find the uniform annual deposit $A$ that accumulates to $F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{A = F \left[ \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} \right]}$$

**Textual Logic:** This factor is used to compute annual *reserve* funds for future capital replacement — a common requirement in industrial energy planning.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Method | Best Used When | Equation | Decision Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Present Worth (PW) | Cash flows are mixed (irregular) | $PW = \sum CF_t (1+i)^{-t}$ | Accept if $PW \geq 0$ |
| Future Worth (FW) | Future value comparison needed | $FW = PW(1+i)^n$ | Accept if $FW \geq 0$ |
| Annual Worth (AW) | Comparing alternatives with different lives | $AW = PW \cdot (A/P,i,n)$ | Accept if $AW \geq 0$ |
| Internal Rate of Return | Computing actual project yield | NPV $= 0$ equation | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ |
| Benefit-Cost Ratio | Public/government projects | $B/C = PV(\text{benefits})/PV(\text{costs})$ | Accept if $B/C \geq 1$ |
| Payback Period | Quick risk assessment | Year when cumulative CF = 0 | Accept if $PB \leq$ target |

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Year 0: Capital Outflow P (arrow down)"] --> B["Year 1: Net Savings A1 (arrow up)"]
    B --> C["Year 2: Net Savings A2 (arrow up)"]
    C --> D["Year 3 to Year n-1: Uniform Savings A"]
    D --> E["Year n: Savings A plus Salvage Value S (arrow up)"]
    E --> F["Time Axis n (years)"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Cash Flow Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP1["Raw Energy Audit Data"] --> INP2["Investment Cost P"]
    INP3["Annual Energy Savings A"] --> INP2
    INP4["Project Life n"] --> INP2
    INP5["MARR i"] --> INP2
    INP2 --> MOD1["Time Value Engine"]
    MOD1 --> MOD2["Factor Selector F/P, P/A, A/P, P/F, F/A, A/F"]
    MOD2 --> MOD3["Equivalence Calculator"]
    MOD3 --> OUT1["Net Present Value NPV"]
    MOD3 --> OUT2["Annual Worth AW"]

    OUT2 --> DEC
    OUT3 --> DEC
    DEC --> RES["Ranked List of ECMs"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Cash Flow Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["New Energy Project Evaluation"] --> Q1{"Are cash flows uniform?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Need to compare alternatives of different lives?"}
    Q1 -- No --> P1["Use NPV / PW method directly"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> AW["Use Annual Worth AW method"]
    Q2 -- No --> PW["Use Present Worth PW method"]
    AW --> DONE["Decision"]
    PW --> DONE
    P1 --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram shows how raw audit data flows through a *Time Value Engine* that selects the appropriate equivalence factor, then routes the output to a *Decision Module*. This mirrors the structure of a professional energy management software like RETScreen or Homer Pro.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Cash Flow Model" as applied in energy management. List any three standard cash flow equivalence factors used in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Cash Flow Model** is a mathematical and graphical representation of all monetary receipts and disbursements occurring over the life of an energy project, reduced to a common time-equivalent value using the **Time Value of Money** principle and a minimum attractive rate of return (MARR) $i$.

Three standard cash flow equivalence factors are:

1. **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$:** $F = P(1+i)^n$
2. **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$:** $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$
3. **Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$:** $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write the formula but forget to *name* the factor. KTU examiners award marks only when both the factor name and its formula are stated.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Time Value of Money (TVM) principle. Why is a rupee available today worth more than a rupee available a year later?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Time Value of Money (TVM) principle** states that the *value of money changes with time* due to its earning potential, inflation, and risk — a given sum of money is worth more *now* than the same sum at a *future* date.

Reasons why a rupee today is worth more:
1. **Investment Opportunity:** Today's rupee can be deposited to earn interest, growing to $(1+i)$ by next year.
2. **Inflation Erosion:** A future rupee will have reduced purchasing power.
3. **Risk and Uncertainty:** Future receipts are not guaranteed.

Mathematically, this is expressed as $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ — a future amount $F$ must be *discounted* to obtain its present equivalent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$** from first principles. Show every algebraic step clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A factory is evaluating the installation of a **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** on a large motor. The capital cost is **₹2,50,000**, the expected annual energy savings is **₹75,000**, the project life is **10 years**, and the salvage value at the end is **₹25,000**. Using MARR $i = 10\%$, determine:
- (i) The Present Worth (PW) of the project
- (ii) Whether the investment is economically justified.

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Derivation of $(P/A, i, n)$**

**Step 1:** Let a uniform amount $A$ be received at the end of each year from year 1 to year $n$. Each payment can be expressed as a present worth at year 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + \dots + A(1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $A(1+i)^{-1}$:

$$
