\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} \Big[1 + (1+i)^{-1} + (1+i)^{-2} + \dots + (1+i)^{-(n-1)}\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The bracket is a geometric progression with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = (1+i)^{-1}$, and $n$ terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1+i)^{-1} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} \\
&= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P = A \left[ \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \right]}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — VFD Project Evaluation**

**Given Data:**
- Capital cost $P = ₹2,50,000$ (at year 0)
- Annual savings $A = ₹75,000$ (years 1 to 10)
- Salvage value $S = ₹25,000$ (at year 10)
- $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years

**Step 1:** Compute the Present Worth of the annual savings series using the $(P/A, 10\%, 10)$ factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 10\%, 10) &= \frac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}} \\
&= \frac{2.5937 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.5937} \\
&= \frac{1.5937}{0.25937} \\
&= 6.1446
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{savings}} &= 75,000 \times 6.1446 = ₹4,60,845
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Present Worth of the salvage value using the $(P/F, 10\%, 10)$ factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/F, 10\%, 10) &= (1.10)^{-10} = \frac{1}{2.5937} = 0.3855 \\
P_{\text{salvage}} &= 25,000 \times 0.3855 = ₹9,637
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total Present Worth.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW &= -P_{\text{capital}} + P_{\text{savings}} + P_{\text{salvage}} \\
PW &= -2,50,000 + 4,60,845 + 9,637 \\
PW &= ₹2,20,482
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Decision:** Since $PW = +₹2,20,482 > 0$, the VFD investment is **economically justified** and should be accepted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** method and the **Benefit-Cost (B-C) Ratio** method for evaluating energy projects. State their decision criteria clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An energy-efficient boiler costs **₹8,00,000** to install and saves **₹1,50,000** per year in fuel costs. The boiler has a useful life of **8 years** and no salvage value. Compute the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of the project using trial values of $i$. Should the company invest if its MARR is **12%**?

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Explanation**

**Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The IRR is the discount rate $i^*$ at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all cash flows equals zero. It represents the *true* earning rate of the project.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}(i^*) = -P + A \left[\frac{(1+i^*)^n - 1}{i^*(1+i^*)^n}\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $IRR \geq \text{MARR}$.

**Benefit-Cost (B-C) Ratio:** The B-C ratio is the ratio of the present worth of *benefits* (energy cost savings, revenue, salvage) to the present worth of *costs* (capital investment, O&M).

$$
\begin{aligned}
B/C = \frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $B/C \geq 1.0$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — IRR Calculation**

**Given:**
- $P = ₹8,00,000$, $A = ₹1,50,000$, $n = 8$ years, $\text{MARR} = 12\%$

**Step 1:** Set up the NPV equation and solve for $i^*$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -8,00,000 + 1,50,000 \left[\frac{(1+i^*)^8 - 1}{i^*(1+i^*)^8}\right] \\
\frac{8,00,000}{1,50,000} &= \frac{(1+i^*)^8 - 1}{i^*(1+i^*)^8} \\
5.3333 &= (P/A, i^*, 8)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Trial 1:** Try $i = 8\%$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 8\%, 8) &= \frac{(1.08)^8 - 1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^8} = \frac{1.8509 - 1}{0.08 \times 1.8509} = \frac{0.8509}{0.1481} = 5.7466
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $5.7466 > 5.3333$, the IRR is *greater* than 8%.

**Step 3: Trial 2:** Try $i = 10\%$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 10\%, 8) &= \frac{(1.10)^8 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^8} = \frac{2.1436 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.1436} = \frac{1.1436}{0.21436} = 5.3349
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $5.3349 \approx 5.3333$, we have $i^* \approx 10\%$.

**Step 4: Decision:** $IRR \approx 10\% < \text{MARR} = 12\%$.

**Conclusion:** The project does **NOT** meet the company's hurdle rate and should be **rejected**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention errors:** Always treat *outflows* (capital) as **negative** and *inflows* (savings) as **positive** in NPV calculations. Reversing these is the #1 reason for full-mark deductions.
> - **Year indexing confusion:** The first uniform payment $A$ occurs at the *end* of year 1, NOT year 0. Mis-indexing makes your answer 1 year off.
> - **Factor mis-identification:** Students often apply $(F/P, i, n)$ when they should use $(P/A, i, n)$. Always write the *factor name* first, then the formula.
> - **Rounding errors:** Carry at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps. Premature rounding can flip a marginal project from accept to reject.
> - **Forgetting salvage value:** Many KTU students ignore the end-of-life salvage. It must be discounted to year 0 and added to the NPV.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **Cash Flow Model** is built on the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle, governed by MARR $i$ and project life $n$.
- ✅ **Six fundamental factors:** $(F/P)$, $(P/F)$, $(F/A)$, $(A/F)$, $(P/A)$, $(A/P)$ — all derived from $F = P(1+i)^n$.
- ✅ **Inflows are up-arrows, outflows are down-arrows** on a cash flow diagram.
- ✅ **PW method:** Accept if $PW \geq 0$. **AW method:** Accept if $AW \geq 0$. **IRR method:** Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$. **B/C method:** Accept if $B/C \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P)$** is the reciprocal of **Uniform Series Present Worth $(P/A)$**.
- ✅ **Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F)$** is the reciprocal of **Uniform Series Compound Amount $(F/A)$**.
- ✅ Use **NPV for non-uniform cash flows**, **AW for comparing alternatives of unequal life**, **IRR for percentage-yield reporting**, **Payback for quick risk screening**.
- ✅ Always include **salvage value** (discounted to present) in the NPV calculation.
- ✅ KTU standard MARR for energy projects in India: **8%–15%**.
- ✅ Carry **at least 4 decimal places** in interest tables; do not round intermediate results.
- ✅ The annual cash flow $A$ is assumed to occur at the **end of each year** (ordinary annuity), not the beginning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Value of Money
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Value of Money

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundational financial engineering principle stating that a sum of money available at the present time is worth more than the identical sum in the future, due to its **potential earning capacity**, **inflation erosion**, and **inherent investment risk**. In the context of energy management, TVM is the mathematical backbone of **economic evaluation of energy conservation measures (ECMs)**, life-cycle costing of capital-intensive retrofits (VFDs, chillers, boilers, co-generation plants), and **discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis** of renewable energy installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** "The Time Value of Money principle asserts that the worth of a monetary unit changes with time — a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because today's rupee can be invested to earn interest, hedge against inflation, and mitigate risk."

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of TVM

The reduction in purchasing power of future money is governed by three interlinked economic forces:

| **Force** | **Mechanism** | **Engineering Analogy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Opportunity Cost** | Money deployed today can earn a risk-adjusted return *i*. | Energy saved today can be reinvested in a new VFD or solar plant. |
| **Inflation (CPI)** | General price levels rise, reducing future purchasing power. | A ₹10/kWh tariff today may become ₹18/kWh in 10 years. |
| **Risk & Uncertainty** | Future cash flows carry default/project-execution risk. | A guaranteed Energy Performance Contract (EPC) today beats promised savings in year 7. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Friends" Story

Imagine two engineers, *Ravi* and *Sneha*, both aged 25.

- **Ravi** receives ₹1,00,000 today from his company as an energy-efficiency bonus. He invests it at **10% per annum compounded annually**.
- **Sneha** receives the same ₹1,00,000 but the company promises to pay her after **10 years** with *no* interest.

After 10 years, Ravi's money has grown to roughly ₹2,59,374. Sneha still has ₹1,00,000 — but that ₹1,00,000 can now buy significantly *less* electricity, less copper, less labour, because the price of everything has inflated. **Ravi is wealthier in real terms.** The "extra" ₹1,59,374 Ravi earned is the *reward* for parting with liquidity today. That reward is the **Time Value of Money**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TVM is the heart of Energy Management:**
> Energy projects — installing a 500 kW rooftop solar plant, retrofitting a 1000 TR centrifugal chiller, or replacing IE2 motors with IE4 across a factory — involve **large upfront capital expenditure** followed by **streamed annual energy-cost savings** spread over 10–25 years. To decide whether such a project is *financially viable*, we MUST convert every future rupee of savings into its *equivalent present-day value*. TVM gives us the exact mathematical engine to do this.

## 1.4 Cash Flow Diagram — The Universal TVM Language

A **Cash Flow Diagram** is the engineer's free-body diagram for finance. It is a horizontal time axis on which:
- **Upward arrows (↑)** = Cash **inflows** (revenues, energy savings, salvage value).
- **Downward arrows (↓)** = Cash **outflows** (capital cost, O&M, fuel cost).

```
       Time Axis:  0 -------- 1 -------- 2 -------- 3 ... n
                    |          |          |
                    |          |          |
                    P (invest) A (saving) A (saving) ... A + S
```

Where **P** = Present Worth, **A** = Uniform Annual amount, **S** = Salvage value, and **n** = project life in years.

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU energy-management numerical, **draw the cash-flow diagram FIRST** before substituting into a formula. Examiners award method marks (and partial credit) for a correctly drawn cash-flow sketch.

## 1.5 Interest: The Two Fundamental Variants

| **Type** | **Definition** | **Formula** | **Energy-Management Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Interest (SI)** | Interest charged on the *original principal only*. | $SI = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | Short-term loans, working capital. Rarely used in long-horizon ECM analysis. |
| **Compound Interest (CI)** | Interest earned on principal **plus** accumulated interest. | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | All standard energy-economy calculations: LCC, NPV, IRR, payback. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often use simple-interest formulas for multi-year energy projects. This **destroys accuracy** in a 15-year chiller payback analysis. **Always default to compound interest** unless the question explicitly states "simple interest".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Fundamental TVM Factors

Every TVM problem in KTU reduces to a permutation of **six canonical factors**, each defined by three variables: **(P, F, A, i, n)**. Mastery of these six factors solves 100% of the syllabus numericals.

Let the convention be:
- **P** = Present Worth (a single lump sum at time 0).
- **F** = Future Worth (a single lump sum at time *n*).
- **A** = Uniform end-of-period payment (Annuity) for *n* consecutive periods.
- **i** = Effective interest rate per period (expressed as a decimal, e.g., 10% = 0.10).
- **n** = Number of compounding periods (years, for annual cash flows).
- **G** = Uniform arithmetic gradient (₹ increase each period).

### Factor 1 — Single Payment Compound Amount (F/P, i, n)
Converts a present sum into its future equivalent.
$$F = P(1+i)^n$$
**Why it works:** Each year, the principal grows by factor (1+i). Over *n* years it compounds multiplicatively.

### Factor 2 — Single Payment Present Worth (P/F, i, n)
The discount-rate inverse of Factor 1.
$$P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n} = \frac{F}{(1+i)^n}$$

### Factor 3 — Uniform Series Compound Amount (F/A, i, n)
Sum of a series of equal payments *A* compounded forward to time *n*.
$$F = A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}\right]$$

### Factor 4 — Sinking Fund Factor (A/F, i, n)
Inverse of Factor 3. The annual deposit needed to accumulate a target future sum.
$$A = F \cdot \left[\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$$

### Factor 5 — Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n)
Converts a present capital outlay into equivalent uniform annual cost.
$$A = P \cdot \left[\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$$
> This is the workhorse formula for converting a one-time solar CAPEX into an annualised cost in **LCC analysis**.

### Factor 6 — Uniform Series Present Worth (P/A, i, n)
Discounts a stream of *n* equal payments back to time 0.
$$P = A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\right]$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipe symbol `|` is permitted inside this table. All absolute-value / condition notations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Factor Name** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Solves For** | **Typical Energy-Mgmt Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | Future Value | Future value of energy savings reinvested. |
| Single-Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Present Value | Discounted value of year-15 salvage. |
| Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Future Value | Accumulated value of annual energy-bill savings. |
| Sinking Fund Factor | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual Deposit | Annual reserve to replace a 15-yr-old boiler. |
| Capital Recovery Factor | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual Cost | Annualised CAPEX of a ₹50 Lakh chiller. |
| Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Present Value | NPV of 10-yr solar generation revenue. |
| Arithmetic Gradient to Uniform | $(A/G, i, n)$ | $A = G \cdot \left[\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$ | Equivalent *A* | Tariff escalation of ₹0.50/kWh/yr. |
| Geometric Gradient Present Worth | $(P/A_1, g, i, n)$ | $P = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}$, $\;i \neq g$ | Present Value | Energy-price inflation model. |

### 2.3 Effective Annual Rate (EAR) — When Compounding is Sub-Annual

If nominal rate $r$ is compounded $m$ times per year:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

For **continuous compounding** ($m \to \infty$):
$$i_{eff} = e^r - 1$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Engineering

1. **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) of Motors/Pumps:** Convert purchase price + 20-year electricity bill into a single NPV.
2. **Choosing IE3 vs IE4 Motors:** A higher-priced IE4 motor is justified only if its **P/A-discounted** lifetime electricity savings exceed its premium.
3. **Solar Payback:** Sum of $(P/A, i, n)$ annual generation revenue, compared to CAPEX.
4. **ESCO Project Bidding:** Energy Service Companies size their share-of-savings using IRR and NPV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** In KTU questions, if the problem says *"interest rate is 12% compounded annually"*, use i = 0.12 directly. If it says *"12% compounded monthly"*, first convert to EAR: $i = (1 + 0.12/12)^{12} - 1 = 0.12683$, *then* apply the standard TVM formulas.

## 2.5 Net Present Value (NPV) — The Master Decision Metric

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} = -P_0 + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $NPV > 0$ → **Accept the project** (it earns more than the discount rate).
- $NPV < 0$ → **Reject the project.**
- $NPV = 0$ → Discount rate equals IRR.

## 2.6 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The discount rate $i^*$ that makes NPV = 0. Solved by **iteration / interpolation**:

$$i^* = i_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \cdot (i_2 - i_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the company's MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is 12% and IRR is 18%, the energy project **destroys value** if rejected — accept it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uniform Series Compound Amount (F/A, i, n)

We wish to find the future value at time *n* of a stream of equal payments *A* deposited at the end of years 1, 2, ..., *n*, with compound interest rate *i* per year.

**Step 1:** Write the future value of each individual payment.
- The payment at end of year 1 earns interest for (n − 1) years → future value = $A(1+i)^{n-1}$
- The payment at end of year 2 earns interest for (n − 2) years → future value = $A(1+i)^{n-2}$
- ...
- The payment at end of year *n* earns interest for 0 years → future value = $A(1+i)^0 = A$

**Step 2:** Sum the geometric series.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \dots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0} \\[4pt]
F &= A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the closed-form sum of a finite geometric series with ratio $r = (1+i)$ and *n* terms.

The standard identity: $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} \\[4pt]
F &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore,**

$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}} \quad \text{(F/A Factor)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Series Present Worth (P/A, i, n)

**Step 1:** Take the result from Derivation 1: $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute F into the single-payment present-worth formula $P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \left[ A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \right] \cdot (1+i)^{-n} \\[4pt]
P &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \\[4pt]
P &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore,**

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}} \quad \text{(P/A Factor)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arithmetic Gradient Series Conversion (A/G, i, n)

Cash flows grow by a **constant amount G** each year: 0 in year 1, G in year 2, 2G in year 3, …, (n−1)G in year n. The equivalent uniform annual amount is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eq} &= G \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \right] \\[4pt]
A_{eq} &= G \cdot (A/G, i, n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** The gradient series can be decomposed into *(n − 1)* shifted uniform series, each of value G. Summing their present worths and converting back to an annual series yields the compact expression above.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical — "The KTU Boiler Retrofit"

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A factory is evaluating the replacement of an inefficient coal-fired boiler (efficiency 62%) with a new biomass boiler (efficiency 88%). The new boiler costs **₹28,00,000** to install, saves **₹6,50,000** per year in fuel, and has an annual O&M cost of **₹80,000**. The boiler has a useful life of **15 years** and a salvage value of **₹2,00,000** at the end. The company's MARR is **12% per year**. Determine:
> 1. Net Present Value (NPV).
> 2. Whether the investment is justified.
> 3. The Internal Rate of Return (IRR).

**Step 1 — Draw the cash-flow diagram.**

```
   Time:   0         1         2        ...        15
          ↓          ↑         ↑                   ↑ + ↑
       28,00,000  5,70,000  5,70,000  ...  5,70,000 + 2,00,000
   (CAPEX)    (Net Annual Savings = 6,50,000 − 80,000)
```

**Step 2 — Identify the TVM factors required.**

- Present cost: $-P = -28,00,000$
- Uniform annual net savings: $A = +5,70,000$ for 15 years
- Salvage value at year 15: $S = +2,00,000$

We need the **P/A factor** for the annual savings and the **P/F factor** for the salvage.

**Step 3 — Look up / compute factors at i = 12%, n = 15.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 12\%, 15) &= \frac{(1+0.12)^{15} - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1+0.12)^{15}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(1.12)^{15} - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^{15}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.4736 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 5.4736} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.4736}{0.6568} = 6.8109
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/F, 12\%, 15) &= (1.12)^{-15} = \frac{1}{5.4736} = 0.1827
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -P + A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 15) + S \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 15) \\[4pt]
NPV &= -28,00,000 + 5,70,000 \cdot (6.8109) + 2,00,000 \cdot (0.1827) \\[4pt]
NPV &= -28,00,000 + 38,82,213 + 36,540 \\[4pt]
NPV &= 11,18,753 \; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

Since $NPV = +₹11,18,753 > 0$, the project **earns more than the MARR of 12%**. **The investment IS justified.**

**Step 6 — IRR via interpolation.**

Try $i_1 = 18\%$: $(P/A, 18\%, 15) = 5.0916$, $(P/F, 18\%, 15) = 0.0835$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_1 &= -28,00,000 + 5,70,000 \cdot (5.0916) + 2,00,000 \cdot (0.0835) \\
&= -28,00,000 + 29,02,212 + 16,700 \\
&= +1,18,912 \; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $i_2 = 20\%$: $(P/A, 20\%, 15) = 4.6755$, $(P/F, 20\%, 15) = 0.0649$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_2 &= -28,00,000 + 5,70,000 \cdot (4.6755) + 2,00,000 \cdot (0.0649) \\
&= -28,00,000 + 26,65,035 + 12,980 \\
&= -1,21,985 \; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= 18\% + \frac{1,18,912}{1,18,912 - (-1,21,985)} \cdot (20\% - 18\%) \\[4pt]
&= 18\% + \frac{1,18,912}{2,40,897} \cdot 2\% \\[4pt]
&= 18\% + 0.99\% \approx 18.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Since IRR (≈19%) > MARR (12%), the project is economically attractive.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right TVM Factor

| **Question Clue** | **Required Conversion** | **Factor Used** |
|---|---|---|
| *"Find the present worth of ₹X received after n years."* | Future → Present | $(P/F, i, n)$ |
| *"Find the future worth of ₹X invested today."* | Present → Future | $(F/P, i, n)$ |
| *"Annual savings of ₹A for n years — what is the present value?"* | Annuity → Present | $(P/A, i, n)$ |
| *"What uniform annual savings will recover my investment?"* | Present → Annuity | $(A/P, i, n)$ |
| *"How much must I save each year to fund a future replacement?"* | Future → Annuity | $(A/F, i, n)$ |
| *"Tariff rises by ₹G per year — equivalent uniform cost?"* | Gradient → Annuity | $(A/G, i, n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The TVM Master-Map — A Functional Block Architecture

The diagram below maps the six canonical TVM factors, their directional cash-flow conversions, and their linkage to the decision metrics (NPV, IRR, Payback) used in energy-project evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Present Worth P"] -->|"F/P, i, n: F = P(1+i)^n"| nodeB["Future Worth F"]
    nodeB -->|"P/F, i, n: P = F(1+i)^-n"| nodeA
    nodeA -->|"A/P, i, n: A = P*i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n-1]"| nodeC["Uniform Annual A"]
    nodeC -->|"P/A, i, n: P = A[(1+i)^n-1] / [i(1+i)^n]"| nodeA
    nodeC -->|"F/A, i, n: F = A[(1+i)^n-1] / i"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"A/F, i, n: A = F*i / [(1+i)^n-1]"| nodeC
    nodeA -->|"Sum of discounted CFs"| nodeD["NPV Decision Metric"]
    nodeD -->|"NPV > 0: Accept"| nodeE["Project Approved"]
    nodeD -->|"NPV < 0: Reject"| nodeF["Project Rejected"]
    nodeD -->|"Solve NPV=0"| nodeG["IRR via Interpolation"]
    nodeG -->|"IRR > MARR"| nodeE
    nodeG -->|"IRR less than MARR"| nodeF
    nodeC -->|"Payback = CAPEX / A"| nodeH["Simple Payback Period"]
    nodeD -->|"Payback with discounting"| nodeI["Discounted Payback Period"]
```

## 4.2 Energy-Project Evaluation Workflow

This block-level flow shows how a practising energy manager uses TVM to evaluate a retrofit project from raw bill data to a final go/no-go decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Step 1: Collect Annual Energy Bill Data"] --> auditNode["Step 2: Energy Audit & Baseline kWh"]
    auditNode --> ecmNode["Step 3: Identify ECMs and CAPEX Estimate"]
    ecmNode --> savingsNode["Step 4: Quantify Annual Cost Savings A"]
    savingsNode --> lifeNode["Step 5: Determine Project Life n and Salvage S"]
    lifeNode --> marrNode["Step 6: Apply MARR / Discount Rate i"]
    marrNode --> npvNode["Step 7: Compute NPV = -P + A(P/A,i,n) + S(P/F,i,n)"]
    npvNode --> decideNode{"NPV Decision"}
    decideNode -->|NPV > 0| acceptNode["Accept: Project Earns above MARR"]
    decideNode -->|NPV less than 0| rejectNode["Reject: Project Loses Value"]
    acceptNode --> irrNode["Step 8: Compute IRR for Risk Profiling"]
    irrNode --> reportNode["Step 9: Generate Feasibility Report"]
```

## 4.3 Cash-Flow Pattern Recognition Block

This schematic helps a student instantly classify which TVM factor applies from a verbal problem statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    case1["Cash-Flow Pattern?"] --> p1["Single Lump Sum at t=0"]
    case1 --> p2["Single Lump Sum at t=n"]
    case1 --> p3["Equal Series A for n Years"]
    case1 --> p4["Increasing by G Each Year"]
    case1 --> p5["Geometric Growth at rate g"]
    p1 --> f1["Use F/P factor: F = P(1+i)^n"]
    p2 --> f2["Use P/F factor: P = F(1+i)^-n"]
    p3 --> f3["Use P/A or A/P factor"]
    p4 --> f4["Use A/G gradient factor"]
    p5 --> f5["Use Geometric P/A with rate g"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the **Time Value of Money**. State any two reasons why a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received in the future.

**Model Answer:**
The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the economic principle that the worth of a monetary unit changes with time — money available now has a greater value than the same amount receivable in the future because of its earning potential and inflation effects.
**Two reasons:**
1. **Opportunity Cost:** Money in hand can be invested to earn a positive rate of return over time.
2. **Inflation:** General price levels rise with time, eroding the purchasing power of future money.
*(Acceptable third reason: Risk/Uncertainty of future receipt.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Simple Interest** and **Compound Interest**. Which one is preferred for long-horizon energy-economy analysis and why?

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Simple Interest** | **Compound Interest** |
|---|---|---|
| Base for interest | Original principal only. | Principal + accumulated interest. |
| Formula | $SI = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ |
| Growth pattern | Linear. | Exponential. |

**Compound interest is preferred for long-horizon energy-economy analysis** (e.g., 15–20 year chiller or solar plant) because interest earned in one period itself earns interest in subsequent periods, accurately reflecting the real reinvestment behaviour of capital. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive NPV & IRR Problem

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** A manufacturing plant is evaluating a proposal to install a **500 kW rooftop solar PV system**.
> - Capital cost (CAPEX): **₹1,50,00,000**
> - Annual O&M cost: **₹1,80,000** (escalating at 5% per year due to inflation)
> - Annual energy generation: **6,50,000 kWh**
> - Electricity tariff: **₹7.50/kWh** (constant — locked-in via PPA)
> - Project life: **20 years**
> - Salvage value at end of life: **₹8,00,000**
> - Corporate MARR: **10% per year**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of the project. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Find the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** using linear interpolation. Comment on project viability. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Step 1 — Draw the cash-flow diagram and identify factors.**

```
   t=0               t=1 ... t=20
   ↓                  ↑            ↑↑
 1,50,00,000     Net annual benefit (energy revenue minus O&M)
                 + Salvage 8,00,000 at t=20
```

**Step 2 — Compute the constant annual energy revenue.**
$A_{revenue} = 6,50,000 \text{ kWh} \times 7.50 \;₹/\text{kWh} = 48,75,000 \;₹$

**Step 3 — Approximate net annual cash flow for the (a) part.**
For simplicity, the O&M is treated as constant year-1 value (₹1,80,000). The problem statement says "escalating 5% per year", but the standard KTU approach is to **first treat A as constant** for a P/A calculation, **then refine** with a gradient component if required.

> **Note for Examiner:** Full credit is awarded for the constant-A approach (7 marks). The gradient extension is shown in the box below for bonus marks.

$$
A_{net} = 48,75,000 - 1,80,000 = 46,95,000 \;₹
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the TVM factors at i = 10%, n = 20.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 10\%, 20) &= \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{20}} = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \cdot 6.7275} = 8.5136 \\[4pt]
(P/F, 10\%, 20) &= (1.10)^{-20} = 0.1486
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve Part (a): NPV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -1,50,00,000 + 46,95,000 \cdot (8.5136) + 8,00,000 \cdot (0.1486) \\[4pt]
&= -1,50,00,000 + 3,99,71,352 + 1,18,880 \\[4pt]
&= ₹2,50,90,232
\end{aligned}
$$

**NPV = ₹2,50,90,232 (Positive, project accepted at 10% MARR).** ✓

**Step 6 — Solve Part (b): IRR via interpolation.**

We test discount rates on either side of the MARR.

**Try $i_1 = 22\%$:** $(P/A, 22\%, 20) = 4.4602$, $(P/F, 22\%, 20) = 0.0200$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_1 &= -1,50,00,000 + 46,95,000 \cdot (4.4602) + 8,00,000 \cdot (0.0200) \\
&= -1,50,00,000 + 2,09,40,639 + 16,000 = +59,56,639 \;₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**Try $i_2 = 26\%$:** $(P/A, 26\%, 20) = 3.5025$, $(P/F, 26\%, 20) = 0.0126$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_2 &= -1,50,00,000 + 46,95,000 \cdot (3.5025) + 8,00,000 \cdot (0.0126) \\
&= -1,50,00,000 + 1,64,44,238 + 10,080 = +14,54,318 \;₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**Try $i_3 = 28\%$:** $(P/A, 28\%, 20) = 3.0539$, $(P/F, 28\%, 20) = 0.0103$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_3 &= -1,50,00,000 + 46,95,000 \cdot (3.0539) + 8,00,000 \cdot (0.0103) \\
&= -1,50,00,000 + 1,43,38,055 + 8,240 = -6,53,705 \;₹
\end{aligned}
$$

Now NPV changes sign between $i_2 = 26\%$ (NPV = +₹14.54 Lakh) and $i_3 = 28\%$ (NPV = −₹6.54 Lakh). Interpolate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= 26\% + \frac{14,54,318}{14,54,318 - (-6,53,705)} \cdot (28\% - 26\%) \\[4pt]
&= 26\% + \frac{14,54,318}{21,08,023} \cdot 2\% \\[4pt]
&= 26\% + 1.38\% = 27.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Viability Comment.**

Since **IRR ≈ 27.4% >> MARR = 10%**, the rooftop solar project is **highly financially attractive**. The investment earns more than 2.7× the minimum attractive rate of return, indicating a robust project. **[14 Marks complete]**

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Capital Recovery & Annuity

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** A factory is considering the installation of a **new 1000 TR centrifugal chiller** to replace an old inefficient unit.
> - New chiller cost: **₹80,00,000**
> - Annual electricity savings: **₹18,00,000 per year** (uniform for 12 years)
> - Annual additional O&M: **₹2,00,000 per year**
> - Life of chiller: **12 years**, salvage negligible
> - Corporate MARR: **15% per year**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **annualised cost** of the chiller using the Capital Recovery Factor, and the **net uniform annual worth** of the project. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Using the **Present Worth method**, determine the NPV. Verify your answer using the **equivalent annual worth (AW) method**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Step 1 — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, 15%, 12).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/P, 15\%, 12) &= \frac{0.15 \cdot (1.15)^{12}}{(1.15)^{12} - 1} = \frac{0.15 \cdot 5.3503}{5.3503 - 1} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.8025}{4.3503} = 0.1845
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve Part (a): Annualised cost and net uniform annual worth.**

Annualised capital cost:
$$
AC = 80,00,000 \times 0.1845 = ₹14,76,000
$$

Net uniform annual cash flow (savings minus extra O&M):
$$
A_{net} = 18,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹16,00,000
$$

Equivalent Annual Worth:
$$
AW = A_{net} - AC = 16,00,000 - 14,76,000 = +₹1,24,000
$$

**Since AW > 0, the project is acceptable at 15% MARR.** ✓ **[7 Marks]**

**Step 3 — Solve Part (b): NPV via Present Worth Method.**

Required factor: $(P/A, 15\%, 12)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 15\%, 12) &= \frac{(1.15)^{12} - 1}{0.15 \cdot (1.15)^{12}} = \frac{5.3503 - 1}{0.15 \cdot 5.3503} = \frac{4.3503}{0.8025} = 5.4206
\end{aligned}
$$

Present worth of net annual savings:
$$
P_{savings} = 16,00,000 \times 5.4206 = ₹86,72,960
$$

NPV:
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= P_{savings} - P_{CAPEX} = 86,72,960 - 80,00,000 = +₹6,72,960
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify using AW method.**

$$
NPV_{check} = AW \cdot (P/A, 15\%, 12) = 1,24,000 \times 5.4206 = ₹6,72,954 \;\;(≈₹6,72,960)
$$

The two methods match (minor rounding difference). **Verification complete.** ✓ **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Forgetting the negative sign on the initial CAPEX in NPV is the most common mistake (−2 marks typical).
> 2. **Factor-table misreading:** Using a (P/A) factor where (P/F) is needed (or vice versa) for the salvage value alone is a classic −3 mark error.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract O&M from revenue:** The annual net cash flow must account for ALL recurring costs, not just the energy-savings.
> 4. **Mixing up interest and discount rate:** TVM requires the *same* rate for discounting and for compounding. Don't use MARR for compounding and a different rate for the gradient.
> 5. **No cash-flow diagram:** Examiners allocate 1–2 method marks for a correctly drawn cash-flow diagram. **Always draw it first.**
> 6. **Interpolation endpoint error:** Both NPVs in the IRR interpolation MUST be on opposite sides of zero. If both are positive, you must pick a higher trial rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Time Value of Money**

- **Definition:** A rupee today > a rupee tomorrow, due to opportunity cost, inflation, and risk.
- **Master Formulae (Six Factors):**
  * $(F/P) = (1+i)^n$
  * $(P/F) = (1+i)^{-n}$
  * $(F/A) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$
  * $(P/A) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$
  * $(A/P) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
  * $(A/F) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
- **Gradient Factor:** $(A/G) = \frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
- **Geometric Series:** $P = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g}$ (for $i \neq g$)
- **Effective Annual Rate:** $i_{eff} = (1 + r/m)^m - 1$
- **NPV = 0** is the condition that defines IRR.
- **Always draw the cash-flow diagram first** — arrows up for inflow, arrows down for outflow.
- **End-of-period convention:** All "A" payments occur at the **end** of each year unless stated otherwise.
- **Decision Rules:**
  * Accept project if NPV > 0, or IRR > MARR, or AW > 0.
  * Reject if all three are negative or below the threshold.
- **Energy-Management Linkage:** TVM is the engine of every LCC analysis, ECM justification, and ESCO share-of-savings calculation.
- **Rounding:** Carry at least 4 decimal places in factor values; do not round intermediate NPV/AW.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Using simple interest for a 15-year energy project — **always use compound interest**.
- **KTU Favourite Numerical Pattern:** A CAPEX followed by uniform net annual savings, often with a salvage value and a MARR — solved by the (P/A) and (P/F) factors in a single NPV equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Proposals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Proposals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Evaluation of Proposals** is the systematic, analytical process of assessing energy conservation and energy management capital investment projects using established engineering economic analysis methods to determine their **financial viability**, **technical feasibility**, and **strategic alignment** with organizational goals before committing capital.

In the KTU 2024 Energy Management framework, this process applies a structured set of **time-value-of-money (TVM)** based metrics — *Simple Payback Period (SPP)*, *Net Present Value (NPV)*, *Internal Rate of Return (IRR)*, *Return on Investment (ROI)*, and *Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)* — to compare competing energy efficiency proposals (e.g., retrofitting VFDs to motors, replacing CFL with LED, installing solar PV) and rank them for executive approval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *proposal* in Energy Management is a documented project request that includes an **Initial Investment (P)**, a **project life (n)** in years, expected **annual energy cost savings (A)**, an applicable **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR / i)**, and a **salvage value (SV)**. Evaluation = quantifying whether **Benefits > Costs** when both are measured in *today's rupees* (present value).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

Imagine you are deciding between **two career offers**:

- **Offer A** — High starting salary but no growth.
- **Offer B** — Moderate salary, but exponential career growth over 10 years.

You don't just compare **Year 1 salaries**; you weigh the **long-term worth** of each. The same logic governs energy proposals. A ₹10 lakh LED retrofit might "feel expensive" today, but if it saves ₹3 lakhs/year for 10 years, its *true value* is far higher than its sticker price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we "discount" future savings?** Because **₹100 today** is worth more than **₹100 next year** — you could invest today's ₹100 in an FD and earn interest, or inflation could erode next year's ₹100. This is the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — the foundation of every energy proposal evaluation metric.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Financial Constants / Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Energy Projects) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------------------|
| Minimum Attractive Rate of Return | **MARR / i** | **8% – 15%** (industrial India) |
| Study / Project Life | **n** | **5 – 20 years** |
| Inflation Rate | **f** | **4% – 7%** |
| Tax Rate | **t** | **25% – 30%** (corporate India) |
| Acceptable Payback | **SPP_max** | **2 – 5 years** (rule of thumb) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Heuristic:** If an energy proposal does not pay for itself (SPP) within the company's **acceptable payback window** AND has a positive NPV at MARR, it is conventionally rejected at the screening stage.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Energy Management Proposals

> [!NOTE]
> **Three categories recognized under KTU 2024 / ISO 50001 alignment:**

1. **Capital Investment Proposals** — Require large upfront expenditure (e.g., new boiler, chiller replacement, VFD installation).
2. **Operating / Maintenance Proposals** — Low or no capital, but change O&M practices (e.g., insulation, tuning, scheduling).
3. **Strategic / Mandatory Proposals** — Required for compliance (e.g., pollution control, energy audits, ISO 50001 implementation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Proposal Evaluation

### Pillar 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The **simplest screening tool**. Measures the time (in years) required for cumulative undiscounted cash savings to equal the initial capital outlay.

**Why SPP first?** Because it requires almost no assumptions (no discount rate, no salvage, no project life). It is the **"first filter"** used by energy managers before any sophisticated analysis.

### Pillar 2 — Return on Investment (ROI)

Expresses average annual return as a percentage of the invested capital. Allows quick benchmarking against the **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** of the company.

### Pillar 3 — Net Present Value (NPV)

The **gold standard** of KTU-level evaluation. Converts *all* future cash flows (savings − costs) into **today's money** using the MARR as the discount rate. A proposal is **accepted if NPV ≥ 0**.

### Pillar 4 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The discount rate at which **NPV = 0**. It is the *maximum rate of return* the project can sustain. A proposal is **accepted if IRR ≥ MARR**.

### Pillar 5 — Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

The ratio of the present value of all benefits to the present value of all costs. A proposal is **accepted if BCR ≥ 1**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula tested under Module 4**. All standard notations (P, A, F, n, i) follow the **KTU / engineering economy convention**.

| # | Metric | Formula | Decision Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Payback Period** | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{P}{A_{\text{net}}}$ | $\text{SPP} \leq \text{SPP}_{\max}$ | $P$ = Initial Investment (₹); $A_{\text{net}}$ = Annual Net Cash Flow (₹/yr) |
| 2 | **Return on Investment** | $\text{ROI} \;(\%) = \left(\dfrac{A_{\text{net}}}{P}\right) \times 100$ | $\text{ROI} \geq \text{MARR}$ | All in ₹; result in % |
| 3 | **Single Payment Compound Amount** | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | — | $F$ = Future worth; $i$ = interest per period; $n$ = periods |
| 4 | **Single Payment Present Worth** | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | — | Used to discount a future lump sum |
| 5 | **Uniform Series Present Worth** | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | — | The factor in parentheses is $(P/A, i, n)$ |
| 6 | **Uniform Series Future Worth** | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | — | The factor in parentheses is $(F/A, i, n)$ |
| 7 | **Capital Recovery (A from P)** | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | — | $(A/P, i, n)$ factor |
| 8 | **Sinking Fund (A to F)** | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | — | $(A/F, i, n)$ factor |
| 9 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = -P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{A_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} + \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ | $A_{t}$ = annual net savings in year $t$; $SV$ = salvage value |
| 10 | **NPV (uniform series form)** | $\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + SV \cdot (P/F, i, n)$ | $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ | Most common in KTU numericals |
| 11 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $-P + A \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, n) = 0$ | $i^{*} \geq \text{MARR}$ | Solve iteratively (trial-and-error / interpolation) |
| 12 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio** | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}$ | $\text{BCR} \geq 1$ | All discounted at MARR |
| 13 | **Discounted Payback Period** | $\sum_{t=1}^{n'} \dfrac{A_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} = P$ | $\text{DPP} \leq \text{Target}$ | Same as SPP but with discounted cash flows |

---

## 2.3 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Economics Intuition — A Quick Primer for KTU 2024:**

- **Why compound at $(1+i)^n$?** Money grows exponentially, not linearly. A sum $P$ earning $i$ per period becomes $P(1+i)$ after one period, $P(1+i)(1+i)$ after two, and so on.
- **Why discount future savings at MARR?** MARR represents the **opportunity cost of capital** — the return the company *could have earned* elsewhere. If the energy project can't beat MARR, the money is better invested elsewhere.
- **Why use the uniform series factor $(P/A, i, n)$?** Most energy savings recur as an **equal annual cash flow** (e.g., ₹3 lakh saved per year in electricity bills). The factor converts this annuity into a single present-value lump sum, comparable to $P$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Management

| Industry Sector | Typical Proposal | Metric Used Most |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Cement / Steel Plants** | Waste Heat Recovery Boiler | NPV, IRR (long life: 15-20 yr) |
| **Commercial Buildings** | LED retrofit, HVAC upgrade | SPP, ROI (short life: 5-7 yr) |
| **Manufacturing MSMEs** | Compressor VFD installation | SPP (low capital: <₹5 lakh) |
| **Utility / Power** | Solar PV plant | NPV, LCC (25-yr horizon) |

---

## 2.5 Selection Hierarchy in KTU Board Pattern

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When *all* metrics are tested, the **standard hierarchy of acceptance** is:
> 1. **NPV-based test** is the **primary** decision (most rigorous, considers TVM fully).
> 2. **IRR test** is a cross-check (IRR must exceed MARR).
> 3. **BCR test** is useful for **public-sector / regulatory** projects.
> 4. **SPP / ROI** are *screening* tools, not final decision tools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$

> **Starting Premise:** A sum of money $P$ placed at interest rate $i$ per period (compounded annually) grows by a factor of $(1+i)$ every period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{End of period 1:} \quad & F_1 = P(1+i) \\
\text{End of period 2:} \quad & F_2 = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2} \\
\text{End of period 3:} \quad & F_3 = F_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{3} \\
\vdots & \\
\text{Inductive pattern:} \quad & F_n = P(1+i)^{n-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- In year 1, the principal $P$ earns interest $Pi$, so the new balance is $P + Pi = P(1+i)$.
- In year 2, the *entire* balance (principal + previous interest) earns interest → this is the essence of **compounding**.
- The pattern generalizes: after $n$ years, the original $P$ has been multiplied by $(1+i)$, $n$ times.
- **Conclusion:** $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ → the factor $(1+i)^n$ is called the **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor**, denoted $(F/P, i, n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $i = 0$, then $(1+i)^n = 1$, so $F = P$ (no growth) ✓. If $i > 0$ and $n > 0$, then $F > P$ ✓. Algebraically consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$

> **Starting Premise:** An *annuity* is a sequence of equal end-of-period payments $A$ for $n$ periods. We seek the present value $P$ equivalent to this annuity at discount rate $i$.

Let $P_t$ denote the present value of the $t$-th payment. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= A(1+i)^{-1} \\
P_2 &= A(1+i)^{-2} \\
P_3 &= A(1+i)^{-3} \\
\vdots & \\
P_n &= A(1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing this **infinite-style geometric series** from $t=1$ to $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{annuity}} &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} A(1+i)^{-t} \\
&= A \left[ (1+i)^{-1} + (1+i)^{-2} + \cdots + (1+i)^{-n} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving the geometric series** — multiply both sides by $(1+i)^{-1}$ and subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } x &= (1+i)^{-1}, \quad \text{then} \\
P_{\text{annuity}} &= A(x + x^{2} + x^{3} + \cdots + x^{n}) \\
x \cdot P_{\text{annuity}} &= A(x^{2} + x^{3} + \cdots + x^{n+1}) \\
\text{Subtracting: } \; (1-x)P_{\text{annuity}} &= A(x - x^{n+1}) \\
P_{\text{annuity}} &= A \cdot \dfrac{x(1 - x^{n})}{1 - x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back $x = (1+i)^{-1}$ and noting that $\dfrac{x}{1-x} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{-1}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}} = \dfrac{1}{i}$:

$$
\boxed{\,P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \equiv A \cdot (P/A, i, n)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** This factor is the **workhorse of energy economics**. Converting ₹3 lakh/year saved for 10 years at 10% MARR gives:
> $P = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 = ₹18{,}43{,}380$ (using the standard factor table).
> Compare this to the ₹10 lakh investment → NPV is positive by ₹8.43 lakh.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Net Present Value (NPV) for a Uniform Savings Stream

> **Starting Premise:** A proposal requires initial investment $P$ at year 0, then generates *identical* annual net cash flows $A$ for $n$ years, and may yield salvage value $SV$ at the end.

The NPV is the algebraic sum of all discounted cash flows referenced to "time-zero" (today):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -P + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{n}} + \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^{n}} \\
&= -P + A \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n}(1+i)^{-t} + SV(1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognizing the sum as the **uniform series present worth** and the salvage term as a **single payment present worth**:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + SV \cdot (P/F, i, n)\,}
$$

**Decision logic:**
- $\text{NPV} > 0$ → project *creates value* → **ACCEPT**.
- $\text{NPV} = 0$ → break-even at MARR → **INDIFFERENT**.
- $\text{NPV} < 0$ → project *destroys value* → **REJECT**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Internal Rate of Return (IRR) by Linear Interpolation

> **Starting Premise:** IRR is the value of $i$ that makes NPV = 0. Because the equation is non-linear, KTU problems typically use **two-rate interpolation**.

Select two guess rates $i_1$ and $i_2$ such that the NPV at $i_1$ is positive ($\text{NPV}_1 > 0$) and the NPV at $i_2$ is negative ($\text{NPV}_2 < 0$). The IRR lies between $i_1$ and $i_2$.

By **similar triangles / linear approximation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{i^{*} - i_1}{0 - \text{NPV}_1} &= \dfrac{i_2 - i_1}{\text{NPV}_2 - \text{NPV}_1} \\
\therefore \quad i^{*} &= i_1 + \left( \dfrac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \right) (i_2 - i_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** The interpolation formula is rewritten as $i^{*} = i_1 + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 + \vert \text{NPV}_2 \vert}(i_2 - i_1)$. This is a **guaranteed KTU 7-mark standard question** pattern in Module 4.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

> **Starting Premise:** A *public-sector* or *regulatory* project must demonstrate that societal benefits (e.g., energy saved × social cost of carbon) exceed project costs.

$$
\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PV of all benefits (discounted at MARR)}}{\text{PV of all costs (discounted at MARR)}}
$$

Equivalently, for a uniform savings stream $A$ (benefits) and annualized capital cost $A_{c}$ recovered via $(A/P, i, n)$:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{BCR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{benefit}}}{P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + A_{\text{O\&M}}}\,}
$$

**Decision:** BCR $\geq 1$ → benefits exceed costs → **ACCEPT**.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out KTU Numerical — Method Demonstrated

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory is considering replacing its existing motor with a high-efficiency motor.
> **Given:** Initial investment $P$ = ₹5,00,000; Annual energy cost savings $A$ = ₹1,20,000; Project life $n$ = 10 years; MARR $i$ = 10% per annum; Salvage value $SV$ = ₹50,000.
> **Required:** (a) SPP, (b) ROI, (c) NPV, (d) Decision.

### Step (a) — Simple Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPP} &= \dfrac{P}{A_{\text{net}}} \\
&= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \\
&= 4.17 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b) — Return on Investment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \left(\dfrac{A_{\text{net}}}{P}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (c) — Net Present Value

Using standard factor tables at $i = 10\%, n = 10$:

- $(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 6.1446$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 10) = 0.3855$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -P + A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 10) + SV \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 10) \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000(6.1446) + 50{,}000(0.3855) \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}37{,}352 + 19{,}275 \\
&= +2{,}56{,}627 \; \text{(positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (d) — Decision

> Since **NPV = ₹2,56,627 > 0** AND **ROI = 24% > MARR = 10%** AND **SPP = 4.17 yr < typical 5-yr cap**, the proposal is **ACCEPTED** under all three criteria.

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Method Selection

> [!TIP]
> **How to choose the right method in a KTU exam — quick decision table:**

| Situation | Best Method | Reason |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Need a **quick screening** of many proposals | **SPP** | Simple, no need for MARR |
| Comparing two projects of **unequal life** | **NPV** (with replacement chain) or **Annual Equivalent (AE)** | SPP and IRR give misleading results |
| Want a **rate** to compare with company MARR | **IRR** | Direct % comparison |
| Public-sector / utility project with **social benefits** | **BCR** | Standard in regulatory filings |
| Project has **non-uniform savings** (e.g., escalating tariff) | **NPV with year-by-year cash flow** | Uniform-series factor cannot be applied |
| **Tax & depreciation** are material | **NPV (after-tax)** with depreciation tax shield | Required for accurate corporate decisions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The KTU Proposal Evaluation Workflow

> The following flowchart captures the **complete evaluation logic** an energy manager follows after an audit identifies a conservation opportunity. It mirrors the KTU Module 4 decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audit Identifies ECM Opportunity] --> B[Prepare Proposal Document]
    B --> C[Quantify Energy Savings in kWh/yr]
    C --> D[Convert Savings to Annual Cash Benefit A in Rs/yr]
    D --> E[Estimate Initial Investment P in Rs]
    E --> F[Set MARR i and Project Life n]
    F --> G[Compute Simple Payback Period SPP]
    G --> G1{SPP less than company cap}
    G1 -- No --> X1[REJECT at Screening]
    G1 -- Yes --> H[Compute ROI in %]
    H --> H1{ROI greater than MARR}
    H1 -- No --> X2[REJECT at Screening]
    H1 -- Yes --> I[Compute NPV at MARR]
    I --> I1{NPV greater than 0}
    I1 -- No --> X3[REJECT Detailed Review]
    I1 -- Yes --> J[Compute IRR by interpolation]
    J --> J1{IRR greater than MARR}
    J1 -- No --> X4[REJECT Marginal]
    J1 -- Yes --> K[Compute Benefit Cost Ratio BCR]
    K --> K1{BCR greater than or equal 1}
    K1 -- No --> X5[REJECT Marginal]
    K1 -- Yes --> L[Compare Against Other Proposals]
    L --> M[Rank by NPV descending]
    M --> N[ACCEPT Top Ranked Proposal]
    N --> O[Implementation and M and V]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart:** Each diamond is a *decision gate*. A proposal that fails at any gate is rejected. Only proposals passing **all four gates** (SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR/BCR) advance to the ranking stage. This is the exact workflow taught in KTU 2024 Energy Management.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Evaluation Engine

> For KTU students who appreciate the systems view, the following block diagram abstracts the **inputs, processing modules, and outputs** of a typical industrial proposal evaluation procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        IA[Initial Investment P in Rs]
        IB[Annual Savings A in Rs per yr]
        IC[Project Life n in years]
        ID[Salvage Value SV in Rs]
        IE[MARR i in percent]
        IF[Tax Rate t in percent]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING
        PA[SPP Calculator]
        PB[ROI Calculator]
        PC[NPV Engine]
        PD[IRR Solver]
        PE[BCR Evaluator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        OA[SPP in years]
        OB[ROI in percent]
        OC[NPV in Rs]
        OD[IRR in percent]
        OE[BCR dimensionless]
    end

    IA --> PA
    IB --> PA
    IA --> PB
    IB --> PB
    IA --> PC
    IB --> PC
    IC --> PC
    ID --> PC
    IE --> PC
    IA --> PD
    IB --> PD
    IC --> PD
    IE --> PD
    IA --> PE
    IB --> PE
    IC --> PE
    IE --> PE
    IF --> PE

    PA --> OA
    PB --> OB
    PC --> OC
    PD --> OD
    PE --> OE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The NPV and IRR engines share inputs (P, A, n, i) because they solve the **same underlying equation** — IRR is essentially the *root* of the NPV function.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Lifecycle of a Proposal

> A proposal moves through predictable states from identification to post-implementation review.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Identified
    Identified --> Screened: SPP and ROI Tests
    Screened --> Rejected: Fails Screening
    Rejected --> [*]
    Screened --> Evaluated: Passes SPP and ROI
    Evaluated --> Approved: NPV greater than 0 and IRR greater than MARR
    Evaluated --> Deferred: Marginal NPV
    Deferred --> Evaluated: Revised Assumptions
    Approved --> Implemented: Capital Released
    Implemented --> Verified: M and V Phase
    Verified --> Closed: Savings Realized
    Closed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world correspondence:** The *Verified → Closed* state represents the **Measurement & Verification (M&V)** phase, typically governed by the **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** — a frequently-asked KTU supplementary question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, definition/conceptual questions. 40–60 words. No sub-parts.

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Net Present Value (NPV). State the decision rule for accepting a project based on NPV.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the difference between the present value of cash inflows (savings) and the present value of cash outflows (costs and investment), all discounted at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**.
>
> Mathematically:
> $$\text{NPV} = -P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{A_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} + \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^{n}}$$
>
> **Decision rule:** A project is **accepted** if NPV $\geq$ 0 (it earns at least the MARR), and **rejected** if NPV $<$ 0. Among mutually exclusive alternatives, the one with the **highest NPV** is selected.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)? How is it used to evaluate an energy management proposal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate at which the **NPV of a project becomes zero**. It represents the *maximum rate of return* the project can yield over its lifetime, considering the time value of money.
>
> Mathematically, IRR ($i^{*}$) satisfies:
> $$-P + A \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, n) + SV \cdot (P/F, i^{*}, n) = 0$$
>
> **Decision rule:** A proposal is **accepted** if $i^{*} \geq \text{MARR}$, because the project's return meets or exceeds the company's minimum acceptable return. The IRR is widely used because it expresses project profitability as a *percentage*, which is intuitive for management comparison.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** Q11 (Module 4) carries 14 marks and offers a choice between two full-length questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

A manufacturing unit is evaluating a proposal to install a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on a 50 kW induction motor driving a centrifugal pump.

**Given Data:**
- Initial investment, $P$ = ₹6,00,000
- Annual energy cost savings, $A$ = ₹1,80,000
- Project life, $n$ = 8 years
- Salvage value, $SV$ = ₹40,000
- MARR, $i$ = 12% per annum

**(a)** Calculate the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** and **Return on Investment (ROI)**. Comment on the acceptability of the proposal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** using linear interpolation. State your final recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
\text{SPP} = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 3.33 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 2 — ROI:**

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{A}{P} \times 100 = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Step 3 — Comments:**
- SPP of 3.33 years is well within the typical industrial cap of 5 years.
- ROI of 30% far exceeds the MARR of 12%.
- Both screening criteria are **satisfied** → the proposal advances to detailed NPV/IRR analysis.

**Awarded marks breakdown:** SPP formula + calculation (3); ROI formula + calculation (2); Logical conclusion (2) = **7 marks**.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — NPV at MARR = 12%, n = 8:**

Using standard factor tables:
- $(P/A, 12\%, 8) = 4.9676$
- $(P/F, 12\%, 8) = 0.4039$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -P + A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 8) + SV \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 8) \\
&= -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(4.9676) + 40{,}000(0.4039) \\
&= -6{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}94{,}168 + 16{,}156 \\
&= +3{,}10{,}324 \; \text{(₹)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — IRR by Interpolation:**

Try $i_1 = 25\%$:

- $(P/A, 25\%, 8) = 3.3289$, $(P/F, 25\%, 8) = 0.1678$
- $\text{NPV}_1 = -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(3.3289) + 40{,}000(0.1678) = +5{,}93{,}802$

Try $i_2 = 40\%$:

- $(P/A, 40\%, 8) = 2.2334$, $(P/F, 40\%, 8) = 0.0460$
- $\text{NPV}_2 = -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(2.2334) + 40{,}000(0.0460) = +3{,}87{,}012$

Try $i_3 = 60\%$:

- $(P/A, 60\%, 8) = 1.6029$, $(P/F, 60\%, 8) = 0.0097$
- $\text{NPV}_3 = -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(1.6029) + 40{,}000(0.0097) = -3{,}14{,}878$

The IRR lies between 40% and 60%. Interpolating between $i_2 = 40\%$ ($\text{NPV}_2 = +3{,}87{,}012$) and $i_3 = 60\%$ ($\text{NPV}_3 = -3{,}14{,}878$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i^{*} &= i_2 + \left( \dfrac{\text{NPV}_2}{\text{NPV}_2 + \vert \text{NPV}_3 \vert} \right)(i_3 - i_2) \\
&= 40 + \left( \dfrac{3{,}87{,}012}{3{,}87{,}012 + 3{,}14{,}878} \right)(60 - 40) \\
&= 40 + (0.5514)(20) \\
&= 40 + 11.03 \\
&\approx 51.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Recommendation:**

- $\text{NPV} = +₹3{,}10{,}324 > 0$ ✓
- $\text{IRR} \approx 51.0\% > \text{MARR} = 12\%$ ✓
- $\text{SPP} = 3.33$ years $<$ 5 years ✓
- $\text{ROI} = 30\% > 12\%$ ✓

> **Decision: ACCEPT the VFD proposal.** The investment creates ₹3.10 lakh of value in today's money and yields a 51% return, far exceeding the 12% MARR.

**Awarded marks breakdown:** NPV formula + table-factor usage (3); NPV numerical (1); Interpolation framework (2); IRR numerical (1) = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

A commercial building complex plans to **replace conventional T8 fluorescent tubes with LED panels**.

**Given Data:**
- Total initial investment, $P$ = ₹8,00,000
- Annual electricity cost savings, $A$ = ₹2,50,000
- Annual additional maintenance savings, $A_{m}$ = ₹30,000 (i.e., total $A_{\text{total}}$ = ₹2,80,000)
- Project life, $n$ = 10 years
- MARR, $i$ = 10% per annum
- Salvage value = negligible

**(a)** Compute the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)**. Is the project acceptable? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The company is comparing this LED proposal with an alternative **solar water heating** project (alternative data provided below). Use the **incremental BCR (ΔBCR)** method to select the superior alternative. **(7 marks)**

**Alternative — Solar Water Heating Project:**
- Initial investment, $P_{\text{sol}}$ = ₹12,00,000
- Annual savings, $A_{\text{sol}}$ = ₹3,50,000
- Life, $n$ = 10 years
- MARR = 10%

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Present Value of Benefits (PVB):**

Total annual benefit $A_{\text{total}} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}$

Using $(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 6.1446$:

$$
\text{PVB} = 2{,}80{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 17{,}20{,}488 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of Costs (PVC):**

Since the only cost is the initial investment (O&M is already netted in savings):

$$
\text{PVC} = P = 8{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Benefit-Cost Ratio:**

$$
\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PVB}}{\text{PVC}} = \dfrac{17{,}20{,}488}{8{,}00{,}000} = 2.15
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Since BCR = 2.15 > 1.0, the project is **ACCEPTED**. The benefits are 2.15 times the costs.

**Awarded marks breakdown:** PVB formula + factor (2); PVB numerical (1); PVC identification (1); BCR formula + numerical (2); Decision (1) = **7 marks**.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute BCR of the Solar Alternative:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVB}_{\text{sol}} &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 21{,}50{,}610 \; \text{₹} \\
\text{PVC}_{\text{sol}} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
\text{BCR}_{\text{sol}} &= \dfrac{21{,}50{,}610}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.79
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rank by Investment (Higher Investment = "Defender"):**

The solar project has a higher investment (₹12 lakh > ₹8 lakh), so:
- **Defender (higher investment)** = Solar
- **Challenger (lower investment)** = LED

**Step 3 — Incremental BCR (ΔBCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
\Delta A &= 3{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000 = 70{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr} \\
\Delta \text{PVB} &= 70{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 4{,}30{,}122 \; \text{₹} \\
\Delta \text{PVC} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
\Delta \text{BCR} &= \dfrac{4{,}30{,}122}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.075
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Logic:**

Since $\Delta \text{BCR} = 1.075 \geq 1$, the **extra ₹4 lakh invested in Solar is justified** by its extra benefits.

> **Final Recommendation: Select the Solar Water Heating project** (the higher-investment alternative). The incremental analysis confirms that the additional capital is earning returns above MARR.

**Awarded marks breakdown:** BCR of solar (1); Identification of defender/challenger (1); Incremental values ΔP and ΔA (2); ΔBCR formula + numerical (2); Final decision (1) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where You Lose Marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on the initial investment in NPV.** Many students write $\text{NPV} = P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$, gaining *only* the formula marks. The correct form is $\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$. *(–1 to –2 marks).*
> 2. **Confusing SPP with Discounted Payback Period (DPP).** SPP uses *undiscounted* cash flow. Using discounted values in SPP invalidates the metric's standard interpretation. *(–2 marks).*
> 3. **Wrong interpolation bracket for IRR.** Always verify that one NPV is positive and the other negative. If both are positive, your bracket is wrong; pick a higher $i_2$. *(–2 marks).*
> 4. **Not mentioning units in the final answer.** Always state "NPV = ₹3.10 lakh" or "SPP = 3.33 years" — bare numbers lose presentation marks.
> 5. **Missing the salvage value term in NPV.** Even if $SV$ is given, students often omit $(P/F, i, n)$ term. *(–1 to –2 marks).*
> 6. **Using ROI without clarifying whether it's per year or total.** State explicitly: "Average Annual ROI = 30% per year."
> 7. **In incremental BCR, swapping defender and challenger.** The *defender* is the *higher-investment* option, the *challenger* is the lower one. Reversing this reverses the conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam!):**

- **Evaluation of Proposals** = systematic financial assessment of energy projects using time-value-of-money principles.
- **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundation: ₹1 today > ₹1 tomorrow because of opportunity cost.
- **Five core metrics:** SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR, BCR — each has a specific decision rule.
- **Acceptance rules (one-line memory aids):**
    - SPP: lower is better, must be $< SPP_{\max}$.
    - ROI: must exceed MARR.
    - NPV: must be $\geq 0$.
    - IRR: must exceed MARR.
    - BCR: must be $\geq 1$.
- **NPV is the gold standard** — KTU board values use it as the primary decision tool.
- **IRR via linear interpolation** between two rates giving opposite NPV signs:
  $i^{*} = i_1 + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 + \vert \text{NPV}_2 \vert}(i_2 - i_1)$.
- **For mutually exclusive alternatives**, use **incremental analysis** (ΔNPV or ΔBCR), not individual metric comparison.
- **Standard factor notations** $(P/A, i, n)$, $(P/F, i, n)$, $(F/P, i, n)$ are *always* used in KTU numericals — memorizing their values from factor tables saves time.
- **Salvage value** is discounted to present using $(P/F, i, n)$; it is *not* added to the final year's cash flow directly.
- **Common energy proposals** tested: VFD installation, LED retrofit, motor replacement, waste heat recovery, solar PV, HVAC upgrade, power factor correction.
- **IPMVP** (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol) is the standard for post-implementation savings verification.
- **Inflation effect** on evaluation: if savings escalate at rate $f$ per year, replace the discount rate with the *real* rate $i_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{1+i}{1+f} - 1$.
- **Tax and depreciation** (when asked) reduce the net annual savings; depreciation tax shield = depreciation $\times$ tax rate.
- **For unequal-life alternatives**, use the **Annual Equivalent (AE) method**:
  $\text{AE} = \text{NPV} \times (A/P, i, n)$, then select the one with the highest AE.
- **Always end with a clear decision** statement — "ACCEPT", "REJECT", or "Select Alternative X" — to score the final 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** Module 4 typically carries **14–20%** of total marks, with at least one full 14-mark question in the ESE.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Evaluation of Proposals**
> *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (Energy Management) syllabus and valuation patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pay-Back Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pay-Back Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** is defined as the length of time required to recover the original capital investment of an energy efficiency / energy conservation project from the net annual cash inflows (energy cost savings) generated by that project.

Mathematically, the Pay-Back Period represents the exact point in time (usually expressed in **years** or **months**) at which the **cumulative net cash flow** turns from a negative value (loss zone) to a positive value (profit zone). It is one of the simplest, most widely used, and most intuitively appealing **financial appraisal techniques** in the domain of Energy Management and Energy Auditing.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the Pay-Back Period falls under the broader umbrella of **"Economic Analysis of Energy Projects"** and is treated as a *non-discounted* (or *simple*) measure of project profitability, alongside other techniques such as **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **Return on Investment (ROI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** For the KTU board examination, students must clearly distinguish between the **Simple (Undiscounted) Pay-Back Period** and the **Discounted Pay-Back Period**. The latter incorporates the **time value of money**, making it a more realistic metric for long-term energy projects (such as solar PV installations or HVAC retrofits with lifespans exceeding 10–15 years).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you borrow **₹1,00,000** from a friend to install a **5-kW solar rooftop system** on your house. You promise to repay him from the money you save on your monthly electricity bills (which previously went to the Kerala State Electricity Board — KSEB).

- If your average monthly electricity savings = **₹2,500**, then:
  - Savings per year = ₹2,500 × 12 = **₹30,000**
  - Time to repay ₹1,00,000 = 1,00,000 ÷ 30,000 ≈ **3.33 years**

That **3.33 years** is your **Pay-Back Period**. After this point, every rupee you save on electricity is essentially **"free profit"** — until the system needs major maintenance or replacement.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of the Pay-Back Period as the **"break-even time"** of an energy project. The shorter the PBP, the faster your investment "pays for itself" and starts generating pure savings. Industries love short PBPs because they reduce **financial risk** and improve **liquidity**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Capital Investment | $C_0$ or $I$ | ₹ (Indian Rupees) / $ (USD) |
| Annual Cash Inflow (Savings) | $A$ or $CF$ | ₹/year |
| Project Life | $n$ | years |
| Discount Rate (for Discounted PBP) | $i$ or $r$ | **% per annum** |
| Cumulative Cash Flow | $CCF$ | ₹ |
| **Acceptance Threshold (Typical Industrial)** | PBP | **< 3 to 5 years** (varies by sector) |

> [!NOTE]
> **No universal physical constant** applies here — Pay-Back Period is purely a financial/economic metric, not a physical one. The "standard" benchmark is industry-dependent: a PBP of **2 years** may be excellent for an LED retrofit, but unacceptable for a **25-year wind energy project**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Pay-Back Period can be evaluated under **two distinct scenarios**, each requiring a different mathematical approach.

### 2.1.1 Case A: Uniform (Constant) Annual Cash Inflows

When the energy savings are **approximately constant** year after year (e.g., a permanent LED lighting retrofit producing a fixed monthly reduction in KSEB bill):

1. Compute the **net annual cash inflow** ($A$) = Annual energy cost savings − Annual operating & maintenance (O&M) cost.
2. Apply the simple ratio formula to find the time required.
3. Compare against the company's **maximum acceptable PBP** (decision rule).

### 2.1.2 Case B: Non-Uniform (Variable) Annual Cash Inflows

When cash flows **vary** year to year (e.g., production volumes change, tariffs escalate, O&M costs rise), the analyst must:

1. Tabulate the **year-wise net cash flow**.
2. Compute the **cumulative cash flow** (running total).
3. Identify the **last year with a negative cumulative balance**.
4. Use linear interpolation to find the fractional year at which cumulative cash flow = 0.

### 2.1.3 Case C: Discounted Pay-Back Period (Time Value of Money)

For long-duration projects, a rupee saved in Year 10 is **worth less** than a rupee saved in Year 1, due to **inflation, opportunity cost, and risk**. The Discounted Pay-Back Period corrects for this by:

1. Discounting each year's cash inflow using the present value factor $\frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$.
2. Building a cumulative **discounted** cash flow table.
3. Locating the year in which cumulative discounted cash flow first becomes non-negative.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Energy Management question paper under Module 4.

| # | Technique | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple PBP (Uniform Cash Flows)** | $PBP = \dfrac{C_0}{A}$ | $C_0$ = Initial Investment (₹); $A$ = Annual Net Cash Inflow (₹/year) | **Accept** if $PBP \leq$ Target PBP |
| 2 | **Simple PBP (Non-Uniform Cash Flows)** | $PBP = Y + \dfrac{\vert CCF_Y \vert}{CF_{Y+1}}$ | $Y$ = Last year with negative cumulative CF; $CCF_Y$ = Cumulative CF at end of year $Y$ (negative); $CF_{Y+1}$ = Cash flow in year $Y+1$ | **Accept** if $PBP \leq$ Target PBP |
| 3 | **Discounted PBP (Uniform)** | $PBP = \dfrac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{C_0 \cdot i}{A}\right)}{\ln(1+i)}$ | $i$ = Discount rate (decimal); $A$ = Annual uniform discounted savings | **Accept** if $DPBP \leq$ Target PBP |
| 4 | **Discounted Cash Flow (per year)** | $DCF_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $t$ = Year number (1, 2, 3, …) | Required for cumulative discounted table |
| 5 | **Present Value of Annuity Factor** | $PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | $n$ = Number of years; $i$ = Discount rate | Used to convert annuity to PV |

> **Critical Notation Note (Anti-Break Rule):** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above have been deliberately written using the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` symbol, to prevent any markdown table rendering corruption.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pay-Back Period is the **lingua franca** of energy managers, plant engineers, and Chief Financial Officers (CFOs) across every industrial sector. Its practical applications include:

- **Lighting Retrofits:** Replacing fluorescent / HID lamps with LEDs in a factory — typical PBP: **1.5 – 3 years**.
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs):** Installing VFDs on pumps and fans in process plants — typical PBP: **2 – 4 years**.
- **HVAC Upgrades:** Replacing old chillers / AHUs with energy-efficient inverter-driven units — typical PBP: **3 – 6 years**.
- **Renewable Energy:** Rooftop solar PV, biomass gasifiers, wind turbines — typical PBP: **4 – 8 years** (with subsidies, can drop to 3 years).
- **Cogeneration / Waste Heat Recovery:** Capturing flue gas heat for boiler feedwater preheating — typical PBP: **2 – 5 years**.
- **Compressed Air System Audits:** Fixing leaks, optimizing pressure — typical PBP: **< 1 year** (often the fastest ROI in any plant).

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb (frequently asked in KTU vivas):** A Pay-Back Period of **less than half the useful life** of the equipment is generally considered a "good" investment in the energy conservation domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Pay-Back Period Formula (Uniform Cash Flows)

**Starting Premise:** We want the time $T$ (in years) at which the **cumulative cash inflow equals the initial investment** $C_0$.

If the annual cash inflow is constant at $A$ rupees per year, then over $T$ years, the total inflow is simply:

$$
\text{Total Inflow} = A \times T
$$

Setting this equal to the initial investment (break-even condition):

$$
A \times T = C_0
$$

Solving for $T$ (the Pay-Back Period):

$$
T = \frac{C_0}{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,PBP = \frac{C_0}{A}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental Pay-Back Period identity** — derived from the break-even condition, with no time-value-of-money adjustment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Non-Uniform Cash Flows (Linear Interpolation Method)

When cash flows vary year to year, the analyst must build a cumulative table and interpolate. The interpolation formula is derived from the principle of **proportionality** within the recovery year.

**Step 1 — Tabulation:** Let the cumulative cash flow at the end of year $Y$ be negative (i.e., $CCF_Y < 0$), and let the cash flow in year $Y+1$ be $CF_{Y+1}$.

**Step 2 — Residual Unrecovered Amount:** The unrecovered portion of the investment at the **start** of year $Y+1$ is $\vert CCF_Y \vert$.

**Step 3 — Fractional Year:** Assuming the cash flow $CF_{Y+1}$ arrives uniformly during year $Y+1$, the fraction of that year required to clear the residual deficit is:

$$
\text{Fraction} = \frac{\vert CCF_Y \vert}{CF_{Y+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PBP:**

$$
\boxed{\,PBP = Y + \frac{\vert CCF_Y \vert}{CF_{Y+1}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Discounted Pay-Back Period (Uniform Case)
