The Present Value of a uniform annual cash flow $A$ over $n$ years at discount rate $i$ is:

$$
PV = A \times \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

For the Discounted PBP, we want the year $n$ at which this present value equals the initial investment $C_0$:

$$
C_0 = A \times \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $n$:

$$
\frac{C_0 \cdot i}{A} = 1 - (1+i)^{-n}
$$

$$
(1+i)^{-n} = 1 - \frac{C_0 \cdot i}{A}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-n \cdot \ln(1+i) = \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{C_0 \cdot i}{A}\right)
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
\boxed{\,DPBP = \frac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{C_0 \cdot i}{A}\right)}{\ln(1+i)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This closed-form expression is valid **only when** $\dfrac{C_0 \cdot i}{A} < 1$ (i.e., the project is fundamentally viable at the given discount rate). Otherwise, the logarithm is undefined and the project **never** recovers its investment at that discount rate.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
> A Kochi-based seafood processing plant invests **₹12,00,000** to install a **waste heat recovery (WHR) system** on its boiler. The expected annual energy cost savings are:
> - Year 1: ₹3,00,000
> - Year 2: ₹3,50,000
> - Year 3: ₹4,00,000
> - Year 4: ₹4,50,000
> - Year 5: ₹5,00,000
>
> **Required:** (a) Compute the Simple Pay-Back Period. (b) If the company's required rate of return is **10% per annum**, compute the Discounted Pay-Back Period.

### Part (a) — Simple PBP

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −12,00,000 | −12,00,000 | Loss |
| 1 | +3,00,000 | −9,00,000 | Loss |
| 2 | +3,50,000 | −5,50,000 | Loss |
| 3 | +4,00,000 | −1,50,000 | Loss |
| 4 | +4,50,000 | +3,00,000 | **Profit** |

Here, $Y = 3$ (last year with negative cumulative CF), $CCF_3 = -1{,}50{,}000$, and $CF_4 = 4{,}50{,}000$.

$$
PBP = 3 + \frac{\vert -1{,}50{,}000 \vert}{4{,}50{,}000}
$$

$$
PBP = 3 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000}
$$

$$
PBP = 3 + 0.3333
$$

$$
\boxed{\,PBP = 3.33 \text{ years} \approx 3 \text{ years and 4 months}\,}
$$

### Part (b) — Discounted PBP at $i = 10\%$

**Discounted Cash Flow** for each year: $DCF_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1.10)^t}$

| Year | $CF_t$ (₹) | $(1.10)^t$ | $DCF_t$ (₹) | Cumulative DCF (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −12,00,000 | 1.0000 | −12,00,000 | −12,00,000 |
| 1 | +3,00,000 | 1.1000 | +2,72,727 | −9,27,273 |
| 2 | +3,50,000 | 1.2100 | +2,89,256 | −6,38,017 |
| 3 | +4,00,000 | 1.3310 | +3,00,526 | −3,37,491 |
| 4 | +4,50,000 | 1.4641 | +3,07,354 | −30,137 |
| 5 | +5,00,000 | 1.6105 | +3,10,461 | +2,80,324 |

Here, $Y = 4$, $CCF_4 = -30{,}137$, $DCF_5 = 3{,}10{,}461$.

$$
DPBP = 4 + \frac{\vert -30{,}137 \vert}{3{,}10{,}461}
$$

$$
DPBP = 4 + 0.0971
$$

$$
\boxed{\,DPBP \approx 4.10 \text{ years} \approx 4 \text{ years and 1 month}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Simple PBP (3.33 years) is **optimistic** because it ignores the time value of money. The Discounted PBP (4.10 years) is more realistic and tells the management that, even after accounting for the opportunity cost of capital, the WHR system pays for itself in just over 4 years.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pay-Back Period Calculation Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of how a Pay-Back Period is computed in a structured energy audit workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Energy Audit] --> B[Identify Energy Conservation Measure]
    B --> C[Estimate Initial Investment C0]
    C --> D[Estimate Annual Cash Inflows CFt]
    D --> E{Cash Flows Uniform?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Apply Formula PBP = C0 divide by A]
    E -- No --> G[Build Cumulative Cash Flow Table]
    G --> H[Locate Last Negative Cumulative Year Y]
    H --> I[Apply Interpolation PBP = Y plus absCCFY divide CF Y+1]
    F --> J[Compare with Target PBP]
    I --> J
    J --> K{PBP less than or equal to Target?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Accept Project]
    K -- No --> M[Reject or Reject for Review]
    L --> N[Perform Discounted Analysis DPBP if needed]
    M --> O[End]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Simple PBP vs. Discounted PBP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Investment Decision Required] --> B{Project Life Exceeds 5 Years?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Simple Pay-Back Period]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Discounted Pay-Back Period]
    C --> E[C0 divided by A]
    D --> F[Discount Each Years Cash Flow]
    F --> G[Build Cumulative Discounted Table]
    G --> H[Interpolate to Find DPBP]
    E --> I[Final Decision]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Should We Accept the Project?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Project Proposal Received] --> Q1[Compute Simple PBP]
    Q1 --> Q2{Simple PBP less than 3 Years?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Acc[Strongly Recommend Approval]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Compute Discounted PBP at WACC]
    Q3 --> Q4{Discounted PBP less than Project Life divided by 2?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Cond[Conditionally Approve - Subject to NPV and IRR]
    Q4 -- No --> Rej[Reject Project]
    Cond --> Stop[Submit Detailed Feasibility Report]
    Acc --> Stop
    Rej --> Stop
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In Mermaid syntax, every node has been given an alphanumeric ID (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `Start`, `Q1`) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph`. All node labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without bold or italic formatting to ensure clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **Pay-Back Period**. State its major limitation.

**Model Answer:**

> **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** is the time period required to recover the original capital cost of an energy conservation project from the net annual cash inflows (energy cost savings) generated by it. It is expressed in years.
>
> **Mathematical Form:** $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{C_0}{A}$, where $C_0$ is the initial investment and $A$ is the uniform annual cash inflow.
>
> **Major Limitation:** The simple Pay-Back Period **ignores the time value of money**. It treats a rupee earned in Year 10 as equal in value to a rupee earned in Year 1, which is unrealistic for long-duration energy projects. Additionally, it disregards cash flows occurring **after** the pay-back period, so two projects with the same PBP but very different long-term returns are treated as identical.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **Simple Pay-Back Period** and **Discounted Pay-Back Period**.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Simple PBP | Discounted PBP |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Value of Money** | **Not considered** | **Considered** |
| **Discounting Applied** | No | Yes, at the cost of capital (WACC) |
| **Formula (Uniform CF)** | $C_0 / A$ | $\dfrac{-\ln(1 - C_0 \cdot i / A)}{\ln(1+i)}$ |
| **Result** | Always shorter (optimistic) | Always longer (realistic) |
| **Use Case** | Short-life projects (< 5 years) | Long-life projects (> 5 years) |
| **Computational Effort** | Very low | Moderate (requires PV table) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** In the End Semester Examination, Module 4 typically carries 14 marks, and students must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b) from this module. Below are **TWO independent alternatives** — answer **any ONE**.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**A manufacturing company is evaluating two mutually exclusive energy conservation proposals:**

| Parameter | Proposal X (LED Retrofit) | Proposal Y (VFD Installation) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | ₹5,00,000 | ₹8,00,000 |
| Year 1 Savings | ₹2,00,000 | ₹1,50,000 |
| Year 2 Savings | ₹2,00,000 | ₹2,50,000 |
| Year 3 Savings | ₹2,00,000 | ₹3,00,000 |
| Year 4 Savings | ₹2,00,000 | ₹3,50,000 |
| Year 5 Savings | ₹2,00,000 | ₹4,00,000 |

The company's maximum acceptable Pay-Back Period is **3 years**.

#### (a) Compute the Simple Pay-Back Period for both proposals and recommend the better option. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Proposal X (LED Retrofit) — Uniform Cash Flows:**

Since the annual savings are constant at ₹2,00,000:

$$
PBP_X = \frac{C_0}{A} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.5 \text{ years}
$$

**Cumulative Table Verification for Proposal X:**

| Year | CF (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −5,00,000 | −5,00,000 |
| 1 | +2,00,000 | −3,00,000 |
| 2 | +2,00,000 | −1,00,000 |
| 3 | +2,00,000 | +1,00,000 |

$$
PBP_X = 2 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ years}
$$

**Proposal Y (VFD Installation) — Non-Uniform Cash Flows:**

| Year | CF (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −8,00,000 | −8,00,000 |
| 1 | +1,50,000 | −6,50,000 |
| 2 | +2,50,000 | −4,00,000 |
| 3 | +3,00,000 | −1,00,000 |
| 4 | +3,50,000 | +2,50,000 |

$$
PBP_Y = 3 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 3 + 0.2857 = 3.29 \text{ years}
$$

**Recommendation:** Proposal X has a PBP of **2.5 years** (well within the 3-year limit), whereas Proposal Y's PBP of **3.29 years exceeds the acceptable threshold**. Therefore, **Proposal X (LED Retrofit) is recommended.**

#### (b) If the cost of capital is 12% per annum, compute the Discounted Pay-Back Period for Proposal Y and comment on the decision. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

Discounted Cash Flows: $DCF_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1.12)^t}$

| Year | $CF_t$ (₹) | $(1.12)^t$ | $DCF_t$ (₹) | Cumulative DCF (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −8,00,000 | 1.0000 | −8,00,000 | −8,00,000 |
| 1 | +1,50,000 | 1.1200 | +1,33,929 | −6,66,071 |
| 2 | +2,50,000 | 1.2544 | +1,99,299 | −4,66,772 |
| 3 | +3,00,000 | 1.4049 | +2,13,545 | −2,53,227 |
| 4 | +3,50,000 | 1.5735 | +2,22,438 | −30,789 |
| 5 | +4,00,000 | 1.7623 | +2,26,996 | +1,96,207 |

$$
DPBP_Y = 4 + \frac{30{,}789}{2{,}26{,}996} = 4 + 0.1356 = 4.14 \text{ years}
$$

**Comment:** The Discounted Pay-Back Period for Proposal Y is **4.14 years**, which is **even longer** than its Simple PBP of 3.29 years. This confirms that when the time value of money is accounted for, Proposal Y becomes **even less attractive**. The earlier decision to reject Proposal Y is therefore **reinforced** by the discounted analysis.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A textile mill in Tirur installs a cogeneration plant with the following data:**

- Initial investment: **₹30,00,000**
- Expected uniform annual energy cost savings: **₹6,00,000**
- Additional annual O&M cost: **₹1,00,000**
- Project life: **10 years**
- Discount rate (cost of capital): **15% per annum**
- Company target Pay-Back Period: **5 years**

#### (a) Calculate the Simple Pay-Back Period. Should the project be accepted? (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Inflow:**

$$
A = \text{Annual Savings} - \text{Annual O\&M Cost}
$$

$$
A = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PBP Formula:**

$$
PBP = \frac{C_0}{A} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 6 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since $PBP = 6$ years **exceeds** the company's target of 5 years, the project **should not be accepted** on the basis of Simple PBP alone.

#### (b) Compute the Discounted Pay-Back Period using the closed-form formula and re-evaluate the decision. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Parameters:**

$C_0 = 30{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $A = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year, $i = 0.15$

**Step 2 — Apply the Discounted PBP Formula:**

$$
DPBP = \frac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{C_0 \cdot i}{A}\right)}{\ln(1+i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Argument of the Logarithm:**

$$
\frac{C_0 \cdot i}{A} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15}{5{,}00{,}000} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.90
$$

$$
1 - 0.90 = 0.10
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithms:**

$$
\ln(0.10) = -2.3026
$$

$$
\ln(1.15) = 0.1398
$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation:**

$$
DPBP = \frac{-(-2.3026)}{0.1398} = \frac{2.3026}{0.1398}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,DPBP \approx 16.47 \text{ years}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-evaluation:**

The Discounted Pay-Back Period of **16.47 years** is **far greater** than the project's useful life of **10 years** and grossly exceeds the company's 5-year target. The project is **firmly rejected** on the Discounted PBP criterion. The energy savings, while substantial in absolute terms, are **insufficient** to recover the high initial outlay at a 15% cost of capital.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Pay-Back Period problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract O&M costs** from gross savings before computing net annual cash inflow. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for this oversight.
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention** in the cumulative cash flow table — the initial investment at Year 0 must be entered as a **negative** number.
> 3. **Misapplying the interpolation formula** — students often write $PBP = Y + \dfrac{CCF_Y}{CF_{Y+1}}$ without the **absolute value** on $CCF_Y$, leading to an incorrect fractional year.
> 4. **Mixing up Simple and Discounted PBP** — the question must be read carefully. "Pay-Back Period" alone typically implies **Simple PBP** in KTU papers unless "discounted" is explicitly mentioned.
> 5. **Unit errors** — the answer must be expressed in **years** (or years + months). Avoid writing "6" without units.
> 6. **Skipping the recommendation** — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the **decision statement** ("Accept" or "Reject" with justification). Always close with a clear recommendation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pay-Back Period**

- ✅ **Definition:** Time required to recover the initial investment from net annual cash inflows (energy cost savings).
- ✅ **Simple PBP Formula (Uniform):** $PBP = C_0 / A$
- ✅ **Simple PBP Formula (Non-Uniform):** $PBP = Y + \vert CCF_Y \vert / CF_{Y+1}$ — derived from cumulative cash flow table + linear interpolation.
- ✅ **Discounted PBP Formula (Uniform):** $DPBP = \dfrac{-\ln(1 - C_0 i / A)}{\ln(1+i)}$ — applicable only when $C_0 i / A < 1$.
- ✅ **Net Annual Cash Inflow:** $A = \text{Annual Savings} - \text{Annual O\&M Cost}$ (do not ignore O&M!).
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** **Accept** the project if $PBP \leq$ Target PBP set by management.
- ✅ **Typical Industrial Benchmarks:** LED = 1.5–3 years, VFD = 2–4 years, HVAC = 3–6 years, Solar = 4–8 years, WHR = 2–5 years.
- ✅ **Key Limitation of Simple PBP:** Ignores time value of money; ignores cash flows beyond pay-back year; biased toward short-term gains.
- ✅ **Discounted PBP is always longer than Simple PBP** (because each future rupee is shrunk by the discount factor).
- ✅ **Comparison with Other Methods:** PBP measures **liquidity / risk**; NPV measures **absolute wealth creation**; IRR measures **percentage return**. KTU may ask students to **compare and contrast** — know the difference cold.
- ✅ **Rule of Thumb:** A "good" energy project has a PBP less than **half** the equipment's useful life.
- ✅ **Sign Convention in Tables:** Year 0 = negative outflow; Years 1 to $n$ = positive inflows; cumulative column must be built carefully.
- ✅ **Memorize the log-form DPBP formula** — examiners love testing the closed-form expression for uniform cash flows.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Average Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — AVERAGE RATE OF RETURN (ARR) METHOD

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** method, also known as the **Accounting Rate of Return (AARR)** or **Return on Investment (ROI)** method, is a classical **non-discounted capital budgeting technique** used in engineering economic analysis to evaluate the profitability of an investment proposal (especially energy conservation, energy efficiency, and replacement projects) based on **accounting profits** rather than cash flows.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET612 — Energy Management) Module 4, ARR is formally defined as:

> **ARR** = the ratio of the **average annual net accounting profit (after depreciation)** generated by a project to the **average book value of the investment** over the useful life of the asset, expressed as a percentage.

Mathematically, the standard KTU definition is written as:

$$
\text{ARR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit after Depreciation}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100
$$

The project is **accepted** if the computed ARR is **greater than or equal to** the **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** or **hurdle rate** (also called the **target rate of return** or **cut-off rate**), which is set by the firm's management based on the cost of capital, opportunity cost, and risk profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Focus Point:** ARR uses **accounting profit (not cash flow)** and **book value of investment (not present value)**. This is the single most distinguishing feature students must remember. It is a **non-time-value-based** method, hence it ignores the time value of money.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Score Average" Intuition

Imagine you are a student who invested **₹1,00,000** in a coaching class for **5 years**, and every year on average, after deducting the cost of the class (depreciation), you earned a **net "profit"** (knowledge gained translated into income) of **₹15,000**.

A simple, intuitive way to judge whether the coaching class was "worth it" is to ask:

> *"On average, every year, what percentage of my invested money did I recover as profit?"*

That percentage is essentially the **Average Rate of Return**.

$$
\text{Your ARR} = \frac{15{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

(Note: the average investment is ₹50,000, because the value of the coaching "asset" declines from ₹1,00,000 to ₹0 over 5 years — i.e., **₹1,00,000 / 2 = ₹50,000**.)

If the benchmark "good investment" rate is, say, 12%, then 30% is excellent — the project would be accepted.

**Real-World Energy Management Connection:** In energy management, ARR is widely used by plant managers to evaluate:
- Replacement of old motors with energy-efficient IE3/IE4 motors
- Installation of Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)
- Upgrading boiler systems
- Solar PV installations
- LED retrofitting projects

These are exactly the kinds of capital investment decisions covered in Module 4 of OEEET612.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method matters in Energy Management:** Although discounted methods like NPV and IRR are theoretically superior, **ARR is still widely used in industry** because it relies on **readily available accounting data** (profit & loss statements and balance sheets), requires no complex discounting, and is intuitively simple for top management to interpret.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Students MUST Know

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Average Annual Net Profit** | Total accounting profit over project life ÷ number of years |
| **Average Investment** | (Initial Investment + Salvage Value) / 2 |
| **Book Value** | Original cost − accumulated depreciation |
| **Hurdle Rate / MARR** | Pre-set minimum required return |
| **Salvage Value (SV)** | Expected resale value at end of useful life |
| **Useful Life (n)** | Economic life of the asset in years |
| **Initial Investment (I₀)** | Capital outlay at time zero |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How ARR is Computed (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ARR method follows a **deterministic, sequential decision logic**. Below is the structured algorithmic flow used by firms and energy auditors:

### Step 1 — Identify the Initial Investment (I₀)
Determine the total capital outlay required at **t = 0** to implement the project. In energy projects, this includes:
- Equipment cost (motor, drive, boiler, lighting)
- Installation and commissioning
- Civil and electrical infrastructure
- Training cost

### Step 2 — Estimate the Useful Life (n) and Salvage Value (SV)
- Useful life is the economic/engineering life of the asset.
- Salvage value is the expected terminal market value.

### Step 3 — Compute Average Investment
The **average book value** of the asset over its life is:

$$
\text{Average Investment} = \frac{I_0 + SV}{2}
$$

> **Why divide by 2?** Because the book value of a depreciating asset falls linearly (under straight-line depreciation) from I₀ to SV. The arithmetic mean is the standard KTU convention.

### Step 4 — Compute Average Annual Net Profit (After Depreciation)
Two equivalent forms are accepted by KTU examiners:

**Form A (From Cash Flows):**
$$
\text{Average Annual Net Profit} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\text{Net Cash Flow}_t)}{n} - \text{Annual Depreciation}
$$

**Form B (From Total Profit):**
$$
\text{Average Annual Net Profit} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\text{Net Profit}_t)}{n}
$$

where Net Profit already incorporates depreciation.

### Step 5 — Apply the ARR Formula

$$
\boxed{\text{ARR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit (after depreciation)}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Decision Rule

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT}, & \text{if ARR} \geq \text{MARR (Hurdle Rate)} \\ \text{REJECT}, & \text{if ARR} < \text{MARR} \end{cases}
$$

For **mutually exclusive** projects, choose the one with the **highest ARR** (subject to ARR ≥ MARR).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **complete formula compendium** for the ARR method, as expected in the KTU 2024 Scheme Energy Management exam. Memorize this table before the test.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ARR (Primary)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Decision metric |
| 2 | **Average Investment** | $\dfrac{I_0 + SV}{2}$ | ₹ (or any currency) | Book-value-based |
| 3 | **Annual Straight-Line Depreciation** | $\dfrac{I_0 - SV}{n}$ | ₹/year | Used in profit calc. |
| 4 | **Average Annual Net Profit (Form A)** | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Cash Flow}_t}{n} - \text{Depreciation}$ | ₹/year | Cash flow → profit |
| 5 | **Average Annual Net Profit (Form B)** | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Net Profit}_t}{n}$ | ₹/year | Direct from P&L |
| 6 | **Decision Rule** | ARR $\geq$ MARR → Accept | — | Comparative test |
| 7 | **MARR / Hurdle Rate** | Firm-specific (e.g., 12%, 15%) | % | Cost of capital proxy |
| 8 | **Total Book Value at end of year t** | $I_0 - (t \times \text{Depreciation})$ | ₹ | Linear decline |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table cells use `\vert` or `\\vert` style delimiters in any complex formula to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Intuition

- **Why use Average Investment (not Initial)?** Because the asset is "used up" gradually. A motor bought for ₹1,00,000 does not "stay" at ₹1,00,000 throughout its life — it depreciates. Using average book value gives a more realistic denominator.

- **Why subtract Depreciation?** Because accounting profit (per Indian Accounting Standards / Ind AS and KTU convention) must be reported *after* non-cash expenses. Cash flow and profit are different concepts.

- **Why is it called "non-discounted"?** Because it does **not** apply any compounding/discounting factor. A rupee earned in Year 5 is treated equal to a rupee earned in Year 1 — which is its **major weakness** (this is why KTU also teaches NPV, IRR, etc. in adjacent modules).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Engineering

| Industry Application | How ARR is Used |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Motor Retrofit** | Compare ARR of IE2 vs. IE4 motor purchase |
| **VFD Installation on Pumps/Fans** | Compute return on VFD investment from energy savings |
| **Cogeneration / Trigeneration** | Evaluate CHP plant feasibility via ARR |
| **Solar PV Rooftop** | Assess 5–7 year payback using ARR |
| **Power Factor Correction** | Bank-side capacitor installation ROI |
| **HVAC / Chiller Upgrade** | Compare magnetic-bearing chiller ROI |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) certified energy manager / energy auditor** examinations in India (closely aligned with KTU's OEEET612 course), ARR is one of the most frequently tested techniques in the **financial analysis** section of energy audits.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL NUMERICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard ARR Computation (KTU-Model Problem)

### Problem Statement

A manufacturing firm in Kerala is evaluating the installation of an **automatic power factor correction (APFC) panel** at its plant. The relevant data are:

- **Initial Investment (I₀)** = ₹5,00,000
- **Estimated Useful Life (n)** = 10 years
- **Salvage Value (SV)** = ₹50,000
- **Expected Annual Cash Savings (from reduced kVAH penalty & lower I²R losses)** = ₹1,20,000
- **MARR (Hurdle Rate)** = 12%

Determine whether the project is acceptable using the **ARR method**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Depreciation (Straight Line)**

$$
D = \frac{I_0 - SV}{n} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} = 45{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Annual Net Profit**

Since the cash savings of ₹1,20,000 are the gross annual benefit, we subtract depreciation to get accounting profit:

$$
\text{Annual Net Profit} = \text{Cash Savings} - D = 1{,}20{,}000 - 45{,}000 = 75{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

(Profits are constant across years, so the average is simply ₹75,000.)

**Step 3 — Compute Average Investment**

$$
\text{Avg. Investment} = \frac{I_0 + SV}{2} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000}{2} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{2} = 2{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ARR**

$$
\text{ARR} = \frac{75{,}000}{2{,}75{,}000} \times 100
$$

Let us perform the division step-by-step:

$$
\frac{75{,}000}{2{,}75{,}000} = \frac{75}{275} = \frac{15}{55} = \frac{3}{11}
$$

$$
\frac{3}{11} = 0.272727\ldots \approx 0.2727
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = 0.2727 \times 100 = 27.27\%
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Decision Rule**

$$
\text{ARR} = 27.27\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{MARR} = 12\%
$$

$$
27.27\% \;\; > \;\; 12\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT the project}
$$

### Conclusion
The APFC panel project yields a return of **27.27% per year on average book value**, well above the 12% hurdle rate. **The firm should invest.** ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ARR with Variable Annual Profits (Multi-Year Cash Flow)

### Problem Statement

A textile unit in **Erode** is considering the installation of a **500 kWp rooftop solar PV system**. The estimated cash inflows (savings in electricity bills) over its 5-year analysis horizon are:

| Year | Net Cash Inflow (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 12.00 |
| 2 | 13.50 |
| 3 | 14.20 |
| 4 | 15.00 |
| 5 | 16.30 |

- **Initial Investment (I₀)** = ₹60 Lakhs
- **Salvage Value (SV)** = ₹5 Lakhs
- **Useful Life (n)** = 5 years
- **MARR** = 10%

Determine the ARR and the acceptability.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Annual Depreciation**

$$
D = \frac{I_0 - SV}{n} = \frac{60 - 5}{5} = \frac{55}{5} = 11 \text{ Lakhs/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Cash Inflow**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{5} CF_t = 12.00 + 13.50 + 14.20 + 15.00 + 16.30 = 71.00 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Annual Cash Inflow**

$$
\overline{CF} = \frac{71.00}{5} = 14.20 \text{ Lakhs/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Annual Net Profit (After Depreciation)**

$$
\overline{P} = \overline{CF} - D = 14.20 - 11.00 = 3.20 \text{ Lakhs/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Average Investment**

$$
\overline{I} = \frac{I_0 + SV}{2} = \frac{60 + 5}{2} = 32.50 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6 — ARR Computation**

$$
\text{ARR} = \frac{3.20}{32.50} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{3.20}{32.50} = 0.0984615\ldots
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = 9.85\%
$$

**Step 7 — Decision Rule**

$$
9.85\% \;\; < \;\; 10\% \quad (\text{MARR})
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REJECT the solar PV project under the ARR method}
$$

### Important Engineering Interpretation
> [!WARNING]
> **A subtle KTU pitfall:** Even though ARR says "reject", the project may still be **acceptable by NPV or payback** because ARR ignores cash flow timing. Solar PV generates **increasing savings** in later years (due to tariff escalation), which ARR undervalues. This is a classic conceptual question KTU examiners love to ask — *"If ARR rejects a project, can NPV accept it? Why?"* — and the answer is **YES, due to ARR's neglect of time value of money.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mutually Exclusive Energy Projects (ARR Ranking)

### Problem Statement

A sugar mill is evaluating two energy-saving options:

| Parameter | Project A: VFD on Centrifuge | Project B: Condensate Recovery System |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (I₀) | ₹20,00,000 | ₹35,00,000 |
| Useful Life (n) | 8 years | 10 years |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹2,00,000 | ₹5,00,000 |
| Avg. Annual Net Profit | ₹2,50,000 | ₹4,00,000 |
| MARR | 15% | 15% |

Using ARR, which project should be chosen?

---

### Project A — VFD on Centrifuge

$$
\overline{I_A} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{2} = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{ARR}_A = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{11{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 22.73\%
$$

Decision: 22.73% > 15% → **Accept individually**.

---

### Project B — Condensate Recovery

$$
\overline{I_B} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000}{2} = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{ARR}_B = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

Decision: 20.00% > 15% → **Accept individually**.

---

### Ranking for Mutually Exclusive Choice

$$
\text{ARR}_A = 22.73\% \quad > \quad \text{ARR}_B = 20.00\%
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \text{Choose Project A (VFD on Centrifuge)}
$$

### Final Recommendation
Although Project B generates higher absolute profit (₹4,00,000 vs. ₹2,50,000), Project A delivers a **higher return per rupee of capital tied up** and is therefore the better investment under the ARR criterion.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Summary Table (for KTU Board Exam)

| Aspect | ARR > MARR? | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Single Project, ARR ≥ MARR | Yes | Accept ✅ |
| Single Project, ARR < MARR | No | Reject ❌ |
| Two Mutually Exclusive Projects | Both ≥ MARR | Choose higher ARR ✅ |
| Two Mutually Exclusive Projects | One ≥, one < MARR | Choose the one ≥ MARR ✅ |
| Independent Projects (multiple) | Mixed | Accept all with ARR ≥ MARR ✅ |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — ARR Decision Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete decision flow** of the ARR method as it would be implemented in an energy audit / capital budgeting exercise in a plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Energy Project Proposal] --> B[Collect Input Data: I0, SV, n, Annual Cash Flows, MARR]
    B --> C[Compute Annual Depreciation D = I0 - SV divided by n]
    C --> D[Compute Average Annual Net Profit = Avg Cash Flow minus D]
    D --> E[Compute Average Investment = I0 + SV divided by 2]
    E --> F[Compute ARR = Avg Profit divided by Avg Investment times 100]
    F --> G{Is ARR greater than or equal to MARR?}
    G -- Yes --> H[ACCEPT the Project]
    G -- No --> I[REJECT the Project]
    H --> J{Is there a mutually exclusive alternative?}
    I --> K[Archive Report and Halt Process]
    J -- Yes --> L[Compute ARR for Alternative Project]
    J -- No --> K
    L --> M[Compare ARRs: Choose Project with Higher ARR subject to ARR at least MARR]
    M --> N[End: Selected Project Report]
```

**Interpretation of Flowchart:**
- The arrows in **solid lines** represent the strict sequential logic of ARR evaluation.
- The **diamond decision blocks** (G, J) represent the binary decision gates where the engineer must make a yes/no judgment.
- This flowchart is fully aligned with the **BEE Energy Audit Methodology** and KTU Module 4 evaluation framework.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — ARR in the Context of Energy Management Capital Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Audit Report] --> B[Identify Energy Conservation Measures ECM]
    B --> C[Estimate Capital Cost I0]
    B --> D[Estimate Annual Energy Savings as Cash Flow]
    C --> E[ARR Calculation Engine]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply MARR Benchmark]
    F --> G{Priority Sorting}
    G --> H[High ARR: Quick Implementation]
    G --> I[Medium ARR: Detailed Engineering Review]
    G --> J[Low ARR: Discard or Reconsider]
    H --> K[Implementation and Monitoring]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Document Lessons Learned]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`
**Define the Average Rate of Return (ARR) method. State its decision rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** method is a non-discounted capital budgeting technique that measures the profitability of an investment as the ratio of the **average annual net accounting profit (after depreciation)** to the **average book value of the investment**, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{ARR} = \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100
$$

**Decision Rule:** A project is **accepted** if the computed **ARR ≥ MARR** (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return / Hurdle Rate). Otherwise, it is rejected.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M + Formula 1M + Decision Rule 1M.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Why is the Average Investment computed as (I₀ + SV) / 2 in the ARR method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The average investment is taken as the arithmetic mean of the **initial investment (I₀)** and the **salvage value (SV)** because, under the **straight-line depreciation** convention used in accounting, the **book value** of an asset declines **linearly** from I₀ at the beginning to SV at the end of its useful life. The arithmetic mean of these two endpoints represents the **average book value** of the asset over its life, providing a realistic and unbiased denominator for the ARR ratio. Using only I₀ would overstate the capital tied up, while using only SV would understate it.

> **Mark Distribution:** Concept 1M + Justification 1M + Formula mention 1M.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🔷 Question B (Choice A) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Explain the **Average Rate of Return (ARR) method** in detail. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** as a capital budgeting tool in the energy sector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating two energy-saving projects:

| Parameter | Project X: VFD Installation | Project Y: Heat Exchanger Upgrade |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (I₀) | ₹8,00,000 | ₹12,00,000 |
| Useful Life (n) | 6 years | 6 years |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹1,00,000 | ₹2,00,000 |
| Avg. Annual Net Profit | ₹1,40,000 | ₹2,10,000 |

The **MARR** of the firm is **14%**. Using the **ARR method**, advise the company on which project to select. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B-A (a)

The **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** method is one of the most widely used non-discounted techniques for evaluating energy-efficiency and capital investment projects. The steps are:

1. Estimate the **Initial Investment (I₀)**, **Useful Life (n)**, and **Salvage Value (SV)**.
2. Compute **Average Investment** = (I₀ + SV) / 2.
3. Compute **Average Annual Net Profit (after depreciation)** = Average Cash Flow − Annual Depreciation.
4. Apply the formula: ARR = (Average Net Profit / Average Investment) × 100.
5. Compare with **MARR (Hurdle Rate)**. Accept if ARR ≥ MARR.

**Advantages in the Energy Sector:**

- **Simplicity:** Plant managers and energy auditors can compute ARR quickly using readily available accounting data (profit & loss statements, balance sheets).
- **Whole-Life View:** Unlike simple payback, ARR considers returns over the **entire useful life** of the asset, not just the recovery period.
- **Intuitive for Management:** Expressed as a percentage, it is comparable to stock market returns, interest rates, and other benchmarks.
- **Useful for Screening ECMs:** In a typical energy audit with 20+ Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs), ARR helps in quickly ranking the financially attractive ones before detailed NPV/IRR analysis.
- **Handles Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Among ECMs that are financially viable, the one with the highest ARR is chosen.

**Limitations:**

- **Ignores Time Value of Money (TVM):** The biggest flaw. A rupee earned in Year 1 is treated equal to a rupee earned in Year 6. This can mis-rank long-life energy projects (e.g., solar PV, wind).
- **Uses Accounting Profit, Not Cash Flow:** Subject to non-cash adjustments, depreciation methods, and accounting policies — making it less reliable than NPV.
- **Ambiguous Average Investment:** Different firms compute the denominator differently (initial cost only, or (I₀ + SV)/2, or average of yearly book values), leading to inconsistent comparisons.
- **May Conflict with NPV:** A project with higher ARR may have lower NPV if the cash flow timing differs.
- **Subjective MARR:** The hurdle rate is set by management and may be biased.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition & Steps = 3M, Advantages = 2M, Limitations = 2M.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B-A (b)

**Project X — VFD Installation**

$$
\overline{I_X} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{ARR}_X = \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 31.11\%
$$

**Project Y — Heat Exchanger Upgrade**

$$
\overline{I_Y} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{2} = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{ARR}_Y = \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 30.00\%
$$

**Comparison with MARR = 14%:**

| Project | ARR | vs. MARR | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| X | 31.11% | > 14% | Accept ✅ |
| Y | 30.00% | > 14% | Accept ✅ |

**Mutually Exclusive Selection:** Both projects are individually viable, but since the firm can choose only one, the project with the **higher ARR** is selected.

$$
\text{ARR}_X = 31.11\% \;\; > \;\; \text{ARR}_Y = 30.00\%
$$

**Final Recommendation:** The company should implement **Project X (VFD Installation)**, as it offers a higher return on capital tied up (31.11% vs. 30.00%).

> **Mark Distribution:** Project X ARR = 2M, Project Y ARR = 2M, MARR check = 1M, Final choice with reasoning = 2M.

---

### 🔷 Question B (Choice B) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]`
**(a)** A firm is considering the installation of a **500 kVA APFC panel** to avoid power factor penalty charges. The investment data is:

- Initial Investment = ₹6,00,000
- Useful Life = 8 years
- Salvage Value = ₹60,000
- Estimated Annual Cash Savings = ₹1,80,000
- MARR = 13%

Using the **ARR method**, evaluate the project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **ARR method with the NPV method** for energy project evaluation. Why might a project rejected by ARR still be accepted by NPV? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B-B (a)

**Step 1 — Annual Depreciation**

$$
D = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000}{8} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{8} = 67{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Annual Net Profit**

$$
\overline{P} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 67{,}500 = 1{,}12{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Investment**

$$
\overline{I} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000}{2} = 3{,}30{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — ARR**

$$
\text{ARR} = \frac{1{,}12{,}500}{3{,}30{,}000} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{1{,}12{,}500}{3{,}30{,}000} = \frac{112.5}{330} = \frac{225}{660} = \frac{45}{132} = \frac{15}{44} = 0.3409\ldots
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = 34.09\%
$$

**Step 5 — Decision**

$$
34.09\% \;\; > \;\; 13\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ACCEPT the APFC panel project.}
$$

The project is highly attractive, generating an average return of **34.09% per year** on the book value of the investment, well above the 13% hurdle rate.

> **Mark Distribution:** Depreciation 1M, Avg Profit 1M, Avg Investment 1M, ARR 2M, Decision 2M.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B-B (b)

**Comparison of ARR vs. NPV for Energy Projects**

| Criterion | ARR Method | NPV Method |
|---|---|---|
| Time Value of Money | Ignored ❌ | Fully incorporated ✅ |
| Decision Metric | Percentage (%) | Absolute value (₹) |
| Data Requirement | Accounting profit + book value | Discounted cash flows |
| Complexity | Very simple | Moderate (requires discounting) |
| Reliability (KTU preferred) | Lower (initial screening) | Higher (final decision) |
| Handling of Inflation | Not directly addressed | Can be incorporated in discount rate |
| Mutual Ranking Reliability | Sometimes inconsistent with NPV | Theoretically optimal ✅ |

**Why ARR may reject a project that NPV accepts:**

A project may be **rejected by ARR** but **accepted by NPV** because:

1. **Time Value of Money (TVM):** ARR treats all profits equally across years. NPV, in contrast, **weights later profits less** because they are discounted. Projects with **high early-year savings** (e.g., VFDs on motors) and projects with **high late-year savings** (e.g., solar PV with tariff escalation) are treated differently by NPV.
2. **Cash Flow vs. Profit:** ARR uses accounting profit (after depreciation), while NPV uses **actual cash flows**. Depreciation is a non-cash expense and can distort ARR.
3. **Scale of Investment:** ARR is a **ratio** and may favor smaller projects. A small project with a high % return may have a small absolute NPV, while a large project with a lower ARR may have a huge positive NPV.
4. **Discount Rate (MARR) Choice:** If the discount rate used in NPV is low (say 8%) but MARR for ARR is high (say 15%), even a good long-term project may fail ARR but pass NPV.

**Concrete Example:** A solar PV project with 25-year life, low initial returns (years 1–3) but escalating returns later (years 5–25) may have ARR < MARR, but **NPV > 0** because the long stream of discounted future cash flows outweighs the early-year drag.

> **Mark Distribution:** Comparison table 3M, Reasons why ARR-reject / NPV-accept 4M.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Using I₀ (not average investment) as denominator** → Loses 2 marks instantly. Always use (I₀ + SV)/2.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract depreciation** when computing net profit from cash savings → Book profit ≠ cash flow.
> 3. **Not stating MARR comparison explicitly** → The final answer must contain "ARR = X% which is (greater / less) than MARR = Y%, hence ACCEPT/REJECT." Partial credit may be lost otherwise.
> 4. **Choosing the project with highest absolute profit** in mutually exclusive cases → KTU wants **highest ARR per rupee invested**, not highest absolute profit.
> 5. **Confusing ARR with IRR** → ARR is **non-discounted, accounting-profit-based**; IRR is **discounted, cash-flow-based**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** → Writing ARR = 0.27 instead of 27%. Always express as a percentage.
> 7. **Using the wrong formula for Average Investment** when SV = 0 → Then Avg Investment = I₀/2, **not I₀**.
> 8. **Ignoring units** in intermediate steps → Always write "₹/year" or "₹" for clarity; evaluators check.

---

## 5.4 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> Use this section as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU Energy Management exam.

### 🔹 Core Concept
- ARR = **(Average Annual Net Profit / Average Investment) × 100**
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if **ARR ≥ MARR**, else reject.

### 🔹 Key Formulas
- Average Investment = (I₀ + SV) / 2
- Annual Depreciation = (I₀ − SV) / n
- Average Net Profit = Average Cash Flow − Annual Depreciation

### 🔹 Step Sequence
1. Collect data (I₀, SV, n, MARR, Cash Flows)
2. Compute depreciation
3. Compute average profit (cash flow − depreciation)
4. Compute average investment
5. Compute ARR
6. Compare with MARR → decide

### 🔹 Strengths
- Simple, intuitive, fast
- Uses accounting data
- Good for ranking ECMs in an energy audit
- Handles mutually exclusive projects (pick higher ARR)

### 🔹 Weaknesses
- **Ignores time value of money** (BIGGEST flaw)
- Uses profit, not cash flow
- Subjective MARR
- May conflict with NPV rankings

### 🔹 Energy Sector Use Cases
- Motor retrofits (IE2 → IE4)
- VFD installations
- APFC panels
- Solar PV / wind projects
- HVAC upgrades
- Boiler economizers
- Heat recovery systems

### 🔹 Exam-Ready Mantras
- *"Average profit, average investment, decision by MARR."*
- *"ARR is for screening, NPV is for deciding."*
- *"TVM-agnostic, but accounting-aware."*

---

### 📌 Final Note
You have completed **Module 4 — Average Rate of Return (ARR) Method** of **OEEET612 — Energy Management** as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**. Practice at least **3 numerical problems** (one each: single project, mutually exclusive, variable cash flow) and write down the formula sheet from memory before the exam. This will maximize your marks in both **Part A (3M)** and **Part B (14M)** questions. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internal Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is defined as the **discount rate** at which the present worth (or Net Present Value, NPV) of all cash flows — both inflows and outflows — generated by a project over its entire economic life becomes **exactly equal to zero**. Mathematically, it is the value of the interest rate $r$ that satisfies the governing NPV equation:

$$NPV(r) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

where $CF_t$ represents the net cash flow occurring at the end of period $t$, $n$ is the project life, and $r$ is the internal rate of return being solved for.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expected):** *"The Internal Rate of Return is that discount rate which equates the present worth of cash inflows to the present worth of cash outflows, making the Net Present Value of the project equal to zero."*

The IRR is expressed as a **percentage per annum (%)** and represents the **maximum rate of interest** a project could pay on its own capital investment, break even, and still recover all initial and operating costs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you lend **₹5,00,000** to your friend for a small business. Your friend promises to pay you back over **4 years** in unequal installments: **₹1,50,000**, **₹2,00,000**, **₹2,50,000**, and **₹1,50,000**.

Now, you want to know: *"What is the exact interest rate at which the present value of all my future repayments equals exactly the ₹5,00,000 I gave him today?"*

That magic interest rate is the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.

**Simple Intuition:**
- If IRR = **15%** and you could have safely deposited that money in a bank at **10%**, then the business project is *more profitable* than the bank → **ACCEPT** the project.
- If IRR = **8%** and the bank gives **10%**, then the project is *less profitable* than the safe option → **REJECT** the project.

In the context of **Energy Management**, IRR helps decision-makers evaluate energy conservation projects (retrofit investments, solar PV installations, efficient HVAC systems, etc.) by revealing the *effective percentage yield* of the energy-saving investment.

---

## 1.3 Decision Criterion (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Acceptance Rule using IRR:**
> - If $IRR \geq MARR$ (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) → **ACCEPT** the project
> - If $IRR < MARR$ → **REJECT** the project
> - For **mutually exclusive alternatives** → Use **Incremental IRR Analysis**

Where **MARR** is the lowest acceptable rate of return on an investment, typically set by the organization's finance department based on the **cost of capital**, opportunity cost, and risk profile.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Cash Flow Patterns

The nature and number of IRR solutions depend on the **sign changes** in the cash flow stream:

| Cash Flow Pattern | Description | IRR Solutions |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional** (Simple Investment) | One sign change (negative → positives) | **Unique IRR** |
| **Non-Conventional** (Non-Simple) | Multiple sign changes (+, −, +, −) | **Multiple or No Real IRR** |
| **Conventional with net inflow only** | All positives after initial | No real IRR (project is always profitable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiners frequently test the difference between **conventional and non-conventional cash flows**. Always count the sign changes before attempting IRR calculation!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing NPV Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The IRR is mathematically the **root of a polynomial** of degree $n$. The NPV of a typical capital investment project is:

$$NPV = -P + \frac{A_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{A_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{A_n}{(1+r)^n} + \frac{S}{(1+r)^n}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Initial investment (Capital outlay, occurring at $t = 0$)
- $A_t$ = Net annual cash inflow during year $t$
- $S$ = Salvage value at the end of project life
- $n$ = Useful life of the project in years
- $r$ = Discount rate (we solve for $r$ such that $NPV = 0$)

### The "Why" Behind the Logic:
1. We **discount** every future cash flow back to the present using rate $r$.
2. The present value of all future inflows must **exactly recover** the initial outlay.
3. The single rate $r$ that achieves this exact break-even is the **internal rate of return** — internal to the project, independent of external market rates.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **When to Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IRR Governing Equation** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Master equation for all IRR problems | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Linear Interpolation Formula** | $IRR = r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$ | When $r_1 < IRR < r_2$ and NPV changes sign | % per annum |
| **NPV at any rate r** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Trial NPV computation at chosen $r$ | Currency (₹) |
| **Acceptance Criterion** | $IRR \geq MARR$ | Decision rule for independent projects | % per annum |
| **Incremental IRR Condition** | $\Delta IRR \geq MARR$ | Mutually exclusive alternatives | % per annum |
| **Single Payment Compound Amount** | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | Forward compounding in IRR setup | Currency (₹) |
| **Single Payment Present Worth** | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discounting future cash to present | Currency (₹) |
| **Uniform Series Present Worth** | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Equivalent annual savings analysis | Currency (₹) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE — The interpolation formula gives an approximate IRR, not exact.** The accuracy improves as $r_2 - r_1$ is made smaller. Always attempt a **re-check** at the computed IRR to verify the sign of NPV.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Energy Management

The IRR method is extensively used in **Energy Management (KTU Module 4 — Economic Analysis)** for the following applications:

1. **Energy-efficient equipment retrofits** — e.g., replacing standard motors with IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motors in industrial plants.
2. **Solar PV installations** — evaluating the financial viability of rooftop solar systems for captive consumption.
3. **Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants** — comparing returns of waste-heat recovery investments.
4. **HVAC system upgrades** — variable frequency drives (VFDs), chillers, smart thermostats.
5. **Lighting retrofits** — LED conversion projects in commercial and industrial facilities.
6. **Building envelope improvements** — insulation, double-glazing, reflective roofing.

> [!NOTE]
> A high IRR on an energy project indicates strong **financial and environmental co-benefits**, making IRR a powerful tool for justifying green capital expenditures before corporate boards and financial controllers.

---

## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations (Board-Ready Points)

### **Advantages:**
- Considers the **time value of money**.
- Uses **all cash flows** over the entire project life.
- Provides a **percentage measure** directly comparable to MARR and cost of capital.
- Does not require pre-knowledge of MARR for calculation (though needed for decision).
- Intuitive interpretation — *"the project earns X% on its own capital."*

### **Limitations:**
- For **non-conventional cash flows**, multiple or no real IRR may exist.
- The **trial-and-error** method can be cumbersome without a computer.
- For **mutually exclusive projects** of different sizes/durations, IRR can give **misleading rankings** — NPV must be used as a tie-breaker or incremental IRR applied.
- Assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR (a debatable assumption).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Interpolation Formula (Mandatory KTU Derivation)

### **Starting Point:**
Suppose at a lower discount rate $r_1$, the NPV is **positive** ($NPV_1 > 0$), and at a higher discount rate $r_2$, the NPV is **negative** ($NPV_2 < 0$). By the **Intermediate Value Theorem** for continuous functions, an IRR must exist between $r_1$ and $r_2$.

### **Step 1 — Linear Assumption:**
Within the small interval $[r_1, r_2]$, the NPV function is approximated as a **straight line** connecting the two points $(r_1, NPV_1)$ and $(r_2, NPV_2)$.

### **Step 2 — Two-Point Form of a Line:**
The equation of a straight line passing through two known points is given by:

$$\frac{NPV - NPV_1}{r - r_1} = \frac{NPV_2 - NPV_1}{r_2 - r_1}$$

### **Step 3 — Apply the IRR Condition:**
At the true IRR, $NPV = 0$. Substituting this:

$$\frac{0 - NPV_1}{IRR - r_1} = \frac{NPV_2 - NPV_1}{r_2 - r_1}$$

### **Step 4 — Cross-Multiplication:**

$$-NPV_1 \cdot (r_2 - r_1) = (IRR - r_1)(NPV_2 - NPV_1)$$

### **Step 5 — Expand and Rearrange:**

$$-NPV_1(r_2 - r_1) = (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_2 - (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_1$$

$$-NPV_1 \cdot r_2 + NPV_1 \cdot r_1 = (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_2 - (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_1$$

$$\text{Add } (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_1 \text{ to both sides:}$$

$$-NPV_1 \cdot r_2 + NPV_1 \cdot r_1 + (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_1 = (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_2$$

$$\text{Factor } NPV_1 \text{ on the left:}$$

$$NPV_1 \left[ (IRR - r_1) + r_1 - r_2 \right] = (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_2$$

$$NPV_1 \left[ IRR - r_2 \right] = (IRR - r_1) \cdot NPV_2$$

### **Step 6 — Solve for IRR:**

$$IRR \cdot NPV_1 - r_2 \cdot NPV_1 = IRR \cdot NPV_2 - r_1 \cdot NPV_2$$

$$IRR \cdot NPV_1 - IRR \cdot NPV_2 = r_2 \cdot NPV_1 - r_1 \cdot NPV_2$$

$$IRR \cdot (NPV_1 - NPV_2) = r_2 \cdot NPV_1 - r_1 \cdot NPV_2$$

$$\boxed{IRR = r_1 + \left( \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \right) \times (r_2 - r_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Check:** Since $NPV_1 > 0$ and $NPV_2 < 0$, the denominator $(NPV_1 - NPV_2)$ is **positive** and greater than $NPV_1$, ensuring the bracketed fraction is between **0 and 1**, and IRR lies strictly between $r_1$ and $r_2$.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style (Full Step-by-Step)

### **Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing company in Kerala is evaluating an investment of **₹5,00,000** in an energy-efficient boiler system. The projected net cash inflows (energy cost savings) are as follows:

| Year | Net Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1,50,000 |
| 2 | 2,00,000 |
| 3 | 2,50,000 |
| 4 | 1,50,000 |

The MARR set by the finance department is **10% per annum**. Determine whether the project is financially viable using the IRR method.

---

### **Step 1 — Identify the Cash Flow Stream:**

At $t = 0$: $CF_0 = -5,00,000$ (outflow)
At $t = 1$: $CF_1 = +1,50,000$
At $t = 2$: $CF_2 = +2,00,000$
At $t = 3$: $CF_3 = +2,50,000$
At $t = 4$: $CF_4 = +1,50,000$

This is a **conventional cash flow pattern** (one sign change), so a **unique IRR** exists.

---

### **Step 2 — Trial Calculation at r₁ = 10% (Lower Bracket):**

$$NPV_{10\%} = -5,00,000 + \frac{1,50,000}{1.10^1} + \frac{2,00,000}{1.10^2} + \frac{2,50,000}{1.10^3} + \frac{1,50,000}{1.10^4}$$

**Compute each discount factor individually:**

$$1.10^1 = 1.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{1.10} = 1,36,363.64$$

$$1.10^2 = 1.21 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,00,000}{1.21} = 1,65,289.26$$

$$1.10^3 = 1.331 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,50,000}{1.331} = 1,87,828.70$$

$$1.10^4 = 1.4641 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{1.4641} = 1,02,452.43$$

**Sum the present values of inflows:**

$$\sum PV = 1,36,363.64 + 1,65,289.26 + 1,87,828.70 + 1,02,452.43 = 5,91,934.03$$

**Compute NPV:**

$$NPV_{10\%} = -5,00,000 + 5,91,934.03 = +91,934.03$$

Since $NPV_{10\%} > 0$, the true IRR is **greater than 10%**.

---

### **Step 3 — Trial Calculation at r₂ = 20% (Upper Bracket):**

$$NPV_{20\%} = -5,00,000 + \frac{1,50,000}{1.20^1} + \frac{2,00,000}{1.20^2} + \frac{2,50,000}{1.20^3} + \frac{1,50,000}{1.20^4}$$

**Compute each discount factor individually:**

$$1.20^1 = 1.20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{1.20} = 1,25,000.00$$

$$1.20^2 = 1.44 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,00,000}{1.44} = 1,38,888.89$$

$$1.20^3 = 1.728 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,50,000}{1.728} = 1,44,675.93$$

$$1.20^4 = 2.0736 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{2.0736} = 72,337.96$$

**Sum the present values of inflows:**

$$\sum PV = 1,25,000.00 + 1,38,888.89 + 1,44,675.93 + 72,337.96 = 4,80,902.78$$

**Compute NPV:**

$$NPV_{20\%} = -5,00,000 + 4,80,902.78 = -19,097.22$$

Since $NPV_{20\%} < 0$, the true IRR is **less than 20%**.

---

### **Step 4 — Apply the Linear Interpolation Formula:**

We now have $NPV_1 = +91,934.03$ at $r_1 = 10\%$ and $NPV_2 = -19,097.22$ at $r_2 = 20\%$.

$$IRR = 10 + \left( \frac{91,934.03}{91,934.03 - (-19,097.22)} \right) \times (20 - 10)$$

$$IRR = 10 + \left( \frac{91,934.03}{1,11,031.25} \right) \times 10$$

$$IRR = 10 + (0.8279) \times 10$$

$$IRR = 10 + 8.279$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 18.28\% \text{ per annum}}$$

---

### **Step 5 — Apply the Decision Criterion:**

- Computed **IRR = 18.28%**
- Required **MARR = 10%**
- Since $IRR > MARR$, the project is **financially viable** → **ACCEPT** the investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The energy-efficient boiler investment yields an effective return of 18.28% on capital, far exceeding the company's 10% hurdle rate. Apart from monetary savings, the project delivers indirect benefits — reduced carbon footprint, lower maintenance, and improved process reliability.

---

## 3.3 Verification Step (Optional but Recommended in KTU Solutions)

To verify, recompute NPV at $r = 18.28\%$:

$$1.1828^1 = 1.1828 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{1.1828} = 1,26,817.05$$

$$1.1828^2 = 1.3990 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,00,000}{1.3990} = 1,42,958.18$$

$$1.1828^3 = 1.6547 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2,50,000}{1.6547} = 1,51,083.21$$

$$1.1828^4 = 1.9571 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1,50,000}{1.9571} = 76,645.00$$

**Sum of PVs of inflows:**

$$\sum PV \approx 4,97,503.44$$

**NPV at 18.28%:**

$$NPV \approx -5,00,000 + 4,97,503.44 = -2,496.56$$

The small negative residual confirms the **linear approximation error** — the true IRR lies between 18.00% and 18.28%. For a 14-mark KTU answer, this level of precision is fully acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IRR Methodology and Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Project Cash Flow Data]) --> id1[Step A: Tabulate Cash Flows CF sub t for t equals 0 to n]
    id1 --> id2[Step B: Count Sign Changes in Cash Flow Stream]
    id2 --> id3{Conventional Single Sign Change?}
    id3 --> id4[Yes: Unique IRR Exists]
    id3 --> id5[No: Multiple or No Real IRR Possible]
    id4 --> id6[Step C: Assume Lower Rate r1 and Compute NPV1]
    id5 --> id7[Use NPV Method or Modified IRR Instead]
    id6 --> id8{NPV1 greater than 0?}
    id8 --> id9[Yes: IRR is greater than r1]
    id8 --> id10[No: Reduce r1 and Recompute]
    id9 --> id11[Step D: Assume Higher Rate r2 and Compute NPV2]
    id11 --> id12{NPV2 less than 0?}
    id12 --> id13[Yes: IRR is between r1 and r2]
    id12 --> id14[No: Increase r2 and Recompute]
    id13 --> id15[Step E: Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    id15 --> id16[IRR equals r1 plus NPV1 divided by NPV1 minus NPV2 times r2 minus r1]
    id16 --> id17[Step F: Compare IRR with MARR]
    id17 --> id18{IRR greater than or equal to MARR?}
    id18 --> id19[Accept the Project]
    id18 --> id20[Reject the Project]
    id19 --> id21([End: Project Accepted])
    id20 --> id22([End: Project Rejected])
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — NPV vs Discount Rate Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    aAxis[NPV Axis Vertical] --- bAxis[Discount Rate Axis Horizontal]
    aAxis --> cCurve[NPV Curve Starts Positive at r equals 0]
    cCurve --> dCurve[Curve Descends as r Increases]
    dCurve --> ePoint[Cross Point: NPV equals 0 at IRR]
    ePoint --> fCurve[Curve Continues into Negative Region]
    fCurve --> gPoint[NPV becomes large Negative as r grows]
    subgraph region1
        h1[Region A: Accept if r is less than IRR]
    end
    subgraph region2
        h2[Region B: Reject if r is greater than IRR]
    end
    ePoint --- h1
    ePoint --- h2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Incremental IRR Analysis (For Mutually Exclusive Projects)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pStart([Two Mutually Exclusive Alternatives A and B]) --> pStep1[Step 1: Identify Higher Initial Cost Alternative]
    pStep1 --> pStep2[Step 2: Compute Incremental Cash Flows Delta CFt equals CF of Higher Cost minus CF of Lower Cost]
    pStep2 --> pStep3[Step 3: Compute Incremental IRR by Solving NPV of Delta CFt equals 0]
    pStep3 --> pStep4{Delta IRR greater than or equal to MARR?}
    pStep4 --> pYes[Yes: Choose Higher Cost Alternative]
    pStep4 --> pNo[No: Choose Lower Cost Alternative]
    pYes --> pEnd([Decision Finalized])
    pNo --> pEnd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (startA, id1, pStep1, etc.). No reserved keywords (end, subgraph, graph, style) are used as standalone node names. All labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italic formatting.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Energy Project IRR Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eBox1[Energy Audit Data] --> eBox2[Identify Conservation Opportunities]
    eBox2 --> eBox3[Estimate Capital Investment P]
    eBox3 --> eBox4[Estimate Annual Energy Savings CFt]
    eBox4 --> eBox5[Compute IRR via Trial and Error plus Interpolation]
    eBox5 --> eBox6{IRR greater than MARR?}
    eBox6 --> eYes[Implement Energy Project]
    eBox6 --> eNo[Search for Better Alternative or Reject]
    eYes --> eBox7[Monitor Post Implementation kWh Savings]
    eBox7 --> eBox8[Verify Achieved IRR vs Predicted IRR]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of a project. State the decision rule for accepting a project based on the IRR criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Internal Rate of Return is defined as the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all cash flows associated with a project equals zero. It represents the rate of return earned solely by the project's internal cash generation, independent of external market interest rates. Mathematically, IRR is the value of $r$ satisfying:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

**Decision Rule (1 Mark):**
- If $IRR \geq MARR$ (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) → **Accept** the project
- If $IRR < MARR$ → **Reject** the project

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by "conventional" and "non-conventional" cash flow patterns? Why are they significant in IRR analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Conventional Cash Flow (1.5 Marks):** A cash flow pattern in which the sign of the cash flow changes only **once** — typically starting with a single initial outflow (investment) followed by a series of inflows. Such patterns yield a **unique IRR** and are mathematically well-behaved.

**Non-Conventional Cash Flow (1.5 Marks):** A cash flow pattern exhibiting **more than one sign change** during the project life (e.g., outflow-inflow-outflow-inflow). Such patterns can yield **multiple IRRs** or **no real IRR** at all, making the IRR method unreliable unless supplemented with NPV or Modified IRR (MIRR) analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

A textile mill in Ernakulam is evaluating the installation of a **solar PV rooftop system** requiring an initial investment of **₹8,00,000**. The expected net annual savings in electricity bills (cash inflows) over its 5-year useful life are:

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 2,50,000 |
| 3 | 3,00,000 |
| 4 | 2,00,000 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 |

The company's MARR is **12% per annum**. As an energy manager:
**(a)** Calculate the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the proposed solar installation. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Advise whether the textile mill should accept or reject the project. Comment on the engineering and financial implications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — IRR Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Cash Flow Stream:**
- $CF_0 = -8,00,000$
- $CF_1 = +2,00,000$; $CF_2 = +2,50,000$; $CF_3 = +3,00,000$; $CF_4 = +2,00,000$; $CF_5 = +1,50,000$

**Step 2 — Trial NPV at r₁ = 12% (lower bracket guess):**

$$NPV_{12\%} = -8,00,000 + \frac{2,00,000}{1.12} + \frac{2,50,000}{1.12^2} + \frac{3,00,000}{1.12^3} + \frac{2,00,000}{1.12^4} + \frac{1,50,000}{1.12^5}$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.12} = 1,78,571.43$$

$$\frac{2,50,000}{1.2544} = 1,99,298.47$$

$$\frac{3,00,000}{1.4049} = 2,13,538.61$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.5735} = 1,27,106.78$$

$$\frac{1,50,000}{1.7623} = 85,114.51$$

**Sum of present values of inflows:**

$$\sum PV = 1,78,571.43 + 1,99,298.47 + 2,13,538.61 + 1,27,106.78 + 85,114.51 = 8,03,629.80$$

**NPV at 12%:**

$$NPV_{12\%} = -8,00,000 + 8,03,629.80 = +3,629.80$$

Since NPV is marginally positive, IRR is **slightly above 12%**.

**Step 3 — Trial NPV at r₂ = 13%:**

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.13} = 1,76,991.15$$

$$\frac{2,50,000}{1.2769} = 1,95,775.59$$

$$\frac{3,00,000}{1.4429} = 2,07,911.20$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.6305} = 1,22,661.16$$

$$\frac{1,50,000}{1.8424} = 81,413.85$$

**Sum of PVs at 13%:**

$$\sum PV = 1,76,991.15 + 1,95,775.59 + 2,07,911.20 + 1,22,661.16 + 81,413.85 = 7,84,752.95$$

**NPV at 13%:**

$$NPV_{13\%} = -8,00,000 + 7,84,752.95 = -15,247.05$$

Since NPV is negative, IRR lies **between 12% and 13%**.

**Step 4 — Apply Linear Interpolation:**

$$IRR = 12 + \left( \frac{3,629.80}{3,629.80 - (-15,247.05)} \right) \times (13 - 12)$$

$$IRR = 12 + \left( \frac{3,629.80}{18,876.85} \right) \times 1$$

$$IRR = 12 + 0.1923$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 12.19\% \text{ per annum}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Decision and Implications (7 Marks):**

**Decision (3 Marks):**
- Computed IRR = **12.19%**
- MARR = **12.00%**
- Since $IRR > MARR$ (marginally), the solar PV project is **technically acceptable** from a financial standpoint.

**Engineering and Financial Implications (4 Marks):**

1. **Marginal Viability:** The IRR exceeds MARR by only **0.19 percentage points**, indicating the project is on the borderline of financial acceptability. Sensitivity analysis on electricity tariff escalation and capital cost is essential.
2. **Tariff Escalation Dependency:** Solar PV IRR improves significantly if grid electricity tariff rises at 5–7% per annum, a realistic scenario in Kerala (KSEB tariff revisions).
3. **Non-Monetary Benefits:** Beyond IRR, the mill gains carbon credits, reduced diesel generator backup usage, peak-load shaving, and brand-image enhancement — these are not captured in IRR but improve overall project worth.
4. **Recommendation:** Proceed with installation, but negotiate a lower capital cost with the EPC contractor and explore **MNRE/Kerala ANERT subsidies** to improve IRR margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT compute only one trial NPV and conclude.** Always show *two trials* (one positive, one negative NPV) and then apply interpolation — examiners award step marks only for the *complete procedure*.
> - **Sign convention error:** Many students forget the **negative sign on initial investment** at $t = 0$. This is the most common mark-deducting mistake.
> - **Rounding error:** Avoid rounding intermediate cash flows to nearest rupee; retain at least 2 decimals to preserve IRR accuracy.
> - **Missing conclusion:** A 14-mark IRR problem *without* an explicit "Accept" or "Reject" statement with justification will lose 2–3 marks.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A factory is comparing **two mutually exclusive energy conservation alternatives** for compressed air system upgrade:

| Parameter | Alternative X (VFD Retrofit) | Alternative Y (New Compressor) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (₹) | 4,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| Annual Savings (₹) | 1,20,000 | 1,90,000 |
| Life (years) | 5 | 5 |
| Salvage Value (₹) | 30,000 | 50,000 |

MARR = **10% per annum**.

**(a)** Determine the IRR of each alternative and check individual acceptability. **(6 Marks)**
**(b)** Using the **Incremental IRR method**, determine which alternative should be selected. **(8 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — IRR of Each Alternative (6 Marks):**

**Alternative X (VFD Retrofit):**

NPV equation:

$$0 = -4,00,000 + 1,20,000 \times (P/A, r, 5) + 30,000 \times (P/F, r, 5)$$

**Trial at r = 12%:**
$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = 3.6048$; $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$

$$NPV_X = -4,00,000 + 1,20,000(3.6048) + 30,000(0.5674)$$

$$NPV_X = -4,00,000 + 4,32,576 + 17,022 = +49,598$$

NPV positive → IRR > 12%.

**Trial at r = 20%:**
$(P/A, 20\%, 5) = 2.9906$; $(P/F, 20\%, 5) = 0.4019$

$$NPV_X = -4,00,000 + 1,20,000(2.9906) + 30,000(0.4019)$$

$$NPV_X = -4,00,000 + 3,58,872 + 12,057 = -29,071$$

NPV negative → IRR < 20%.

**Interpolation:**

$$IRR_X = 12 + \frac{49,598}{49,598 + 29,071} \times (20 - 12)$$

$$IRR_X = 12 + \frac{49,598}{78,669} \times 8 = 12 + 5.04 = \mathbf{17.04\%}$$

Since $IRR_X = 17.04\% > MARR = 10\%$, **Alternative X is acceptable**.

---

**Alternative Y (New Compressor):**

NPV equation:

$$0 = -7,00,000 + 1,90,000 \times (P/A, r, 5) + 50,000 \times (P/F, r, 5)$$

**Trial at r = 10%:**
$(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 3.7908$; $(P/F, 10\%, 5) = 0.6209$

$$NPV_Y = -7,00,000 + 1,90,000(3.7908) + 50,000(0.6209)$$

$$NPV_Y = -7,00,000 + 7,20,252 + 31,045 = +51,297$$

NPV positive → IRR > 10%.

**Trial at r = 14%:**
$(P/A, 14\%, 5) = 3.4331$; $(P/F, 14\%, 5) = 0.5194$

$$NPV_Y = -7,00,000 + 1,90,000(3.4331) + 50,000(0.5194)$$

$$NPV_Y = -7,00,000 + 6,52,289 + 25,970 = -21,741$$

NPV negative → IRR < 14%.

**Interpolation:**

$$IRR_Y = 10 + \frac{51,297}{51,297 + 21,741} \times (14 - 10)$$

$$IRR_Y = 10 + \frac{51,297}{73,038} \times 4 = 10 + 2.81 = \mathbf{12.81\%}$$

Since $IRR_Y = 12.81\% > MARR = 10\%$, **Alternative Y is also acceptable**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Incremental IRR Analysis (8 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Higher-Cost Alternative:**
Y (₹7,00,000) > X (₹4,00,000), so analyze incremental cash flows (Y − X).

**Step 2 — Incremental Cash Flow Stream:**

$$\Delta CF_0 = -7,00,000 - (-4,00,000) = -3,00,000$$

$$\Delta CF_t \text{ (annual)} = 1,90,000 - 1,20,000 = 70,000 \text{ for } t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$

$$\Delta S = 50,000 - 30,000 = 20,000 \text{ (at } t = 5)$$

**Step 3 — Trial NPV at r = 10%:**

$$NPV_{\Delta} = -3,00,000 + 70,000(3.7908) + 20,000(0.6209)$$

$$NPV_{\Delta} = -3,00,000 + 2,65,356 + 12,418 = -22,226$$

Wait — at 10%, incremental NPV is **negative**, which means incremental IRR is **less than 10%**. Let us confirm at r = 5%:

**Trial at r = 5%:**
$(P/A, 5\%, 5) = 4.3295$; $(P/F, 5\%, 5) = 0.7835$

$$NPV_{\Delta} = -3,00,000 + 70,000(4.3295) + 20,000(0.7835)$$

$$NPV_{\Delta} = -3,00,000 + 3,03,065 + 15,670 = +18,735$$

NPV positive at 5% → incremental IRR between 5% and 10%.

**Step 4 — Interpolation:**

$$\Delta IRR = 5 + \frac{18,735}{18,735 + 22,226} \times (10 - 5)$$

$$\Delta IRR = 5 + \frac{18,735}{40,961} \times 5 = 5 + 2.29 = \mathbf{7.29\%}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since $\Delta IRR = 7.29\% < MARR = 10\%$, the **extra investment of ₹3,00,000 in Alternative Y is NOT justified**.

**Step 6 — Final Selection (2 Marks):**

- Both alternatives individually pass the MARR test.
- However, the **incremental analysis** reveals that the additional capital in Y does not earn the required MARR.
- Therefore, **Alternative X (VFD Retrofit) should be selected** because it provides the required return at lower capital outlay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Incremental IRR:**
> - **Step sequence matters:** First check individual acceptability. If both pass, then only proceed with incremental analysis on the higher-cost option. Reversing this order is a common error.
> - **Sign convention on incremental cash flow:** $\Delta CF_0$ must be **negative** (extra investment). A positive sign indicates the analyst computed the lower-cost minus higher-cost, reversing the logic.
> - **Conclusion clarity:** Do not stop at "ΔIRR = 7.29%". Explicitly state *"Since ΔIRR < MARR, choose the lower-cost alternative X."*
> - **Life mismatch:** When alternatives have unequal lives, KTU examiners may ask for *Annual Worth* comparison as a cross-check. Note this caveat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 4 — IRR Method Revision Sheet:**

- **Definition:** IRR is the discount rate $r$ that makes $NPV = 0$.
- **Governing Equation:** $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$
- **Interpolation Formula:** $IRR = r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$; Reject if $IRR < MARR$.
- **Conventional Cash Flow:** One sign change → Unique IRR.
- **Non-Conventional Cash Flow:** Multiple sign changes → Multiple or no real IRR (use NPV or MIRR).
- **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Use **Incremental IRR** analysis — choose higher-cost option only if $\Delta IRR \geq MARR$.
- **Salvage Value Treatment:** Add as a positive cash flow at the end of project life (year $n$).
- **Reinvestment Assumption:** IRR implicitly assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR itself (often critiqued).
- **Units:** IRR is in **% per annum**; cash flows in **currency units (₹)**.
- **Board Tip:** Always show *two trial NPVs* (one positive, one negative) before applying interpolation — partial marking depends on this procedural completeness.
- **Common Pitfall:** Missing negative sign on initial investment at $t = 0$ → loses 1–2 marks per error.
- **Energy Management Linkage:** IRR is the standard metric used in *Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)* and *ESCO (Energy Service Company)* business models to evaluate shared-savings projects.
- **NPV vs IRR Conflict:** When mutually exclusive alternatives have different scales, NPV and IRR can give different rankings — always cross-check with NPV.
- **MIRR Mention (Bonus Point):** Modified IRR uses the firm's reinvestment rate instead of the project IRR — gaining popularity for resolving multiple-IRR issues in energy projects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Value Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Present Value Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Present Value (PV) Method**, also known as the **Present Worth (PW) Method**, is a discounted cash flow (DCF) technique used in energy management and engineering economics to evaluate the economic viability of an investment by converting all future cash inflows and outflows (energy cost savings, maintenance costs, initial investment, salvage value) into an equivalent lump-sum value at **time zero (the present)**. 

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEEET612, the Present Value Method is the most rigorous time-adjusted method of comparing mutually exclusive energy conservation measures (ECMs) because it correctly accounts for the **time value of money (TVM)** — the principle that a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** *Present Value is the current equivalent worth of a future sum of money or a stream of future cash flows, given a specified rate of return (discount rate, **i**) over a specified period (**n** years).*

The mathematical foundation rests on two core KTU-listed factors:
- **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)** → $\frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$
- **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)** → $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Locker" Intuition

Imagine your grandfather promised to give you **₹1,00,000** ten years from now. Would you accept a lesser amount today? 

If a bank offers a fixed deposit interest rate of **8% per annum**, then logically, if you put **₹46,319** in the bank *today*, the bank will pay you exactly **₹1,00,000** after 10 years (because ₹46,319 compounds at 8% annually to grow into ₹1,00,000). Therefore, the **Present Value** of that future ₹1,00,000 promise is **₹46,319 today**.

**Extension to Energy Management:**
- The "₹1,00,000" is your **future energy cost savings** in Year 10.
- The "₹46,319" is its **present equivalent value** in Year 0.
- The "8%" is the **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** or **discount rate** of the company.
- The "10 years" is the **project life** of the energy efficiency equipment (e.g., a chiller, VFD, or LED retrofit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do this?** Because energy projects involve a **large initial capital outlay today** (to buy a VFD or replace boiler insulation) in exchange for **small annual savings spread over many years**. The PV method lets you compare "apples to apples" by expressing everything in *today's rupees*.

### 1.3 Key KTU Terminology & Constants

| KTU Term | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Discount Rate (Interest Rate) | **i** | Cost of capital / MARR (typically **8%–15%** for energy projects) |
| Project Life | **n** | Useful life of the energy equipment in years |
| Initial Investment | **P** | Capital cost at time zero (₹) |
| Annual Cash Flow / Savings | **A** | Net annual energy cost savings (₹/year) |
| Future Value | **F** | Salvage value or accumulated savings at year *n* |
