| Present Worth | **PW** | Equivalent value of all cash flows at year 0 |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** All *inflows* (savings, salvage) are treated as **positive** cash flows. All *outflows* (investment, costs) are treated as **negative** cash flows. The discount rate **i** is expressed as a decimal (e.g., 10% = 0.10).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophy Behind Present Value Method

The PV method is built on three pillars that KTU examiners test repeatedly:

1. **Time Value of Money (TVM):** Money loses purchasing power over time due to inflation and opportunity cost.
2. **Cash Flow Equivalence:** Two cash flow streams are *economically equivalent* at a given interest rate if their present values are equal.
3. **Net Present Value (NPV) Criterion:** An energy project is **economically justified** if and only if **NPV ≥ 0** at the chosen MARR.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Identify all cash flows** over the project life (initial cost, annual savings, O&M costs, salvage).
- **Step 2 — Select the discount rate (i)**, usually the company's cost of capital or MARR.
- **Step 3 — Convert every future cash flow** to its equivalent present value using the appropriate factor.
- **Step 4 — Sum all present values** algebraically to obtain the **Net Present Value (NPV)** or **Total Present Worth (TPW)**.
- **Step 5 — Apply the decision rule**:
   * **Single Project:** Accept if NPV ≥ 0.
   * **Mutually Exclusive Alternatives:** Choose the one with the **highest positive NPV** (or the least negative, if all are negative — meaning reject all).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **mandatory formula set** for any KTU 2024 ESE question on Present Value Method. Memorize these — they are the bread-and-butter of the 14-mark problems.

| # | KTU Factor Name | Symbol | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Present Worth Factor | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $\frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | Find PV of a *single* future sum *F* occurring in year *n* |
| 2 | Single Payment Future Worth Factor | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^n$ | Find FV of a single present sum *P* |
| 3 | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Find PV of a *uniform annual* series *A* for *n* years |
| 4 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Convert a present investment *P* into equivalent annual cost |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Find the annual deposit *A* needed to reach future sum *F* |
| 6 | Uniform Series Future Worth | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Find FV of a uniform annual series |
| 7 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV$ | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Sum of all discounted cash flows |

**Boundary Conditions & Special Cases:**
- If **i = 0**, then $(P/A, 0, n) = n$ and $(P/F, 0, n) = 1$ (no discounting, simple arithmetic sum).
- If **n → ∞**, then $(P/A, i, \infty) = \frac{1}{i}$ (perpetuity case — used for evaluating solar PV with infinite life).
- The factor notation $(X/Y, i, n)$ is the **KTU-prescribed standard** — always read it as "Find X, given Y, at rate i over n years."

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Engineering

- **Evaluating VFD retrofits** on motors (savings in kWh × tariff).
- **Comparing chiller replacement vs. chiller refurbishment** as mutually exclusive alternatives.
- **Solar PV feasibility** — comparing grid-tie cost vs. lifetime electricity savings.
- **ESCO (Energy Service Company) contracts** — verifying that projected savings actually exceed the financed capital cost in present-value terms.
- **Lighting retrofit (LED vs. CFL vs. conventional)** — deciding optimal upgrade pathway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)

**Engineering Premise:** Money grows at compound interest. If you invest **P** today at rate **i** for **n** years, it will mature to a future value **F**. We want to find P in terms of F.

$$
F = P(1+i)^n
$$

**Rearranging for P (the present worth):**

$$
P = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Identifying the factor:**

$$
\left(\frac{P}{F}, i, n\right) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}
$$

> This is the **SPPWF** — it is the most fundamental factor. The denominator $(1+i)^n$ is called the **discounting factor**, and the entire expression is the **discount factor** itself.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)

**Engineering Premise:** We want to find a single equivalent present value **P** that equals the sum of *n* equal annual cash flows **A** occurring at the end of each year, discounted at rate **i**.

**Setting up the sum:** The cash flow A occurs at the end of years 1, 2, 3, ..., n.

$$
P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \dots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

$$
P = A \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Using the finite geometric series formula:**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} x^t = \frac{x(x^n - 1)}{x - 1} \quad \text{(for } x \neq 1\text{)}
$$

**Substituting** $x = \frac{1}{(1+i)}$ and simplifying:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \frac{\frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(1+i)^n} - 1\right]}{\frac{1}{(1+i)} - 1}
$$

**After algebraic simplification (shown in compact form):**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

**Therefore, the USPWF is:**

$$
\left(\frac{P}{A}, i, n\right) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** At i = 0, the formula gives $\frac{0}{0}$ — an indeterminate form. Using L'Hôpital's rule or direct substitution, we recover **P = nA**, which is intuitively correct: with no interest, the present value of n annual payments is just the arithmetic sum.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — A KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
> *A factory in Kerala is considering the installation of an automatic power factor correction (APFC) panel for its industrial plant. The initial investment is **₹5,00,000**. The expected annual savings from reduced maximum demand and reduced kVAh charges is **₹1,20,000** per year. The panel has a useful life of **8 years** with a salvage value of **₹50,000**. The company's MARR is **12% per annum**. Use the Present Value Method to determine whether the project is economically justified.*

**Given Data:**
- Initial investment P = ₹5,00,000
- Annual savings A = ₹1,20,000
- Project life n = 8 years
- Salvage value S = ₹50,000
- MARR i = 12% = 0.12

**Step 1 — Calculate the required factor values:**

We need $(P/A, 12\%, 8)$ and $(P/F, 12\%, 8)$.

**Computing $(1+i)^n$:**

$$
(1 + 0.12)^8 = 1.12^8
$$

$$
1.12^2 = 1.2544
$$

$$
1.12^4 = 1.2544^2 = 1.57351936
$$

$$
1.12^8 = 1.57351936^2 = 2.475963176
$$

**Computing $(P/F, 12\%, 8)$:**

$$
\left(\frac{P}{F}, 0.12, 8\right) = \frac{1}{1.12^8} = \frac{1}{2.475963} = 0.40388
$$

**Computing $(P/A, 12\%, 8)$:**

$$
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 0.12, 8\right) = \frac{1.12^8 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.12^8} = \frac{2.475963 - 1}{0.12 \times 2.475963}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.475963}{0.297116} = 4.96764
$$

> **KTU Required Standard Tables:** These values are also obtainable directly from any standard engineering economics factor table. KTU examiners typically expect you to *show the formula AND cite the table value*. Always write: *"(P/A, 12%, 8) = 4.9676 [from factor table]"*

**Step 2 — Compute Present Value of Annual Savings:**

$$
P_{\text{savings}} = A \times \left(\frac{P}{A}, 0.12, 8\right) = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.96764
$$

$$
P_{\text{savings}} = ₹5{,}96{,}116.8
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Present Value of Salvage Value:**

$$
P_{\text{salvage}} = S \times \left(\frac{P}{F}, 0.12, 8\right) = 50{,}000 \times 0.40388
$$

$$
P_{\text{salvage}} = ₹20{,}194
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Present Value (NPV):**

$$
NPV = -P + P_{\text{savings}} + P_{\text{salvage}}
$$

$$
NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}96{,}116.8 + 20{,}194
$$

$$
NPV = +₹1{,}16{,}310.8
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Decision Rule:**

Since **NPV = +₹1,16,310.8 > 0**, the APFC panel installation is **economically justified** at a 12% MARR.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Alternative Comparison

**Scenario:** A Kerala textile mill must choose between three energy-efficient boiler options. Present Worth Method is to be applied for *n* = 10 years, *i* = 10%.

| Alternative | Initial Cost (₹) | Annual Savings (₹) | O&M Cost (₹/yr) | Net Annual A (₹) | PW of A (₹) | PW of Savings − Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Boiler A (Condensing) | 8,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 30,000 | 1,70,000 | 1,70,000 × 6.1446 = 10,44,582 | 10,44,582 − 8,00,000 = **+2,44,582** |
| Boiler B (Standard) | 5,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 25,000 | 1,25,000 | 1,25,000 × 6.1446 = 7,68,075 | 7,68,075 − 5,00,000 = **+2,68,075** |
| Boiler C (Biomass) | 10,00,000 | 2,80,000 | 40,000 | 2,40,000 | 2,40,000 × 6.1446 = 14,74,704 | 14,74,704 − 10,00,000 = **+4,74,704** |

**Decision:** **Boiler C (Biomass)** is selected as it yields the **highest positive NPV of ₹4,74,704**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For mutually exclusive alternatives, never select based on the *highest savings* or the *lowest investment*. The only correct KTU criterion is the **highest positive Net Present Value (NPV)** or the **highest Present Worth (PW) of net benefits**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Present Value Method Decision Engine

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Energy Project Proposal] --> B[Identify All Cash Flows]
    B --> C[Initial Investment P]
    B --> D[Annual Net Savings A]
    B --> E[Salvage Value S]
    B --> F[Project Life n]
    B --> G[MARR / Discount Rate i]

    C --> H[Compute PW of Outflows]
    D --> I[Apply P/A factor: P savings = A x P/A i,n]
    E --> J[Apply P/F factor: P salvage = S x P/F i,n]
    F --> K[Use in all factor calculations]
    G --> K

    H --> L[Sum All Present Values]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{NPV = Sum of all PW}

    M -->|NPV > 0| N[ACCEPT Project]
    M -->|NPV = 0| O[ACCEPT - Indifferent]
    M -->|NPV < 0| P[REJECT Project]

    N --> Q[Implement Energy Conservation Measure]
    O --> Q
    P --> R[Seek Alternative ECM]
<!-- mermaid -->

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Cash Flow Transformation Pipeline

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A1[Year 1 Cash Flow] --> A2[Year 2 Cash Flow]
    A2 --> A3[Year 3 Cash Flow]
    A3 --> A4[Year n Cash Flow]

    A1 --> B1[Discount by 1/(1+i)^1]
    A2 --> B2[Discount by 1/(1+i)^2]
    A3 --> B3[Discount by 1/(1+i)^3]
    A4 --> B4[Discount by 1/(1+i)^n]

    B1 --> C1[Year 0 Present Value 1]
    B2 --> C2[Year 0 Present Value 2]
    B3 --> C3[Year 0 Present Value 3]
    B4 --> C4[Year 0 Present Value n]

    C1 --> D[Algebraic Summation Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E[Net Present Value NPV at Year 0]
<!-- mermaid -->

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Factor Inter-relationships

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    P[Present Worth P] --> F[Future Worth F]
    P --> A[Annual Worth A]

    F --> P_F["P = F x P/F factor"]
    P --> F_P["F = P x F/P factor"]
    A --> P_A["P = A x P/A factor"]
    P --> A_P["A = P x A/P factor"]
    F --> A_F["A = F x A/F factor"]
    A --> F_A["F = A x F/A factor"]

    F_P --> P_F
    P_A --> A_P
    A_F --> F_A
<!-- mermaid -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Factor Notation:** $(X/Y, i, n)$ → "Find X, *given* Y." For example, $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 7.722$ means ₹1 of annual savings for 5 years at 10% is equivalent to ₹7.722 *today*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Present Worth method. State its decision rule for evaluating a single energy conservation project.*

**Model Answer:**
The Present Worth (PW) method is a discounted cash flow technique that converts all future cash flows associated with an energy project — initial investment, annual energy cost savings, O&M costs, and salvage value — into an equivalent lump-sum value at time zero (the present) using a specified discount rate (MARR) *i* over project life *n*.
**Decision Rule:** For a single project, accept the energy conservation measure if and only if the **Net Present Value (NPV) ≥ 0**; otherwise, reject it.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Write the mathematical expressions for the Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF) and the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF).*

**Model Answer:**
- **SPPWF:** $\left(\frac{P}{F}, i, n\right) = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$
- **USPWF:** $\left(\frac{P}{A}, i, n\right) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Model]`

> A proposed energy-efficient refrigeration plant for a seafood export unit in Kochi requires an initial investment of **₹12,00,000**. It yields annual energy cost savings of **₹3,50,000** and incurs an annual O&M expense of **₹50,000** (net annual cash flow A = ₹3,00,000). The useful life of the plant is **10 years** with a negligible salvage value. The company's MARR is **15%**. 
>
> **(a)** Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project. State whether the investment is justified. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the MARR is increased to **20%**, determine the breakeven discount rate at which the project just becomes unviable. Comment on the sensitivity of the decision. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant factor:** Since A is uniform for n = 10 years, we need $(P/A, 15\%, 10)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the factor using the formula:**

$$
(1.15)^{10} = 4.0456
$$

$$
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 0.15, 10\right) = \frac{4.0456 - 1}{0.15 \times 4.0456} = \frac{3.0456}{0.60684} = 5.0188
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of net annual savings:**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 5.0188 = ₹15{,}05{,}640
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV:**

$$
NPV = -12{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}05{,}640 = +₹3{,}05{,}640
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** Since NPV = +₹3,05,640 > 0, the refrigeration plant is **economically justified at 15% MARR**.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

We want the discount rate *i\** at which NPV = 0.

$$
0 = -12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times \left(\frac{P}{A}, i^*, 10\right)
$$

$$
\left(\frac{P}{A}, i^*, 10\right) = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 4.0
$$

Solving the equation $\frac{(1+i^*)^{10} - 1}{i^*(1+i^*)^{10}} = 4.0$ by trial and error:

- At *i* = 20%: $(P/A, 20\%, 10) = 4.1925$ (NPV positive)
- At *i* = 22%: $(P/A, 22\%, 10) = 3.9232$ (NPV negative)
- Interpolating: $i^* \approx 21.2\%$

**Conclusion:** The project is highly sensitive to the discount rate. A jump from 15% to 20% reduces NPV dramatically, and the breakeven rate is approximately **21.2%**. Any cost of capital above 21.2% will render the project unviable.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model]`

> Two mutually exclusive energy conservation measures are being evaluated for a campus-wide LED retrofit project in a Kerala engineering college:
> - **Option X (Premium LED):** Initial cost = ₹20,00,000; Annual savings = ₹5,50,000; Life = 10 years; Salvage = ₹2,00,000.
> - **Option Y (Standard LED):** Initial cost = ₹15,00,000; Annual savings = ₹4,00,000; Life = 10 years; Salvage = ₹1,00,000.
>
> The discount rate is **10% per annum**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the Net Present Value of both options using the Present Value Method. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Which option should be selected? Justify your answer using the KTU-recommended NPV criterion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Factor values for i = 10%, n = 10:**

$$
(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937
$$

$$
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 0.10, 10\right) = \frac{2.5937 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.5937} = \frac{1.5937}{0.25937} = 6.1446
$$

$$
\left(\frac{P}{F}, 0.10, 10\right) = \frac{1}{2.5937} = 0.3855
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NPV of Option X:**

$$
P_{\text{savings, X}} = 5{,}50{,}000 \times 6.1446 = ₹33{,}79{,}530
$$

$$
P_{\text{salvage, X}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3855 = ₹77{,}100
$$

$$
NPV_X = -20{,}00{,}000 + 33{,}79{,}530 + 77{,}100 = +₹14{,}56{,}630
$$

**Step 3 — Compute NPV of Option Y:**

$$
P_{\text{savings, Y}} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 = ₹24{,}57{,}840
$$

$$
P_{\text{salvage, Y}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3855 = ₹38{,}550
$$

$$
NPV_Y = -15{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}57{,}840 + 38{,}550 = +₹9{,}96{,}390
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

| Criterion | Option X (Premium) | Option Y (Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Net Present Value (₹) | **+14,56,630** | +9,96,390 |
| Decision | **Selected** ✅ | Rejected ❌ |

**Selection Rule Applied:** Since both alternatives yield positive NPV (so both are individually viable), the KTU-mandated criterion for *mutually exclusive* alternatives is to select the alternative with the **highest positive NPV**.

**Therefore, Option X (Premium LED)** is the recommended energy conservation measure, yielding an additional **₹4,60,240** in present-value benefits over Option Y.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing Present Worth with Future Worth:** Students often calculate $(F/A)$ and $(F/P)$ when the question demands $(P/A)$ and $(P/F)$. Read the question stem carefully — if it says *"in today's rupees"* or *"equivalent present cost"*, you MUST use PW factors.
> 2. **Ignoring Salvage Value:** Forgetting to discount the salvage value at year *n* to year 0. This alone can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong Decision Rule for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives:** Selecting the option with the **lowest cost** or the **shortest payback** instead of the **highest NPV**. KTU specifically tests this in Part B (a) of comparison questions.
> 4. **Sign Convention Errors:** Treating the initial investment as positive. It must be **subtracted** (outflow).
> 5. **Forgetting to state the table value:** KTU examiners expect a reference like *"(P/A, 12%, 8) = 4.9676 [from factor table]"*. Not stating it = lost 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kWh savings with kW demand charges, or annual savings with monthly tariffs. Always normalize to **₹/year** before applying factors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

- ✅ The Present Value (PV) Method discounts *all* future cash flows to time **zero** at the MARR (discount rate) **i**.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept project if **NPV ≥ 0**; for mutually exclusive options, choose the **highest NPV**.
- ✅ Master **two core factors**: $(P/F, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ and $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$.
- ✅ All other factors (A/P, F/A, A/F, F/P) are mathematically derivable from these two.
- ✅ **Standard KTU exam values:** For energy projects, *i* typically lies in the range **8% – 15%**; project life *n* usually **5 – 15 years**.
- ✅ **Three golden equations to remember:**
   1. $P = F \cdot (P/F, i, n)$
   2. $P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$
   3. $NPV = -P_0 + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i, n)$
- ✅ **Boundary check:** At *i* = 0, $(P/A, 0, n) = n$ and $(P/F, 0, n) = 1$.
- ✅ **Always cite the factor table value** with proper notation: *"(P/A, i, n) = X.XXXX"*.
- ✅ **Cash flow sign convention:** Investment = negative (outflow), Savings & Salvage = positive (inflow).
- ✅ **Comparison rule for mutually exclusive alternatives:** Highest NPV wins — *NOT* the lowest investment or shortest payback.
- ✅ The Present Value Method is **superior to the simple payback method** because it accounts for the time value of money and considers cash flows over the *entire* project life, not just up to the payback year.
- ✅ When MARR is **high** (>20%), the project is **highly sensitive** to the discount rate — small changes in *i* cause large changes in NPV.

> **End of Module 4 — Present Value Method Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme | OEEET612)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Cycle Costing Approach, Computer Aided Energy Management Systems (EMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET612 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Costing Approach, Computer Aided Energy Management Systems (EMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Life Cycle Costing (LCC) Approach — Formal Definition

**Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** is an economic engineering analysis methodology that evaluates the **total cost of ownership** of an asset, system, or project over its entire operational life span — from initial acquisition through operation, maintenance, and final disposal — by converting all future expenditures to a common time-equivalent monetary base (Present Value) using a defined **discount rate**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, LCC is the cornerstone of any energy management investment decision because it reveals that the **purchase price of equipment is typically only 5–20 %** of what the asset will actually cost over its useful life. The remaining 80–95 % is consumed by energy, maintenance, downtime, and decommissioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):** *Life Cycle Cost = Sum of all discounted cash flows (initial cost + operating cost + maintenance cost + disposal cost − salvage value) incurred over the life of the asset, expressed in Present Value terms.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car Ownership" Metaphor

Imagine you are choosing between two cars:

- **Car A** costs ₹8,00,000 new, runs 15 km/l, has cheap service costs.
- **Car B** costs ₹6,50,000 new, runs 9 km/l, has expensive imported parts.

If you only look at the **showroom price**, Car B looks cheaper. But if you plan to keep the car for **10 years** and drive 15,000 km/year, Car A's better fuel efficiency and lower service cost will actually make it **cheaper by several lakhs over its life**.

**LCC works exactly the same way for industrial equipment** — a cheaper motor or transformer today may consume ₹50 lakhs more in electricity over 20 years than a premium, more efficient model. LCC exposes this hidden cost.

## 1.3 Computer Aided Energy Management Systems (EMS) — Definition

A **Computer Aided Energy Management System (EMS)** is an integrated hardware–software platform that uses **real-time data acquisition, digital metering, supervisory control, and analytical software** to monitor, analyse, control, and optimise energy consumption across a facility or industrial plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Three-Layer EMS Architecture:**
> 1. **Field Layer** — Sensors, smart meters, CTs/PTs, data loggers
> 2. **Control Layer** — PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers, SCADA servers
> 3. **Management Layer** — EMS software, dashboards, analytics, reporting

## 1.4 Why These Two Topics Are Linked in Module 4

LCC tells you **whether** an energy-saving investment is financially justified, while EMS provides the **continuous data, monitoring, and control** that makes energy savings measurable, verifiable, and sustained over the life cycle. Together they form the **economic-engineering backbone** of any energy management programme.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world industrial practice:** A typical LCC study for a 500 kW motor replacement project in Kerala (KSEB tariff ~₹8.5/kWh) is incomplete without an EMS that continuously tracks the kWh savings, validates the LCC payback, and triggers corrective action if performance drifts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Life Cycle Cost

LCC is the sum of six distinct cost categories. Each must be discounted to present value.

1. **Initial / Acquisition Cost (IC)** — Purchase price, installation, commissioning, training. *Incurred at t = 0, so no discounting required.*
2. **Operating Cost (OC)** — Energy cost, consumables, labour. *Usually annual, discounted using the Uniform Series Present Worth factor.*
3. **Maintenance & Repair Cost (MC)** — Scheduled and unscheduled maintenance, spares, contractor fees.
4. **Downtime / Loss of Production Cost (DC)** — Cost of unscheduled outages.
5. **Decommissioning / Disposal Cost (DEC)** — Dismantling, disposal fees, environmental remediation.
6. **Salvage Value (SV)** — Residual value at end of life. *Treated as a negative cost (inflow) at year n.*

## 2.2 Time-Value-of-Money Foundations

Because money loses value over time (inflation + opportunity cost of capital), future cash flows must be **discounted** to a common present value (year 0). The **discount rate r** (also called the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return, MARR) is typically the company's cost of capital or the lending rate.

## 2.3 Engineering Economic Decision Criteria Used in LCC

- **Present Worth (PW)** — Total LCC expressed in ₹ at year 0. *Lower PW ⇒ better investment.*
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** — PW of benefits minus PW of costs. *Higher NPV ⇒ better investment.*
- **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** — LCC converted to a uniform annual cost. *Easier to compare alternatives with different lives.*
- **Benefit-to-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — PW of benefits ÷ PW of costs. *BCR > 1 ⇒ viable.*
- **Payback Period** — Time for cumulative savings to equal initial investment.
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — Discount rate that makes NPV = 0. *IRR > MARR ⇒ viable.*

## 2.4 Step-by-Step LCC Analysis Procedure (KTU Board Pattern)

1. **Define the boundary** — what system/asset is being evaluated?
2. **Identify alternatives** — e.g., Standard motor vs Premium-efficiency motor.
3. **Establish the study period (n years)** — usually the lesser of equipment life or planning horizon.
4. **Estimate initial cost (P)** — for each alternative.
5. **Estimate annual operating & maintenance cost (A)** — energy cost, spares, labour.
6. **Estimate salvage value (F)** at year n.
7. **Choose the discount rate (r)** — usually 8–12 % in Indian industrial practice.
8. **Convert all future cash flows to Present Worth.**
9. **Sum to obtain LCC for each alternative.**
10. **Select the alternative with the lowest LCC (or highest NPV).**
11. **Perform sensitivity analysis** on r, energy cost escalation, and life.

## 2.5 Computer Aided EMS — Functional Building Blocks

An EMS is a **cyber-physical system** that performs the M-A-C-D-O loop continuously:

- **M — Measure:** Smart meters, temperature/pressure/flow sensors, power analysers.
- **A — Analyse:** Software computes kWh, kVA, PF, specific energy consumption (SEC), Specific Energy Yield, Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI).
- **C — Control:** PLCs/DCS automatically adjust VFDs, valves, lighting, HVAC setpoints.
- **D — Display:** Dashboards, web HMIs, mobile apps, alarm annunciators.
- **O — Optimise:** AI/ML engines recommend load shifting, peak shaving, equipment sequencing.

> [!NOTE]
> **EMS vs SCADA vs BMS — KTU Distinction:**
> - **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** — focuses on real-time process monitoring and remote control of geographically distributed assets (e.g., substations).
> - **BMS (Building Management System)** — sub-type of EMS focused on HVAC, lighting, fire, and access in commercial buildings.
> - **EMS (Energy Management System)** — the top-level platform that integrates metering, analytics, and control for *energy* optimisation across the enterprise.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $LCC = IC + PW(OC) + PW(MC) + PW(DC) + PW(DEC) - PW(SV)$ | Master LCC equation in present worth | ₹ |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n} = F \cdot (P/F, r, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth factor | ₹ |
| 3 | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n} = A \cdot (P/A, r, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth factor | ₹ |
| 4 | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} = P \cdot (A/P, r, n)$ | Capital Recovery (Annual equivalent) | ₹/yr |
| 5 | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} = A \cdot (F/A, r, n)$ | Future Worth of a Uniform Series | ₹ |
| 6 | $F = P \cdot (1+r)^n$ | Single Payment Compound Amount | ₹ |
| 7 | $Energy\;Cost = P_{kW} \times t_h \times C_{kWh} \times E_{esc}^t$ | Annual energy cost (with escalation) | ₹ |
| 8 | $SEC = \dfrac{Total\;Energy}{Unit\;of\;Output}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 9 | $EnPI = \dfrac{Actual\;Energy}{Baseline\;Energy}$ | Energy Performance Indicator | ratio |
| 10 | $Payback = \dfrac{IC}{\Delta Annual\;Savings}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 11 | $NPV = -IC + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} - \dfrac{SV}{(1+r)^n}$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| 12 | $EAC = LCC \times (A/P, r, n)$ | Equivalent Annual Cost | ₹/yr |

> **Notation key:** $P$ = Present value, $F$ = Future value, $A$ = Annual uniform amount, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = life in years, $IC$ = Initial Cost, $E_{esc}$ = annual energy cost escalation factor.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial sector:** Choosing between IE2 vs IE4 motors, VFD retrofit, transformer upgrades, boiler fuel-switching.
- **Buildings:** HVAC chiller replacement with magnetic-bearing chillers, LED retrofit, BEE-5-star equipment selection.
- **Utilities:** Generation mix planning, transmission upgrade justification, substation modernisation.
- **EMS utility:** Real-time energy monitoring in a KSEB HT consumer facility, ISO 50001 EnPI tracking, M&V (Measurement & Verification) per IPMVP protocol.

> [!TIP]
> **Sensitivity Analysis — Board Favourite:** Always state *which variables swing the LCC result the most*. Typical sensitivity order: **discount rate (r) > energy cost escalation rate > annual O&M cost > life (n)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Payment Present Worth Factor

We start from the fundamental compound interest identity. A sum $F$ at the end of year $n$ is equivalent to a present sum $P$ that grows at rate $r$ compounded annually.

$$
F = P \cdot (1 + r)^n
$$

Rearranging to solve for $P$ (the present worth of a single future payment):

$$
P = \frac{F}{(1 + r)^n}
$$

For uniform annual cash flows, we need the **sum of a geometric series** of present values:

$$
P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A}{(1+r)^t} = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+r)} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^n} \right]
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator of the bracketed geometric sum by $(1+r)^n$ and applying the geometric series identity gives:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^n}
$$

This is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (P/A, r, n)**, the workhorse of every LCC calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, r, n)

Inverting the (P/A) factor gives the factor that converts a present cost into an **equivalent annual cost (EAC)** — extremely useful when comparing alternatives with different lives (e.g., 10-year motor vs 15-year transformer).

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{r \cdot (1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style (14-mark problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU-affiliated engineering college plans to replace an existing 75 kW standard-efficiency induction motor driving a chilled-water pump. Two alternatives are available:

| Parameter | Motor X (Standard, IE2) | Motor Y (Premium, IE4) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase + installation (₹) | 1,80,000 | 3,20,000 |
| Efficiency at full load | 89 % | 95.5 % |
| Annual operating hours | 4,500 h | 4,500 h |
| Annual maintenance cost (₹) | 12,000 | 6,000 |
| Expected life (years) | 15 | 15 |
| Salvage value (₹) | 15,000 | 40,000 |
| Electricity tariff | ₹ 8.50 / kWh | ₹ 8.50 / kWh |
| Energy cost escalation | 5 % per year | 5 % per year |
| Discount rate (MARR) | 10 % | 10 % |

**Required:** Compute the Life Cycle Cost of each motor and recommend the better alternative. Also compute the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC).

### Step 1 — Compute Input Power of Each Motor

For Motor X (IE2):
$$
P_{in,X} = \frac{75}{0.89} = 84.27 \text{ kW}
$$

For Motor Y (IE4):
$$
P_{in,Y} = \frac{75}{0.955} = 78.53 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute First-Year Energy Cost

Energy cost in year 1, without escalation, is simply:

$$
EC_1 = P_{in} \times t_h \times C_{kWh}
$$

For Motor X:
$$
EC_{1,X} = 84.27 \times 4{,}500 \times 8.50 = 32{,}23{,}327.5 \approx 32.23 \text{ lakh}
$$

For Motor Y:
$$
EC_{1,Y} = 78.53 \times 4{,}500 \times 8.50 = 30{,}03{,}772.5 \approx 30.04 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Geometric Gradient (5 %/yr Energy Cost Escalation)

Because the tariff escalates at $g = 5\%$ per year while we discount at $r = 10\%$, the effective rate for the geometric series is:

$$
r_{eff} = \frac{r - g}{1 + g} = \frac{0.10 - 0.05}{1.05} = 0.04762
$$

The present worth of a geometric gradient series is:

$$
PW = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^n}{r - g}
$$

Substituting $A_1 = EC_1$, $g = 0.05$, $r = 0.10$, $n = 15$:

$$
\frac{1+g}{1+r} = \frac{1.05}{1.10} = 0.9545
$$

$$
\left(0.9545\right)^{15} = 0.5063
$$

$$
PW = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.5063}{0.10 - 0.05} = A_1 \cdot \frac{0.4937}{0.05} = A_1 \cdot 9.874
$$

For Motor X (present worth of 15 years of energy cost):
$$
PW_{EC,X} = 32.23 \text{ lakh} \times 9.874 = 318.27 \text{ lakh}
$$

For Motor Y:
$$
PW_{EC,Y} = 30.04 \text{ lakh} \times 9.874 = 296.61 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 4 — Present Worth of Annual Maintenance Cost

The maintenance cost is a *uniform* annual series (no escalation assumed), so we use the standard (P/A, 10 %, 15) factor:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 15) = \frac{(1.10)^{15} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{15}} = \frac{4.1772 - 1}{0.41772} = 7.6061
$$

For Motor X:
$$
PW_{MC,X} = 12{,}000 \times 7.6061 = 91{,}273 \approx 0.91 \text{ lakh}
$$

For Motor Y:
$$
PW_{MC,Y} = 6{,}000 \times 7.6061 = 45{,}637 \approx 0.46 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 5 — Present Worth of Salvage Value (Subtract — It is an Inflow)

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 15) = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{15}} = \frac{1}{4.1772} = 0.2394
$$

For Motor X:
$$
PW_{SV,X} = 15{,}000 \times 0.2394 = 3{,}591 \approx 0.036 \text{ lakh}
$$

For Motor Y:
$$
PW_{SV,Y} = 40{,}000 \times 0.2394 = 9{,}576 \approx 0.096 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Total LCC

$$
LCC_X = IC_X + PW_{EC,X} + PW_{MC,X} - PW_{SV,X}
$$

$$
LCC_X = 1.80 + 318.27 + 0.91 - 0.04 = 320.94 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
LCC_Y = IC_Y + PW_{EC,Y} + PW_{MC,Y} - PW_{SV,Y}
$$

$$
LCC_Y = 3.20 + 296.61 + 0.46 - 0.10 = 300.17 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 7 — Decision

Since $LCC_Y < LCC_X$:

$$
\Delta LCC = 320.94 - 300.17 = 20.77 \text{ lakh SAVED over 15 years}
$$

**Recommendation:** **Motor Y (IE4 Premium)** is the better economic choice despite a ₹1.40 lakh higher initial cost — the efficiency saving (₹21.66 lakh present worth) and lower maintenance more than offset the premium.

### Step 8 — Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Check

The (A/P, 10 %, 15) factor is the reciprocal of (P/A, 10 %, 15):

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 15) = \frac{1}{7.6061} = 0.13147
$$

For Motor X:
$$
EAC_X = 320.94 \times 0.13147 = 42.19 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

For Motor Y:
$$
EAC_Y = 300.17 \times 0.13147 = 39.46 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

**Motor Y saves ₹2.73 lakh per year** on an equivalent annual basis — a useful figure for management reporting.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — LCC vs EMS Decision Matrix

| Decision Stage | LCC Contribution | EMS Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Option generation | Compares competing technologies on ₹/lifetime basis | Identifies *where* energy is being wasted through sub-metering |
| Feasibility study | Computes NPV, IRR, payback | Provides baseline energy data for the baseline model |
| Implementation | Locks the financial commitment | Real-time monitors commissioning & validates savings (M&V) |
| Operation phase | Schedules major overhauls/replacements | Continuous control & drift detection ensures savings persist |
| End of life | Calculates optimum replacement time | Logs degradation data (motor PF, current, vibration) to predict failure |

## 3.5 Simple Payback Verification (Cross-Check)

Annual saving (year 1) = $EC_{1,X} - EC_{1,Y} + MC_X - MC_Y$

$$
= (32.23 - 30.04) + (0.12 - 0.06) = 2.19 + 0.06 = 2.25 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

Extra initial investment = $3.20 - 1.80 = 1.40 \text{ lakh}$

$$
Simple\;Payback = \frac{1.40}{2.25} = 0.62 \text{ years} \approx 7.4 \text{ months}
$$

This extraordinarily short payback confirms the LCC result — premium motors almost always win for high-duty-cycle applications.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Life Cycle Costing (LCC) — Computational Flow

This diagram shows the **algorithmic decision flow** a KTU board examiner expects a student to reproduce when solving a 14-mark LCC problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Asset and Alternatives] --> B[Identify Initial Cost IC at t=0]
    B --> C[Estimate Annual Operating Cost OC - Energy Tariff x Hours x Power]
    C --> D[Estimate Annual Maintenance Cost MC]
    D --> E[Estimate Salvage Value SV at Year n]
    E --> F[Choose Discount Rate r - MARR]
    F --> G[Choose Study Period n years]
    G --> H[Convert OC, MC to Present Worth using P/A r n]
    H --> I[Convert SV to Present Worth using P/F r n]
    I --> J[Compute LCC = IC plus PW OC plus PW MC minus PW SV]
    J --> K{Compare LCC of Alternatives}
    K -->|Lowest LCC| L[Recommended Alternative]
    K -->|Sensitivity r and g| M[Sensitivity Analysis on r g n]
    M --> N[Compute EAC = LCC x A/P r n for Management Reporting]
    L --> O[End Decision]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Computer Aided Energy Management System (EMS) — Architecture

        SM1[Smart Energy Meter HT Side]

        SM5[Gas and Water Meters]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER["CONTROL LAYER - PLC DCS RTU"]

flowchart LR

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Life Cycle Costing (LCC). List any four cost components included in an LCC analysis of an industrial motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Life Cycle Costing is an economic analysis method that computes the **total cost of an asset over its entire useful life**, expressed in present value, by summing all discounted future cash flows.

**Four LCC cost components for a motor:**
1. **Initial cost** — purchase, installation, civil foundation, cabling, commissioning. *(0.75 m)*
2. **Operating cost** — annual electricity cost based on hours of use, load, and tariff. *(0.75 m)*
3. **Maintenance & repair cost** — lubricants, bearings, rewinding, spares, service contracts. *(0.75 m)*
4. **Disposal / salvage cost** — dismantling cost minus residual scrap value at end of life. *(0.75 m)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Computer Aided Energy Management System (EMS)? Mention its two primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Computer Aided Energy Management System is an integrated hardware-software platform that uses **smart metering, data acquisition, and analytical software** to monitor, control, and optimise energy use in real time across a plant or building. *(1.5 m)*

**Two primary functions:**
1. **Real-time monitoring and analysis** of electrical parameters (kWh, kVA, power factor, harmonics) and thermal/process parameters (steam flow, compressed air, water) at sub-circuit level, providing Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and EnPI dashboards. *(0.75 m)*
2. **Automated supervisory control** of energy-consuming equipment (VFDs, HVAC, lighting, boilers) to enforce setpoint schedules, peak-demand limiting, and load-shifting strategies. *(0.75 m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. You must answer EITHER (a) OR (b). We provide both alternatives below for exam practice.

---

### Question A (14 marks) — Internal Choice Option

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A manufacturing plant must choose between two motors for a continuous-duty pump:

| Parameter | Motor P (Standard) | Motor Q (Premium IE4) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial cost (₹) | 1,50,000 | 2,70,000 |
| Full-load efficiency | 88 % | 96 % |
| Rated power | 50 kW | 50 kW |
| Annual operating hours | 5,000 | 5,000 |
| Annual maintenance (₹) | 10,000 | 5,000 |
| Life (years) | 12 | 12 |
| Salvage value (₹) | 10,000 | 30,000 |
| Electricity tariff | ₹ 9 / kWh | ₹ 9 / kWh |
| Discount rate (MARR) | 10 % | 10 % |

Compute the **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** and **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** of each motor. Recommend the better alternative. *(14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Input power of each motor** *(1 m)*

$$
P_{in,P} = \frac{50}{0.88} = 56.82 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{in,Q} = \frac{50}{0.96} = 52.08 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Annual energy cost (no escalation — uniform series)** *(1 m)*

$$
EC_P = 56.82 \times 5{,}000 \times 9 = 25{,}56{,}900 \approx 25.57 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

$$
EC_Q = 52.08 \times 5{,}000 \times 9 = 23{,}43{,}750 \approx 23.44 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Uniform series present worth factor (P/A, 10 %, 12)** *(1 m)*

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 12) = \frac{(1.10)^{12} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{12}} = \frac{3.1384 - 1}{0.31384} = 6.8137
$$

**Step 4: Present worth of energy cost** *(1 m)*

$$
PW_{EC,P} = 25.57 \times 6.8137 = 174.22 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
PW_{EC,Q} = 23.44 \times 6.8137 = 159.71 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 5: Present worth of maintenance + present worth of salvage (negative)** *(1 m)*

$$
PW_{MC,P} = 10{,}000 \times 6.8137 = 0.68 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
PW_{MC,Q} = 5{,}000 \times 6.8137 = 0.34 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 12) = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{12}} = \frac{1}{3.1384} = 0.3186
$$

$$
PW_{SV,P} = 10{,}000 \times 0.3186 = 0.032 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
PW_{SV,Q} = 30{,}000 \times 0.3186 = 0.096 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 6: Total LCC for Motor P** *(1 m)*

$$
LCC_P = 1.50 + 174.22 + 0.68 - 0.032 = 176.37 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
LCC_Q = 2.70 + 159.71 + 0.34 - 0.096 = 162.65 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 7: Recommendation** *(1 m)*

Since $LCC_Q = 162.65$ lakh $< LCC_P = 176.37$ lakh, **Motor Q (Premium IE4) is recommended** with a lifetime saving of **₹13.72 lakh**.

#### Part (b) — EAC Comparison & Decision (7 marks)

**Step 1: Capital recovery factor (A/P, 10 %, 12)** *(1 m)*

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 12) = \frac{1}{6.8137} = 0.14676
$$

**Step 2: EAC calculation** *(2 m)*

$$
EAC_P = 176.37 \times 0.14676 = 25.89 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

$$
EAC_Q = 162.65 \times 0.14676 = 23.87 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Annual saving and simple payback** *(2 m)*

$$
\Delta EAC = 25.89 - 23.87 = 2.02 \text{ lakh/yr}
$$

Annual energy + maintenance saving (year 1) = $(25.57 - 23.44) + (0.10 - 0.05) = 2.13 + 0.05 = 2.18$ lakh/yr

$$
Simple\;Payback = \frac{2.70 - 1.50}{2.18} = 0.55 \text{ yr} \approx 6.6 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity note** *(1 m)*

At a discount rate of 10 % over 12 years, energy cost is the dominant LCC driver. A ±2 % change in tariff changes LCC by approximately ±5 % (₹8–9 lakh). The recommendation is **robust** even with a 20 % drop in energy savings.

**Step 5: Final statement** *(1 m)*

**Motor Q (IE4 Premium) yields an EAC saving of ₹2.02 lakh/year and a simple payback of less than 7 months** — economically and environmentally the correct choice. The marginal extra initial cost of ₹1.20 lakh is recovered in less than one year.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Internal Choice Option

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of a Computer Aided Energy Management System (EMS)**. List any **four benefits** of installing an EMS in a manufacturing plant. *(7 marks)*

(b) A company spends ₹45,00,000 per year on electricity. The plant engineer proposes to install a Power Monitoring System (EMS) at a cost of ₹18,00,000, which is expected to deliver sustained savings of 6 % on the electricity bill. The system has a life of 8 years and a salvage value of ₹1,50,000. Using MARR = 12 %, evaluate the proposal using **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** methods. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Solution: EMS Architecture & Benefits (7 marks)

**Step 1: Block diagram explanation** *(4 marks)*

Refer to the **EMS Architecture diagram in Section 4.2**. Describe the four layers:

- **Field Layer (1 m):** Smart meters (HT/LT sides), CTs/PTs, temperature/pressure/flow transmitters, gas and water meters. They convert physical quantities to 4–20 mA / Modbus / pulse signals.
- **Control Layer (1 m):** PLCs, RTUs, and DCS controllers gather field data and execute local control. SCADA server hosts the real-time tag database (RTDB), historian, alarms.
- **Management Layer (1 m):** EMS software computes kWh, kVA, PF, harmonics, Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), Energy Performance Indicators (EnPI), carbon footprint. Provides web dashboards, automated M&V reports, and AI-based load forecasting.
- **Enterprise Layer (1 m):** Bidirectional integration with SAP/ERP for cost-centre-wise energy cost allocation, KSEB bill verification, and a mobile app for plant managers to view real-time KPIs.

**Step 2: Four benefits of installing an EMS** *(3 marks — 0.75 m each)*

1. **Visibility & accountability** — sub-metering reveals wastage, identifies "energy vampires" (idle equipment, leaking compressed air), and assigns cost to each department.
2. **Sustained savings of 5–15 %** — automated peak-demand limiting, load shifting to off-peak hours, and PF correction scheduling reduce both kWh and kVA charges.
3. **ISO 50001 / EnPI compliance** — continuous data feeds the Energy Performance Indicator tracking required for certification and statutory audits (BEE PAT scheme).
4. **Predictive maintenance & equipment life extension** — trend analysis of motor current, vibration, and bearing temperature enables condition-based maintenance, reducing unplanned downtime by 30–50 %.

#### Part (b) — Solution: NPV & IRR Evaluation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Quantify annual savings** *(1 m)*

$$
A = 6\% \times 45{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}70{,}000 \text{ per year (assumed uniform)}
$$

**Step 2: Present worth of savings (P/A, 12 %, 8)** *(1 m)*

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 8) = \frac{(1.12)^8 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^8}
$$

$$
(1.12)^8 = 2.4760
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 8) = \frac{2.4760 - 1}{0.12 \times 2.4760} = \frac{1.4760}{0.29712} = 4.9676
$$

$$
PW_{savings} = 2{,}70{,}000 \times 4.9676 = 13{,}41{,}252
$$

**Step 3: Present worth of salvage value** *(1 m)*

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 8) = \frac{1}{(1.12)^8} = \frac{1}{2.4760} = 0.4039
$$

$$
PW_{SV} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.4039 = 60{,}585
$$

**Step 4: Compute NPV** *(1 m)*

$$
NPV = -IC + PW_{savings} + PW_{SV}
$$

$$
NPV = -18{,}00{,}000 + 13{,}41{,}252 + 60{,}585 = -3{,}98{,}163
$$

**NPV at 12 % is negative (₹ –3.98 lakh)** ⇒ the proposal is **NOT acceptable at MARR = 12 %**.

**Step 5: Compute IRR by trial** *(2 m)*

Try $r = 7\%$:

$$
(1.07)^8 = 1.7182
$$

$$
(P/A, 7\%, 8) = \frac{1.7182 - 1}{0.07 \times 1.7182} = \frac{0.7182}{0.1203} = 5.9712
$$

$$
(P/F, 7\%, 8) = \frac{1}{1.7182} = 0.5820
$$

$$
NPV_{7\%} = -18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \times 5.9712 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5820
$$

$$
= -18{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}12{,}224 + 87{,}300 = -1{,}00{,}476
$$

Try $r = 6\%$:

$$
(1.06)^8 = 1.5938
$$

$$
(P/A, 6\%, 8) = \frac{0.5938}{0.06 \times 1.5938} = \frac{0.5938}{0.0956} = 6.2098
$$

$$
(P/F, 6\%, 8) = \frac{1}{1.5938} = 0.6274
$$

$$
NPV_{6\%} = -18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \times 6.2098 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6274
$$

$$
= -18{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}76{,}646 + 94{,}110 = -29{,}244
$$

Try $r = 5.8\%$:

$$
(1.058)^8 \approx 1.5789
$$

$$
(P/A, 5.8\%, 8) \approx 6.281 \quad ; \quad (P/F, 5.8\%, 8) \approx 0.6333
$$

$$
NPV \approx -18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \times 6.281 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6333
$$

$$
= -18{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}95{,}870 + 94{,}995 \approx -9{,}135
$$

Try $r = 5.7\%$:

$$
NPV_{5.7\%} \approx +9{,}000 \text{ (approximately zero crossover)}
$$

**IRR ≈ 5.7 % to 5.8 %**, which is well below MARR = 12 %.

**Step 6: Decision** *(1 m)*

Since $IRR \approx 5.8\% < MARR = 12\%$ and $NPV < 0$ at MARR, **the EMS investment is NOT financially justified at the current 6 % saving assumption**. The plant should either (i) renegotiate for higher savings (≥ 9 % required to reach IRR ≈ 12 %), (ii) reduce system cost below ₹14.5 lakh, or (iii) increase project life beyond 8 years to improve NPV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on LCC and EMS questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract salvage value** — many candidates add it instead of subtracting (Salvage is a *negative* cost / cash inflow at year n). Costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up (P/A) and (A/P) factors** — (P/A) gives present worth of a series; (A/P) gives annual equivalent of a present sum. Wrong factor = completely wrong answer. **Costs 3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring energy cost escalation** — when the problem states tariff rises 5 %/yr, you must use the **geometric gradient series** formula, not the uniform series. **Costs 2–3 marks**.
> 4. **In EMS diagrams, drawing SCADA and EMS as the same box** — they are different layers. SCADA = control layer; EMS = management/analytics layer. **Costs 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Not stating the MARR/discount rate explicitly in the conclusion** — the examiner wants you to declare the decision criterion (e.g., "Since NPV > 0 at 12 %, the project is accepted").
> 6. **Skipping units** — always write ₹, kWh, years, etc. A numerical answer without units is treated as incomplete.
> 7. **Confusing simple payback with discounted payback** — the LCC syllabus expects **discounted payback** when r is given.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **One-page revision checklist for Module 4 — LCC & EMS:**

- **LCC** = present worth of *all* costs of owning an asset over its life.
- **Master LCC formula:** $LCC = IC + PW(OC) + PW(MC) + PW(DC) + PW(DEC) - PW(SV)$
- **Discount rate (r)** = MARR, typically 8–12 % in India. Future costs are *smaller* in present value when r is higher.
- **Three core time-value factors to memorise:**
  - $(P/F, r, n) = (1+r)^{-n}$
  - $(P/A, r, n) = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$
  - $(A/P, r, n) = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$
- **Geometric gradient** (for escalating energy tariff): $PW = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^n}{r - g}$
- **Decision rules:** Lowest LCC wins; highest NPV wins; IRR > MARR ⇒ accept; payback < target life ⇒ accept.
- **LCC procedure:** define → alternatives → study period → estimate costs → discount → compare → sensitivity.
- **EMS** = integrated IT/OT platform for real-time energy monitoring, analysis, control, display, and optimisation.
- **EMS architecture (4 layers):** Field (sensors/meters) → Control (PLC/SCADA) → Management (EMS software/dashboards) → Enterprise (ERP/MIS).
- **SCADA ≠ EMS** — SCADA is the real-time control layer; EMS is the analytics/optimisation layer on top of SCADA.
- **BMS** is a sub-type of EMS dedicated to building HVAC, lighting, and fire systems.
- **EMS benefits:** visibility, 5–15 % sustained savings, ISO 50001 EnPI compliance, predictive maintenance, peak-demand control, M&V support.
- **Key EMS metrics:** kWh, kVAh, PF, SEC (kWh/unit), EnPI (actual/baseline), carbon footprint (tCO₂).
- **Always perform sensitivity analysis** on r, g (energy escalation), and n — these three dominate the LCC result.
- **KTU board hot keywords:** "discount all future cash flows to present value", "lowest life cycle cost alternative is recommended", "EMS integrates metering, control, and analytics in real time".
- **Pitfall to avoid:** A cheaper initial price is *not* a cheaper LCC — efficiency and maintenance dominate the long-term cost.
